item.title: AI Cybersecurity and Governance Certifications Are Not Keeping Up with the Problem
item.category: "AI security" , "AI governance" , "AI certifications" , "NIST AI RMF" , "NIST AI 600-1" , "ISO 42001" , "IAPP AIGP" , "prompt injection" , "AI cybersecurity" , "LLM security" , "OWASP LLM Top 10" , "MITRE ATLAS" , "AI risk management" , "AI compliance" , "machine learning security" , "model supply chain" , "adversarial AI" , "AI agents" , "MCP security" , "AI red teaming" , "AI governance certifications" , "agentic AI" , "Google SAIF"
item.description: A professional opinion on the gap between AI governance certifications and actual AI security practice. We passed several of them and came away disappointed. The frameworks are early and governance-focused. The attack surface grew faster.
item.content:encoded: <p>We sat the exams. We passed. We came away with certificates and a level of disappointment I want to be specific about.</p> <p>This is not a complaint about the people who built these programs. They are working with incomplete material. AI security as a discipline is young. The attack research is moving faster than the defensive tooling. The governance frameworks arrived before the engineering guidance.</p> <p>The problem is the gap between what the certifications teach and what you need to know to actually secure AI systems in production.</p> <h2 id="three-layers-that-are-frequently-confused"> <a href="#three-layers-that-are-frequently-confused" title="Three Layers That Are Frequently Confused">Three Layers That Are Frequently Confused</a> <a href="#three-layers-that-are-frequently-confused" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Three Layers That Are Frequently Confused">#</a> </h2> <p>Before explaining what is missing, it helps to separate what currently exists.</p> <p>The first layer is governance. Documents like NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0, 2023), ISO/IEC 42001:2023, and the EU AI Act operate at the organizational and process level. They describe how to manage AI risk, structure oversight, and document accountability. They are intentionally governance-focused rather than control-prescriptive. That is by design.</p> <p>The second layer is threat taxonomy. MITRE ATLAS documents adversarial tactics against AI systems in the same format as ATT&CK. The OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications enumerates the attack classes most relevant to deployed LLMs. These documents name the attacks and describe how they work. They do not prescribe defenses.</p> <p>The third layer is technical guidance. This includes Google’s Secure AI Framework (SAIF), Microsoft’s AI Security SDL, OWASP AI Exchange, NIST AI 600-1 (the Generative AI Profile), and vendor-specific security documentation from Anthropic, OpenAI, Meta, and others. These provide engineering-level guidance on secure deployment, evaluation practices, and runtime controls.</p> <p>Most AI governance certifications cover the first layer thoroughly. They reference the second layer at a summary level. They rarely touch the third.</p> <h2 id="what-the-certifications-cover"> <a href="#what-the-certifications-cover" title="What the Certifications Cover">What the Certifications Cover</a> <a href="#what-the-certifications-cover" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What the Certifications Cover">#</a> </h2> <p>The AI governance and security certifications currently available, including the IAPP AI Governance Professional (AIGP), ISACA’s AI Fundamentals Certificate, ISO 42001 certifications, and CompTIA AI+, cover a consistent set of topics.</p> <p>You learn the NIST AI RMF and how to map its four functions, Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage, to your organization’s AI deployment. You learn the EU AI Act’s risk tier classifications and what conformity assessment looks like for high-risk systems. You learn about bias, fairness, transparency, and accountability as governance principles. You learn how to write AI governance policies and conduct impact assessments.</p> <p>These are real skills. Organizations need people who understand governance frameworks. They need people who read the NIST AI RMF and know what it is asking them to build.</p> <p>What the certifications do not teach with the same depth:</p> <ul> <li>How attackers currently compromise AI systems in production</li> <li>What defense-in-depth for prompt injection looks like operationally and why no single control eliminates it</li> <li>How to verify the integrity of models before deployment</li> <li>What AI-specific red teaming involves and how to scope it</li> <li>How to evaluate model behavior against adversarial inputs before launch</li> <li>What AI observability looks like at inference time</li> <li>How AI incident response differs from standard IR playbooks</li> <li>What securing AI agents with tool access and external integrations requires</li> </ul> <h2 id="the-nist-ai-rmf-is-governance-not-engineering"> <a href="#the-nist-ai-rmf-is-governance-not-engineering" title="The NIST AI RMF Is Governance, Not Engineering">The NIST AI RMF Is Governance, Not Engineering</a> <a href="#the-nist-ai-rmf-is-governance-not-engineering" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The NIST AI RMF Is Governance, Not Engineering">#</a> </h2> <p>The NIST AI RMF is a well-constructed document. NIST designed it to be technology-neutral, sector-agnostic, and applicable across different AI development approaches. This produces a framework that applies broadly.</p> <p>It also means the framework does not prescribe technical controls for specific attack classes. If your organization adopts the AI RMF fully and maps all its functions to your AI deployment, you will have documented risk processes. You will not necessarily have a defense against prompt injection on your deployed language model.</p> <p>NIST acknowledges this. NIST AI 600-1, the Generative AI Profile released in 2024, extends the AI RMF specifically for generative AI and large language models. It covers risks including prompt injection, data poisoning, and information hazards at a level of specificity the base AI RMF does not reach. If your certification covered the base AI RMF without AI 600-1, you missed the document most relevant to currently deployed systems.</p> <h2 id="iso-42001-and-the-management-system-comparison"> <a href="#iso-42001-and-the-management-system-comparison" title="ISO 42001 and the Management System Comparison">ISO 42001 and the Management System Comparison</a> <a href="#iso-42001-and-the-management-system-comparison" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="ISO 42001 and the Management System Comparison">#</a> </h2> <p>ISO 42001:2023 is an AI management system standard. It provides a structure for governing AI development and deployment at the organizational level. Security professionals will recognize the parallel to ISO 27001 for information security.</p> <p>ISO 27001 is widely adopted. Certified organizations still get breached. Certification documents that a management system exists, follows a defined process, and gets reviewed. It does not certify that the systems governed by that process resist the attacks being used against them.</p> <p>ISO 42001 provides organizational discipline. Achieving certification tells stakeholders that your AI processes are documented, reviewed, and subject to governance. It does not tell them your deployed models produce consistent outputs under adversarial conditions, your agents operate within defined trust boundaries, or your fine-tuned models were built from verified training data.</p> <p>That is the same gap ISO 27001 has. In traditional cybersecurity we learned to live with it. We should not pretend AI governance certifications close it when they share the same structural limitation.</p> <h2 id="the-eu-ai-act-creates-outcome-requirements-without-engineering-specifications"> <a href="#the-eu-ai-act-creates-outcome-requirements-without-engineering-specifications" title="The EU AI Act Creates Outcome Requirements Without Engineering Specifications">The EU AI Act Creates Outcome Requirements Without Engineering Specifications</a> <a href="#the-eu-ai-act-creates-outcome-requirements-without-engineering-specifications" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The EU AI Act Creates Outcome Requirements Without Engineering Specifications">#</a> </h2> <p>The EU AI Act sorts AI systems by risk level: unacceptable (prohibited), high risk (conformity assessment required), limited risk (transparency obligations), and minimal risk (no specific requirements).</p> <p>High-risk systems, including those used in critical infrastructure, biometric identification, employment screening, education, and law enforcement, face technical documentation requirements, human oversight obligations, and robustness requirements. The Act explicitly requires that high-risk AI systems be robust against attempts to alter behavior through adversarial manipulation.</p> <p>That requirement is in the text. The Act intentionally specifies outcomes rather than prescribing technical controls. The technical methods for demonstrating adversarial robustness across all deployment contexts do not yet have consensus answers for every system type and use case.</p> <p>Certifications built around the EU AI Act prepare you to classify AI systems, write technical documentation, and structure oversight protocols. They prepare you for audit. The engineering work that produces a system compliant with the Act’s robustness requirements sits in a different discipline than the certifications currently cover.</p> <h2 id="what-is-actually-attacking-ai-systems"> <a href="#what-is-actually-attacking-ai-systems" title="What Is Actually Attacking AI Systems">What Is Actually Attacking AI Systems</a> <a href="#what-is-actually-attacking-ai-systems" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Is Actually Attacking AI Systems">#</a> </h2> <p>MITRE ATLAS and OWASP LLM Top 10 document the operational threat landscape. These are the resources that enumerate attacks at a useful level of detail. Governance frameworks reference threats at a higher abstraction. The following comes from those security-specific sources.</p> <p>Prompt injection works by providing input to a language model that overrides or manipulates system instructions. Direct injection targets the model’s input directly. Indirect injection embeds malicious instructions in content the model retrieves, processes, or summarizes. Your RAG pipeline reads an attacker-controlled document and acts on instructions hidden in it. Your browsing agent visits an attacker-controlled page and follows its embedded directives. Your customer support bot summarizes a support article containing instructions to ignore its safety guidelines.</p> <p>There is no universally effective mitigation for prompt injection as of 2026. Defense-in-depth reduces risk: input filtering, output validation, privilege-limited tool scopes, sandboxed execution environments, and human approval gates on consequential actions. None of these eliminate the attack class. NIST, OWASP, Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, and Microsoft all recommend layered controls rather than single solutions.</p> <p>Training data poisoning introduces malicious examples into training data to degrade model behavior, introduce backdoors, or implant trigger-based behaviors. The signal for successful poisoning is often absent until the model encounters specific trigger inputs. If your organization fine-tunes models on user-generated content, retrieved documents, or third-party datasets without verifying their provenance, you face this risk.</p> <p>Model supply chain compromise is the threat most organizations treat as an afterthought. Model repositories often distribute executable code alongside model weights, and unsafe serialization formats like pickle have repeatedly created supply chain risks. Packages accompanying model downloads may install dependencies with their own vulnerabilities. Many organizations download and appear to apply significantly less supply-chain scrutiny to models from public repositories than they apply to software dependencies. The attack surface is comparable to npm but the security culture around it is much earlier.</p> <p>Model extraction allows attackers to reconstruct functionally similar models through repeated inference queries against your API. This represents both intellectual property loss and a means of studying your model offline to develop more targeted attacks.</p> <p>Membership inference allows attackers to determine with varying confidence whether specific data records were in your training set, depending on model architecture and training regime. This creates privacy risk for organizations that trained on personal information.</p> <p>Adversarial inputs manipulate model outputs through crafted perturbations. The technique is most studied in image classification but applies to text, audio, and multimodal systems. If your AI makes decisions about fraud detection, creditworthiness, medical imaging, or physical access, adversarial robustness is a security property you need to test against, not just document.</p> <p>Data leakage through AI systems is a category that deserves direct attention. RAG pipelines expose documents from your knowledge base, sometimes to users who should not have access to them. Prompt leakage from system instructions reveals operational details you intended to keep confidential. Multi-tenant AI deployments create isolation requirements that traditional application security engineers sometimes underestimate. These are operational risks that appear in deployed systems regularly.</p> <h2 id="ai-agents-change-the-attack-surface-entirely"> <a href="#ai-agents-change-the-attack-surface-entirely" title="AI Agents Change the Attack Surface Entirely">AI Agents Change the Attack Surface Entirely</a> <a href="#ai-agents-change-the-attack-surface-entirely" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="AI Agents Change the Attack Surface Entirely">#</a> </h2> <p>Most AI security certifications were written when AI systems primarily meant chatbots and classifiers. Enterprise AI in 2026 increasingly means agents.</p> <p>Agents differ from chatbots in one operationally important way: they take actions. An agent with tool access to your email system, internal databases, file systems, browser, and code execution environments is not a chatbot with more features. It is an autonomous process with significant access to real systems, operating based on language model outputs.</p> <p>OWASP now maintains a separate Agentic AI Top 10 because the threat model for agents differs enough from LLM chat applications to require separate documentation.</p> <p>Prompt injection in an agent context does not produce an unwanted text response. It produces an unwanted action. An indirect injection in a retrieved document instructs the agent to delete files, exfiltrate data, or send emails. The consequence is not an inappropriate answer. It is an unauthorized action taken against systems the agent has access to.</p> <p>The attack surface for agents includes:</p> <ul> <li>Tool invocation limits: whether the agent is restricted to a minimal set of tools appropriate for each task</li> <li>Credential scope: whether the credentials the agent holds are limited to what each task requires</li> <li>Action reversibility: whether consequential actions require human approval before execution</li> <li>Output filtering: whether the agent’s outputs are validated before they trigger downstream actions</li> <li>Sandboxing: whether the agent’s execution environment prevents unintended access to connected systems</li> </ul> <p>Most AI governance certifications do not cover agent security design at this level of specificity.</p> <h2 id="model-context-protocol-creates-a-new-enterprise-attack-surface"> <a href="#model-context-protocol-creates-a-new-enterprise-attack-surface" title="Model Context Protocol Creates a New Enterprise Attack Surface">Model Context Protocol Creates a New Enterprise Attack Surface</a> <a href="#model-context-protocol-creates-a-new-enterprise-attack-surface" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Model Context Protocol Creates a New Enterprise Attack Surface">#</a> </h2> <p>Model Context Protocol (MCP) has become a widely adopted standard for connecting AI agents to external tools, data sources, and services. MCP servers expose capabilities that agents discover and use. The integration is fast and flexible. The security implications are not always receiving equivalent attention.</p> <p>MCP-specific risks include:</p> <ul> <li>Malicious MCP servers that misrepresent their capabilities to an agent and execute unintended actions</li> <li>Tool poisoning where a legitimate MCP server returns attacker-controlled data embeds instructions in what should be data outputs</li> <li>Overprivileged tools where MCP integrations hold permissions beyond what the task requires</li> <li>Trust boundary confusion where agents receive instructions from attached MCP tools that appear equivalent to user instructions</li> </ul> <p>Organizations deploying agents with MCP integrations need a framework for evaluating MCP server trust, auditing tool permissions, and validating that tool responses are treated as data rather than instructions.</p> <h2 id="evaluation-is-the-operational-practice-certifications-skip"> <a href="#evaluation-is-the-operational-practice-certifications-skip" title="Evaluation Is the Operational Practice Certifications Skip">Evaluation Is the Operational Practice Certifications Skip</a> <a href="#evaluation-is-the-operational-practice-certifications-skip" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Evaluation Is the Operational Practice Certifications Skip">#</a> </h2> <p>AI red teaming and evaluation suites are replacing static security assessments as the primary methods for understanding AI model risk before and after deployment.</p> <p>Red teaming for AI involves:</p> <ul> <li>Structured adversarial testing of model behavior against known attack techniques</li> <li>Jailbreak benchmarking against established prompt attack datasets</li> <li>Adversarial robustness testing that measures output drift under perturbed inputs</li> <li>Behavioral regression testing between model versions</li> <li>Safety benchmark evaluation against published evaluation suites</li> </ul> <p>NIST, Anthropic, OpenAI, Microsoft, Google, and CISA all recommend AI-specific red teaming before deployment for high-risk systems. This is becoming standard expectation, not optional practice.</p> <p>None of the current AI governance certifications adequately prepare practitioners to scope, execute, or interpret a red teaming exercise against a deployed model or agent system. They describe what red teaming is. They do not teach you to do it.</p> <h2 id="ai-observability-is-a-separate-discipline"> <a href="#ai-observability-is-a-separate-discipline" title="AI Observability Is a Separate Discipline">AI Observability Is a Separate Discipline</a> <a href="#ai-observability-is-a-separate-discipline" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="AI Observability Is a Separate Discipline">#</a> </h2> <p>Traditional security logging does not transfer directly to AI systems. Monitoring an LLM or agent in production requires different data collection and different analysis.</p> <p>AI observability infrastructure covers:</p> <ul> <li>Prompt and output telemetry for anomaly detection and policy violation identification</li> <li>Tool invocation logs for agents, including what tools were called with what arguments</li> <li>Retrieval quality monitoring for RAG pipelines</li> <li>Jailbreak attempt detection and classification</li> <li>Output consistency monitoring to detect model drift between versions</li> <li>Hallucination rate tracking for applications where factual accuracy matters</li> <li>Latency patterns that may indicate prompt injection attempts inflating context size</li> </ul> <p>This is an emerging discipline. Most organizations deploying AI in 2026 have significantly less observability into their AI components than into their traditional infrastructure. Most governance certifications do not describe what adequate observability looks like for AI systems.</p> <h2 id="ai-incident-response-is-not-like-regular-ir"> <a href="#ai-incident-response-is-not-like-regular-ir" title="AI Incident Response Is Not Like Regular IR">AI Incident Response Is Not Like Regular IR</a> <a href="#ai-incident-response-is-not-like-regular-ir" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="AI Incident Response Is Not Like Regular IR">#</a> </h2> <p>When a traditional system gets compromised, your IR playbook covers containment, forensics, and recovery. AI incidents introduce questions the standard playbook does not address.</p> <p>Questions you need playbooks for before you need them:</p> <ul> <li>How do you determine whether a model was poisoned during fine-tuning</li> <li>How do you assess whether a RAG retrieval was abused to return attacker-controlled content</li> <li>How do you identify whether an agent executed unauthorized actions and what their scope was</li> <li>How do you verify whether a model update from a third-party provider changed behavior in security-relevant ways</li> <li>How do you establish what a model’s behavior was before an incident to compare to post-incident behavior</li> </ul> <p>These require preparation before the incident. They require logs and telemetry you have to set up in advance. They require AI-specific runbooks that dedicate space to forensics on model behavior, not just network traffic and endpoint logs.</p> <h2 id="the-certification-update-problem"> <a href="#the-certification-update-problem" title="The Certification Update Problem">The Certification Update Problem</a> <a href="#the-certification-update-problem" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Certification Update Problem">#</a> </h2> <p>One structural reason certifications lag behind current practice: AI security changes faster than certification update cycles allow.</p> <p>Security+, CISSP, and ISO 27001 cover domains that evolve over years. The core attack surfaces of networks, endpoints, and applications are relatively stable. AI attack techniques evolve across months. Prompt injection techniques, adversarial attack methods, and agentic attack surfaces in 2026 look different from what existed when the first AI certifications launched in 2023 and 2024.</p> <p>Certification bodies update materials on schedules. The OWASP LLM Top 10 published a significant revision within its first year. MCP did not exist as an enterprise concern when many current AI certifications were designed. Agentic AI security frameworks post-date most current certification curricula.</p> <p>This is a structural problem, not a failure of intent. You need to read primary sources on an ongoing basis rather than treat a certification as a fixed body of knowledge.</p> <h2 id="what-needs-to-be-in-ai-security-certification-content"> <a href="#what-needs-to-be-in-ai-security-certification-content" title="What Needs to Be in AI Security Certification Content">What Needs to Be in AI Security Certification Content</a> <a href="#what-needs-to-be-in-ai-security-certification-content" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Needs to Be in AI Security Certification Content">#</a> </h2> <p>For certification curricula to reflect current AI security practice, they need to cover:</p> <ul> <li>Prompt injection defense-in-depth: input filtering, output validation, tool scoping, sandboxing, and human approval gates, along with the documented limitations of each</li> <li>Model supply chain verification: unsafe serialization risks, SBOM requirements, provenance documentation, and signed artifact verification</li> <li>AI agent security architecture: trust boundaries, minimal privilege tool access, action reversibility, and monitoring requirements</li> <li>MCP and external integration security: trust evaluation for tool servers, tool permission auditing, and data vs. instruction separation</li> <li>Evaluation and red teaming: how to scope an adversarial evaluation, what benchmarks and evaluation datasets exist, and how to interpret results</li> <li>AI observability: what logs and telemetry AI systems require, and how to use them for incident detection and response</li> <li>AI-specific incident response: pre-planning for AI incident scenarios, evidence collection for model behavior questions, and recovery considerations unique to AI systems</li> <li>Data leakage prevention: RAG isolation, prompt confidentiality, multi-tenant access controls, and output filtering</li> </ul> <h2 id="what-you-should-do-right-now"> <a href="#what-you-should-do-right-now" title="What You Should Do Right Now">What You Should Do Right Now</a> <a href="#what-you-should-do-right-now" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What You Should Do Right Now">#</a> </h2> <p>If you are responsible for AI systems in your organization:</p> <p>Read the OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications and the OWASP Agentic AI Top 10. They are free. They are more operationally specific than any current paid certification curricula.</p> <p>Review MITRE ATLAS before your next threat modeling session on any AI component. Know what adversary tactics apply to your architecture before you finalize your deployment design.</p> <p>Read NIST AI 600-1. It extends the base AI RMF specifically for generative AI and is significantly more relevant to LLM and agent deployments than the base framework alone.</p> <p>Review Google SAIF, Microsoft’s AI SDL, and OWASP AI Exchange for engineering-level guidance that governance frameworks do not provide.</p> <p>Verify the provenance of every model your organization deploys. Check model cards. Scan serialization formats for known exploit classes before loading weights.</p> <p>Map every AI agent in your environment against the access it holds. An agent with read and write access to your internal knowledge base, email, and file system is a prompt injection amplifier. Minimize its credentials to what each task requires.</p> <p>Require AI-specific red teaming before deploying any model or agent into a high-consequence context. Treat it as mandatory, not optional.</p> <p>Build AI-specific incident response runbooks now, before you need them.</p> <p>Treat your governance certification as documentation of your process layer. It is not documentation of your security posture.</p> <h2 id="references"> <a href="#references" title="References">References</a> <a href="#references" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="References">#</a> </h2> <ul> <li>NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0), 2023</li> <li>NIST AI 600-1: Generative AI Profile, 2024</li> <li>NIST SP 1270: Towards a Standard for Identifying and Managing Bias in Artificial Intelligence</li> <li>ISO/IEC 42001:2023 Artificial Intelligence Management Systems</li> <li>EU AI Act, Regulation (EU) 2024/1689</li> <li>OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications, 2025</li> <li>OWASP Agentic AI Top 10</li> <li>OWASP AI Exchange</li> <li>MITRE ATLAS: Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems</li> <li>Google Secure AI Framework (SAIF)</li> <li>Microsoft AI Security SDL documentation</li> <li>CISA Guidance on AI Cybersecurity, 2024</li> <li>Barreno et al., Can Machine Learning Be Secure?, 2006</li> <li>Biggio et al., Poisoning Attacks Against Support Vector Machines, 2012</li> <li>Goodfellow et al., Explaining and Harnessing Adversarial Examples, ICLR 2015</li> <li>IAPP AI Governance Professional (AIGP) program documentation</li> <li>ISACA AI Fundamentals Certificate program documentation</li> </ul> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.ch/

item.title: MeshCore vs Meshtastic: Which Off-Grid LoRa Mesh Network Is Right for You?
item.category: "LoRa" , "Mesh Networking" , "Off-Grid Communications" , "Emergency Communications" , "Tactical Communications" , "Ham Radio" , "Open Source" , "Disaster Preparedness" , "Wireless Networks" , "Privacy"
item.description: A direct comparison of MeshCore and Meshtastic for off-grid LoRa mesh networking. Learn which protocol wins for your use case, when each falls apart, and what the real tradeoffs are in 2026.
item.content:encoded: <h2 id="the-short-answer"> <a href="#the-short-answer" title="The Short Answer">The Short Answer</a> <a href="#the-short-answer" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Short Answer">#</a> </h2> <p>Meshtastic and MeshCore both run on the same cheap LoRa hardware. Both let you send encrypted messages without cellular or internet infrastructure. <strong>The two protocols are not compatible with each other.</strong></p> <p><strong>The routing philosophy differs.</strong> Meshtastic floods packets to all nearby nodes. MeshCore routes traffic through planned infrastructure. Those different approaches produce different performance at scale.</p> <p><em>Meshtastic works for most people.</em> The mobile app is polished, the community is large, the documentation is solid, and setup takes under 20 minutes. MeshCore works better for planned deployments where airtime efficiency matters more than spontaneous self-organization.</p> <p><strong>RF fundamentals matter more than firmware.</strong> Neither protocol rescues a poorly placed antenna.</p> <hr> <h2 id="what-each-system-does"> <a href="#what-each-system-does" title="What Each System Does">What Each System Does</a> <a href="#what-each-system-does" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Each System Does">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="meshtastic"> <a href="#meshtastic" title="Meshtastic">Meshtastic</a> <a href="#meshtastic" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Meshtastic">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Meshtastic</strong> is an open-source project launched in 2020 by Kevin Hester. The firmware turns commodity LoRa hardware into a text-messaging mesh network with no internet or cellular dependency. Broadcasts use managed flooding: each node rebroadcasts packets up to a configurable hop limit. Since version 2.6, <strong>direct messages use next-hop routing</strong> after route discovery instead of flooding, which reduces airtime for point-to-point traffic.</p> <p>The firmware runs on hardware most LoRa users already own or purchase inexpensively. <strong>LILYGO T-Beam, Heltec WiFi LoRa 32, RAK WisBlock, and Seeed SenseCAP Indicator</strong> all flash Meshtastic without specialized tooling. The iOS and Android apps are well-maintained. Non-technical users get on the network in under ten minutes. The ecosystem includes MQTT bridging, Home Assistant integration, and ATAK plugins.</p> <h3 id="meshcore"> <a href="#meshcore" title="MeshCore">MeshCore</a> <a href="#meshcore" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="MeshCore">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>MeshCore</strong> gained traction in 2023-2024. The firmware runs on much of the same LoRa hardware but uses a <strong>hybrid routing architecture</strong>. Route discovery happens first. After discovery, traffic moves through repeaters toward the destination rather than fanning out to everyone. This is not pure store-and-forward in the delay-tolerant networking sense.</p> <p>Repeaters do most of the forwarding. <strong>Room servers are optional.</strong> They store group messages for clients to retrieve on reconnect. Clients carry a companion LoRa radio and connect through infrastructure when present, or communicate directly with nearby nodes when no repeater exists.</p> <hr> <h2 id="the-core-technical-difference"> <a href="#the-core-technical-difference" title="The Core Technical Difference">The Core Technical Difference</a> <a href="#the-core-technical-difference" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Core Technical Difference">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="how-meshtastic-routing-works"> <a href="#how-meshtastic-routing-works" title="How Meshtastic Routing Works">How Meshtastic Routing Works</a> <a href="#how-meshtastic-routing-works" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="How Meshtastic Routing Works">#</a> </h3> <p>Broadcasts use <strong>managed flooding</strong>. Each node rebroadcasts a packet up to the hop limit. No node needs to know the topology. New nodes join without configuration. Dead nodes get bypassed automatically. Since version 2.6, <strong>direct messages use next-hop routing</strong> after discovery, which cuts airtime for point-to-point traffic.</p> <p><strong>The actual constraint is airtime, not node count.</strong> LoRa operates on shared ISM bands with regional duty cycle limits, typically 1% in Europe. A network with frequent GPS beacons, telemetry packets, and active channel traffic saturates airtime before hitting any absolute node limit. Ten active nodes sending frequent telemetry create more contention than fifty quiet sensors. Dense meshes with several dozen active users often see increased airtime contention, but no hard ceiling exists. The threshold depends on spreading factor, airtime preset, telemetry intervals, terrain, and repeater placement.</p> <p>Meshtastic generates overhead from node info broadcasts, battery telemetry, GPS packets, and neighbor discovery. Recent firmware versions reduced this overhead. <em>Tuning those settings often solves performance problems before a protocol change becomes necessary.</em></p> <h3 id="how-meshcore-routing-works"> <a href="#how-meshcore-routing-works" title="How MeshCore Routing Works">How MeshCore Routing Works</a> <a href="#how-meshcore-routing-works" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="How MeshCore Routing Works">#</a> </h3> <p>MeshCore performs route discovery, learns routes, then routes traffic toward infrastructure rather than rebroadcasting to everyone. <strong>Repeaters forward traffic between nodes.</strong> Room servers optionally host group conversations and store messages for clients reconnecting later.</p> <p>Channel occupancy scales with active conversations, not total node count. A large deployment with many quiet nodes does not degrade performance the way a dense flooding network with high overhead does. MeshCore targets significantly larger deployments by design, though actual performance depends on deployment quality.</p> <p>Without a repeater, client nodes communicate directly when in range. <em>Infrastructure is where the architecture performs best. Infrastructure is not a hard requirement.</em></p> <hr> <h2 id="hardware"> <a href="#hardware" title="Hardware">Hardware</a> <a href="#hardware" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Hardware">#</a> </h2> <p>Both protocols run on the same popular LoRa boards.</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Board</th> <th>Meshtastic</th> <th>MeshCore</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>LILYGO T-Beam (ESP32 + SX1276/SX1262)</td> <td>Yes</td> <td>Yes</td> </tr> <tr> <td>LILYGO T-Echo (nRF52840 + SX1262)</td> <td>Yes</td> <td>Yes</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Heltec WiFi LoRa 32 V3</td> <td>Yes</td> <td>Yes</td> </tr> <tr> <td>RAK WisBlock 4631</td> <td>Yes</td> <td>Yes</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Seeed SenseCAP Indicator</td> <td>Yes</td> <td>Partial</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Station G2</td> <td>Yes</td> <td>Yes</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p><strong>You flash the same physical device with either firmware.</strong> Switching protocols requires no new hardware. Experimenting costs nothing beyond your time.</p> <hr> <h2 id="ease-of-setup"> <a href="#ease-of-setup" title="Ease of Setup">Ease of Setup</a> <a href="#ease-of-setup" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Ease of Setup">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="meshtastic-setup"> <a href="#meshtastic-setup" title="Meshtastic Setup">Meshtastic Setup</a> <a href="#meshtastic-setup" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Meshtastic Setup">#</a> </h3> <ol> <li>Download firmware from meshtastic.org</li> <li>Flash using the web flasher at flasher.meshtastic.org, no drivers needed</li> <li>Install the Meshtastic app on your phone</li> <li>Pair over Bluetooth</li> <li>Join or create a channel with a shared name and password</li> </ol> <p><strong>Total time for a first-time user: under 20 minutes.</strong> Channel encryption uses AES-256 pre-shared keys. The default public channel connects you to any Meshtastic node in range immediately. The app shows GPS positions on a map when nodes have GPS enabled.</p> <h3 id="meshcore-setup"> <a href="#meshcore-setup" title="MeshCore Setup">MeshCore Setup</a> <a href="#meshcore-setup" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="MeshCore Setup">#</a> </h3> <ol> <li>Flash your infrastructure node (repeater or room server) using PlatformIO or prebuilt binaries</li> <li>Flash your client companion radio</li> <li>Install the MeshCore app</li> <li>Configure the app with the address and credentials for your infrastructure</li> <li>Connect your companion radio over Bluetooth</li> </ol> <p><strong>Setup requires more planning.</strong> You need to understand node roles before deploying. Room servers are optional but add message persistence for offline clients. <em>Two friends who want to communicate quickly should use Meshtastic. A team deploying a base station with a rooftop repeater should use MeshCore.</em></p> <hr> <h2 id="airtime-is-the-real-constraint"> <a href="#airtime-is-the-real-constraint" title="Airtime Is the Real Constraint">Airtime Is the Real Constraint</a> <a href="#airtime-is-the-real-constraint" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Airtime Is the Real Constraint">#</a> </h2> <p>Both protocols run on LoRa. <strong>LoRa capacity comes from airtime and duty cycle budget, not node count.</strong></p> <p>The variables determining network performance:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Spreading factor.</strong> Higher values improve range but slow data rate, reducing total capacity.</li> <li><strong>Airtime preset.</strong> Long-range presets consume more duty cycle per message.</li> <li><strong>Telemetry interval.</strong> GPS, battery, and environment packets from Meshtastic add continuous overhead.</li> <li><strong>Message rate.</strong> Active users sending frequently affect performance more than passive nodes.</li> <li><strong>Repeater placement.</strong> A well-placed repeater spreads load. A poorly placed one adds to congestion.</li> <li><strong>Terrain.</strong> Nodes unable to hear each other add traffic without causing contention. Nodes within range of many others simultaneously create contention.</li> </ul> <p><em>Meshtastic minimizes planning and accepts some airtime overhead as the cost of self-organization. MeshCore requires planning and rewards planning with more predictable airtime efficiency.</em> Switching from Meshtastic to MeshCore without improving network planning does not reliably improve performance.</p> <hr> <h2 id="regulatory-considerations"> <a href="#regulatory-considerations" title="Regulatory Considerations">Regulatory Considerations</a> <a href="#regulatory-considerations" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Regulatory Considerations">#</a> </h2> <p>Performance comparisons vary by region because regulations differ:</p> <ul> <li><strong>915 MHz (US):</strong> no hard duty cycle cap under FCC Part 15, but power limits apply</li> <li><strong>868 MHz (EU):</strong> 1% duty cycle under ETSI, meaning 36 seconds of transmit time per hour per channel</li> <li><strong>433 MHz (EU/Asia):</strong> stricter limits in many regions, often 10% on certain sub-bands</li> </ul> <p><strong>The 1% duty cycle restriction in Europe is a hard regulatory constraint.</strong> Dense Meshtastic meshes in EU deployments hit duty cycle limits faster than equivalent US deployments. MeshCore’s more efficient airtime use produces a proportionally larger benefit in duty-cycle-restricted regions.</p> <hr> <h2 id="privacy-and-security"> <a href="#privacy-and-security" title="Privacy and Security">Privacy and Security</a> <a href="#privacy-and-security" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Privacy and Security">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="meshtastic-1"> <a href="#meshtastic-1" title="Meshtastic">Meshtastic</a> <a href="#meshtastic-1" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Meshtastic">#</a> </h3> <p>Meshtastic encrypts non-public channel traffic with <strong>AES-256 pre-shared keys</strong>. All messages on a channel share the same key. Anyone holding the channel key reads all messages, including GPS positions. <strong>The default public channel uses a well-known key distributed with the firmware</strong>, so public channel traffic offers no confidentiality from anyone running Meshtastic nearby.</p> <p>GPS positions on a private encrypted channel are only visible to nodes holding the key. On the public channel, position broadcasts are visible to anyone. <em>For use cases where tracking has operational consequences, this matters.</em></p> <p>Meshtastic 2.6 improved direct message routing. Group traffic still uses channel PSK. Direct messages offer better isolation but are not end-to-end encrypted in the Signal Protocol sense.</p> <h3 id="meshcore-1"> <a href="#meshcore-1" title="MeshCore">MeshCore</a> <a href="#meshcore-1" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="MeshCore">#</a> </h3> <p>MeshCore’s hybrid routing means <strong>a passive listener cannot reconstruct the full network topology</strong> by monitoring. Traffic routes toward specific infrastructure rather than fanning out. <strong>Direct messages use per-contact encryption</strong> rather than a shared channel key. Other nodes on the same infrastructure cannot decrypt your direct conversation even when sharing the same repeater.</p> <p>Infrastructure nodes relay encrypted traffic. For direct messages with per-contact encryption, the repeater forwards ciphertext without decrypting. For room traffic, room servers observe message content for rooms they host. <em>The relevant question is whether you control the room server.</em></p> <p>MeshCore is not end-to-end encrypted in the strict Signal Protocol sense. <strong>Per-contact encryption for direct messages is a meaningful improvement over channel PSK.</strong> High-stakes operational use cases should evaluate the implementation details carefully.</p> <hr> <h2 id="power-consumption"> <a href="#power-consumption" title="Power Consumption">Power Consumption</a> <a href="#power-consumption" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Power Consumption">#</a> </h2> <p>MeshCore generates less radio transmission overhead than Meshtastic with default settings. <strong>Less transmitting means better battery life on client nodes.</strong> Meshtastic’s telemetry packets, GPS broadcasts, and managed flooding each consume transmit time. You reduce Meshtastic’s overhead significantly by tuning telemetry intervals and disabling GPS broadcast on nodes without GPS. Default-for-default, <strong>MeshCore transmits less</strong>.</p> <p>A well-tuned Meshtastic deployment with minimal telemetry matches MeshCore battery performance closely. Defaults favor MeshCore for battery life.</p> <hr> <h2 id="network-resilience"> <a href="#network-resilience" title="Network Resilience">Network Resilience</a> <a href="#network-resilience" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Network Resilience">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Meshtastic’s managed flooding is resilient.</strong> Losing a relay node often has little operational impact because other nodes reroute naturally. No advance knowledge of available nodes is required. The mesh heals passively.</p> <p><strong>MeshCore relies more heavily on repeater placement.</strong> A well-designed MeshCore network with good repeater coverage performs predictably. A MeshCore network losing its only repeater in a coverage area loses connectivity for nodes depending on the repeater for routing. <em>MeshCore rewards upfront planning. Meshtastic is more forgiving of node failures.</em></p> <p>Meshtastic accepts airtime overhead in exchange for resilience. MeshCore accepts infrastructure dependency in exchange for efficiency.</p> <hr> <h2 id="gps-and-position-tracking"> <a href="#gps-and-position-tracking" title="GPS and Position Tracking">GPS and Position Tracking</a> <a href="#gps-and-position-tracking" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="GPS and Position Tracking">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Meshtastic has a stronger GPS ecosystem.</strong> The mobile app includes a map view. Community nodes feed public tracking maps like meshmap.net via MQTT gateways. GPS telemetry is a first-class feature. Position sharing for hiking groups, search-and-rescue coordination, and event tracking is well-developed.</p> <p>MeshCore does not focus on position tracking. <em>If GPS awareness is a primary requirement, Meshtastic’s ecosystem is more developed and more mature in 2026.</em></p> <hr> <h2 id="ecosystem-and-community"> <a href="#ecosystem-and-community" title="Ecosystem and Community">Ecosystem and Community</a> <a href="#ecosystem-and-community" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Ecosystem and Community">#</a> </h2> <p>Meshtastic has a <strong>larger community</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Active Discord with thousands of members</li> <li>Active subreddit at r/meshtastic</li> <li>Extensive documentation at meshtastic.org</li> <li>Third-party integrations including MQTT, Home Assistant, Node-RED, ATAK, and Python libraries</li> <li>Regular firmware releases</li> <li>Large base of community-contributed hardware guides</li> </ul> <p>MeshCore is growing but remains smaller. <em>Fewer forum posts, fewer tutorials, fewer third-party integrations exist. When something breaks, you solve problems from the firmware source or the project’s Discord rather than finding existing answers.</em></p> <p>Both projects are open source. Their licenses differ. <strong>Meshtastic uses a BSD-style license. MeshCore uses GPL.</strong> Some organizations care about this for deployment and distribution purposes.</p> <hr> <h2 id="when-meshtastic-wins"> <a href="#when-meshtastic-wins" title="When Meshtastic Wins">When Meshtastic Wins</a> <a href="#when-meshtastic-wins" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="When Meshtastic Wins">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Small informal groups.</strong> A hiking club wanting off-grid text messaging and position sharing should use Meshtastic. Setup is easy, the map view works well, and the network self-organizes.</p> <p><strong>Mobile deployments.</strong> Users moving through terrain and joining or leaving unpredictably work well with Meshtastic’s self-organizing flooding. No pre-planned infrastructure needs to accommodate moving nodes.</p> <p><strong>Consumer-facing deployments.</strong> Non-technical people self-provisioning without your help. Meshtastic’s onboarding is approachable.</p> <p><strong>Quick single-event deployment.</strong> Festival comms, race coordination, or search-and-rescue setups deployed today and torn down next week. Fast setup and zero infrastructure dependency make Meshtastic practical.</p> <p><strong>GPS tracking.</strong> For position awareness as a primary use case, Meshtastic is the right choice in 2026.</p> <hr> <h2 id="when-meshcore-wins"> <a href="#when-meshcore-wins" title="When MeshCore Wins">When MeshCore Wins</a> <a href="#when-meshcore-wins" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="When MeshCore Wins">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Planned infrastructure deployments.</strong> You have a repeater on a rooftop or a room server running on reliable power. MeshCore’s hybrid routing takes advantage of fixed infrastructure.</p> <p><strong>Large networks with dedicated infrastructure.</strong> Deployments where airtime efficiency and predictable performance at scale matter more than spontaneous self-organization.</p> <p><strong>Message persistence for intermittent connectivity.</strong> Room servers store messages and deliver them when clients reconnect. <strong>This is one of MeshCore’s most concrete practical advantages over Meshtastic.</strong></p> <p><strong>Fixed versus mobile separation.</strong> You need some nodes to be infrastructure and others to be clients. MeshCore’s node role model was built for this separation.</p> <p><strong>Duty-cycle-constrained regions.</strong> European deployments under ETSI 1% duty cycle limits benefit more from MeshCore’s efficient airtime use.</p> <p><strong>Per-contact encryption.</strong> For direct message encryption requirements, MeshCore’s model is meaningfully better than Meshtastic’s channel-PSK approach.</p> <hr> <h2 id="when-each-one-falls-apart"> <a href="#when-each-one-falls-apart" title="When Each One Falls Apart">When Each One Falls Apart</a> <a href="#when-each-one-falls-apart" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="When Each One Falls Apart">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="meshtastic-weaknesses"> <a href="#meshtastic-weaknesses" title="Meshtastic Weaknesses">Meshtastic Weaknesses</a> <a href="#meshtastic-weaknesses" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Meshtastic Weaknesses">#</a> </h3> <ul> <li><strong>Airtime contention at scale.</strong> Dense meshes with high-overhead defaults, frequent GPS broadcasts, and active channels degrade. Configuration addresses this before a protocol switch becomes necessary.</li> <li><strong>No message persistence without infrastructure.</strong> Without an MQTT gateway, missed messages stay missed.</li> <li><strong>Position privacy on the public channel.</strong> Any listener sees all GPS broadcasts. Acceptable for casual use, wrong for operational security requirements.</li> <li>Major firmware releases occasionally introduce compatibility issues until networks upgrade to a matching version.</li> </ul> <h3 id="meshcore-weaknesses"> <a href="#meshcore-weaknesses" title="MeshCore Weaknesses">MeshCore Weaknesses</a> <a href="#meshcore-weaknesses" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="MeshCore Weaknesses">#</a> </h3> <ul> <li><strong>Setup complexity.</strong> You need to understand infrastructure roles before deploying meaningfully.</li> <li><strong>Smaller community.</strong> Meshtastic’s documentation, tutorials, and third-party integrations remain more developed.</li> <li><strong>Less hardware variety.</strong> MeshCore targets a smaller supported hardware list.</li> <li><strong>Less mature mobile apps.</strong> Functional but not as polished as Meshtastic’s iOS and Android apps.</li> <li><strong>Infrastructure dependency for optimal performance.</strong> Without repeaters, clients communicate directly but routing efficiency disappears. If your group does not commit to fixed infrastructure, the architecture advantage does not materialize.</li> <li><strong>Network healing.</strong> Losing a repeater in a coverage area causes connectivity loss for dependent nodes. Meshtastic’s flooding is more forgiving of node failures.</li> <li><strong>Zero protocol interoperability.</strong> MeshCore and Meshtastic do not exchange messages. The same hardware runs either firmware, but you manage two separate networks when running both.</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="rf-matters-more-than-firmware"> <a href="#rf-matters-more-than-firmware" title="RF Matters More Than Firmware">RF Matters More Than Firmware</a> <a href="#rf-matters-more-than-firmware" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="RF Matters More Than Firmware">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Antenna quality, antenna height, feedline loss, terrain, and radio settings have a larger impact on network performance than the choice of firmware.</strong></p> <p>A well-placed Meshtastic node on a hilltop with a good antenna outperforms a poorly placed MeshCore repeater in a metal enclosure in a basement. The protocol comparison assumes roughly equivalent physical deployment quality.</p> <p>Before switching protocols to solve a performance problem, verify:</p> <ul> <li>Antenna connections are clean and low-loss</li> <li>Nodes are as high as physically practical</li> <li>Airtime preset matches your distance requirements</li> <li><strong>Telemetry intervals are not saturating the channel</strong></li> <li>Spreading factor fits your node density</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="side-by-side-summary"> <a href="#side-by-side-summary" title="Side-by-Side Summary">Side-by-Side Summary</a> <a href="#side-by-side-summary" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Side-by-Side Summary">#</a> </h2> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Factor</th> <th>Meshtastic</th> <th>MeshCore</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>Network model</td> <td>Managed flooding plus next-hop DM routing</td> <td>Hybrid route discovery plus directed forwarding</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Setup difficulty</td> <td>Easy</td> <td>Moderate to hard</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Scale design intent</td> <td>Flexible, degrades with airtime contention</td> <td>Designed for larger planned networks</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Mobile app quality</td> <td>Excellent</td> <td>Functional</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Community size</td> <td>Very large</td> <td>Growing</td> </tr> <tr> <td>GPS/position tracking</td> <td>Strong, ecosystem developed</td> <td>Minimal focus</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Message persistence</td> <td>No. Requires MQTT gateway</td> <td>Yes via room servers (optional)</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Direct message encryption</td> <td>Channel PSK</td> <td>Per-contact encryption</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Infrastructure need</td> <td>None required</td> <td>Optimized for infrastructure nodes</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Network healing</td> <td>Resilient, self-healing</td> <td>Depends on repeater placement</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Hardware support</td> <td>Broad</td> <td>Focused subset</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Best for</td> <td>Mobile, ad hoc, small groups</td> <td>Planned, fixed-infrastructure deployments</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <hr> <h2 id="which-one-to-choose"> <a href="#which-one-to-choose" title="Which One to Choose">Which One to Choose</a> <a href="#which-one-to-choose" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Which One to Choose">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Start with Meshtastic.</strong> You will be on the air in an afternoon. You will find people to test with. The app works. The documentation is excellent. Meshtastic 2.6’s next-hop routing for direct messages narrows the efficiency gap for point-to-point traffic.</p> <p><strong>Move to MeshCore when Meshtastic’s defaults do not fit your deployment.</strong> If you have dedicated repeater infrastructure, need message persistence for intermittent users, or are building a large planned network where airtime efficiency matters more than self-organization, MeshCore’s architecture fits and the setup investment pays off.</p> <p>Before switching protocols, <strong>tune your Meshtastic deployment</strong>. Reduce telemetry intervals. Disable GPS broadcast on nodes without GPS. Adjust the airtime preset for your node density. <em>Many networks with performance problems have configuration problems, not protocol problems.</em></p> <p>The two protocols serve different points on the planning-versus-spontaneity tradeoff. Meshtastic minimizes planning and accepts the airtime overhead of self-organization. MeshCore requires planning and rewards planning with more predictable performance. <strong>Neither protocol will replace the other because the use cases differ.</strong></p> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.ch/

item.title: OT, ICS, and PLC Cybersecurity Is a Problem Industry Cannot Honestly Solve
item.category: "OT security" , "ICS security" , "PLC security" , "IoT security" , "industrial cybersecurity" , "SCADA security" , "NIST 800-82" , "IEC 62443" , "NERC CIP" , "operational technology" , "critical infrastructure" , "analog sensors" , "air gap" , "legacy systems" , "cybersecurity opinion" , "industrial control systems" , "SCADA" , "Stuxnet" , "control system security" , "cyber-physical security" , "supply chain security" , "OT supply chain"
item.description: A professional opinion on why OT, ICS, and PLC cybersecurity guidance cannot keep pace with the actual problem. The systems were never designed to be secured. Compliance with written standards is not the same as being secure.
item.content:encoded: <p>I have spent enough time in industrial environments to say this plainly: <strong>most OT, ICS, and PLC cybersecurity programs are theater</strong>. They produce compliance documentation. They do not produce security. The gap between the two is where critical infrastructure gets hit.</p> <p>This is not an attack on the people writing standards. <strong>NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3</strong>, <strong>IEC 62443</strong>, and <strong>NERC CIP</strong> are technically sound documents. The problem is not the guidance. <em>The problem is what the guidance is applied to.</em></p> <h2 id="the-systems-were-built-to-work-not-to-be-secured"> <a href="#the-systems-were-built-to-work-not-to-be-secured" title="The Systems Were Built to Work, Not to Be Secured">The Systems Were Built to Work, Not to Be Secured</a> <a href="#the-systems-were-built-to-work-not-to-be-secured" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Systems Were Built to Work, Not to Be Secured">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>PLCs, SCADA systems, distributed control systems (DCS), and legacy industrial IoT hardware</strong> were designed for one thing: run reliably for a very long time. <strong>Availability was the only design goal worth discussing.</strong> Confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and logging were not requirements. In many cases they were not even concepts on the table when these systems were engineered.</p> <p>NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 (2023) is honest about this. It describes OT environments as having <em>“unique performance, reliability, and safety requirements”</em> where <em>“security cannot interfere with system operation.”</em> Read that again. <strong>The primary security guidance document for operational technology explicitly acknowledges that security comes second.</strong> This is not a flaw in the document. It is an accurate description of the environment.</p> <p>You cannot apply role-based access control to a PLC with no concept of user roles. You cannot patch firmware on hardware whose manufacturer no longer exists. <strong>Legacy serial protocols, Modbus RTU and Profibus DP among them, provide no native authentication.</strong> They transmit commands and data to whoever asks. There is no verification of who is asking.</p> <p><em>The guidance is sound. The systems often are not capable of receiving it.</em> These are not the same problem.</p> <h2 id="there-are-two-completely-different-categories-of-ot-systems"> <a href="#there-are-two-completely-different-categories-of-ot-systems" title="There Are Two Completely Different Categories of OT Systems">There Are Two Completely Different Categories of OT Systems</a> <a href="#there-are-two-completely-different-categories-of-ot-systems" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="There Are Two Completely Different Categories of OT Systems">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Legacy PLCs from the 1980s through the early 2000s</strong> were designed for isolated, physical-only operation. They run proprietary operating systems. They are often managed from engineering workstations that also touch corporate networks. Their configurations are stored in formats with no integrity verification. These systems represent a significant portion of deployed infrastructure in water treatment, energy generation, manufacturing, and transportation.</p> <p><strong>Modern security-capable controllers are different.</strong> Siemens, Schneider, Rockwell, Beckhoff, and Phoenix Contact now ship platforms with secure boot, signed firmware, role-based access control, TPM-backed identity, and encrypted communications. EtherNet/IP CIP Security, PROFINET Security Class, and OPC UA with authentication exist as shipping features on current hardware.</p> <p>I am not dismissing modern OT security engineering. Progress is real. <strong>The problem is that most of the deployed base is not modern.</strong> When people say “OT cybersecurity,” they are usually describing someone trying to secure a 20-year-old programmable controller with a cybersecurity framework written in 2023. That is the gap I am talking about.</p> <h2 id="what-actually-works"> <a href="#what-actually-works" title="What Actually Works">What Actually Works</a> <a href="#what-actually-works" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Actually Works">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Physical security and network isolation are the most reliable controls available for legacy OT environments.</strong> Every major ICS security framework says the same thing. IEC 62443 organizes OT environments into security zones with defined conduits. The intent is to make lateral movement require passing through controlled boundaries rather than sliding across a flat network.</p> <p>Network isolation reduces the network-based attack surface substantially. It does not eliminate all risk. Removable media, insider access, maintenance laptops, temporary engineering connections, and supply chain compromise all represent documented entry paths into systems with no network exposure. <strong>Stuxnet</strong>, which reached air-gapped Iranian centrifuge facilities via infected USB drives, is the canonical example of this. <em>Network isolation is necessary. It is not sufficient.</em></p> <p><strong>Human-in-the-loop monitoring of physical process parameters</strong> remains one of the most reliable detection mechanisms available. A trained operator watching pressure, temperature, and flow in real time will notice things no intrusion detection system will see, because the IDS cannot verify whether the digital value matches physical reality.</p> <p>Controls that reduce risk in the right contexts:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Data diodes</strong> allow telemetry out without allowing any inbound connections</li> <li><strong>Application whitelisting</strong> on HMI workstations restricts what executes on machines with access to control systems</li> <li><strong>Passive anomaly detection platforms</strong> from Claroty, Dragos, and Nozomi analyze traffic without touching control plane communications</li> <li><strong>Network segmentation</strong> between OT zones slows lateral movement without requiring full air gaps</li> <li><strong>Zero trust principles</strong>, referenced in NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3, add per-session verification requirements to some modern OT architectures</li> </ul> <p><em>None of these solve the underlying design constraints. They reduce risk at the edges of systems that were never built for this.</em></p> <h2 id="analog-signals-cannot-be-authenticated"> <a href="#analog-signals-cannot-be-authenticated" title="Analog Signals Cannot Be Authenticated">Analog Signals Cannot Be Authenticated</a> <a href="#analog-signals-cannot-be-authenticated" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Analog Signals Cannot Be Authenticated">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>4-20mA current loops, 0-10V signals, thermocouple outputs, and RTD readings transmit as varying electrical signals.</strong> There is no mechanism in the physical signal to verify authenticity. Anyone who puts the right signal on the wire gets believed.</p> <p>Stuxnet made this concrete. The attack manipulated PLC logic executing on the Siemens S7 controllers while simultaneously replaying previously recorded normal process data to operator interfaces. <strong>The operators watched screens showing normal readings while the centrifuges were being driven past their operational limits.</strong> The deception held long enough to cause physical damage that appeared as equipment failure rather than an attack.</p> <p>Electromagnetic interference from power cables, variable frequency drives, lightning, and improper grounding corrupts analog measurements in normal operation. IEC 61000 exists because of this. Industrial installations use shielded cabling, proper grounding, filtering, and physical separation to manage it.</p> <p>Modern smart field devices convert analog measurements to digital form internally before transmitting over HART-IP, WirelessHART, EtherNet/IP CIP Security, or OPC UA. Authenticated digital communications are available at the device level on modern hardware. <strong>The analog 4-20mA wire connecting a legacy transmitter to a legacy PLC input carries no authentication and never will.</strong> For a significant portion of deployed instrumentation, this is still the wire in use.</p> <h2 id="sensor-validation-is-a-safety-control-not-a-security-control"> <a href="#sensor-validation-is-a-safety-control-not-a-security-control" title="Sensor Validation Is a Safety Control, Not a Security Control">Sensor Validation Is a Safety Control, Not a Security Control</a> <a href="#sensor-validation-is-a-safety-control-not-a-security-control" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Sensor Validation Is a Safety Control, Not a Security Control">#</a> </h2> <p>Process safety systems perform <strong>redundant sensor voting</strong>. A 2-of-3 arrangement with two sensors reading 230 PSI and one reading 14 PSI flags the outlier. This provides limited resilience against single-point sensor manipulation. <em>It is a safety engineering control, not a cybersecurity control.</em></p> <p>Standard PLCs have no cryptographic validation for their analog inputs. A signal generator injected on the loop is indistinguishable from a legitimate transmitter. The PLC reads the current and acts on it.</p> <p><strong>Safety Instrumented Systems were supposed to be the independent last line of defense.</strong> In 2017, <strong>TRITON</strong> (also known as <strong>TRISIS</strong>) targeted Schneider Electric Triconex SIS units with the specific goal of disabling that layer. The attackers reached the safety system through the engineering workstation network. <em>The independence of the layer depended on network separation that had not been maintained.</em></p> <p>IEC 62443-3-3, IEC 62443-4-2, and the coordination with functional safety under IEC 61511 now reflect this lesson. For years, process safety and cybersecurity were treated as separate disciplines by separate groups. <strong>TRITON demonstrated in practice what independent analysis had argued in theory:</strong> an attacker who neutralizes the safety system before triggering the hazardous condition removes the last control preventing physical consequences.</p> <h2 id="your-supply-chain-is-the-vector-you-are-probably-ignoring"> <a href="#your-supply-chain-is-the-vector-you-are-probably-ignoring" title="Your Supply Chain Is the Vector You Are Probably Ignoring">Your Supply Chain Is the Vector You Are Probably Ignoring</a> <a href="#your-supply-chain-is-the-vector-you-are-probably-ignoring" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Your Supply Chain Is the Vector You Are Probably Ignoring">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Most OT security programs focus on network architecture. Most OT compromises in recent years have used entry points that network architecture does not stop.</strong></p> <p>OT supply chain risk includes:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Firmware integrity before installation</strong> — specifically whether hardware arrives with unverified firmware from the factory or distributor</li> <li><strong>Vendor remote access sessions</strong>, which remain persistent exposures at sites that rely on manufacturer support</li> <li><strong>Engineering workstations</strong> that connect to both the corporate network and the OT network, often because it is operationally convenient</li> <li>The absence of <strong>software bills of materials (SBOMs)</strong> for most legacy OT deployments, making software component tracking largely impossible</li> <li><strong>Maintenance contractors</strong> who bring laptops and USB drives into operationally isolated environments</li> <li><strong>Signed firmware update support</strong>, which most older platforms do not have</li> </ul> <p>NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 dedicates specific sections to vendor dependencies and third-party access. <em>If your air gap is intact internally but your equipment vendor maintains a persistent remote access portal into your engineering network, you do not have an air gap. You have a gap with a door in it that someone else controls.</em></p> <h2 id="hardening-legacy-systems-costs-more-than-it-should"> <a href="#hardening-legacy-systems-costs-more-than-it-should" title="Hardening Legacy Systems Costs More Than It Should">Hardening Legacy Systems Costs More Than It Should</a> <a href="#hardening-legacy-systems-costs-more-than-it-should" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Hardening Legacy Systems Costs More Than It Should">#</a> </h2> <p>Each PLC program is custom-written for a specific process. The logic that runs a refinery’s surge control system differs entirely from the logic that runs a water treatment chlorination sequence or a turbine governor. This is not a choice. Physical processes are different.</p> <p>Hardening legacy OT systems <strong>frequently costs more than anticipated</strong> because of engineering validation requirements, necessary downtime, vendor support constraints, documentation gaps, and testing cycles that exceed initial estimates. In some cases involving hardware with no vendor support, hardening costs approach or exceed the replacement cost of the system.</p> <p><strong>NERC CIP compliance for bulk electric system cyber assets costs individual utilities millions of dollars per year.</strong> A 2019 survey from the American Public Power Association documented compliance costs ranging widely, with smaller utilities reporting disproportionate burden relative to their scale. Many water systems and smaller utilities operate outside NERC CIP requirements entirely and face no comparable compliance obligation.</p> <h2 id="the-systems-should-be-replaced"> <a href="#the-systems-should-be-replaced" title="The Systems Should Be Replaced">The Systems Should Be Replaced</a> <a href="#the-systems-should-be-replaced" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Systems Should Be Replaced">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Replacing legacy OT systems is the right answer.</strong> At large facilities this means tens of millions of dollars, extended transition periods, and the risk of encoding complex operational knowledge incorrectly during migration. These are real costs and real risks.</p> <p>What industry guidance actually recommends, through CISA and ICS-CERT, is applying compensating controls while replacement planning proceeds. This is a rational response to the constraints. <em>Read plainly, it acknowledges that full security is not achievable on legacy equipment, so apply the controls that fit and plan for eventual replacement.</em></p> <p><strong>The practical reality is that many of these systems will remain in service for decades.</strong> This is a funding and policy problem. The technical community has been clear about what needs to happen. The operational budgets and replacement schedules have not kept pace.</p> <h2 id="adding-network-connectivity-to-ot-systems-often-makes-things-worse"> <a href="#adding-network-connectivity-to-ot-systems-often-makes-things-worse" title="Adding Network Connectivity to OT Systems Often Makes Things Worse">Adding Network Connectivity to OT Systems Often Makes Things Worse</a> <a href="#adding-network-connectivity-to-ot-systems-often-makes-things-worse" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Adding Network Connectivity to OT Systems Often Makes Things Worse">#</a> </h2> <p>Many organizations have added remote access and monitoring capabilities to OT environments that were originally isolated. The operational argument is straightforward: remote visibility reduces response time, and vendor support is faster with a remote connection. <strong>The security consequence is that isolated systems with no remote attack surface now have one.</strong></p> <p>The <strong>2021 Oldsmar water treatment incident</strong> happened through a TeamViewer remote access connection. The attacker changed sodium hydroxide dosing settings through a legitimate remote access tool added for convenience. The <strong>2021 Colonial Pipeline incident</strong> began with an IT network compromise. The operator shut down OT pipeline operations proactively because they could not confirm the OT network was unaffected. <em>The attack did not breach the OT network directly. The uncertainty about whether it had been breached caused the shutdown.</em></p> <p>Adding network connectivity to legacy OT systems for operational benefit, without engineering that connectivity to appropriate standards, <strong>produces more risk than the benefit justifies in many cases</strong>. The Purdue Model, while useful as a reference architecture, is no longer treated by NIST as sufficient on its own. Modern IIoT, cloud connectivity, remote operations, and hybrid architectures require more deliberate design than zone segmentation alone provides.</p> <h2 id="compliance-with-written-standards-is-not-security"> <a href="#compliance-with-written-standards-is-not-security" title="Compliance With Written Standards Is Not Security">Compliance With Written Standards Is Not Security</a> <a href="#compliance-with-written-standards-is-not-security" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Compliance With Written Standards Is Not Security">#</a> </h2> <p>NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3, IEC 62443, and NERC CIP describe the right controls for what these systems are. I am not dismissing them. I am pointing out what they say explicitly: <strong>not every OT system can implement every control.</strong> The frameworks use tiered security levels and compensating control provisions precisely because the systems they apply to frequently cannot meet the full requirements.</p> <p>The gap between what the standards describe and what a given legacy deployment can achieve is not a documentation error. The systems do not support the controls. The guidance acknowledges this. <strong>Achieving a compliant audit result on legacy OT does not mean the environment is secure.</strong> It means you documented what compensating controls are in place, and an auditor accepted them.</p> <p>NIST’s risk management framework is explicit that residual risk remains after controls are applied. Risk acceptance is one of the four outcomes in the framework alongside risk transfer, risk reduction, and risk avoidance. <em>Acknowledgment of residual risk is baked into the official guidance.</em> <strong>When someone tells you that meeting compliance requirements makes your OT environment secure, they are saying something the frameworks they are referencing do not support.</strong></p> <h2 id="what-you-should-do-with-legacy-ot"> <a href="#what-you-should-do-with-legacy-ot" title="What You Should Do With Legacy OT">What You Should Do With Legacy OT</a> <a href="#what-you-should-do-with-legacy-ot" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What You Should Do With Legacy OT">#</a> </h2> <p>If you operate legacy OT environments:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Treat network isolation as your primary control</strong> and audit everything that crosses the OT boundary</li> <li><strong>Give your engineering workstations their own hardening plan.</strong> They touch both worlds and are frequently the entry point</li> <li><strong>Control and log all removable media</strong> in OT areas</li> <li><strong>Audit vendor remote access</strong> and close every session not actively in use</li> <li>Implement redundant sensor monitoring where the process design allows</li> <li><strong>Build a replacement timeline with real cost estimates</strong>, even if replacement is years away</li> <li><strong>Stop treating compliance audit completion as a security milestone</strong></li> </ul> <p>If you are procuring new OT systems:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Write security requirements into the procurement specification</strong> before vendors respond</li> <li>Choose platforms with documented support for authenticated communications, signed firmware updates, and role-based access control</li> <li><strong>Design IT/OT boundaries as explicit conduits per IEC 62443</strong>, not as a vague “keep them separate” policy</li> <li><strong>Require SBOMs for OT software components</strong> as a contract deliverable</li> </ul> <p>The industry has documented the problem well. The standards are technically accurate. <em>The systems in the field frequently cannot receive what the standards prescribe.</em> Acknowledging that openly is the starting point for making decisions about how to manage the risk that remains.</p> <h2 id="references"> <a href="#references" title="References">References</a> <a href="#references" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="References">#</a> </h2> <ul> <li>NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3: Guide to Operational Technology (OT) Security (2023)</li> <li>IEC 62443: Industrial Automation and Control Systems Security series</li> <li>IEC 62443-3-3: System security requirements and security levels</li> <li>IEC 62443-4-2: Technical security requirements for IACS components</li> <li>IEC 61511: Functional Safety for Safety Instrumented Systems</li> <li>NERC CIP: Critical Infrastructure Protection standards for the bulk electric system</li> <li>IEC 61000: Electromagnetic Compatibility standards</li> <li>CISA ICS-CERT Advisories and Best Practices</li> <li>MITRE ATT&CK for ICS framework</li> <li>Stuxnet technical analysis, Langner Communications, 2011</li> <li>TRITON/TRISIS technical analysis, Dragos, 2017</li> <li>Oldsmar Water Treatment incident review, CISA, 2021</li> <li>American Public Power Association NERC CIP Compliance Cost Survey, 2019</li> </ul> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.ch/

item.title: The State of AI Cybersecurity in 2026: Deploy Fast, Secure Later, Pay Eventually
item.category: "AI security" , "AI cybersecurity 2026" , "prompt injection" , "AI agents" , "MCP security" , "AI supply chain" , "shadow AI" , "AI red teaming" , "LLM security" , "AI observability" , "agentic AI" , "AI threats" , "AI attacks" , "model security" , "AI governance" , "NIST AI 600-1" , "OWASP LLM" , "MITRE ATLAS" , "AI incident response" , "enterprise AI security" , "AI identity" , "context poisoning" , "tool poisoning" , "AI authorization"
item.description: A professional assessment of where AI cybersecurity actually stands in 2026. Organizations adopted AI at a pace that guidance, tooling, and operational practices did not match. The gap is real, documented, and growing.
item.content:encoded: <p>Organizations deployed AI systems throughout 2023, 2024, and 2025 at a pace that defensive guidance, security tooling, and operational practices did not match. <strong>The result in 2026 is a large, poorly instrumented attack surface connected to real business systems, with defenses that are still being assembled.</strong></p> <p>I want to be specific about what concerns me and why. This is not a general warning about AI risks. This is a description of what the actual attack surface looks like, where the gaps are documented, and what organizations need to address.</p> <h2 id="why-this-gap-exists"> <a href="#why-this-gap-exists" title="Why This Gap Exists">Why This Gap Exists</a> <a href="#why-this-gap-exists" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Why This Gap Exists">#</a> </h2> <p>Traditional software security matured over roughly three decades. Decades of incident response experience, vulnerability research, tooling development, and hard-won operational knowledge produced the frameworks, products, and practices that modern security programs build on.</p> <p><strong>Enterprise generative AI reached millions of production deployments in roughly two years.</strong></p> <p>The disciplines that make software security work — threat modeling for specific architectures, hardened deployment patterns, mature incident response playbooks, established audit and observability practices — did not have time to develop before organizations began deploying AI at scale. <em>The guidance arrived after the deployment. The tooling arrived after the guidance. The operational expertise is still developing.</em></p> <p>This is not blame.
