It is an explanation for why the gaps are structural rather than accidental.</p> <h2 id="the-four-layers-of-ai-security"> <a href="#the-four-layers-of-ai-security" title="The Four Layers of AI Security">The Four Layers of AI Security</a> <a href="#the-four-layers-of-ai-security" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Four Layers of AI Security">#</a> </h2> <p>Much of the confusion in AI security discussions comes from treating governance documents, threat taxonomy, engineering guidance, and operational controls as if they are the same thing. They are not.</p> <p><strong>Layer 1 is governance.</strong> NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001, and the EU AI Act operate at the organizational and process level. They describe how to manage AI risk, structure oversight, and document accountability. They are governance frameworks, not technical controls.</p> <p><strong>Layer 2 is threat taxonomy.</strong> MITRE ATLAS documents adversarial tactics against AI systems. The OWASP Top 10 for LLMs and the OWASP Agentic AI Top 10 enumerate specific attack classes. These documents name the attacks. They do not prescribe defenses.</p> <p><strong>Layer 3 is engineering guidance.</strong> Google SAIF, Microsoft AI SDL, OWASP AI Exchange, and NIST AI 600-1 provide guidance on how to build and deploy AI securely. NIST AI 600-1 is substantially more specific than the base AI RMF, covering prompt injection, data poisoning, and information hazards for generative AI deployments.</p> <p><strong>Layer 4 is operations.</strong> Monitoring, incident response, runtime controls, logging, least privilege, evaluation pipelines, and access governance are operational practices. They require organizational process, not just documentation.</p> <p><em>Most organizations have incomplete coverage at layers 3 and 4. That is where almost all of the operational risk lives.</em></p> <h2 id="what-is-in-production"> <a href="#what-is-in-production" title="What Is In Production">What Is In Production</a> <a href="#what-is-in-production" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Is In Production">#</a> </h2> <p>Enterprise AI in 2026 is not just chatbots. The systems in production include:</p> <ul> <li><strong>RAG systems</strong> pulling from internal document repositories, wikis, databases, and customer records</li> <li><strong>Customer-facing support agents</strong> with access to account information and case management systems</li> <li><strong>Internal productivity assistants</strong> integrated with email, calendars, file systems, and communication platforms</li> <li><strong>Code review and generation tools</strong> with access to source repositories</li> <li><strong>Automated agents</strong> running scheduled workflows with credentials to internal APIs</li> <li><strong>Document, contract, and financial data processors</strong></li> <li><strong>AI models embedded in fraud detection, hiring, and access control decisions</strong></li> </ul> <p>Each system represents a different attack surface. A RAG system over your internal knowledge base is simultaneously an information disclosure risk and a prompt injection target. <strong>An agent with email access and a persistent credential is an autonomous process with real leverage over real systems.</strong></p> <p><em>Security teams were often not involved in the decision to deploy these systems. They are frequently discovering existing AI deployments through audit rather than design review.</em></p> <h2 id="ai-is-now-on-both-sides"> <a href="#ai-is-now-on-both-sides" title="AI Is Now on Both Sides">AI Is Now on Both Sides</a> <a href="#ai-is-now-on-both-sides" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="AI Is Now on Both Sides">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>The same AI capabilities available to your security team are available to attackers.</strong></p> <p><strong>AI-assisted development</strong> reduces the time required to adapt public vulnerability disclosures into working proof-of-concepts and operational tooling. The speed of moving from reading a CVE to having functional code has dropped for anyone using these tools, including attackers.</p> <p><strong>AI-generated phishing content</strong> produces emails with better grammar, more convincing context, and fewer detectable errors than many human-written attacks. The formatting signals and linguistic patterns your users were trained to spot are less reliable when the content is AI-generated.</p> <p><strong>Voice cloning for vishing campaigns</strong> impersonates executives and colleagues in real-time calls. The barrier to entry for targeted social engineering dropped as voice synthesis quality improved and access costs fell.</p> <p><strong>Deepfake video for business email compromise</strong> has moved from theoretical to operational. Financial fraud using AI-generated video of executives authorizing transactions has been documented across multiple sectors since 2024. <em>Your awareness training was built for a different threat model.</em></p> <h2 id="prompt-injection-and-context-poisoning"> <a href="#prompt-injection-and-context-poisoning" title="Prompt Injection and Context Poisoning">Prompt Injection and Context Poisoning</a> <a href="#prompt-injection-and-context-poisoning" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Prompt Injection and Context Poisoning">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Understanding prompt injection is the starting point for understanding AI system security.</strong></p> <p>A language model follows instructions embedded in its context window. The context window includes the system prompt, conversation history, tool outputs, and retrieved documents. <strong>The model cannot reliably distinguish instructions from the application developer from instructions an attacker embedded in content the model is processing.</strong> This is the core of prompt injection as OWASP defines it.</p> <p><em>Direct prompt injection</em> targets the model’s input directly. The user provides text designed to override system instructions.</p> <p><em>Indirect prompt injection</em> is more serious for enterprise deployments. Your RAG agent retrieves a document from your knowledge base. That document contains instructions telling the agent to take a different action. Your summarization tool processes a web page containing hidden directives. Your support bot reads a customer attachment containing instructions. The agent processes the instructions and acts on them.</p> <p><strong>Context poisoning</strong> is a broader category. Attackers do not need to compromise your model to compromise your AI system. They need to get malicious content into your model’s context. This includes poisoned RAG documents, poisoned memory entries, maliciously crafted email content your agent processes, adversarial PDFs, and attacker-controlled web pages your browsing agent visits. <em>These are distinct from model poisoning. The model is fine. The context is not.</em></p> <p>Defense-in-depth reduces this risk. Input filtering, output validation, privilege-limited tool scopes, sandboxed execution, and human approval gates on consequential actions all help. <strong>None of these defenses close the attack class.</strong> OWASP, NIST, Anthropic, OpenAI, and Microsoft all recommend layered approaches because no single control is sufficient.</p> <p><em>Design for the assumption that prompt injection will succeed against some percentage of inputs. Limit the consequences accordingly.</em></p> <h2 id="ai-agents-permission-boundaries-and-the-blast-radius-problem"> <a href="#ai-agents-permission-boundaries-and-the-blast-radius-problem" title="AI Agents, Permission Boundaries, and the Blast Radius Problem">AI Agents, Permission Boundaries, and the Blast Radius Problem</a> <a href="#ai-agents-permission-boundaries-and-the-blast-radius-problem" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="AI Agents, Permission Boundaries, and the Blast Radius Problem">#</a> </h2> <p>Agents differ from chatbots in one operationally critical way: <strong>they take actions</strong>.</p> <p>An agent connected to your email, GitHub, Jira, Slack, Salesforce, AWS, and internal APIs is an autonomous process with access to the same systems your most connected employees use. <strong>A successful prompt injection against this agent does not produce an unwanted text response. It produces an unwanted action on a real system.</strong></p> <p><strong>The blast radius of a compromise is determined by what the agent has access to.</strong> Most current agent deployments hold access far above what any individual task requires. An agent that needs to read a Jira ticket should not also have write access to your GitHub main branch. An agent processing support requests should not hold credentials to your billing system.</p> <p><strong>AI authorization is a distinct problem from user authorization.</strong> Traditional applications ask whether a user is authorized for an action. Agent architectures require asking whether this agent is authorized to perform this specific action for this specific user at this specific time, based on the current context. Most current agent deployments do not implement it.</p> <p><strong>Tool poisoning</strong> is distinct from prompt injection. A malicious or compromised tool connected to an agent returns descriptions or data designed to alter the agent’s behavior. Researchers increasingly treat tool poisoning as a separate attack class from prompt injection, particularly with MCP-based integrations.</p> <p><em>Human approval workflows are supposed to be the backstop for consequential agent actions. Organizations are discovering they also face approval fatigue. When agents regularly request approval for routine actions, users begin approving automatically without reviewing the request. The backstop becomes a formality.</em></p> <h2 id="ai-identity-is-an-enterprise-security-problem"> <a href="#ai-identity-is-an-enterprise-security-problem" title="AI Identity Is an Enterprise Security Problem">AI Identity Is an Enterprise Security Problem</a> <a href="#ai-identity-is-an-enterprise-security-problem" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="AI Identity Is an Enterprise Security Problem">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Agents hold credentials.</strong> OAuth tokens, API keys, service account credentials, and cloud IAM roles all appear in AI agent deployments. These are non-human identities with real access.</p> <p>Specific gaps in current deployments:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Agent credentials are often long-lived</strong> and not rotated on schedules comparable to service accounts</li> <li><strong>Agent token scopes are frequently broader</strong> than required by the tasks the agent performs</li> <li>Audit logging for actions taken under agent identities varies widely</li> <li><strong>Credential leakage through prompts</strong> is a documented risk. An agent that includes its API keys in context or outputs exposes them to anyone who reads the output or retrieves the conversation.</li> <li>Agents obtaining additional credentials through tool calls create <strong>identity chains that are difficult to audit</strong></li> </ul> <p><em>Govern your agent identities the same way you govern privileged service accounts. That currently requires deliberate effort because most identity governance tools do not have native support for AI agent identity patterns.</em></p> <h2 id="persistent-agent-memory-creates-long-horizon-attack-surface"> <a href="#persistent-agent-memory-creates-long-horizon-attack-surface" title="Persistent Agent Memory Creates Long-Horizon Attack Surface">Persistent Agent Memory Creates Long-Horizon Attack Surface</a> <a href="#persistent-agent-memory-creates-long-horizon-attack-surface" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Persistent Agent Memory Creates Long-Horizon Attack Surface">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Agents with persistent memory present an attack surface that does not exist in stateless systems.</strong></p> <p>An attacker who can inject into an agent’s memory builds a position that persists across sessions. <em>The attack does not need to succeed in a single interaction. Influence accumulated in memory over days or weeks shapes future agent behavior.</em> This is sometimes called a <strong>long-horizon or sleeper-context attack</strong>.</p> <p>Very little operational guidance exists for this specific risk. Organizations deploying agents with persistent memory storage need to:</p> <ul> <li>Treat <strong>memory stores as high-value data</strong> that requires access controls</li> <li><strong>Validate memory content</strong> before agents act on it</li> <li>Build the ability to <strong>audit and roll back memory state</strong> into their architecture</li> </ul> <h2 id="the-model-supply-chain-is-not-treated-like-software-supply-chain"> <a href="#the-model-supply-chain-is-not-treated-like-software-supply-chain" title="The Model Supply Chain Is Not Treated Like Software Supply Chain">The Model Supply Chain Is Not Treated Like Software Supply Chain</a> <a href="#the-model-supply-chain-is-not-treated-like-software-supply-chain" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Model Supply Chain Is Not Treated Like Software Supply Chain">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Organizations downloading pre-trained models from public repositories are accepting executable AI artifacts from external sources. The scrutiny applied to these downloads does not typically match what those same organizations apply to npm, PyPI, or Maven packages.</strong></p> <p>Specific risks in model repositories:</p> <ul> <li><strong>PyTorch pickle-format model files</strong> execute arbitrary Python code during loading. This has been exploited in documented supply chain attacks. <strong>SafeTensors</strong> is the format designed to address this specifically. Prefer it when available.</li> <li>Malicious model loaders that install dependencies or execute setup code alongside the model</li> <li>Models trained on <strong>poisoned datasets</strong> producing subtly incorrect outputs in specific contexts</li> <li>Models with <strong>embedded backdoors</strong> that activate under trigger conditions</li> <li><strong>Repository name-squatting</strong> to deliver malicious models under familiar names</li> </ul> <p><em>Few organizations maintain a software bill of materials covering their AI systems.</em> Most cannot tell you what base model a production system started from, what version of the training data was used for fine-tuning, or whether the weights in deployment match the weights that were last evaluated. That level of traceability is a prerequisite for meaningful supply chain security. It is not prevalent today.</p> <h2 id="shadow-ai-creates-uncontrolled-data-flows"> <a href="#shadow-ai-creates-uncontrolled-data-flows" title="Shadow AI Creates Uncontrolled Data Flows">Shadow AI Creates Uncontrolled Data Flows</a> <a href="#shadow-ai-creates-uncontrolled-data-flows" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Shadow AI Creates Uncontrolled Data Flows">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Personal consumer AI accounts are where your data is moving without controls.</strong></p> <p>ChatGPT Enterprise, Claude Enterprise, and Microsoft Copilot for M365 include contractual protections for customer data. <strong>Personal ChatGPT, personal Claude, personal Gemini, and similar consumer accounts do not provide these guarantees by default.</strong></p> <p>Employees using personal accounts to process work documents are moving legal strategy documents, customer records, source code, financial projections, personnel decisions, and internal communications through pipelines your organization does not control. <em>Security teams frequently do not have accurate information about the volume of this activity or what categories of data are involved.</em></p> <p>Your DLP controls do not catch data moving through a web browser to a consumer AI service. Your data retention policies do not apply to conversation history on a third-party platform. <strong>Your regulatory obligations under GDPR, HIPAA, SOX, and sector-specific rules do not change based on whether the data left accidentally or through a browser tab.</strong></p> <p><em>Discovering the actual scope before building controls is the necessary first step. What you assume about this problem is almost certainly an underestimate.</em></p> <h2 id="ai-systems-leak-data-in-ways-traditional-applications-do-not"> <a href="#ai-systems-leak-data-in-ways-traditional-applications-do-not" title="AI Systems Leak Data in Ways Traditional Applications Do Not">AI Systems Leak Data in Ways Traditional Applications Do Not</a> <a href="#ai-systems-leak-data-in-ways-traditional-applications-do-not" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="AI Systems Leak Data in Ways Traditional Applications Do Not">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>RAG over-retrieval</strong> returns documents to users who should not have access to them. An employee asks a question. The retrieval component returns a document from a restricted segment of the knowledge base. The answer includes information from that document. <em>The access control failure occurred at the retrieval layer, not the application layer.</em> Many RAG deployments were built without enforcing document-level permissions matching the source system.</p> <p><strong>System prompt leakage</strong> reveals the operational instructions built into your AI product. System prompts should be treated as confidential. Credential leakage through prompts, where agent credentials appear in model context or outputs, is typically a more serious exposure than system prompt content alone.</p> <p><strong>Multi-tenant AI isolation failures</strong> occur when fine-tuned models trained on multiple customers’ data surface one customer’s information in another customer’s context. This is a documented risk category for multi-tenant SaaS AI products.</p> <p><strong>Model memorization</strong> causes models to reproduce content from training data verbatim. The risk is not eliminated, particularly in models fine-tuned on small or insufficiently de-duplicated private datasets. Organizations fine-tuning on sensitive internal data need to assess memorization risk for the specific training approach and data they use.</p> <h2 id="organizations-lack-visibility-at-inference-time"> <a href="#organizations-lack-visibility-at-inference-time" title="Organizations Lack Visibility at Inference Time">Organizations Lack Visibility at Inference Time</a> <a href="#organizations-lack-visibility-at-inference-time" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Organizations Lack Visibility at Inference Time">#</a> </h2> <p>Traditional security programs have extensive log coverage of their infrastructure. <strong>Most AI deployments do not have equivalent coverage of their AI components.</strong></p> <p>Monitoring a deployed language model or agent requires different telemetry than monitoring an application server. Organizations need to collect:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Prompt and output content</strong> in a format suitable for policy review and anomaly detection</li> <li><strong>Tool invocation logs</strong> for agents, including tool names, arguments, and responses</li> <li><strong>Retrieval logs</strong> for RAG systems, including queries, documents returned, and access control decisions</li> <li><strong>Classification signals</strong> for jailbreak and injection attempts</li> <li><strong>Output consistency monitoring</strong> to detect behavioral drift across model versions</li> <li><strong>Latency patterns</strong> that may indicate context-stuffing attempts</li> </ul> <p><em>Many organizations that deployed AI in 2023 and 2024 have HTTP status codes and latency metrics. The telemetry needed to detect or investigate an AI security incident often does not exist in those environments. Before an incident is not the time to discover this.</em></p> <h2 id="ai-incident-response-requires-its-own-playbooks"> <a href="#ai-incident-response-requires-its-own-playbooks" title="AI Incident Response Requires Its Own Playbooks">AI Incident Response Requires Its Own Playbooks</a> <a href="#ai-incident-response-requires-its-own-playbooks" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="AI Incident Response Requires Its Own Playbooks">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Your existing IR playbooks cover endpoints, networks, applications, and identity. They do not cover AI-specific scenarios.</strong></p> <p>Questions your IR team will face that current playbooks do not address:</p> <ul> <li>How do you determine whether a model was poisoned during a fine-tuning run</li> <li>How do you scope the blast radius from a successful indirect injection against an agent with write access to multiple systems</li> <li>How do you assess whether training or fine-tuning data was exfiltrated during a supply chain compromise</li> <li>How do you establish a <strong>behavioral baseline</strong> for a model to compare against post-incident behavior</li> <li>How do you respond when a model update from a third-party provider introduces behavior that appears intentional rather than accidental</li> <li>How do you determine whether an <strong>agent’s memory store was manipulated over time</strong></li> </ul> <p><em>These scenarios require preparation before they occur. You need telemetry in place before the incident. You need model behavior baselines documented before you need to compare against them.</em></p> <h2 id="evaluation-pipelines-are-becoming-standard-engineering-practice"> <a href="#evaluation-pipelines-are-becoming-standard-engineering-practice" title="Evaluation Pipelines Are Becoming Standard Engineering Practice">Evaluation Pipelines Are Becoming Standard Engineering Practice</a> <a href="#evaluation-pipelines-are-becoming-standard-engineering-practice" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Evaluation Pipelines Are Becoming Standard Engineering Practice">#</a> </h2> <p>AI security increasingly relies on <strong>structured evaluation before deployment</strong> rather than only on post-deployment monitoring.</p> <p>Pre-deployment evaluation for security includes:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Prompt injection testing</strong> against established injection datasets and your specific use case</li> <li><strong>Jailbreak benchmarking</strong> against published adversarial prompt suites</li> <li><strong>Adversarial robustness assessment</strong> for models making consequential decisions</li> <li><strong>Regression testing</strong> between model versions to identify behavioral changes</li> <li><strong>Policy evaluation</strong> against documented acceptable use requirements</li> <li><strong>Red teaming exercises</strong> conducted by humans working to defeat specific defenses</li> </ul> <p>NIST, CISA, Anthropic, OpenAI, Microsoft, and Google increasingly expect this for high-risk deployments. <em>Building minimal evaluation pipelines before high-consequence deployments is achievable at smaller scale than most organizations assume.</em> OWASP AI Exchange and the published evaluation frameworks from major AI providers are reasonable starting points.</p> <h2 id="your-ai-asset-inventory-is-probably-incomplete"> <a href="#your-ai-asset-inventory-is-probably-incomplete" title="Your AI Asset Inventory Is Probably Incomplete">Your AI Asset Inventory Is Probably Incomplete</a> <a href="#your-ai-asset-inventory-is-probably-incomplete" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Your AI Asset Inventory Is Probably Incomplete">#</a> </h2> <p>Traditional asset management covers servers, endpoints, applications, and network devices. <strong>AI systems introduce asset categories that most inventories do not yet track.</strong></p> <p>An AI system inventory needs to cover:</p> <ul> <li>Every <strong>model in deployment</strong>, including base models, fine-tuned variants, and embedding models</li> <li>All <strong>prompts and system instructions</strong> operating in production</li> <li>Every <strong>agent</strong> running in your environment and the tools it holds access to</li> <li>All <strong>RAG indexes</strong>, including what data sources they draw from and what access control is enforced at retrieval</li> <li>All <strong>vector databases</strong> and their contents</li> <li>Every <strong>MCP server or equivalent integration</strong> framework connected to any agent</li> <li><strong>External AI services</strong> your applications call, including what data passes to each</li> </ul> <p><em>You cannot govern what you have not inventoried. Most organizations doing AI security work in 2026 are discovering assets they did not know existed.</em></p> <h2 id="where-the-defense-actually-stands"> <a href="#where-the-defense-actually-stands" title="Where the Defense Actually Stands">Where the Defense Actually Stands</a> <a href="#where-the-defense-actually-stands" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Where the Defense Actually Stands">#</a> </h2> <p>The defensive tooling, frameworks, and practices for AI security are in active and meaningful development. The current state is early and improving.</p> <p><strong>NIST AI 600-1</strong> extends the base AI RMF for generative AI with substantially more operational specificity. Organizations maintaining AI compliance postures should be reading AI 600-1, not only the base framework.</p> <p><strong>MITRE ATLAS</strong> provides adversarial TTP documentation in ATT&CK format for AI systems. Pre-deployment threat modeling against ATLAS produces more precise security requirements than working from governance frameworks alone.</p> <p><strong>OWASP LLM Top 10</strong> and <strong>OWASP Agentic AI Top 10</strong> provide the most operationally specific public guidance currently available. Both are more directly useful to practitioners than most formal curricula.</p> <p><strong>AI red teaming</strong> before deployment is increasingly expected for high-risk deployments by NIST, CISA, and major AI providers. The external AI red teaming market exists and is growing for organizations without internal capability.</p> <p>AI-specific security products covering monitoring, guardrails, authorization, and runtime policy enforcement are in early commercial stages. <em>The category is real and worth evaluating. Because the market is early, marketing claims frequently exceed demonstrated capability. Evaluate against your specific deployment architecture rather than against vendor-described scenarios.</em></p> <h2 id="what-you-should-do"> <a href="#what-you-should-do" title="What You Should Do">What You Should Do</a> <a href="#what-you-should-do" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What You Should Do">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Inventory what is deployed.</strong> Know what is running, what data it accesses, what credentials it holds, what tools it calls, and what actions it takes. This is the prerequisite for everything else.</p> <p><strong>Treat AI agents as privileged accounts.</strong> Apply least privilege. Scope credentials to the minimum access required for each task. Audit what every agent holds access to and remove what is not required.</p> <p><strong>Implement AI-specific observability before deployment</strong>, not after an incident. Prompt and output logging, tool invocation logging, and retrieval logging are the minimum telemetry for security analysis.</p> <p><strong>Assess your shadow AI exposure.</strong> Find out which AI services employees use for work tasks. Determine what categories of data are moving through personal accounts. Build policy and controls based on actual findings.</p> <p><strong>Enforce document-level access controls in RAG systems.</strong> If your retrieval layer does not enforce the access rules of your source systems, fix it before it surfaces a restricted document to an unauthorized user.</p> <p><strong>Audit your model supply chain.</strong> Document every base model in use. Prefer SafeTensors over pickle formats. Apply supply chain scrutiny to model artifacts comparable to what you apply to software dependencies.</p> <p><strong>Govern agent identities.</strong> Manage agent OAuth tokens and API keys with the same lifecycle, scope review, and rotation practices you apply to privileged service accounts.</p> <p><strong>Build AI-specific IR runbooks now.</strong> Define before an incident how you would investigate AI-specific scenarios, what evidence you need, and what your response options are.</p> <p><strong>Run evaluation before deploying AI to high-consequence contexts.</strong> Start with available public frameworks if you do not have internal tooling.</p> <p><em>Do not treat governance compliance as a security posture. Governance frameworks describe process and risk management. They do not describe technically defensive systems. Both are required.</em></p> <h2 id="references"> <a href="#references" title="References">References</a> <a href="#references" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="References">#</a> </h2> <ul> <li>NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0), 2023</li> <li>NIST AI 600-1: Generative AI Profile, 2024</li> <li>OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications, 2025</li> <li>OWASP Agentic AI Top 10</li> <li>OWASP AI Exchange</li> <li>MITRE ATLAS: Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems</li> <li>Google Secure AI Framework (SAIF)</li> <li>Microsoft AI Security SDL</li> <li>CISA Guidance on AI Cybersecurity, 2024</li> <li>ISO/IEC 42001:2023 Artificial Intelligence Management Systems</li> <li>EU AI Act, Regulation (EU) 2024/1689</li> <li>SafeTensors format documentation, Hugging Face</li> </ul> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.ch/

item.title: How to Humanize AI Writing: The Best Free Prompt to Fix AI Text
item.category: "humanize ai writing" , "ai writing prompt" , "chatgpt prompt" , "ai text humanizer" , "remove ai writing signs" , "ai content" , "llm prompts" , "chatgpt tips" , "ai writing style" , "prompt engineering" , "ai copywriting" , "ai slop" , "chatgpt customization" , "ai text improvement" , "writing prompt" , "content creation" , "ai tools" , "chatgpt settings" , "custom instructions chatgpt" , "ai writing guide"
item.description: Stop your AI-generated text from sounding like a robot. Use this free prompt with ChatGPT, Claude, or any LLM to remove telltale AI writing signs and produce clean, direct, human-sounding output.
item.content:encoded: <p>AI writes in a way that most humans immediately recognize. <strong>Em dashes everywhere. Bold text for emphasis. Words like “groundbreaking,” “delve,” and “revolutionize.”</strong> Hashtags on LinkedIn posts that nobody asked for.</p> <p>If you copy-paste raw AI output and publish it without editing, readers notice. It signals low effort. <em>It buries your actual ideas under a layer of generic filler.</em></p> <p>The good news: <strong>you do not need to pay for a separate tool to fix this.</strong> One well-structured prompt, saved once to your AI settings, changes the output quality across every conversation.</p> <p>This article walks you through what that prompt looks like, why each rule in it matters, and how to save it so ChatGPT uses it automatically.</p> <h2 id="why-ai-text-sounds-like-ai-text"> <a href="#why-ai-text-sounds-like-ai-text" title="Why AI Text Sounds Like AI Text">Why AI Text Sounds Like AI Text</a> <a href="#why-ai-text-sounds-like-ai-text" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Why AI Text Sounds Like AI Text">#</a> </h2> <p>Large language models are trained on enormous amounts of web content. Over time, they learn that certain patterns appear in “good” writing, even when those patterns are overused clichés.</p> <p>A few of the <strong>most common AI tells</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Em dashes</strong> used to connect every clause, often where a period or comma would work better</li> <li><strong>Bold markdown formatting</strong> applied to random phrases mid-sentence</li> <li>Opening lines like <em>“In today’s fast-paced world…”</em> or <em>“In conclusion…”</em></li> <li>Words that signal vagueness: “may,” “could,” “perhaps,” “certainly”</li> <li><strong>Marketing hype</strong> that overstates everything: “unlock,” “revolutionize,” “game-changer”</li> <li>Hashtags appended to LinkedIn posts even when they add nothing</li> </ul> <p>None of these are technically wrong. <em>They are just signals that a model defaulted to trained patterns instead of producing something direct and specific.</em></p> <p><strong>The fix is to tell the model exactly what you do not want.</strong></p> <h2 id="the-prompt"> <a href="#the-prompt" title="The Prompt">The Prompt</a> <a href="#the-prompt" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Prompt">#</a> </h2> <p>Use this prompt with any LLM. Append it to your request, paste it into your system instructions, or save it to ChatGPT’s custom instructions. <strong>It works best for social media posts, blog articles, and marketing copy.</strong></p> <pre tabindex="0"><code># FOLLOW THIS WRITING STYLE: • SHOULD use clear, simple language. • SHOULD be spartan and informative. • SHOULD use short, impactful sentences. • SHOULD use active voice; avoid passive voice. • SHOULD focus on practical, actionable insights. • SHOULD use bullet point lists in social media posts. • SHOULD use data and examples to support claims when possible. • SHOULD use "you" and "your" to directly address the reader. • AVOID using em dashes (—) anywhere in your response. Use only commas, periods, or other standard punctuation. If you need to connect ideas, use a period or a semicolon, but never an em dash. • AVOID constructions like "...not just this, but also this". • AVOID metaphors and clichés. • AVOID generalizations. • AVOID common setup language in any sentence, including: in conclusion, in closing, etc. • AVOID output warnings or notes, just the output requested. • AVOID unnecessary adjectives and adverbs. • AVOID hashtags. • AVOID semicolons. • AVOID markdown. • AVOID asterisks. • AVOID these words: "can, may, just, that, very, really, literally, actually, certainly, probably, basically, could, maybe, delve, embark, enlightening, esteemed, shed light, craft, crafting, imagine, realm, game-changer, unlock, discover, skyrocket, abyss, not alone, in a world where, revolutionize, disruptive, utilize, utilizing, dive deep, tapestry, illuminate, unveil, pivotal, intricate, elucidate, hence, furthermore, realm, however, harness, exciting, groundbreaking, cutting-edge, remarkable, it, remains to be seen, glimpse into, navigating, landscape, stark, testament, in summary, in conclusion, moreover, boost, skyrocketing, opened up, powerful, inquiries, ever-evolving" # IMPORTANT: Review your response and ensure no em dashes! </code></pre><h2 id="what-each-rule-does"> <a href="#what-each-rule-does" title="What Each Rule Does">What Each Rule Does</a> <a href="#what-each-rule-does" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Each Rule Does">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Active voice over passive voice</strong></p> <p>Passive voice reads as evasive. <em>“Mistakes were made”</em> vs. <em>“We made mistakes.”</em> Active voice is more direct and easier to read.</p> <p><strong>Short sentences</strong></p> <p>Long, stacked sentences slow readers down. Short sentences move faster. <strong>They also force you to cut filler.</strong></p> <p><strong>No em dashes</strong></p> <p><strong>Em dashes are the single most common AI writing tell.</strong> The model uses them constantly to link clauses instead of breaking them into separate sentences. Banning them forces cleaner sentence construction.</p> <p><strong>No markdown or asterisks</strong></p> <p>AI formatting often includes bold phrases mid-paragraph. This formatting is designed for documentation or code, not prose. <em>In plain text social posts or email copy, it looks out of place.</em></p> <p><strong>The banned word list</strong></p> <p>This list targets two categories:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Vague filler words:</strong> “may,” “could,” “perhaps,” “just,” “very,” “basically”</li> <li><strong>Marketing hype words:</strong> “revolutionize,” “groundbreaking,” “game-changer,” “unlock,” “powerful”</li> </ol> <p>Removing these forces the model to replace vague claims with specific ones. Instead of <em>“this tool is powerful and revolutionary,”</em> you get something like <em>“this tool reduced our deployment time from 4 hours to 20 minutes.”</em></p> <p><strong>No hashtags</strong></p> <p>Hashtags are rarely useful outside of Instagram or Twitter. On LinkedIn, they look spammy. <em>Most AI models default to adding them anyway.</em></p> <p><strong>No “in conclusion” or similar</strong></p> <p>These phrases add length without adding information. <strong>Good writing does not need to announce that it is ending.</strong></p> <h2 id="adapting-the-prompt-for-different-content-types"> <a href="#adapting-the-prompt-for-different-content-types" title="Adapting the Prompt for Different Content Types">Adapting the Prompt for Different Content Types</a> <a href="#adapting-the-prompt-for-different-content-types" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Adapting the Prompt for Different Content Types">#</a> </h2> <p>The default prompt is tuned for short-form content: social media posts, LinkedIn updates, email copy, and blog introductions.</p> <p>If you write <strong>long-form content</strong> like reports, research summaries, or technical documentation, remove these lines:</p> <ul> <li><code>SHOULD be spartan and informative.</code></li> <li><code>SHOULD use short, impactful sentences.</code></li> <li><code>SHOULD use bullet point lists in social media posts.</code></li> </ul> <p>Long-form writing benefits from more developed paragraphs. <strong>The rest of the rules still apply.</strong></p> <p><strong>Adding your own voice</strong></p> <p>The prompt removes obvious AI patterns. <em>It does not add your personality.</em></p> <p>To go further, <strong>append a short writing sample</strong> at the end of the prompt. Three to five paragraphs of your own writing, labeled clearly as a style example. The model will pick up on your sentence rhythm, word choices, and tone.</p> <p>You get cleaner output without a generic feel.</p> <h2 id="how-to-save-it-to-chatgpt"> <a href="#how-to-save-it-to-chatgpt" title="How to Save It to ChatGPT">How to Save It to ChatGPT</a> <a href="#how-to-save-it-to-chatgpt" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="How to Save It to ChatGPT">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>You do not need to paste this prompt into every conversation.</strong> ChatGPT lets you save custom instructions that apply automatically to every new chat.</p> <p><strong>Steps:</strong></p> <ol> <li>Open ChatGPT and click your profile name in the bottom-left corner</li> <li>Click <strong>“Customize ChatGPT”</strong></li> <li>Find the input labeled <strong>“What traits should ChatGPT have?”</strong></li> <li>Paste the prompt into that field</li> <li>Trim it down to fit the <strong>1,500 character limit</strong> if needed</li> <li>Click <strong>“Save”</strong></li> </ol> <p>After saving, open a new conversation. Ask ChatGPT to write anything. <em>The output should be noticeably different: shorter sentences, no em dashes, no hype language, no hashtags.</em></p> <p>The custom instructions apply to every new chat automatically. You do not have to remember to add the prompt each time.</p> <p><strong>Fitting within the character limit</strong></p> <p>The full prompt is slightly over 1,500 characters. To trim it:</p> <ul> <li>Remove the rules you care least about</li> <li>Shorten the banned word list to your highest-priority terms</li> <li><strong>Keep the em dash rule and the markdown/asterisk rules</strong>, as these address the most common issues</li> </ul> <p>Other LLMs like Claude and Gemini have similar system prompt or instruction features. The same prompt works in those interfaces with minor formatting adjustments.</p> <h2 id="a-practical-test"> <a href="#a-practical-test" title="A Practical Test">A Practical Test</a> <a href="#a-practical-test" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="A Practical Test">#</a> </h2> <p>To see the difference clearly, <strong>run the same request twice</strong>.</p> <p>First, without the prompt: ask ChatGPT to write a short LinkedIn post about a topic you know well.</p> <p>Then, with the prompt appended: run the same request again.</p> <p>Compare the outputs side by side. Look for:</p> <ul> <li>How many <strong>em dashes</strong> appear</li> <li>Whether the post ends with <strong>hashtags</strong></li> <li>Whether phrases like <em>“in today’s landscape”</em> or <em>“it’s time to unlock”</em> appear</li> <li>Whether the sentences are <strong>shorter and more direct</strong></li> </ul> <p><em>The difference is visible after a single test.</em> The humanized version reads faster, makes stronger claims without fluff, and does not immediately read as generated by a model.</p> <h2 id="what-this-prompt-does-not-fix"> <a href="#what-this-prompt-does-not-fix" title="What This Prompt Does Not Fix">What This Prompt Does Not Fix</a> <a href="#what-this-prompt-does-not-fix" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What This Prompt Does Not Fix">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>No prompt removes all AI writing patterns completely.</strong></p> <p>A model following these rules might still:</p> <ul> <li>Use slightly unnatural word choices in places</li> <li>Overuse a particular sentence structure throughout a long piece</li> <li>Miss the specific experience and opinion that comes from having lived through what you are writing about</li> </ul> <p><em>The prompt is a floor, not a ceiling.</em> It eliminates the easiest-to-spot patterns. Getting to truly natural writing still requires reviewing and editing the output yourself.</p> <p><strong>Use the prompt to get a cleaner first draft. Edit that draft with your own perspective before publishing.</strong></p> <h2 id="summary"> <a href="#summary" title="Summary">Summary</a> <a href="#summary" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Summary">#</a> </h2> <p>AI writing is recognizable because models default to trained patterns: em dashes, bold formatting, hype words, and generic openers. These patterns signal to readers that no human thought went into the content.</p> <p>The prompt above removes those patterns by giving the model explicit rules about what to avoid. <strong>Saving it to your ChatGPT custom instructions means every conversation starts with those rules applied automatically.</strong></p> <p>Start with the full prompt. Remove rules that do not apply to your writing type. Add your own writing samples to push the output closer to your voice. Edit the result before publishing.</p> <p><em>The goal is not to make your AI sound human. The goal is to make your ideas land without the reader getting distracted by obvious machine habits.</em></p> <h2 id="source"> <a href="#source" title="Source">Source</a> <a href="#source" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Source">#</a> </h2> <p>This article is based on the original prompt and guide published by Sabrina Ramonov: <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Best AI Prompt to Humanize AI Writing"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://www.sabrina.dev/p/best-ai-prompt-to-humanize-ai-writing"> <a href="https://www.sabrina.dev/p/best-ai-prompt-to-humanize-ai-writing" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Best AI Prompt to Humanize AI Writing</a> </span> .</p> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.ch/

item.title: Eye Spy: Passive Surveillance Detector for the M5Stack Atom Lite (ESP32)
item.category: "Privacy Tools" , "Counter-Surveillance" , "IoT Security" , "Embedded Systems" , "Security Research" , "WiFi Security" , "Bluetooth Security" , "ESP32 Projects" , "Hardware Security" , "Open Source Security"
item.description: A complete technical reference for Eye Spy v1.1 - an open-source passive BLE and WiFi surveillance detector running on the M5Stack Atom Lite (ESP32-PICO-D4) that scans for body cameras, ALPR systems, AirTags, drones, and hidden cameras using a confidence-score threat model and a single RGB LED.
item.content:encoded: <p><strong>A Thumb-Sized Passive Sensor That Tells You When Something Is Watching</strong></p> <h2 id="introduction-the-surveillance-landscape-you-cant-see"> <a href="#introduction-the-surveillance-landscape-you-cant-see" title="Introduction: The Surveillance Landscape You Can’t See">Introduction: The Surveillance Landscape You Can’t See</a> <a href="#introduction-the-surveillance-landscape-you-cant-see" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Introduction: The Surveillance Landscape You Can’t See">#</a> </h2> <p>The physical world is increasingly instrumented with devices that watch, record, and track - license-plate readers on street corners, body cameras on law enforcement, rental property cameras, commercial AirTag-style trackers hidden in bags or cars, and commercial surveillance cameras at every retail entrance. Most of these devices communicate wirelessly over <strong>Bluetooth LE</strong> or <strong>WiFi</strong>, and <em>most of those communications are broadcast into the open air for anyone with the right receiver to detect</em>.</p> <p> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Eye Spy"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://github.com/simeononsecurity/eye-spy"> <a href="https://github.com/simeononsecurity/eye-spy" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" ><strong>Eye Spy</strong></a> </span> is a passive surveillance detection tool that exploits exactly this fact. Running on the <strong>M5Stack Atom Lite</strong> - an ESP32-PICO-D4 development board roughly the size of a sugar cube - Eye Spy continuously monitors the BLE and WiFi spectrums for the electronic signatures of recording devices, surveillance cameras, <strong>ALPR</strong> (automatic license plate reader) systems, drones, and personal trackers. When it finds something, its RGB LED changes color.</p> <p><em>It doesn’t connect to anything. It doesn’t transmit.</em> It watches, scores, and lights up.</p> <p>This article is a complete technical reference: what Eye Spy detects, how the confidence-score system works, the engineering behind each detection engine, how to build and flash it, and what its practical limitations are.</p> <hr> <h2 id="led-indicators-the-entire-user-interface"> <a href="#led-indicators-the-entire-user-interface" title="LED Indicators: The Entire User Interface">LED Indicators: The Entire User Interface</a> <a href="#led-indicators-the-entire-user-interface" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="LED Indicators: The Entire User Interface">#</a> </h2> <p>Like the <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="ESP32 WiFi Canary"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://simeononsecurity.com/articles/esp32-wifi-canary-passive-wifi-threat-detection-2026/"> <a href="https://simeononsecurity.com/articles/esp32-wifi-canary-passive-wifi-threat-detection-2026/" rel="follow me" >ESP32 WiFi Canary</a> </span> , Eye Spy’s entire output is a single SK6812 RGB NeoPixel on GPIO 27 of the M5Stack Atom Lite. The LED communicates a four-state threat level at all times:</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Color</th> <th>Meaning</th> <th>Score Range</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>🔵 Blue pulse</td> <td>Startup / first scan</td> <td>–</td> </tr> <tr> <td>🟢 Green solid</td> <td>Clear - nothing detected</td> <td>0–2</td> </tr> <tr> <td>🟡 Yellow solid</td> <td>Caution - possible recording device nearby</td> <td>3–5</td> </tr> <tr> <td>🔴 Red flashing</td> <td>Alert - definite surveillance / tracking device detected</td> <td>6+</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p><strong>A single high-confidence detection (Axon body camera, Flock Safety camera, ALPR OUI match, AirTag) scores enough points to immediately push the LED to red in a single detection cycle.</strong> Multiple medium-confidence detections accumulate to yellow and can combine toward red.</p> <hr> <h2 id="hardware"> <a href="#hardware" title="Hardware">Hardware</a> <a href="#hardware" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Hardware">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="primary-target-m5stack-atom-lite"> <a href="#primary-target-m5stack-atom-lite" title="Primary Target: M5Stack Atom Lite">Primary Target: M5Stack Atom Lite</a> <a href="#primary-target-m5stack-atom-lite" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Primary Target: M5Stack Atom Lite">#</a> </h3> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Component</th> <th>Detail</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>Board</td> <td>M5Stack Atom Lite</td> </tr> <tr> <td>MCU</td> <td>ESP32-PICO-D4</td> </tr> <tr> <td>LED</td> <td>SK6812 NeoPixel on GPIO 27</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Button</td> <td>GPIO 39 (input only)</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Flash</td> <td>4 MB</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p>The Atom Lite is a complete self-contained platform. <strong>No soldering, no breadboard, no external components.</strong> Plug it into USB and it runs.</p> <h3 id="generic-esp32-devkit"> <a href="#generic-esp32-devkit" title="Generic ESP32 DevKit">Generic ESP32 DevKit</a> <a href="#generic-esp32-devkit" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Generic ESP32 DevKit">#</a> </h3> <p>A second PlatformIO build environment (<code>esp32dev</code>) targets any standard ESP32 DevKit with an onboard LED on GPIO 2. All detection logic runs identically. The DevKit build is useful for development, testing detection logic, and deployment when the Atom Lite form factor isn’t required.</p> <hr> <h2 id="the-scoring-system"> <a href="#the-scoring-system" title="The Scoring System">The Scoring System</a> <a href="#the-scoring-system" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Scoring System">#</a> </h2> <p>Eye Spy uses a <strong>confidence-score model</strong> that aggregates signals from all detection engines into a single integer. The score drives LED state (green / yellow / red) and is subject to two automatic management mechanisms:</p> <h3 id="score-decay"> <a href="#score-decay" title="Score Decay">Score Decay</a> <a href="#score-decay" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Score Decay">#</a> </h3> <p>The score drops <strong>−1 point every 60 seconds</strong> without new detections. If you move away from a detected device, the LED returns to green within a few minutes without any user intervention.</p> <h3 id="re-score-cooldown"> <a href="#re-score-cooldown" title="Re-Score Cooldown">Re-Score Cooldown</a> <a href="#re-score-cooldown" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Re-Score Cooldown">#</a> </h3> <p>Each detection <em>type</em> has a <strong>120-second cooldown</strong> before it can add points again from the same source. <em>This prevents a single persistent device from infinitely stacking the score</em> - a Flock Safety camera that remains in range adds +5 once, then waits 120 seconds before it can contribute again.</p> <p>These two mechanisms together mean:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Transient detections</strong> (a car with an AirTag driving past) resolve automatically</li> <li><strong>Persistent surveillance</strong> (a fixed body-cam deployment) keeps the LED at alert as long as you remain in range</li> <li><strong>No runaway scoring</strong> from one device seen repeatedly</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="detection-engines"> <a href="#detection-engines" title="Detection Engines">Detection Engines</a> <a href="#detection-engines" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Detection Engines">#</a> </h2> <p>Eye Spy operates three distinct scanning phases in a continuous rotation:</p> <p><strong>BLE passive (9 s) → WiFi scan (~3 s) → Promiscuous sniff (5 s) → repeat</strong></p> <p>BLE is explicitly stopped before any WiFi operations to respect the shared ESP32 radio. It restarts cleanly at the beginning of each new cycle.</p> <hr> <h3 id="engine-1-ble---passive-scanning"> <a href="#engine-1-ble---passive-scanning" title="Engine 1: BLE - Passive Scanning">Engine 1: BLE - Passive Scanning</a> <a href="#engine-1-ble---passive-scanning" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Engine 1: BLE - Passive Scanning">#</a> </h3> <p>BLE scanning is implemented using <strong>NimBLE with no scan requests transmitted</strong>. The device listens for BLE advertising packets without sending any response. <em>This makes Eye Spy electronically invisible to the equipment it is scanning for</em> - a passive scanner can’t be detected by the target.</p> <p>Devices weaker than <strong>−90 dBm</strong> are ignored to reduce false positives in dense environments.</p> <h4 id="ble-detection-table"> <a href="#ble-detection-table" title="BLE Detection Table">BLE Detection Table</a> <a href="#ble-detection-table" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="BLE Detection Table">#</a> </h4> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>#</th> <th>Target</th> <th>Detection Method</th> <th>Score</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>1</td> <td><strong>Axon body camera</strong></td> <td>BLE MAC OUI <code>00:25:df</code> (Axon - body cams, tasers, LE equipment)</td> <td>+5 🔴</td> </tr> <tr> <td>2</td> <td><strong>Ray-Ban Meta smart glasses</strong></td> <td>BLE service UUID <code>0xFD5F</code></td> <td>+5 🔴</td> </tr> <tr> <td>3</td> <td><strong>Flock Safety BLE</strong></td> <td>BLE device name containing <code>Flock</code>, <code>Penguin</code>, <code>Pigvision</code>, or <code>FS Ext Battery</code></td> <td>+5 🔴</td> </tr> <tr> <td>4</td> <td><strong>Card skimmer (HC-03/05/06)</strong></td> <td>BLE device name exact match - Bluetooth modules commonly found in payment-terminal skimmers</td> <td>+5 🔴</td> </tr> <tr> <td>5</td> <td><strong>Apple AirTag</strong></td> <td>Manufacturer data <code>0x004C</code> subtype <code>0x12</code>/<code>0x1E</code>, or raw payload <code>1E FF 4C 00</code> / <code>4C 00 12</code></td> <td>+4 🔴</td> </tr> <tr> <td>6</td> <td><strong>Drone (OpenDroneID BLE)</strong></td> <td>BLE service UUID <code>0xFFFA</code>, or raw AD service-data payload with app code <code>0x0D</code></td> <td>+4 🔴</td> </tr> <tr> <td>7</td> <td><strong>Samsung SmartTag</strong></td> <td>BLE service UUID <code>0xFD5A</code></td> <td>+3 🟡</td> </tr> <tr> <td>8</td> <td><strong>Tile tracker</strong></td> <td>BLE service UUID <code>0xFEED</code> or <code>0xFEEC</code></td> <td>+3 🟡</td> </tr> <tr> <td>9</td> <td><strong>MeshCore node</strong></td> <td>BLE device name prefix <code>MeshCore-</code></td> <td>+2 🟡</td> </tr> <tr> <td>10</td> <td><strong>iBeacon (retail/venue tracking)</strong></td> <td>Manufacturer data <code>0x004C 0x02 0x15</code> - deployed in stores, airports, and stadiums to track movement</td> <td>+2 🟡</td> </tr> <tr> <td>11</td> <td><strong>Unknown persistent device</strong></td> <td>Any unclassified BLE MAC seen ≥3× over ≥5 minutes (device scout / follower detection)</td> <td>+2 🟡</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <h4 id="notable-ble-detections-in-depth"> <a href="#notable-ble-detections-in-depth" title="Notable BLE Detections in Depth">Notable BLE Detections in Depth</a> <a href="#notable-ble-detections-in-depth" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Notable BLE Detections in Depth">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Axon Body Camera (+5)</strong>: Axon (formerly TASER International) manufactures the most widely deployed body camera systems for law enforcement in the United States. The OUI <code>00:25:df</code> is registered to Axon and appears in their wearable hardware. <strong>A single detection immediately reaches the Alert threshold.</strong></p> <p><strong>Ray-Ban Meta Smart Glasses (+5)</strong>: The Ray-Ban Meta collaboration produces consumer smart glasses capable of video and photo recording. The BLE service UUID <code>0xFD5F</code> is the characteristic advertisement used by these devices. <em>Notably, these are a consumer product and may appear in crowded public spaces</em> - any detection at this score level is worth awareness regardless of the wearer’s intent.</p> <p><strong>Card Skimmer Bluetooth Modules (+5)</strong>: HC-03, HC-05, and HC-06 are cheap commodity Bluetooth serial modules frequently discovered in payment terminal overlays and ATM skimmer hardware. Detection uses exact device name matching against known module firmware default names. <strong>A red alert here warrants immediate physical inspection of nearby payment terminals.</strong></p> <p><strong>Apple AirTag (+4)</strong>: AirTags advertise using Apple’s proprietary <strong>Nearby Interaction</strong> protocol. The detection targets the manufacturer-specific data header (<code>0x004C</code> = Apple) with the AirTag-specific subtypes (<code>0x12</code> for the standard advertisement, <code>0x1E</code> for the lost-item advertisement). Raw payload patterns provide redundant detection coverage.</p> <p><strong>OpenDroneID BLE (+4)</strong>: The <strong>ASTM F3411 Remote ID</strong> standard (also adopted by FAA for commercial drones in the US) defines a broadcast protocol for drones to announce their identity and position. Eye Spy looks for the GATT service UUID <code>0xFFFA</code> (the designated Remote ID service) and the application code <code>0x0D</code> in AD service-data payloads. <em>Any compliant commercial drone operating nearby will trigger this detection.</em></p> <p><strong>Unknown Persistent Device (+2)</strong>: This is the <strong>follower detection</strong> engine. Any BLE MAC not classified by the specific detections above is tracked. If the same unclassified MAC appears three or more times over five or more minutes, it scores. The device persistence tracker watches up to 50 unknown MACs simultaneously, with entries purged after 30 minutes of absence. <strong>This catches custom or modified trackers that don’t match any known service UUID or manufacturer pattern.</strong></p> <p><strong>iBeacon (+2)</strong>: Apple’s iBeacon format (<code>0x4C 0x00 0x02 0x15</code>) is used by retailers, airports, and stadiums to track device movement through physical spaces. The detection fires on the standard advertisement format regardless of UUID - targeting the deployment type, not any specific UUID. <em>A hit here means you’re likely in a location that is actively tracking Bluetooth device presence.</em></p> <hr> <h3 id="engine-2-wifi-scan---active-channel-scan"> <a href="#engine-2-wifi-scan---active-channel-scan" title="Engine 2: WiFi Scan - Active Channel Scan">Engine 2: WiFi Scan - Active Channel Scan</a> <a href="#engine-2-wifi-scan---active-channel-scan" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Engine 2: WiFi Scan - Active Channel Scan">#</a> </h3> <p>The WiFi scan engine uses the ESP32’s standard AP scanning interface to enumerate nearby access points and compare their BSSIDs and SSIDs against known surveillance device fingerprints.</p> <h4 id="wifi-scan-detection-table"> <a href="#wifi-scan-detection-table" title="WiFi Scan Detection Table">WiFi Scan Detection Table</a> <a href="#wifi-scan-detection-table" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="WiFi Scan Detection Table">#</a> </h4> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>#</th> <th>Target</th> <th>Detection Method</th> <th>Score</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>12</td> <td><strong>Flock Safety camera (OUI)</strong></td> <td>BSSID matches 22-entry Flock Safety OUI table (<code>d4:bb:e6</code>, <code>3c:61:05</code>, FS-Ext-Battery prefixes)</td> <td>+5 🔴</td> </tr> <tr> <td>13</td> <td><strong>ALPR / LPR camera (OUI)</strong></td> <td>BSSID matches Motorola Solutions / Vigilant Solutions OUI <code>00:0e:58</code></td> <td>+5 🔴</td> </tr> <tr> <td>14</td> <td><strong>Flock keyword SSID</strong></td> <td>SSID contains: <code>flock</code>, <code>flocksafety</code>, <code>fs ext</code>, <code>penguin</code>, <code>pigvision</code></td> <td>+5 🔴</td> </tr> <tr> <td>15</td> <td><strong>ALPR keyword SSID</strong></td> <td>SSID contains: <code>alpr</code>, <code>lpr</code>, <code>vigilant</code>, <code>plateread</code>, <code>licenseplat</code>, <code>motorola</code>, <code>automate</code></td> <td>+4 🔴</td> </tr> <tr> <td>16</td> <td><strong>Surveillance camera vendor (OUI)</strong></td> <td>BSSID matches 31-entry camera OUI table - Hikvision, Dahua, Axis, Ring, Nest, Arlo, Wyze, Reolink, FLIR, Amcrest, Vivotek, Hanwha, Mobotix, Ubiquiti UniFi</td> <td>+3 🟡</td> </tr> <tr> <td>17</td> <td><strong>Camera keyword SSID</strong></td> <td>SSID contains: <code>cam</code>, <code>ipcam</code>, <code>cctv</code>, <code>nvr</code>, <code>dvr</code>, <code>doorbell</code>, <code>surv</code>, <code>blink</code>, <code>lorex</code>, <code>protect</code>, <code>genetec</code>, and more</td> <td>+2 🟡</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <h4 id="the-flock-safety-oui-table"> <a href="#the-flock-safety-oui-table" title="The Flock Safety OUI Table">The Flock Safety OUI Table</a> <a href="#the-flock-safety-oui-table" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Flock Safety OUI Table">#</a> </h4> <p>The 22-entry <code>FLOCK_OUIS</code> table is the most detailed lookup in the project. It covers:</p> <ul> <li><code>d4:bb:e6</code> - IEEE-registered Flock Safety OUI</li> <li><code>3c:61:05</code> - IEEE-registered Flock Safety OUI</li> <li>20 additional MAC prefixes observed on Flock FS-Ext-Battery and Flock Wi-Fi camera hardware in the field</li> </ul> <p>These 22 entries represent hardware MAC ranges that have been observed on deployed Flock Safety ALPR camera systems. <strong>Detection via OUI is independent of SSID</strong> - a Flock camera with a non-keyword SSID still scores +5 if its BSSID OUI matches.</p> <p>Flock Safety and Vigilant Solutions OUIs are in separate tables specifically so both can score independently in the same scan cycle. <em>A location with both vendor types could accumulate +10 in a single WiFi scan pass.</em></p> <h4 id="surveillance-camera-vendor-oui-table"> <a href="#surveillance-camera-vendor-oui-table" title="Surveillance Camera Vendor OUI Table">Surveillance Camera Vendor OUI Table</a> <a href="#surveillance-camera-vendor-oui-table" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Surveillance Camera Vendor OUI Table">#</a> </h4> <p>The 31-entry surveillance camera table covers the major IP camera manufacturers whose hardware is likely to appear in retail, commercial, and residential surveillance deployments:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Enterprise/commercial</strong>: Hikvision, Dahua, Axis, Vivotek, Hanwha (Samsung Techwin), Mobotix, Genetec</li> <li><strong>Consumer/SOHO</strong>: Ring, Nest, Arlo, Wyze, Reolink, Blink, Lorex, Amcrest</li> <li><strong>Infrastructure</strong>: Ubiquiti UniFi (access points and cameras share OUI space), FLIR</li> </ul> <p>A match here scores +3 (Caution-range) without any keyword confirmation. Combined with a keyword SSID match, the same camera network scores +5 on the OUI alone, then +2 more from the SSID, reaching Alert in a single scan cycle.</p> <hr> <h3 id="engine-3-wifi-promiscuous---passive-frame-sniffing"> <a href="#engine-3-wifi-promiscuous---passive-frame-sniffing" title="Engine 3: WiFi Promiscuous - Passive Frame Sniffing">Engine 3: WiFi Promiscuous - Passive Frame Sniffing</a> <a href="#engine-3-wifi-promiscuous---passive-frame-sniffing" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Engine 3: WiFi Promiscuous - Passive Frame Sniffing">#</a> </h3> <p>The promiscuous engine drops the ESP32 radio into <strong>monitor mode</strong> and captures raw 802.11 management frames. This enables detection of devices that don’t advertise an SSID - specifically, drones using the <strong>Remote ID</strong> protocol over <strong>WiFi Neighbor Awareness Networking (NaN)</strong>.</p> <p>During the promiscuous phase, the radio <strong>channel-hops</strong> across <code>{1, 6, 11, 3, 8, 13}</code> every 400 ms to maximize coverage of the drone NaN frame broadcast channels.</p> <h4 id="promiscuous-detection-table"> <a href="#promiscuous-detection-table" title="Promiscuous Detection Table">Promiscuous Detection Table</a> <a href="#promiscuous-detection-table" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Promiscuous Detection Table">#</a> </h4> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>#</th> <th>Target</th> <th>Detection Method</th> <th>Score</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>18</td> <td><strong>Drone (OpenDroneID WiFi NaN)</strong></td> <td>802.11 Management frame to destination <code>51:6f:9a:01:00:00</code> - ASTM F3411 Remote ID broadcast</td> <td>+4 🔴</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p><strong>OpenDroneID WiFi NaN (+4)</strong>: The ASTM F3411 standard defines a multicast destination address <code>51:6f:9a:01:00:00</code> for all Remote ID WiFi NaN frames. Any commercially regulated drone broadcasting its position and identity over WiFi will send frames to this destination. Eye Spy simply watches for management frames addressed to this multicast MAC - passive, reliable, and <em>unpatchable by the drone operator short of disabling Remote ID entirely (which would itself be a regulatory violation)</em>.</p> <p>This detection complements the BLE OpenDroneID engine (detection #6). <em>A drone may advertise over BLE, WiFi, or both depending on its hardware and configuration.</em> Eye Spy covers both.</p> <hr> <h2 id="phase-schedule-and-radio-management"> <a href="#phase-schedule-and-radio-management" title="Phase Schedule and Radio Management">Phase Schedule and Radio Management</a> <a href="#phase-schedule-and-radio-management" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Phase Schedule and Radio Management">#</a> </h2> <pre tabindex="0"><code>BLE passive (9 s) → WiFi scan (~3 s) → Promiscuous sniff (5 s) → repeat </code></pre><p>The ESP32-PICO-D4 has a <strong>single shared 2.4 GHz radio</strong> that handles both BLE and WiFi. Eye Spy manages this carefully:</p> <ol> <li><strong>BLE phase</strong> (9 seconds): NimBLE stack active, passive scan running, no scan requests transmitted</li> <li><strong>BLE shutdown</strong>: BLE stack explicitly stopped before touching WiFi</li> <li><strong>WiFi scan</strong> (~3 seconds): Active AP scan across all channels, OUI and SSID matching</li> <li><strong>Promiscuous sniff</strong> (5 seconds): Passive 802.11 frame capture with channel hopping</li> <li><strong>WiFi shutdown</strong>: WiFi stopped, BLE restarted for the next cycle</li> </ol> <p>This explicit phase management prevents radio conflicts and ensures both BLE and WiFi engines get clean access to the radio every cycle.</p> <hr> <h2 id="serial-output"> <a href="#serial-output" title="Serial Output">Serial Output</a> <a href="#serial-output" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Serial Output">#</a> </h2> <p>All output is prefixed with <code>[eyespy]</code> for easy filtering. The serial monitor runs at <strong>115200 baud</strong>.</p> <h3 id="startup"> <a href="#startup" title="Startup">Startup</a> <a href="#startup" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Startup">#</a> </h3> <pre tabindex="0"><code>[eyespy] Eye Spy v1.1 starting [eyespy] init OK </code></pre><h3 id="normal-cycle"> <a href="#normal-cycle" title="Normal Cycle">Normal Cycle</a> <a href="#normal-cycle" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Normal Cycle">#</a> </h3> <pre tabindex="0"><code>[eyespy] BLE scan start [eyespy] WiFi scan [eyespy] WiFi done score=0 [eyespy] promisc ON [eyespy] status score=0 CLEAR phase=PROMISC tracked=3 </code></pre><h3 id="detection-events"> <a href="#detection-events" title="Detection Events">Detection Events</a> <a href="#detection-events" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Detection Events">#</a> </h3> <pre tabindex="0"><code>[eyespy] Flock-cam OUI d4:bb:e6 "Flock_CAM_0032" [eyespy] +5 (Flock-cam-OUI) score=5 [eyespy] WiFi done score=5 [eyespy] promisc ON [eyespy] status score=5 CAUTION phase=PROMISC tracked=12 [eyespy] Axon-cam RSSI=-62 [eyespy] +5 (Axon-cam) score=10 [eyespy] status score=10 ALERT phase=BLE tracked=12 [eyespy] decay score=9 </code></pre><p>The <code>tracked=N</code> field shows how many unique BLE MACs are currently in the persistence tracker - useful for understanding the density of the BLE environment.</p> <h3 id="score-fields"> <a href="#score-fields" title="Score Fields">Score Fields</a> <a href="#score-fields" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Score Fields">#</a> </h3> <p>Each status line includes:</p> <ul> <li><strong>score</strong> - current threat score</li> <li><strong>state</strong> - <code>CLEAR</code> / <code>CAUTION</code> / <code>ALERT</code></li> <li><strong>phase</strong> - which engine is currently active (<code>BLE</code> / <code>WIFI</code> / <code>PROMISC</code>)</li> <li><strong>tracked</strong> - number of unique BLE MACs in the persistence table</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="build-and-flash"> <a href="#build-and-flash" title="Build and Flash">Build and Flash</a> <a href="#build-and-flash" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Build and Flash">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="requirements"> <a href="#requirements" title="Requirements">Requirements</a> <a href="#requirements" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Requirements">#</a> </h3> <ul> <li><strong>PlatformIO</strong> (CLI or VS Code extension)</li> <li><strong>M5Stack Atom Lite</strong> or any ESP32 DevKit</li> <li>USB-C cable</li> </ul> <h3 id="dependencies"> <a href="#dependencies" title="Dependencies">Dependencies</a> <a href="#dependencies" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Dependencies">#</a> </h3> <p>PlatformIO installs these automatically:</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-ini" data-lang="ini"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#a6e22e">lib_deps</span> <span style="color:#f92672">=</span><span style="color:#e6db74"> </span></span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#e6db74"> adafruit/Adafruit NeoPixel @ ^1.15.1 </span></span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#e6db74"> h2zero/NimBLE-Arduino @ ^1.4.3</span> </span></span></code></pre></div><p><strong>Adafruit NeoPixel</strong> drives the SK6812 RGB LED. <strong>NimBLE-Arduino</strong> provides the passive BLE scanning stack - it is preferred over the default ESP32 BLE library because it supports passive scan mode cleanly, avoids sending scan request packets, and has lower memory overhead.</p> <h3 id="flash-to-m5stack-atom-lite"> <a href="#flash-to-m5stack-atom-lite" title="Flash to M5Stack Atom Lite">Flash to M5Stack Atom Lite</a> <a href="#flash-to-m5stack-atom-lite" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Flash to M5Stack Atom Lite">#</a> </h3> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span>git clone https://github.com/simeononsecurity/eye-spy.git </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>cd eye-spy </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Build and flash for Atom Lite</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>pio run -e atom-lite -t upload </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Serial monitor at 115200 baud</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>pio device monitor -b <span style="color:#ae81ff">115200</span> </span></span></code></pre></div><h3 id="flash-to-generic-esp32-devkit"> <a href="#flash-to-generic-esp32-devkit" title="Flash to Generic ESP32 DevKit">Flash to Generic ESP32 DevKit</a> <a href="#flash-to-generic-esp32-devkit" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Flash to Generic ESP32 DevKit">#</a> </h3> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span>pio run -e esp32dev -t upload </span></span></code></pre></div><h3 id="project-structure"> <a href="#project-structure" title="Project Structure">Project Structure</a> <a href="#project-structure" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Project Structure">#</a> </h3> <pre tabindex="0"><code>eye-spy/ ├── src/ │ └── main.cpp # All firmware logic ├── platformio.ini # Build environments (atom-lite, esp32dev) ├── partitions_4mb.csv # 4 MB flash partition table └── README.md </code></pre><h3 id="platformioini"> <a href="#platformioini" title="platformio.ini">platformio.ini</a> <a href="#platformioini" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="platformio.ini">#</a> </h3> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-ini" data-lang="ini"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#66d9ef">[env:atom-lite]</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#a6e22e">platform</span> <span style="color:#f92672">=</span> <span style="color:#e6db74">espressif32</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#a6e22e">board</span> <span style="color:#f92672">=</span> <span style="color:#e6db74">m5stick-c</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#a6e22e">framework</span> <span style="color:#f92672">=</span> <span style="color:#e6db74">arduino</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#66d9ef">[env:esp32dev]</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#a6e22e">platform</span> <span style="color:#f92672">=</span> <span style="color:#e6db74">espressif32</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#a6e22e">board</span> <span style="color:#f92672">=</span> <span style="color:#e6db74">esp32dev</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#a6e22e">framework</span> <span style="color:#f92672">=</span> <span style="color:#e6db74">arduino</span> </span></span></code></pre></div><p><em>The <code>atom-lite</code> environment uses the <code>m5stick-c</code> board definition</em> - same ESP32-PICO-D4 silicon with a compatible pin mapping for GPIO 27 NeoPixel.</p> <hr> <h2 id="detection-notes-and-practical-limitations"> <a href="#detection-notes-and-practical-limitations" title="Detection Notes and Practical Limitations">Detection Notes and Practical Limitations</a> <a href="#detection-notes-and-practical-limitations" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Detection Notes and Practical Limitations">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="what-eye-spy-cannot-do"> <a href="#what-eye-spy-cannot-do" title="What Eye Spy Cannot Do">What Eye Spy Cannot Do</a> <a href="#what-eye-spy-cannot-do" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Eye Spy Cannot Do">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>5 GHz WiFi</strong>: The ESP32 is a <strong>2.4 GHz-only device</strong>. Any surveillance camera, ALPR system, or access point operating exclusively on 5 GHz bands won’t be visible to the WiFi scan or promiscuous engines. <em>Many modern IP cameras are 2.4 GHz capable even if they also support 5 GHz, but dedicated 5 GHz-only deployments will be missed.</em></p> <p><strong>Encrypted BLE</strong>: Several high-end surveillance products encrypt or obfuscate their BLE advertisements. Eye Spy detects devices that broadcast identifiable signatures - OUIs, service UUIDs, manufacturer data - in plaintext. <em>Devices that rotate MAC addresses (a privacy feature increasingly common in consumer trackers) will evade MAC-based detection</em> and may only be caught by the persistence tracker if they rotate on a schedule slower than 5 minutes.</p> <p><strong>Wired cameras</strong>: <strong>IP cameras connected via Ethernet and not running a WiFi radio will produce no wireless emissions for Eye Spy to detect.</strong> Hidden cameras without network connectivity (purely local recording) similarly produce no RF signature.</p> <p><strong>Range limitations</strong>: The ESP32 antenna has practical indoor receive range of <strong>20–40 meters</strong> for strong signals, less for weak or obscured signals. <em>Devices at the edge of range or behind significant RF shielding may score zero or fail the −90 dBm RSSI threshold filter.</em></p> <h3 id="false-positives-to-expect"> <a href="#false-positives-to-expect" title="False Positives to Expect">False Positives to Expect</a> <a href="#false-positives-to-expect" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="False Positives to Expect">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Consumer cameras at neighbors’ homes</strong>: Ring, Nest, Wyze, Arlo, and Reolink cameras are ubiquitous in residential neighborhoods. Their OUIs appear in the 31-entry camera table. <em>In residential environments, expect some yellow (Caution, +3) hits from neighbors’ doorbell cameras.</em> These aren’t false positives in the technical sense - the device <em>is</em> detecting a camera - but context matters.</p> <p><strong>Retail iBeacon deployments</strong>: Major retailers deploy iBeacon infrastructure in virtually every store. <em>Any detection trip to a mall or grocery store will likely trigger the iBeacon detection (+2).</em> Again, the device is doing its job - the retail tracking infrastructure really is there.</p> <p><strong>Ubiquiti UniFi infrastructure</strong>: UniFi access points appear in the surveillance camera OUI table because Ubiquiti manufactures both networking and security camera products under overlapping OUI ranges. <em>A deployment that uses UniFi networking gear will score +3 on OUI matches from the WiFi scan engine.</em></p> <p><strong>Samsung SmartTag vs. consumer devices</strong>: The SmartTag UUID (<code>0xFD5A</code>) is a Samsung-registered service. <em>Samsung SmartTags are consumer product trackers with legitimate personal use.</em> Any Samsung SmartTag owner in vicinity will trigger this detection.</p> <h3 id="what-each-score-level-actually-means-in-practice"> <a href="#what-each-score-level-actually-means-in-practice" title="What Each Score Level Actually Means in Practice">What Each Score Level Actually Means in Practice</a> <a href="#what-each-score-level-actually-means-in-practice" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Each Score Level Actually Means in Practice">#</a> </h3> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Score</th> <th>Likely Real-World Situation</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>6–10 (🔴)</td> <td>Credible high-confidence detection: Axon camera within ~30m, Flock Safety camera with matching OUI, confirmed AirTag following pattern, or drone broadcasting Remote ID</td> </tr> <tr> <td>3–5 (🟡)</td> <td>Multiple consumer trackers, neighbor cameras accumulating, possible Flock camera, or a single OpenDroneID detection</td> </tr> <tr> <td>0–2 (🟢)</td> <td>No significant surveillance signatures detected; normal consumer device chatter present but scored below threshold</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <hr> <h2 id="comparison-to-related-projects"> <a href="#comparison-to-related-projects" title="Comparison to Related Projects">Comparison to Related Projects</a> <a href="#comparison-to-related-projects" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Comparison to Related Projects">#</a> </h2> <p>Eye Spy targets physical surveillance in the environment; the <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="ESP32 WiFi Canary"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://simeononsecurity.com/articles/esp32-wifi-canary-passive-wifi-threat-detection-2026/"> <a href="https://simeononsecurity.com/articles/esp32-wifi-canary-passive-wifi-threat-detection-2026/" rel="follow me" >ESP32 WiFi Canary</a> </span> targets network-layer WiFi attacks against your own devices. <em>They are complementary tools addressing different threat models.</em></p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Capability</th> <th>Eye Spy</th> <th>ESP32 WiFi Canary</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr>
