<td>AirTag / tracker detection</td> <td>✅</td> <td>❌</td> </tr> <tr> <td>ALPR / Flock camera detection</td> <td>✅</td> <td>❌</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Body camera detection</td> <td>✅</td> <td>❌</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Drone (Remote ID) detection</td> <td>✅</td> <td>❌</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Evil-twin AP detection</td> <td>❌</td> <td>✅</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Deauth attack detection</td> <td>❌</td> <td>✅</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Security downgrade detection</td> <td>❌</td> <td>✅</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Baseline learning</td> <td>❌</td> <td>✅</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Passive BLE</td> <td>✅</td> <td>❌</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Same hardware platform</td> <td>✅</td> <td>✅</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p>Both projects run on the same M5Stack Atom Lite hardware using the same LED indicators and PlatformIO build system. For a comprehensive physical + network surveillance awareness kit, both can be flashed to different Atom Lite units and carried simultaneously.</p> <p>Eye Spy also complements the <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Flock You detection project"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://simeononsecurity.com/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/"> <a href="https://simeononsecurity.com/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/" rel="follow me" >Flock You detection project</a> </span> for users specifically concerned about ALPR surveillance infrastructure.</p> <hr> <h2 id="use-cases"> <a href="#use-cases" title="Use Cases">Use Cases</a> <a href="#use-cases" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Use Cases">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="counter-surveillance-awareness"> <a href="#counter-surveillance-awareness" title="Counter-Surveillance Awareness">Counter-Surveillance Awareness</a> <a href="#counter-surveillance-awareness" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Counter-Surveillance Awareness">#</a> </h3> <p>The primary audience for Eye Spy is anyone who wants ambient awareness of surveillance infrastructure in their immediate vicinity. Walking through a neighborhood, driving past a traffic intersection, or attending a public event - Eye Spy provides a real-time signal that collapses complex RF analysis into a <strong>single LED state</strong>.</p> <h3 id="airtag-stalking-detection"> <a href="#airtag-stalking-detection" title="AirTag Stalking Detection">AirTag Stalking Detection</a> <a href="#airtag-stalking-detection" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="AirTag Stalking Detection">#</a> </h3> <p>AirTag-based stalking is a documented problem. Eye Spy’s <strong>follower detection engine</strong> (unknown persistent BLE MAC seen ≥3× over ≥5 minutes) specifically addresses modified or custom trackers that don’t match Apple’s known advertisement format. Combined with the direct AirTag detection, it provides <strong>two independent paths to catching a tracker that’s following you</strong>.</p> <h3 id="rental-property--hotel-room-inspection"> <a href="#rental-property--hotel-room-inspection" title="Rental Property / Hotel Room Inspection">Rental Property / Hotel Room Inspection</a> <a href="#rental-property--hotel-room-inspection" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Rental Property / Hotel Room Inspection">#</a> </h3> <p>Entering a new hotel room or rental property with Eye Spy running gives a first-pass indication of unexpected BLE and WiFi-broadcasting devices. A camera keyword SSID match or surveillance OUI in the WiFi scan engine adds Caution-level points. <em>This isn’t a substitute for a proper RF sweep</em>, but it adds a passive ambient layer to any physical inspection.</p> <h3 id="alpr-deployments--privacy-research"> <a href="#alpr-deployments--privacy-research" title="ALPR Deployments / Privacy Research">ALPR Deployments / Privacy Research</a> <a href="#alpr-deployments--privacy-research" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="ALPR Deployments / Privacy Research">#</a> </h3> <p>For researchers documenting surveillance infrastructure, journalists working in surveilled environments, or privacy advocates mapping ALPR deployments, Eye Spy provides field-portable detection hardware at minimal cost. The serial output provides a log of every detection with RSSI, making it suitable for use as a simple data collection device.</p> <h3 id="travel-security"> <a href="#travel-security" title="Travel Security">Travel Security</a> <a href="#travel-security" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Travel Security">#</a> </h3> <p>Like the WiFi Canary, Eye Spy is designed for travel form factor. The Atom Lite fits in any pocket or attaches to a bag. During travel through airports, train stations, or public events with known surveillance density, it provides continuous passive monitoring without requiring any interaction.</p> <hr> <h2 id="architecture-notes"> <a href="#architecture-notes" title="Architecture Notes">Architecture Notes</a> <a href="#architecture-notes" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Architecture Notes">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="why-passive-ble-matters"> <a href="#why-passive-ble-matters" title="Why Passive BLE Matters">Why Passive BLE Matters</a> <a href="#why-passive-ble-matters" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Why Passive BLE Matters">#</a> </h3> <p>The choice of passive BLE scanning (no scan request packets) has meaningful security consequences. In standard BLE scanning, a scanner transmits a <strong>SCAN_REQ</strong> packet requesting additional advertising data from each advertiser. <em>This means active BLE scanning is mutually observable</em> - the device being scanned sees the scanner’s address in the scan request.</p> <p>NimBLE passive mode listens only to undirected advertising packets (ADV_IND, ADV_NONCONN_IND, ADV_SCAN_IND) without ever transmitting a SCAN_REQ. <strong>The eye-spy device produces zero BLE transmission during the scan phase.</strong> An Axon body camera, Flock device, or AirTag being detected can’t observe or react to the scanner’s presence.</p> <h3 id="the-persistence-tracker-design"> <a href="#the-persistence-tracker-design" title="The Persistence Tracker Design">The Persistence Tracker Design</a> <a href="#the-persistence-tracker-design" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Persistence Tracker Design">#</a> </h3> <p>The unknown-device persistence tracker maintains a table of up to <strong>50 BLE MAC addresses</strong> with first-seen and last-seen timestamps. Detection fires when a MAC has been seen three or more times across a window of five or more minutes.</p> <p>The 50-entry limit and 30-minute purge window are engineering choices that balance detection sensitivity against RAM constraints on the ESP32. <em>In a dense BLE environment (transit, conference, mall), the table may fill quickly with consumer devices.</em> The −90 dBm RSSI threshold reduces this by filtering out distant devices, keeping the tracker focused on nearby persistent sources.</p> <h3 id="score-decay-and-cooldown-interaction"> <a href="#score-decay-and-cooldown-interaction" title="Score Decay and Cooldown Interaction">Score Decay and Cooldown Interaction</a> <a href="#score-decay-and-cooldown-interaction" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Score Decay and Cooldown Interaction">#</a> </h3> <p>The <strong>120-second re-score cooldown</strong> prevents a device that permanently stays in range from continuously stacking points every 9-second BLE cycle. Without the cooldown, a single Flock camera would add +5 every 15 seconds, reaching hundreds of points in minutes.</p> <p>The <strong>60-second score decay</strong> means that once a device leaves range (and its cooldown expires without re-triggering), the score drops by 1 per minute. <em>A single +5 detection that doesn’t re-trigger will decay to zero in 5 minutes.</em> This gives the device a natural “all clear” time that matches plausible transit scenarios.</p> <hr> <h2 id="contributing-and-project-status"> <a href="#contributing-and-project-status" title="Contributing and Project Status">Contributing and Project Status</a> <a href="#contributing-and-project-status" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Contributing and Project Status">#</a> </h2> <p>Eye Spy is available at <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="github.com/simeononsecurity/eye-spy"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://github.com/simeononsecurity/eye-spy"> <a href="https://github.com/simeononsecurity/eye-spy" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >github.com/simeononsecurity/eye-spy</a> </span> under the Apache-2.0 license.</p> <p>Potential areas for extension include:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Additional OUI entries</strong>: The Flock Safety and camera OUI tables can be extended as new hardware MACs are documented in the field</li> <li><strong>Additional BLE service UUIDs</strong>: New Smart Glasses, cameras, or trackers entering the market introduce new UUIDs</li> <li><strong>Persistence tracker tuning</strong>: The 50-entry limit, 3-sighting threshold, and 5-minute window are adjustable constants</li> <li><strong>Serial data logging</strong>: The serial output format is designed for programmatic parsing - an external logger could aggregate detection events from multiple Eye Spy units</li> </ul> <p>The firmware is a single <code>main.cpp</code> in the <code>src/</code> directory, making it straightforward to read, audit, and modify.</p> <hr> <h2 id="conclusion"> <a href="#conclusion" title="Conclusion">Conclusion</a> <a href="#conclusion" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Conclusion">#</a> </h2> <p>Eye Spy addresses a narrow but meaningful problem: the physical surveillance environment around you is increasingly instrumented, and most of that instrumentation broadcasts detectable RF signatures. <strong>A $15 M5Stack Atom Lite running the Eye Spy firmware becomes a continuous ambient scanner</strong> that turns the complexity of BLE packet analysis and WiFi OUI lookups into a single RGB LED.</p> <p>The confidence-scoring model reflects realistic threat weighting: an Axon body camera at law enforcement density scores +5 and immediately illuminates red; a retail iBeacon scores +2 and contributes to a broader awareness picture without triggering a false alarm on its own. Score decay and re-score cooldowns keep the device from crying wolf on transient or persistent low-level signals.</p> <p>For counter-surveillance work, travel security, AirTag detection, or simply wanting to know whether something nearby is watching - Eye Spy is a practical, open-source, passively-operating tool that earns its place in any security practitioner’s kit.</p> <p><strong>GitHub</strong>: <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="github.com/simeononsecurity/eye-spy"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://github.com/simeononsecurity/eye-spy"> <a href="https://github.com/simeononsecurity/eye-spy" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >github.com/simeononsecurity/eye-spy</a> </span> </p> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.ch/

item.title: ESP32 WiFi Canary: Passive 2.4 GHz Threat Detection with RGB LED Alerts
item.category: "Network Security" , "WiFi Security" , "IoT Security" , "Security Research" , "Embedded Systems" , "Privacy Tools" , "ESP32 Projects" , "Hardware Security" , "Wireless Security" , "Open Source Security"
item.description: A detailed breakdown into the ESP32 WiFi Canary project - a compact, passive 2.4 GHz awareness sensor for the M5Stack Atom Lite that silently watches for evil-twin APs, deauthentication attacks, security downgrades, and beacon floods using a confidence-scored threat model and a single RGB LED.
item.content:encoded: <p><strong>A Thumb-Sized Passive WiFi Threat Sensor That Never Talks Back</strong></p> <h2 id="introduction-the-problem-with-public-wifi"> <a href="#introduction-the-problem-with-public-wifi" title="Introduction: The Problem With Public WiFi">Introduction: The Problem With Public WiFi</a> <a href="#introduction-the-problem-with-public-wifi" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Introduction: The Problem With Public WiFi">#</a> </h2> <p>Every time you connect to hotel WiFi, a coffee shop hotspot, or an airport network, you’re trusting that the access point in front of you is the real one. The problem is that <strong>802.11 management frames</strong> - the very frames that announce networks, manage connections, and coordinate clients - are <em>completely unauthenticated in most deployments</em>. Anyone with modest hardware can clone an SSID, blast deauthentication frames at clients, or set up an open decoy next to a legitimate WPA2 network.</p> <p>The <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="ESP32 WiFi Canary"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://github.com/simeononsecurity/esp32-wifi-canary"> <a href="https://github.com/simeononsecurity/esp32-wifi-canary" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" ><strong>ESP32 WiFi Canary</strong></a> </span> is a passive awareness sensor that addresses this reality with the smallest possible footprint. It fits on the M5Stack Atom Lite - a device roughly the size of a sugar cube - plugs into any USB port, learns the surrounding environment, and lights up an RGB LED when it detects patterns consistent with wireless threats.</p> <p>It doesn’t connect to anything. It doesn’t capture credentials. It doesn’t transmit a single frame. It watches, scores, and tells you what color the situation is.</p> <p>This article is a complete technical reference for the project: what it detects, how the confidence model works, how to build and flash it, and what its real-world limitations are.</p> <hr> <h2 id="what-the-esp32-wifi-canary-does-and-doesnt-do"> <a href="#what-the-esp32-wifi-canary-does-and-doesnt-do" title="What the ESP32 WiFi Canary Does (and Doesn’t Do)">What the ESP32 WiFi Canary Does (and Doesn’t Do)</a> <a href="#what-the-esp32-wifi-canary-does-and-doesnt-do" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What the ESP32 WiFi Canary Does (and Doesn’t Do)">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="passive-only-always"> <a href="#passive-only-always" title="Passive-Only, Always">Passive-Only, Always</a> <a href="#passive-only-always" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Passive-Only, Always">#</a> </h3> <p>The WiFi Canary operates in two radio modes, never simultaneously:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Promiscuous mode</strong> - receives and inspects 802.11 management frames (deauth, disassoc) without associating to any network</li> <li><strong>Scan mode</strong> - performs active WiFi scans to enumerate nearby access points and compare them to a learned baseline</li> </ol> <p>The device never:</p> <ul> <li>Associates with or connects to any network</li> <li>Captures data frames or credentials</li> <li>Transmits 802.11 frames of any kind</li> <li>Stores anything to persistent flash</li> <li>Communicates over the internet</li> </ul> <p><strong>Everything it learns is held in RAM and reset on reboot.</strong> This design is intentional: the canary is a <strong>sensor</strong>, not a capture device.</p> <h3 id="the-led-is-the-interface"> <a href="#the-led-is-the-interface" title="The LED Is the Interface">The LED Is the Interface</a> <a href="#the-led-is-the-interface" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The LED Is the Interface">#</a> </h3> <p>There is no display, no app, no web UI. The entire output of the device is a single <strong>SK6812 RGB NeoPixel</strong> on GPIO 27 of the M5Stack Atom Lite. The LED speaks a four-state language:</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>LED State</th> <th>Meaning</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>🔵 Blue (slow pulse)</td> <td>Startup - building baseline reference</td> </tr> <tr> <td>🟢 Green (solid)</td> <td>Normal - no high-confidence issues</td> </tr> <tr> <td>🟡 Yellow (solid)</td> <td>Caution - suspicious pattern detected</td> </tr> <tr> <td>🔴 Red (fast pulse)</td> <td>Alert - higher-confidence threat detected</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p>Startup takes approximately <strong>24 seconds</strong> (3 scans × 8 seconds each). Once the device transitions out of blue, it has a working baseline and begins active monitoring.</p> <hr> <h2 id="hardware"> <a href="#hardware" title="Hardware">Hardware</a> <a href="#hardware" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Hardware">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="primary-target-m5stack-atom-lite"> <a href="#primary-target-m5stack-atom-lite" title="Primary Target: M5Stack Atom Lite">Primary Target: M5Stack Atom Lite</a> <a href="#primary-target-m5stack-atom-lite" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Primary Target: M5Stack Atom Lite">#</a> </h3> <p>The project is designed around the M5Stack Atom Lite - a complete ESP32 development platform in a 24 × 24 mm enclosure.</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Component</th> <th>Detail</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>MCU</td> <td>ESP32-PICO-D4</td> </tr> <tr> <td>LED</td> <td>Single SK6812 RGB NeoPixel (GPIO 27)</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Button</td> <td>GPIO 39, active-low</td> </tr> <tr> <td>USB</td> <td>CP2104 UART bridge</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Power</td> <td>USB-C, ~80–120 mA during scanning</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p><strong>No breadboard, no external components, no soldering.</strong> Plug it into USB power and it runs.</p> <h3 id="generic-esp32-devkit-for-developmenttesting"> <a href="#generic-esp32-devkit-for-developmenttesting" title="Generic ESP32 DevKit (for Development/Testing)">Generic ESP32 DevKit (for Development/Testing)</a> <a href="#generic-esp32-devkit-for-developmenttesting" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Generic ESP32 DevKit (for Development/Testing)">#</a> </h3> <p>The project includes a second PlatformIO build environment (<code>esp32dev</code>) that targets any standard ESP32 development board. In this configuration:</p> <ul> <li>GPIO 2 (onboard LED) is used instead of the NeoPixel</li> <li>Full serial debug output is enabled</li> <li>All detection logic runs identically to the Atom Lite build</li> </ul> <p>This makes the project accessible to anyone with a $5 ESP32 DevKit, and it allows testing detection logic without the target hardware.</p> <hr> <h2 id="the-baseline-learning-process"> <a href="#the-baseline-learning-process" title="The Baseline Learning Process">The Baseline Learning Process</a> <a href="#the-baseline-learning-process" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Baseline Learning Process">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="why-a-baseline-matters"> <a href="#why-a-baseline-matters" title="Why a Baseline Matters">Why a Baseline Matters</a> <a href="#why-a-baseline-matters" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Why a Baseline Matters">#</a> </h3> <p>A canary that fires on every open network in a city would be useless. The ESP32 WiFi Canary solves this by learning its environment before it starts scoring threats. The first phase of operation is dedicated to building a <strong>reference table</strong> of access points that legitimately exist in the surrounding area.</p> <h3 id="three-scans-24-seconds"> <a href="#three-scans-24-seconds" title="Three Scans, 24 Seconds">Three Scans, 24 Seconds</a> <a href="#three-scans-24-seconds" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Three Scans, 24 Seconds">#</a> </h3> <p>On startup, the device runs three sequential WiFi scans, each separated by a pause. After all three complete, the learned set of APs - SSID, BSSID, encryption type, signal strength - is stored as the baseline.</p> <p>Serial output during this phase looks like:</p> <pre tabindex="0"><code>============================================== Travel WiFi Canary v1.0 Passive 2.4 GHz awareness sensor ============================================== [canary] setup done - starting baseline learning [canary] baseline scan start (aps=0 score=0) [canary] scan found 18 APs [canary] baseline scan 1/3 [canary] baseline scan start (aps=18 score=0) [canary] scan found 18 APs [canary] baseline scan 2/3 [canary] baseline scan start (aps=18 score=0) [canary] scan found 19 APs [canary] baseline complete: 19 APs learned [00] MyHomeWifi aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff ch06 WPA2 -52 dBm [01] XFINITY 11:22:33:44:55:66 ch01 WPA2 -71 dBm ... [canary] STARTUP → NORMAL (score=0 "") </code></pre><p>Once the LED transitions from blue to green, the device is in full operation.</p> <hr> <h2 id="what-it-detects-threat-categories"> <a href="#what-it-detects-threat-categories" title="What It Detects: Threat Categories">What It Detects: Threat Categories</a> <a href="#what-it-detects-threat-categories" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What It Detects: Threat Categories">#</a> </h2> <p>The WiFi Canary monitors five distinct threat patterns. Each contributes points to a <strong>confidence score</strong> that drives LED state. <em>No single indicator alone is treated as certain</em> - the model is designed to accumulate corroborating evidence before escalating.</p> <h3 id="1-deauthentication--disassociation-bursts"> <a href="#1-deauthentication--disassociation-bursts" title="1. Deauthentication / Disassociation Bursts">1. Deauthentication / Disassociation Bursts</a> <a href="#1-deauthentication--disassociation-bursts" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="1. Deauthentication / Disassociation Bursts">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>802.11 management frame subtypes 10 (disassoc) and 12 (deauth)</strong> are the workhorses of WiFi attacks. Any device can send these frames, and any client receiving them will disconnect from their AP.</p> <p>The canary monitors these frames in <strong>promiscuous mode</strong> and counts them per source MAC within a <strong>5-second rolling window</strong>.</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Condition</th> <th>Points Added</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>≥ 8 frames from one source in 5 s</td> <td>+2</td> </tr> <tr> <td>≥ 20 frames from one source in 5 s</td> <td>+4</td> </tr> <tr> <td>≥ 5 broadcast deauth frames</td> <td>+1</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p><strong>Why thresholds matter</strong>: A client roaming between APs will generate a handful of legitimate deauth/disassoc frames. <em>What deauth attack tools generate is qualitatively different</em> - hundreds of frames per second sustained against a target. The 5-second window and 8-frame floor filter out normal roaming noise while catching the sustained burst signature of tools like <code>aireplay-ng</code>.</p> <h3 id="2-open-clone-of-known-encrypted-network-evil-twin"> <a href="#2-open-clone-of-known-encrypted-network-evil-twin" title="2. Open Clone of Known Encrypted Network (Evil Twin)">2. Open Clone of Known Encrypted Network (Evil Twin)</a> <a href="#2-open-clone-of-known-encrypted-network-evil-twin" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="2. Open Clone of Known Encrypted Network (Evil Twin)">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>The highest-confidence detection.</strong> An evil-twin attack often works by:</p> <ol> <li>Standing up an open (no password) copy of a known WPA2 SSID</li> <li>Making it stronger than the real AP so clients auto-connect</li> </ol> <p>After baseline learning, if a new scan reveals an SSID that was WPA/WPA2/WPA3-only in the baseline now appearing as <strong>OPEN</strong>:</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Condition</th> <th>Points Added</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>Same SSID, was encrypted, now open</td> <td>+3</td> </tr> <tr> <td>BSSID not seen in baseline</td> <td>+1</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Clone signal ≥ 10 dB stronger than known AP</td> <td>+1</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p>An open clone of the hotel WiFi SSID that is also 10 dB stronger than the real AP is essentially textbook. <em>This combination pushes the score to 5 (Caution) in a single scan cycle.</em></p> <p><strong>Serial example</strong>:</p> <pre tabindex="0"><code>[canary] OPEN CLONE: SSID='MyHomeWifi' baseline=WPA2 clone=OPEN bssid=de:ad:be:ef:00:01 rssi=-48/-52 [canary] score +4 → 4 "open clone of encrypted SSID 'MyHomeWifi'" [canary] NORMAL → CAUTION (score=4 "open clone of encrypted SSID 'MyHomeWifi'") </code></pre><h3 id="3-original-encrypted-ap-missing--open-clone-present"> <a href="#3-original-encrypted-ap-missing--open-clone-present" title="3. Original Encrypted AP Missing + Open Clone Present">3. Original Encrypted AP Missing + Open Clone Present</a> <a href="#3-original-encrypted-ap-missing--open-clone-present" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="3. Original Encrypted AP Missing + Open Clone Present">#</a> </h3> <p>A more advanced variant: the attacker’s stronger clone causes clients to prefer it, and the real AP is simultaneously deauthed off the air.</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Condition</th> <th>Points Added</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>Baseline encrypted AP gone + matching open network appeared</td> <td>+3</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p>This covers the scenario where the real AP is still broadcasting but simply loses the signal-strength competition - or is actively disrupted.</p> <h3 id="4-security-downgrade"> <a href="#4-security-downgrade" title="4. Security Downgrade">4. Security Downgrade</a> <a href="#4-security-downgrade" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="4. Security Downgrade">#</a> </h3> <p>Same SSID as a baseline entry, but observed with weaker encryption than recorded.</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Condition</th> <th>Points Added</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>WPA3 → WPA2</td> <td>+1</td> </tr> <tr> <td>WPA2 → WPA</td> <td>+1</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Drop of 2+ encryption ranks</td> <td>+3</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p><em>Open downgrades are handled by the evil-twin detection path above with higher scores.</em> This category focuses on partial downgrades that might be missed in a pure open-vs-encrypted comparison.</p> <h3 id="5-duplicate-ssid-from-unexpected-vendor"> <a href="#5-duplicate-ssid-from-unexpected-vendor" title="5. Duplicate SSID from Unexpected Vendor">5. Duplicate SSID from Unexpected Vendor</a> <a href="#5-duplicate-ssid-from-unexpected-vendor" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="5. Duplicate SSID from Unexpected Vendor">#</a> </h3> <p>Same SSID, same security type as a baseline AP, but from a BSSID with a <strong>different vendor OUI</strong> (first 3 bytes of the MAC address).</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Condition</th> <th>Points Added</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>Different OUI from baseline AP of same SSID</td> <td>+1</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Clone is also ≥ 10 dB stronger</td> <td>+2</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p><em>This is intentionally low-scored.</em> Enterprise networks, mesh systems, and ISP-deployed APs legitimately have many BSSIDs under the same SSID with different vendors. This signal is designed to accumulate alongside other evidence rather than trigger independently.</p> <h3 id="6-beacon--ssid-flood"> <a href="#6-beacon--ssid-flood" title="6. Beacon / SSID Flood">6. Beacon / SSID Flood</a> <a href="#6-beacon--ssid-flood" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="6. Beacon / SSID Flood">#</a> </h3> <p>Counts new SSIDs (not present in baseline) appearing within a <strong>30-second rolling window</strong>.</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Condition</th> <th>Points Added</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>≥ 15 new SSIDs in 30 s</td> <td>+2</td> </tr> <tr> <td>≥ 30 new SSIDs in 30 s</td> <td>+3</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p><strong>Beacon flood attacks</strong> use tools that broadcast thousands of fake SSIDs to confuse client scanning tables or denial-of-service legitimate beaconing. 15 new unknown SSIDs in 30 seconds is an unusual event in most environments.</p> <hr> <h2 id="the-confidence-scoring-model"> <a href="#the-confidence-scoring-model" title="The Confidence Scoring Model">The Confidence Scoring Model</a> <a href="#the-confidence-scoring-model" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Confidence Scoring Model">#</a> </h2> <p>All detected signals feed into a single integer <strong>threat score</strong>. The score drives LED state:</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Score Range</th> <th>LED State</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>0–2</td> <td>Normal (green)</td> </tr> <tr> <td>3–5</td> <td>Caution (yellow)</td> </tr> <tr> <td>6+</td> <td>Alert (red, fast pulse)</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <h3 id="score-decay"> <a href="#score-decay" title="Score Decay">Score Decay</a> <a href="#score-decay" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Score Decay">#</a> </h3> <p>The score <strong>decays by 1 point every 60 seconds</strong> without new triggering events. This is one of the most practically important design decisions in the project:</p> <ul> <li>A single deauth burst will push the score to Caution, then automatically decay back to Normal over several minutes if the attack stops</li> <li>A sustained attack that continues generating events holds the Alert state indefinitely</li> <li>The device self-resets without any user intervention or reboot</li> </ul> <p>This means:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Brief anomalies</strong> (roaming events, neighbor network transients) resolve themselves</li> <li><strong>Sustained attacks</strong> stay flagged</li> <li><strong>No alert fatigue</strong> from single-event triggers that don’t recur</li> </ul> <h3 id="button-reset"> <a href="#button-reset" title="Button Reset">Button Reset</a> <a href="#button-reset" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Button Reset">#</a> </h3> <p>Pressing the GPIO 39 button on the Atom Lite does two things:</p> <ol> <li>Dumps the full current AP table to serial output - useful for auditing exactly what the device sees</li> <li>Resets the threat score to 0 - forces an immediate return to the green state so you can observe the next scan cycle fresh</li> </ol> <hr> <h2 id="building-and-flashing"> <a href="#building-and-flashing" title="Building and Flashing">Building and Flashing</a> <a href="#building-and-flashing" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Building and Flashing">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="requirements"> <a href="#requirements" title="Requirements">Requirements</a> <a href="#requirements" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Requirements">#</a> </h3> <ul> <li><strong>PlatformIO</strong> (CLI or VS Code extension)</li> <li><strong>M5Stack Atom Lite</strong> (or any ESP32 DevKit for testing)</li> <li>USB-C cable</li> </ul> <h3 id="flash-to-m5stack-atom-lite"> <a href="#flash-to-m5stack-atom-lite" title="Flash to M5Stack Atom Lite">Flash to M5Stack Atom Lite</a> <a href="#flash-to-m5stack-atom-lite" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Flash to M5Stack Atom Lite">#</a> </h3> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span>git clone https://github.com/simeononsecurity/esp32-wifi-canary.git </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>cd esp32-wifi-canary </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Build and flash</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>pio run -e atom-lite --target upload </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Open serial monitor at 115200 baud</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>pio device monitor -b <span style="color:#ae81ff">115200</span> </span></span></code></pre></div><h3 id="flash-to-generic-esp32-devkit"> <a href="#flash-to-generic-esp32-devkit" title="Flash to Generic ESP32 DevKit">Flash to Generic ESP32 DevKit</a> <a href="#flash-to-generic-esp32-devkit" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Flash to Generic ESP32 DevKit">#</a> </h3> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span>pio run -e esp32dev --target upload </span></span></code></pre></div><p>The DevKit build uses GPIO 2 (onboard LED) and outputs the full serial log. No NeoPixel is driven. All detection logic is identical to the Atom Lite build.</p> <h3 id="project-structure"> <a href="#project-structure" title="Project Structure">Project Structure</a> <a href="#project-structure" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Project Structure">#</a> </h3> <pre tabindex="0"><code>esp32-wifi-canary/ ├── main.cpp # All firmware logic ├── platformio.ini # Build environments (atom-lite, esp32dev) ├── partitions_4mb.csv # 4MB flash partition table └── README.md </code></pre><p>The entire firmware is a <strong>single C++ source file</strong>. There are no external libraries for the detection logic - just the ESP32 Arduino framework for WiFi scanning, promiscuous mode callbacks, and NeoPixel control.</p> <h3 id="platformioini-environments"> <a href="#platformioini-environments" title="platformio.ini Environments">platformio.ini Environments</a> <a href="#platformioini-environments" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="platformio.ini Environments">#</a> </h3> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-ini" data-lang="ini"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#66d9ef">[env:atom-lite]</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#a6e22e">platform</span> <span style="color:#f92672">=</span> <span style="color:#e6db74">espressif32</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#a6e22e">board</span> <span style="color:#f92672">=</span> <span style="color:#e6db74">m5stick-c</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#a6e22e">framework</span> <span style="color:#f92672">=</span> <span style="color:#e6db74">arduino</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#66d9ef">[env:esp32dev]</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#a6e22e">platform</span> <span style="color:#f92672">=</span> <span style="color:#e6db74">espressif32</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#a6e22e">board</span> <span style="color:#f92672">=</span> <span style="color:#e6db74">esp32dev</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#a6e22e">framework</span> <span style="color:#f92672">=</span> <span style="color:#e6db74">arduino</span> </span></span></code></pre></div><p>The <code>atom-lite</code> environment uses the <code>m5stick-c</code> board definition (same ESP32-PICO-D4 silicon, compatible pin mapping for the NeoPixel on GPIO 27).</p> <hr> <h2 id="serial-output-reference"> <a href="#serial-output-reference" title="Serial Output Reference">Serial Output Reference</a> <a href="#serial-output-reference" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Serial Output Reference">#</a> </h2> <p>The device produces comprehensive logging at <strong>115200 baud</strong>. All log lines are prefixed with <code>[canary]</code> for easy filtering.</p> <h3 id="startup-sequence"> <a href="#startup-sequence" title="Startup Sequence">Startup Sequence</a> <a href="#startup-sequence" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Startup Sequence">#</a> </h3> <pre tabindex="0"><code>============================================== Travel WiFi Canary v1.0 Passive 2.4 GHz awareness sensor ============================================== [canary] setup done - starting baseline learning [canary] baseline scan start (aps=0 score=0) [canary] scan found 18 APs [canary] baseline scan 1/3 ... [canary] baseline complete: 19 APs learned [00] MyHomeWifi aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff ch06 WPA2 -52 dBm [01] XFINITY 11:22:33:44:55:66 ch01 WPA2 -71 dBm [canary] STARTUP → NORMAL (score=0 "") </code></pre><h3 id="detection-events"> <a href="#detection-events" title="Detection Events">Detection Events</a> <a href="#detection-events" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Detection Events">#</a> </h3> <pre tabindex="0"><code>[canary] normal scan start (aps=19 score=0) [canary] scan found 21 APs [canary] OPEN CLONE: SSID='HotelWiFi' baseline=WPA2 clone=OPEN bssid=de:ad:be:ef:00:01 rssi=-48/-52 [canary] score +4 → 4 "open clone of encrypted SSID 'HotelWiFi'" [canary] NORMAL → CAUTION (score=4 "open clone of encrypted SSID 'HotelWiFi'") </code></pre><h3 id="score-transitions"> <a href="#score-transitions" title="Score Transitions">Score Transitions</a> <a href="#score-transitions" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Score Transitions">#</a> </h3> <pre tabindex="0"><code>[canary] CAUTION → NORMAL (score=2 "score decay") [canary] NORMAL → ALERT (score=6 "sustained deauth burst + open clone") </code></pre><p>The serial log is sufficient to understand exactly what the device saw, when it saw it, and why the score moved. This makes it useful as a <strong>passive audit log</strong> even if you’re not watching the LED.</p> <hr> <h2 id="detection-notes-and-practical-limitations"> <a href="#detection-notes-and-practical-limitations" title="Detection Notes and Practical Limitations">Detection Notes and Practical Limitations</a> <a href="#detection-notes-and-practical-limitations" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Detection Notes and Practical Limitations">#</a> </h2> <p>The README is unusually honest about what this device can’t do, and that honesty is worth repeating in detail.</p> <h3 id="what-can-cause-false-positives"> <a href="#what-can-cause-false-positives" title="What Can Cause False Positives">What Can Cause False Positives</a> <a href="#what-can-cause-false-positives" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Can Cause False Positives">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Enterprise and mesh networks</strong> are the biggest source of false positives. A large enterprise deployment, a hotel with many APs, or a mesh system like Eero or Google WiFi may legitimately show:</p> <ul> <li>Multiple BSSIDs for the same SSID with different vendor OUIs (vendor detection)</li> <li>Security configuration differences between bands (security downgrade detection)</li> <li>Access points appearing and disappearing as the mesh adjusts</li> </ul> <p><strong>Airbnb and SOHO routers</strong> with multiple SSIDs for different bands can also trigger vendor OUI mismatches if the 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz radios use sequential MACs from different blocks.</p> <p><em>Neighbor network transients</em>: An encrypted network that temporarily disappears from scan and reappears with different parameters (firmware update, reboot, reconfiguration) can momentarily trigger detection.</p> <p>The confidence scoring model and decay are designed to <em>reduce - not eliminate -</em> these false positives.</p> <h3 id="what-can-cause-false-negatives"> <a href="#what-can-cause-false-negatives" title="What Can Cause False Negatives">What Can Cause False Negatives</a> <a href="#what-can-cause-false-negatives" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Can Cause False Negatives">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>A well-crafted evil-twin attack</strong> that:</p> <ul> <li>Spoofs the exact BSSID of the legitimate AP (so OUI matches)</li> <li>Matches the security type exactly (WPA2 with correct IE configuration)</li> <li>Operates at signal strength within 10 dB of the real AP</li> </ul> <p><em>…may not accumulate enough score to cross the Caution threshold.</em> The deauth component of such an attack (needed to pull clients off the real AP) would add points, but a sophisticated attacker minimizing deauth frames could potentially stay under threshold.</p> <h3 id="radio-switching-gap"> <a href="#radio-switching-gap" title="Radio Switching Gap">Radio Switching Gap</a> <a href="#radio-switching-gap" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Radio Switching Gap">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>The ESP32 WiFi radio can’t be in promiscuous mode and perform an active AP scan simultaneously.</strong> The firmware switches between these modes, which means <strong>deauth frames that arrive during the ~3 second scan window won’t be captured</strong>. <em>An attacker that precisely times deauth bursts to coincide with scan windows could theoretically evade detection</em> - though this would require knowledge of the device’s scanning schedule.</p> <h3 id="24-ghz-only"> <a href="#24-ghz-only" title="2.4 GHz Only">2.4 GHz Only</a> <a href="#24-ghz-only" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="2.4 GHz Only">#</a> </h3> <p>The ESP32 radio is a 2.4 GHz device. <strong>5 GHz and 6 GHz networks aren’t scanned or monitored</strong>. <em>In environments where 5 GHz evil twins are the attack vector, this device won’t detect them.</em></p> <h3 id="passive-range"> <a href="#passive-range" title="Passive Range">Passive Range</a> <a href="#passive-range" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Passive Range">#</a> </h3> <p>Deauth detection requires the device to be within receive range of the deauth frames. <em>A distant or highly directional attacker, or an attacker specifically targeting a client far from the canary, may not generate frames strong enough to trigger the counters.</em></p> <hr> <h2 id="use-cases"> <a href="#use-cases" title="Use Cases">Use Cases</a> <a href="#use-cases" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Use Cases">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="traveling-with-sensitive-work"> <a href="#traveling-with-sensitive-work" title="Traveling with Sensitive Work">Traveling with Sensitive Work</a> <a href="#traveling-with-sensitive-work" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Traveling with Sensitive Work">#</a> </h3> <p>The canary is designed primarily for travel. Plug it into a laptop’s USB port, a hotel room USB outlet, or a portable battery bank, and let it learn the hotel or conference center environment. Any subsequent appearance of an open clone of the hotel SSID - the most common attack scenario - will trigger at minimum a Caution state.</p> <h3 id="coffee-shops-and-public-wifi"> <a href="#coffee-shops-and-public-wifi" title="Coffee Shops and Public WiFi">Coffee Shops and Public WiFi</a> <a href="#coffee-shops-and-public-wifi" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Coffee Shops and Public WiFi">#</a> </h3> <p>Open WiFi environments are the most common attack surface for evil-twin setups. The canary’s baseline approach means it learns the legitimate coffee shop AP on arrival, then watches for competing SSIDs appearing alongside it.</p> <h3 id="security-awareness-and-education"> <a href="#security-awareness-and-education" title="Security Awareness and Education">Security Awareness and Education</a> <a href="#security-awareness-and-education" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Security Awareness and Education">#</a> </h3> <p>The device’s serial output provides a detailed, human-readable log of exactly what it sees. For security training, demonstrating to someone what a deauth burst looks like in real-time - and watching the LED change color - is substantially more effective than a slide deck.</p> <h3 id="passive-lab-monitoring"> <a href="#passive-lab-monitoring" title="Passive Lab Monitoring">Passive Lab Monitoring</a> <a href="#passive-lab-monitoring" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Passive Lab Monitoring">#</a> </h3> <p>In a home lab or small office, the canary can serve as a persistent ambient monitor. The LED provides at-a-glance status without requiring active monitoring. The button-triggered AP dump gives on-demand visibility into what networks the device currently sees.</p> <hr> <h2 id="architecture-notes"> <a href="#architecture-notes" title="Architecture Notes">Architecture Notes</a> <a href="#architecture-notes" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Architecture Notes">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="why-a-single-score-instead-of-separate-alerts"> <a href="#why-a-single-score-instead-of-separate-alerts" title="Why a Single Score Instead of Separate Alerts">Why a Single Score Instead of Separate Alerts</a> <a href="#why-a-single-score-instead-of-separate-alerts" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Why a Single Score Instead of Separate Alerts">#</a> </h3> <p>The confidence scoring model aggregates disparate signals into a single number rather than presenting separate alert categories. This is a deliberate UX decision: the output interface is one LED with three states. A separate alert per detection category would require a display or app. <em>The scoring model translates noisy, partially-correlated signals into a single actionable indicator.</em></p> <h3 id="why-score-decay"> <a href="#why-score-decay" title="Why Score Decay">Why Score Decay</a> <a href="#why-score-decay" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Why Score Decay">#</a> </h3> <p>Without decay, a single false-positive event would require manual intervention (button press) to clear. With 60-second decay, brief anomalies clear themselves within a few minutes. <strong>This means the canary can run unattended</strong> - in a bag, a hotel room, or a car - and return to baseline without user intervention after transient events.</p> <h3 id="why-three-baseline-scans"> <a href="#why-three-baseline-scans" title="Why Three Baseline Scans">Why Three Baseline Scans</a> <a href="#why-three-baseline-scans" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Why Three Baseline Scans">#</a> </h3> <p>A single baseline scan might miss an AP that wasn’t broadcasting during that window (AP was in a scan gap, AP was temporarily powered off, etc.). <em>Three scans over ~24 seconds provide a more complete picture of the stable environment before monitoring begins.</em></p> <hr> <h2 id="contributing-and-project-status"> <a href="#contributing-and-project-status" title="Contributing and Project Status">Contributing and Project Status</a> <a href="#contributing-and-project-status" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Contributing and Project Status">#</a> </h2> <p>The project is available at <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="github.com/simeononsecurity/esp32-wifi-canary"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://github.com/simeononsecurity/esp32-wifi-canary"> <a href="https://github.com/simeononsecurity/esp32-wifi-canary" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >github.com/simeononsecurity/esp32-wifi-canary</a> </span> under a passive-use license. The codebase is a single <code>main.cpp</code> file, making it straightforward to read, audit, and extend.</p> <p>If you’re working with PlatformIO and ESP32, the project is structured to compile with no modification for both the Atom Lite and the standard DevKit environments.</p> <hr> <h2 id="comparison-to-other-wifi-monitoring-approaches"> <a href="#comparison-to-other-wifi-monitoring-approaches" title="Comparison to Other WiFi Monitoring Approaches">Comparison to Other WiFi Monitoring Approaches</a> <a href="#comparison-to-other-wifi-monitoring-approaches" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Comparison to Other WiFi Monitoring Approaches">#</a> </h2> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Approach</th> <th>Passive?</th> <th>No Credentials</th> <th>Single LED Output</th> <th>Travel Form Factor</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td>ESP32 WiFi Canary</td> <td>✅</td> <td>✅</td> <td>✅</td> <td>✅</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Kismet (laptop)</td> <td>✅</td> <td>✅</td> <td>❌ (requires display)</td> <td>❌ (heavy)</td> </tr> <tr> <td>WiFi Pineapple</td> <td>❌ (active)</td> <td>❌</td> <td>❌</td> <td>Partial</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Wireless IDS (enterprise)</td> <td>✅</td> <td>✅</td> <td>❌ (requires infrastructure)</td> <td>❌</td> </tr> <tr> <td>Manual scanning (phone app)</td> <td>Partial</td> <td>✅</td> <td>❌</td> <td>✅</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p>The canary occupies a specific niche: <strong>zero-interaction, zero-network, always-on ambient awareness</strong> in a form factor that can live permanently on a keychain or in a laptop bag.</p> <hr> <h2 id="conclusion"> <a href="#conclusion" title="Conclusion">Conclusion</a> <a href="#conclusion" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Conclusion">#</a> </h2> <p>The ESP32 WiFi Canary is a tightly scoped tool that does one thing: watch the 2.4 GHz environment around you and change color when something looks wrong. It doesn’t try to be a full wireless intrusion detection system, a packet capture tool, or a forensic analyzer. It’s a canary - a passive sensor whose job is to notice when the mine gets dangerous.</p> <p>The <strong>confidence scoring model</strong>, <strong>score decay</strong>, and <strong>three-phase baseline approach</strong> reflect careful thinking about the false-positive problem that plagues ambient security sensors. The result is a device that can run unattended in a hotel room or conference center and reliably signal when something meaningfully unusual is happening - while staying quiet during normal network churn.</p> <p>For anyone building a portable security toolkit, working in environments with untrusted WiFi infrastructure, or looking for an ESP32 project with real practical utility, the WiFi Canary is worth an afternoon and a $15 M5Stack Atom Lite.</p> <p><strong>GitHub</strong>: <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="github.com/simeononsecurity/esp32-wifi-canary"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://github.com/simeononsecurity/esp32-wifi-canary"> <a href="https://github.com/simeononsecurity/esp32-wifi-canary" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >github.com/simeononsecurity/esp32-wifi-canary</a> </span> </p> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.ch/

item.title: DagShell Custom Firmware for Orbic RCL400: Complete Installation and Usage Guide 2026
item.category: "Custom Firmware" , "Mobile Security" , "Privacy Tools" , "Network Security" , "Wardriving" , "Penetration Testing" , "IoT Hacking" , "Security Research" , "Hardware Hacking" , "Privacy Technology"
item.description: Comprehensive guide to DagShell custom firmware for Orbic RCL400 hotspot including installation, privacy tools, hacking features, wardriving capabilities, and why it pairs perfectly with RayHunter for mobile security research.
item.content:encoded: <p><strong>Transform Your Orbic RCL400 Into a Mobile Security Research Laboratory</strong></p> <h2 id="introduction-a-hackers-hotspot"> <a href="#introduction-a-hackers-hotspot" title="Introduction: A Hacker’s Hotspot">Introduction: A Hacker’s Hotspot</a> <a href="#introduction-a-hackers-hotspot" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Introduction: A Hacker’s Hotspot">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>DagShell</strong> is open-source custom firmware for the <strong>Orbic RCL400 mobile hotspot</strong> that transforms an ordinary cellular device into a <strong>portable security research and privacy toolkit</strong>. Created by security researcher “dag,” this terminal-styled firmware provides <strong>hacking tools, privacy features, and network monitoring capabilities</strong> in a sleek, green-on-black hacker aesthetic interface.</p> <p>This comprehensive guide covers:</p> <ul> <li><strong>What DagShell is</strong> and its complete feature set</li> <li><strong>Step-by-step installation</strong> instructions (webflasher and manual methods)</li> <li><strong>All tools and capabilities</strong> explained in detail</li> <li><strong>Raspberry Pi companion</strong> setup for extended functionality</li> <li><strong>Why pair DagShell with RayHunter</strong> for ultimate mobile security</li> <li><strong>Real-world use cases</strong> for security researchers and privacy advocates</li> <li><strong>Legal and ethical considerations</strong></li> </ul> <p><strong>TL;DR</strong>: DagShell + RayHunter on Orbic RCL400 = <strong>Complete mobile security laboratory</strong> for IMSI catcher detection, wardriving, network analysis, and privacy protection.</p> <p><strong>Pre-Flashed Devices Available</strong>: This article is sponsored by <strong>STS Collective</strong>, offering pre-flashed Orbic RCL400 hotspots with both <strong>RayHunter and DagShell</strong> pre-installed and ready to use: <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="stscollective.com/products/orbic-rcl400-rayhunter-dagshell-hotspot"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://stscollective.com/products/orbic-rcl400-rayhunter-dagshell-hotspot"> <a href="https://stscollective.com/products/orbic-rcl400-rayhunter-dagshell-hotspot" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >stscollective.com/products/orbic-rcl400-rayhunter-dagshell-hotspot</a> </span> </p> <hr> <h2 id="what-is-dagshell"> <a href="#what-is-dagshell" title="What Is DagShell?">What Is DagShell?</a> <a href="#what-is-dagshell" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Is DagShell?">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="overview"> <a href="#overview" title="Overview">Overview</a> <a href="#overview" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Overview">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>DagShell</strong> is open-source custom firmware that replaces the stock Orbic RCL400 web interface with a <strong>comprehensive security toolkit</strong> featuring:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Terminal-style interface</strong> with ASCII art and hacker aesthetics</li> <li><strong>TLS 1.2+ encrypted</strong> web interface (self-signed certificate)</li> <li><strong>Privacy protection tools</strong> (TTL masking, MAC spoofing, DNS-based ad blocking)</li> <li><strong>Network monitoring</strong> (active connections, routing tables, DNS queries)</li> <li><strong>Hacking tools</strong> (IMSI catcher detection, port scanning, ARP discovery)</li> <li><strong>Attack capabilities</strong> (Evil Twin AP, captive portal phishing, deauth attacks)</li> <li><strong>GPS tracking and wardriving</strong> with Wigle-compatible CSV export</li> <li><strong>Raspberry Pi companion</strong> for GPS, Bluetooth scanning, and WiFi reconnaissance</li> <li><strong>File system access</strong> with browser-based file manager</li> <li><strong>SMS functionality</strong> via AT commands</li> <li><strong>Persistence</strong> - Auto-starts on boot</li> </ul> <h3 id="technical-specifications"> <a href="#technical-specifications" title="Technical Specifications">Technical Specifications</a> <a href="#technical-specifications" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Technical Specifications">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Platform</strong>: Orbic RCL400 mobile hotspot <strong>Architecture</strong>: ARM Linux (kernel 3.18) <strong>Language</strong>: C/C++ (static ARM binary) <strong>Encryption</strong>: TLS 1.2+ with self-signed certificates (2-tier PKI) <strong>Web Server</strong>: Custom embedded HTTPS server (port 8443) <strong>Interface</strong>: Browser-based terminal UI <strong>License</strong>: MIT (open-source) <strong>GitHub</strong>: <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="github.com/dagnazty/DagShell"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://github.com/dagnazty/DagShell"> <a href="https://github.com/dagnazty/DagShell" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >github.com/dagnazty/DagShell</a> </span> </p> <h3 id="visual-design"> <a href="#visual-design" title="Visual Design">Visual Design</a> <a href="#visual-design" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Visual Design">#</a> </h3> <p>DagShell features a <strong>retro hacker aesthetic</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>ASCII art logo</strong> on every page</li> <li><strong>Green-on-black color scheme</strong> (Matrix-style)</li> <li><strong>Monospace font</strong> (Fira Code)</li> <li><strong>Scanline effects</strong> and glowing text</li> <li><strong>Terminal-inspired layout</strong></li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="complete-feature-breakdown"> <a href="#complete-feature-breakdown" title="Complete Feature Breakdown">Complete Feature Breakdown</a> <a href="#complete-feature-breakdown" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Complete Feature Breakdown">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="-dashboard"> <a href="#-dashboard" title="🏠 Dashboard">🏠 Dashboard</a> <a href="#-dashboard" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="🏠 Dashboard">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>System Overview</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Uptime display</strong> - How long device has been running</li> <li><strong>AT command interface</strong> - Direct modem control for advanced users</li> <li><strong>Quick status</strong> - IP address, connection status</li> </ul> <p><strong>AT Commands</strong> enable low-level modem control:</p> <pre tabindex="0"><code>Example AT Commands: AT+CSQ - Signal quality AT+COPS? - Current network operator AT+CREG? - Network registration status AT+CIMI - Get IMSI (subscriber identity) AT+CGSN - Get IMEI (device identifier) </code></pre><h3 id="-network-tools"> <a href="#-network-tools" title="🌐 Network Tools">🌐 Network Tools</a> <a href="#-network-tools" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="🌐 Network Tools">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Current Network Info</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>IP address and interface</strong> details</li> <li><strong>Subnet mask, gateway</strong>, broadcast address</li> <li><strong>Network statistics</strong> (packets sent/received)</li> </ul> <p><strong>Routing Table Viewer</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>See all network routes</li> <li>Default gateway information</li> <li>Interface mapping</li> </ul> <p><strong>Active Connections Monitor</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Real-time list of <strong>all TCP/UDP connections</strong></li> <li>Local and remote IP addresses</li> <li>Port numbers and connection states</li> <li>Useful for <strong>monitoring what the device is communicating with</strong></li> </ul> <h3 id="-privacy-protection-suite"> <a href="#-privacy-protection-suite" title="🔒 Privacy Protection Suite">🔒 Privacy Protection Suite</a> <a href="#-privacy-protection-suite" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="🔒 Privacy Protection Suite">#</a> </h3> <h4 id="ttl-fix"> <a href="#ttl-fix" title="TTL Fix">TTL Fix</a> <a href="#ttl-fix" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="TTL Fix">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Purpose</strong>: Mask hotspot traffic from carrier detection</p> <p><strong>How It Works</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Modifies <strong>Time To Live (TTL)</strong> value in IP packets to <strong>65</strong></li> <li>Carriers detect tethering by TTL decrements (phone=64, tethered device=63)</li> <li>Setting TTL to 65 makes <strong>all traffic appear local</strong></li> </ul> <p><strong>Use Case</strong>: Bypass carrier tethering restrictions/throttling</p> <p><strong>Commands</strong>:</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Enable TTL fix</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>iptables -t mangle -A POSTROUTING -j TTL --ttl-set <span style="color:#ae81ff">65</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Disable TTL fix </span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>iptables -t mangle -D POSTROUTING -j TTL --ttl-set <span style="color:#ae81ff">65</span> </span></span></code></pre></div><h4 id="mac-address-spoofing"> <a href="#mac-address-spoofing" title="MAC Address Spoofing">MAC Address Spoofing</a> <a href="#mac-address-spoofing" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="MAC Address Spoofing">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Purpose</strong>: Randomize device MAC address for privacy</p> <p><strong>How It Works</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Changes MAC address of <strong>wlan0</strong> (WiFi interface)</li> <li>Generates <strong>random MAC</strong> or allows custom input</li> <li>Makes device <strong>untraceable</strong> across sessions</li> </ul> <p><strong>Use Case</strong>: Prevent MAC-based tracking by networks you connect to</p> <p><strong>Process</strong>:</p> <ol> <li>Interface goes down</li> <li>New MAC address applied</li> <li>Interface comes back up</li> <li>Connection re-establishes</li> </ol> <h4 id="dns-based-adblock"> <a href="#dns-based-adblock" title="DNS-Based AdBlock">DNS-Based AdBlock</a> <a href="#dns-based-adblock" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="DNS-Based AdBlock">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Purpose</strong>: Block ads and tracking at DNS level</p> <p><strong>How It Works</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Modifies <code>/etc/hosts</code> file with <strong>blocklist</strong></li> <li>Domains on list resolve to <strong>127.0.0.1</strong> (localhost)</li> <li>Blocks ads <strong>for all connected devices</strong></li> </ul> <p><strong>Use Case</strong>: Network-wide ad blocking without per-device configuration</p> <p><strong>Blocklist Source</strong>: Common ad/tracking domains (customizable)</p> <h3 id="-sms-management"> <a href="#-sms-management" title="📱 SMS Management">📱 SMS Management</a> <a href="#-sms-management" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="📱 SMS Management">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Send SMS</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Send text messages via <strong>AT commands</strong> to modem</li> <li>Useful for <strong>remote notifications</strong> or testing</li> </ul> <p><strong>View Messages</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Link to Orbic’s native inbox</li> <li>Read received SMS messages</li> </ul> <p><strong>AT Command Used</strong>:</p> <pre tabindex="0"><code>AT+CMGS="PHONE_NUMBER" Message text here^Z </code></pre><h3 id="-hacking-tools"> <a href="#-hacking-tools" title="🔧 Hacking Tools">🔧 Hacking Tools</a> <a href="#-hacking-tools" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="🔧 Hacking Tools">#</a> </h3> <h4 id="imsi-catcher-detector"> <a href="#imsi-catcher-detector" title="IMSI Catcher Detector">IMSI Catcher Detector</a> <a href="#imsi-catcher-detector" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="IMSI Catcher Detector">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Purpose</strong>: Monitor cell tower information for anomalies that indicate <strong>IMSI catcher/Stingray</strong> devices</p> <p><strong>How It Works</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Queries modem for <strong>cell tower data</strong>: <ul> <li><strong>Cell ID</strong> (tower identifier)</li> <li><strong>LAC</strong> (Location Area Code)</li> <li><strong>MCC/MNC</strong> (Mobile Country/Network Code)</li> <li><strong>Signal strength</strong> (RSSI)</li> <li><strong>Network generation</strong> (2G/3G/4G)</li> </ul> </li> <li><strong>Logs changes</strong> to detect suspicious tower switches</li> <li><strong>Baseline tracking</strong> - Establishes normal towers in area</li> </ul> <p><strong>Detection Indicators</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Sudden cell tower switch</strong> while stationary</li> <li><strong>Downgrade to 2G</strong> <em>(IMSI catchers often force 2G to strip encryption)</em></li> <li><strong>Unknown Cell ID</strong> appearing</li> <li><strong>Weak signal</strong> from fake tower</li> <li><strong>Frequent reconnections</strong></li> </ul> <p><strong>Synergy with RayHunter</strong>: DagShell provides <strong>cell tower monitoring</strong>, RayHunter provides <strong>dedicated IMSI catcher detection</strong> with more sophisticated analysis (see section below)</p> <h4 id="port-scanner"> <a href="#port-scanner" title="Port Scanner">Port Scanner</a> <a href="#port-scanner" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Port Scanner">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Purpose</strong>: Scan target IP addresses for open ports</p> <p><strong>How It Works</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>TCP SYN scanning</strong> - Sends SYN packets to target ports</li> <li><strong>Timeout detection</strong> - Determines open/closed/filtered</li> <li><strong>Range support</strong> - Scan single port or port ranges</li> </ul> <p><strong>Use Cases</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Network reconnaissance</strong></li> <li><strong>IoT device discovery</strong></li> <li><strong>Security auditing</strong> of local networks</li> <li><strong>Service identification</strong></li> </ul> <p><strong>Command</strong>:</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Scan ports 1-1000 on target</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>nc -zv -w <span style="color:#ae81ff">2</span> TARGET_IP 1-1000 2>&<span style="color:#ae81ff">1</span> </span></span></code></pre></div><h4 id="firewall-manager"> <a href="#firewall-manager" title="Firewall Manager">Firewall Manager</a> <a href="#firewall-manager" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Firewall Manager">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Purpose</strong>: Block or unblock IP addresses using iptables</p> <p><strong>How It Works</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>iptables rules</strong> to DROP packets from/to specified IPs</li> <li><strong>Persistent rules</strong> survive reboots (if saved)</li> <li><strong>Whitelist/Blacklist</strong> functionality</li> </ul> <p><strong>Use Cases</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Block malicious IPs</strong></li> <li><strong>Prevent unauthorized access</strong></li> <li><strong>Traffic shaping/filtering</strong></li> <li><strong>Parental controls</strong></li> </ul> <p><strong>Commands</strong>:</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Block IP</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>iptables -I INPUT -s IP_ADDRESS -j DROP </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>iptables -I OUTPUT -d IP_ADDRESS -j DROP </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Unblock IP</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>iptables -D INPUT -s IP_ADDRESS -j DROP </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>iptables -D OUTPUT -d IP_ADDRESS -j DROP </span></span></code></pre></div><h3 id="-attack-tools"> <a href="#-attack-tools" title="⚔️ Attack Tools">⚔️ Attack Tools</a> <a href="#-attack-tools" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="⚔️ Attack Tools">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>IMPORTANT LEGAL DISCLAIMER</strong>: These tools are for <strong>authorized security testing ONLY</strong>. Using them against networks you don’t own or have explicit written permission to test is <strong>ILLEGAL</strong> in most jurisdictions (Computer Fraud and Abuse Act in US, Computer Misuse Act in UK, etc.). Only use on <strong>your own networks</strong> or in <strong>controlled lab environments</strong>.</p> <h4 id="dns-sniffer"> <a href="#dns-sniffer" title="DNS Sniffer">DNS Sniffer</a> <a href="#dns-sniffer" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="DNS Sniffer">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Purpose</strong>: Log DNS queries from connected clients</p> <p><strong>How It Works</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>iptables logging</strong> on port 53 (DNS)</li> <li><strong>Does NOT require promiscuous mode</strong></li> <li>Logs all DNS requests from hotspot clients</li> <li>Reveals <strong>what websites/services</strong> clients are accessing</li> </ul> <p><strong>Use Cases</strong> (Authorized):</p> <ul> <li><strong>Network traffic analysis</strong></li> <li><strong>Parental monitoring</strong> (own family)</li> <li><strong>Security research</strong> on own devices</li> <li><strong>Malware behavior analysis</strong></li> </ul> <p><em>This captures metadata (domains visited) from connected clients — deploy only on networks you own or administer.</em></p> <h4 id="arp-scanner"> <a href="#arp-scanner" title="ARP Scanner">ARP Scanner</a> <a href="#arp-scanner" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="ARP Scanner">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Purpose</strong>: Discover devices on local network</p> <p><strong>How It Works</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Sends <strong>ARP requests</strong> to all IPs in subnet</li> <li>Devices respond with their <strong>MAC addresses</strong></li> <li><strong>OUI lookup</strong> identifies manufacturer (Apple, Samsung, etc.)</li> <li>Creates <strong>network map</strong> of active devices</li> </ul> <p><strong>Use Cases</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Network inventory</strong></li> <li><strong>Unknown device detection</strong></li> <li><strong>BYOD network analysis</strong></li> <li><strong>IoT device discovery</strong></li> </ul> <p><strong>Output Example</strong>:</p> <pre tabindex="0"><code>IP: 192.168.1.50 MAC: A4:83:E7:XX:XX:XX Vendor: Apple, Inc. IP: 192.168.1.75 MAC: 2C:54:91:XX:XX:XX Vendor: Samsung Electronics </code></pre><h4 id="traceroute"> <a href="#traceroute" title="Traceroute">Traceroute</a> <a href="#traceroute" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Traceroute">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Purpose</strong>: Visualize network path to destination</p> <p><strong>How It Works</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Sends packets with <strong>incrementing TTL</strong></li> <li>Each hop decrements TTL and returns <strong>ICMP Time Exceeded</strong></li> <li>Reveals <strong>routers and path</strong> to destination</li> <li>Measures <strong>Round-Trip Time (RTT)</strong> per hop</li> </ul> <p><strong>Use Cases</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Network troubleshooting</strong></li> <li><strong>Route analysis</strong></li> <li><strong>Latency diagnosis</strong></li> <li><strong>ISP peering investigation</strong></li> </ul> <h4 id="evil-twin-ap"> <a href="#evil-twin-ap" title="Evil Twin AP">Evil Twin AP</a> <a href="#evil-twin-ap" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Evil Twin AP">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Purpose</strong>: Create fake WiFi access point cloning existing SSIDs</p> <p><strong>How It Works</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Uses <strong>wlan1</strong> (second WiFi interface if available via Pi companion)</li> <li>Clones <strong>SSID, encryption type</strong> of target network</li> <li><strong>Captures clients</strong> attempting to connect</li> <li>Serves <strong>captive portal</strong> for credential harvesting</li> </ul> <p><strong>Attack Scenario</strong> (Lab Environment):</p> <ol> <li>Scan for legitimate WiFi networks</li> <li>Create <strong>fake AP</strong> with same SSID</li> <li>Use <strong>deauth attack</strong> to kick clients off real AP</li> <li>Clients auto-reconnect to <strong>fake AP</strong></li> <li>Captive portal captures credentials</li> </ol> <p><em>Detection: Clients see <strong>duplicate SSIDs</strong> if both APs are visible simultaneously.</em></p> <h4 id="captive-portal"> <a href="#captive-portal" title="Captive Portal">Captive Portal</a> <a href="#captive-portal" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Captive Portal">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Purpose</strong>: Phishing page templates for credential harvesting</p> <p><strong>Templates Included</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>WiFi login page</strong> (generic ISP/hotel style)</li> <li><strong>Social media logins</strong> (Facebook, Twitter, Instagram lookalikes)</li> <li><strong>Corporate login</strong> portals</li> <li><strong>Airport/Coffee shop</strong> WiFi gates</li> </ul> <p><strong>How It Works</strong>:</p> <ol> <li>Evil Twin AP redirects <strong>all traffic</strong> to portal</li> <li>Client sees <strong>login page</strong></li> <li>Client enters credentials</li> <li>Credentials <strong>logged to file</strong></li> <li>Client either granted internet or shown error</li> </ol> <p><strong>Educational Purpose</strong>: Demonstrates <strong>social engineering risks</strong> and why users should verify URLs</p> <h3 id="-gps-tracker--wardriving"> <a href="#-gps-tracker--wardriving" title="📍 GPS Tracker & Wardriving">📍 GPS Tracker & Wardriving</a> <a href="#-gps-tracker--wardriving" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="📍 GPS Tracker & Wardriving">#</a> </h3> <h4 id="gps-functionality"> <a href="#gps-functionality" title="GPS Functionality">GPS Functionality</a> <a href="#gps-functionality" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="GPS Functionality">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>GPS Source</strong>: <strong>Raspberry Pi companion ONLY</strong></p> <ul> <li><em>The Orbic RCL400 has <strong>no built-in GPS</strong> — skipping the Pi companion means no location data</em></li> <li>Pi connects <strong>USB GPS dongle</strong> (U-Blox 7 chipset)</li> <li>Pi sends coordinates to Orbic via <strong>shared data files</strong></li> <li><strong>ECEF to Lat/Long</strong> conversion handled automatically</li> </ul> <p><strong>Dashboard Display</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Latitude/Longitude</strong> in decimal degrees</li> <li><strong>Altitude</strong> above sea level</li> <li><strong>GPS fix status</strong> (accuracy)</li> <li><strong>Auto-refresh</strong> every 5 seconds</li> <li><strong>No browser geolocation prompts</strong> (doesn’t use browser API)</li> </ul> <p><strong>Shared GPS State</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>GPS data available to <strong>all DagShell processes</strong></li> <li>Wardriving automatically uses GPS</li> <li>No conflicts between features</li> </ul> <h4 id="wardriving-mode"> <a href="#wardriving-mode" title="Wardriving Mode">Wardriving Mode</a> <a href="#wardriving-mode" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Wardriving Mode">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Purpose</strong>: Scan WiFi networks with GPS coordinates for mapping</p> <p><strong>How It Works</strong>:</p> <ol> <li>Waits for <strong>valid GPS fix</strong> (no 0,0 coordinates logged)</li> <li>Scans WiFi networks every <strong>5 seconds</strong> (continuous loop)</li> <li>Logs network data: <ul> <li><strong>SSID</strong> (network name)</li> <li><strong>BSSID</strong> (MAC address)</li> <li><strong>Encryption type</strong> (WPA2, WPA3, Open)</li> <li><strong>Signal strength</strong> (RSSI in dBm)</li> <li><strong>Channel number</strong></li> <li><strong>GPS coordinates</strong> (lat/long)</li> <li><strong>Timestamp</strong></li> </ul> </li> <li>Exports to <strong>Wigle-compatible CSV</strong> format</li> </ol> <p><strong>Wigle Integration</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>WiGLE</strong> (wigle.net) is global WiFi mapping project</li> <li>DagShell CSV is <strong>directly uploadable</strong> to WiGLE</li> <li><strong>Browser-based upload</strong> from Files page (no external tools needed)</li> <li>Contributes to <strong>public database</strong> of WiFi locations</li> </ul> <p><strong>Use Cases</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>WiFi coverage mapping</strong></li> <li><strong>Network surveying</strong> for ISPs</li> <li><strong>Signal strength analysis</strong></li> <li><strong>Security research</strong> (open networks, weak encryption)</li> <li><strong>Location-based analytics</strong></li> </ul> <p><strong>CSV Format Example</strong>:</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-csv" data-lang="csv"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#e6db74">MAC</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">SSID</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">AuthMode</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">FirstSeen</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">Channel</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">RSSI</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">Latitude</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">Longitude</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">AltitudeMeters</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#e6db74">A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">HomeNetwork</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">WPA2</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">2026-05-28 10:30:15</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">6</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">-45</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">40.7128</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">-74.0060</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">10</span> </span></span></code></pre></div><h3 id="-raspberry-pi-companion"> <a href="#-raspberry-pi-companion" title="🥧 Raspberry Pi Companion">🥧 Raspberry Pi Companion</a> <a href="#-raspberry-pi-companion" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="🥧 Raspberry Pi Companion">#</a> </h3> <p>The <strong>Raspberry Pi companion</strong> extends DagShell capabilities with <strong>external hardware</strong>:</p> <h4 id="hardware-requirements"> <a href="#hardware-requirements" title="Hardware Requirements">Hardware Requirements</a> <a href="#hardware-requirements" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Hardware Requirements">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Minimum</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Raspberry Pi 3B+</strong> or newer <em>(Pi Zero lacks USB power for peripherals)</em></li> <li><strong>USB GPS dongle</strong> (U-Blox 7 chipset recommended)</li> <li><strong>Power supply</strong> <em>(Pi requires separate power — the Orbic USB port does not supply enough)</em></li> </ul> <p><strong>Optional</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>WiFi adapter</strong> (second interface for scanning while maintaining link)</li> <li><strong>Bluetooth dongle</strong> (if Pi has weak internal BT)</li> </ul> <h4 id="gps-module-u-blox-7"> <a href="#gps-module-u-blox-7" title="GPS Module (U-Blox 7)">GPS Module (U-Blox 7)</a> <a href="#gps-module-u-blox-7" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="GPS Module (U-Blox 7)">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Connection</strong>: USB GPS dongle to Pi <strong>Protocol</strong>: NMEA sentences via serial <strong>Conversion</strong>: Pi converts ECEF to Lat/Long automatically <strong>Data sharing</strong>: Writes GPS coordinates to <strong>shared file</strong> on Orbic</p> <p><strong>Sentence Parsing</strong>:</p> <pre tabindex="0"><code>$GPGGA,123519,4807.038,N,01131.000,E,1,08,0.9,545.4,M,46.9,M,,*47 | | | | | | | Time Latitude Longitude Fix Sats Altitude </code></pre><h4 id="bluetooth-scanning-ble"> <a href="#bluetooth-scanning-ble" title="Bluetooth Scanning (BLE)">Bluetooth Scanning (BLE)</a> <a href="#bluetooth-scanning-ble" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Bluetooth Scanning (BLE)">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Purpose</strong>: Discover nearby Bluetooth devices</p> <p><strong>How It Works</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Scans for <strong>BLE advertisements</strong></li> <li>Captures <strong>MAC addresses</strong></li> <li><strong>OUI lookup</strong> for manufacturer identification</li> <li>Logs to <strong>CSV format</strong></li> </ul> <p><strong>Remote Control</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Start/Stop</strong> scanning from DagShell web UI</li> <li>No need to SSH into Pi</li> <li>Real-time status updates</li> </ul> <p><strong>Use Cases</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Device tracking</strong> and counting</li> <li><strong>Foot traffic analysis</strong></li> <li><strong>Bluetooth device discovery</strong></li> <li><strong>IoT security research</strong></li> </ul> <p><strong>CSV Output</strong>:</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-csv" data-lang="csv"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#e6db74">Timestamp</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">MAC</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">RSSI</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">Name</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">Manufacturer</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#e6db74">2026-05-28 10:30:15</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">-65</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">Fitbit Charge</span>,<span style="color:#e6db74">Fitbit Inc.</span> </span></span></code></pre></div><h4 id="wifi-scanning"> <a href="#wifi-scanning" title="WiFi Scanning">WiFi Scanning</a> <a href="#wifi-scanning" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="WiFi Scanning">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Purpose</strong>: Pi scans WiFi networks and sends data to Orbic</p> <p><strong>Advantage</strong>: Dedicated interface for scanning while <strong>Orbic maintains hotspot</strong></p> <p><strong>How It Works</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Pi uses <strong>second WiFi adapter</strong> or built-in WiFi (if not needed for connectivity)</li> <li>Scans <strong>all
