item.title: Flock Safety Camera Security Vulnerabilities: Critical Analysis of 50+ Discovered Flaws in 2026
item.category: "Security Research" , "Vulnerability Analysis" , "Surveillance Technology" , "Cybersecurity" , "Privacy Security" , "IoT Security" , "Physical Security" , "Network Security" , "Critical Infrastructure" , "Government Technology"
item.description: Comprehensive analysis of 50+ critical security vulnerabilities discovered in Flock Safety ALPR cameras including hardcoded passwords, lack of encryption, unauthorized data collection, and physical access exploits based on independent security research.
item.content:encoded: <p><strong>50+ Critical Security Vulnerabilities Expose Nation’s Largest Private Surveillance Network</strong></p> <h2 id="introduction-a-national-security-crisis"> <a href="#introduction-a-national-security-crisis" title="Introduction: A National Security Crisis">Introduction: A National Security Crisis</a> <a href="#introduction-a-national-security-crisis" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Introduction: A National Security Crisis">#</a> </h2> <p>In late 2024 and throughout 2025, independent security researchers uncovered what may be <strong>the most significant security failure in law enforcement surveillance technology</strong> in American history. Over <strong>50 critical vulnerabilities</strong> have been discovered in Flock Safety’s camera systems - the same cameras that photograph and track over <strong>150 million vehicles daily</strong> across more than <strong>80,000 deployments</strong> nationwide.</p> <p>This article provides a comprehensive technical analysis of these vulnerabilities based on:</p> <ul> <li><strong>GainSec’s formal white paper</strong> “Examining the Security Posture of an Anti-Crime Ecosystem” (51 findings, 22 assigned CVEs, 8 pending)</li> <li><strong>Ben Jordan’s investigative journalism</strong> and hands-on security testing</li> <li><strong>404 Media’s reporting</strong> on publicly exposed camera feeds</li> <li><strong>Official responses</strong> from Flock Safety and U.S. Senators</li> <li><strong>National Vulnerability Database</strong> (NVD) published disclosures</li> </ul> <p>For context on <strong>why these cameras exist</strong> and <strong>privacy implications</strong>, see our article: <strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Flock Safety Camera Surveillance: Prevalence, Privacy Concerns, and Protection Strategies"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/articles/flock-safety-camera-surveillance-prevalence-privacy-protection-2026/"> <a href="/articles/flock-safety-camera-surveillance-prevalence-privacy-protection-2026/" rel="follow me" >Flock Safety Camera Surveillance: Prevalence, Privacy Concerns, and Protection Strategies</a> </span> </strong></p> <p>For information on <strong>detecting these cameras</strong>, see: <strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Flock-You Detection Project: Counter-Surveillance Hardware Guide"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/"> <a href="/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/" rel="follow me" >Flock-You Detection Project: Counter-Surveillance Hardware Guide</a> </span> </strong></p> <hr> <h2 id="the-scope-of-the-problem"> <a href="#the-scope-of-the-problem" title="The Scope of the Problem">The Scope of the Problem</a> <a href="#the-scope-of-the-problem" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Scope of the Problem">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="scale-of-vulnerable-infrastructure"> <a href="#scale-of-vulnerable-infrastructure" title="Scale of Vulnerable Infrastructure">Scale of Vulnerable Infrastructure</a> <a href="#scale-of-vulnerable-infrastructure" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Scale of Vulnerable Infrastructure">#</a> </h3> <p>As of May 2026:</p> <ul> <li><strong>80,000+ Flock Safety cameras</strong> deployed across the United States</li> <li><strong>5,000+ cities and towns</strong> using Flock services</li> <li><strong>3,500+ law enforcement agencies</strong> with system access</li> <li><strong>22,000+ law enforcement users</strong> accessing databases</li> <li><strong>Billions of data points</strong> stored in searchable databases</li> </ul> <h3 id="vulnerability-timeline"> <a href="#vulnerability-timeline" title="Vulnerability Timeline">Vulnerability Timeline</a> <a href="#vulnerability-timeline" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Vulnerability Timeline">#</a> </h3> <ul> <li><strong>Late 2024</strong>: Initial vulnerabilities discovered by security researcher Jon “GainSec” Gaines</li> <li><strong>February 2025</strong>: Responsible disclosure to Flock Safety begins</li> <li><strong>April 2025</strong>: First CVE assignments published</li> <li><strong>November 2025</strong>: Formal white paper published with 51 findings</li> <li><strong>December 2025</strong>: Ben Jordan demonstrates vulnerabilities on video</li> <li><strong>January 2026</strong>: 404 Media discovers 60+ publicly accessible camera feeds</li> <li><strong>February 2026</strong>: U.S. Senators request FTC investigation</li> <li><strong>May 2026</strong>: Ongoing disclosure process continues</li> </ul> <h3 id="vulnerability-categories"> <a href="#vulnerability-categories" title="Vulnerability Categories">Vulnerability Categories</a> <a href="#vulnerability-categories" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Vulnerability Categories">#</a> </h3> <p>The 50+ vulnerabilities span multiple categories:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Authentication & Authorization</strong> (hardcoded passwords, lack of MFA)</li> <li><strong>Cryptography & Encryption</strong> (unencrypted data at rest and in transit)</li> <li><strong>Network Security</strong> (exposed WiFi access points, clear-text credentials)</li> <li><strong>Physical Security</strong> (button press exploits, exposed USB ports)</li> <li><strong>Data Privacy</strong> (unauthorized data collection, extended retention)</li> <li><strong>System Design</strong> (outdated software, inadequate access controls)</li> <li><strong>Information Disclosure</strong> (exposed API keys, public camera feeds)</li> </ol> <hr> <h2 id="critical-vulnerability-1-button-press-wireless-access-point"> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-1-button-press-wireless-access-point" title="Critical Vulnerability #1: Button Press Wireless Access Point">Critical Vulnerability #1: Button Press Wireless Access Point</a> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-1-button-press-wireless-access-point" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Critical Vulnerability #1: Button Press Wireless Access Point">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="cve-2025-xxxxx-pending-assignment"> <a href="#cve-2025-xxxxx-pending-assignment" title="CVE-2025-XXXXX (Pending Assignment)">CVE-2025-XXXXX (Pending Assignment)</a> <a href="#cve-2025-xxxxx-pending-assignment" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="CVE-2025-XXXXX (Pending Assignment)">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Severity</strong>: CRITICAL (CVSS 9.8)</p> <h3 id="the-exploit"> <a href="#the-exploit" title="The Exploit">The Exploit</a> <a href="#the-exploit" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Exploit">#</a> </h3> <p>The most alarming vulnerability discovered allows <strong>anyone with physical access</strong> to a Flock Safety camera to gain complete control in under 60 seconds:</p> <p><strong>Step 1</strong>: Press the button on the back of Falcon/Sparrow camera <strong>three times</strong> <strong>Step 2</strong>: Device creates open WiFi access point <strong>Step 3</strong>: Connect to WiFi network (hardcoded password: documented in GainSec white paper) <strong>Step 4</strong>: Send ADB (Android Debug Bridge) enable command <strong>Step 5</strong>: Connect via ADB and obtain root shell access</p> <h3 id="technical-details"> <a href="#technical-details" title="Technical Details">Technical Details</a> <a href="#technical-details" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Technical Details">#</a> </h3> <pre tabindex="0"><code>Device: Flock Safety Falcon/Sparrow ALPR Camera Hardware: Android Things 8.0/8.1 (EOL 2021) Attack Vector: Physical button press sequence Authentication: Hardcoded WiFi password (universal across devices) Impact: Complete device compromise, data exfiltration, malware installation </code></pre><h3 id="what-this-enables"> <a href="#what-this-enables" title="What This Enables">What This Enables</a> <a href="#what-this-enables" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What This Enables">#</a> </h3> <p>Once shell access is obtained, an attacker can:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Extract all stored imagery</strong> (including people, not just vehicles)</li> <li><strong>Modify or delete evidence</strong> stored on device</li> <li><strong>Install persistent malware</strong> (survives reboots)</li> <li><strong>Clone device identity</strong> for spoofing</li> <li><strong>Intercept and modify</strong> video streams</li> <li><strong>Use device as botnet client</strong> for DDoS attacks</li> <li><strong>Capture WiFi credentials</strong> from nearby devices (honeypot attacks)</li> <li><strong>Disable camera</strong> or cause denial of service</li> </ul> <h3 id="demonstration"> <a href="#demonstration" title="Demonstration">Demonstration</a> <a href="#demonstration" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Demonstration">#</a> </h3> <p>Security researcher Ben Jordan demonstrated this exploit on YouTube, showing:</p> <ul> <li>Connection to WiFi access point in <strong>under 30 seconds</strong></li> <li>Root shell access obtained in <strong>under 60 seconds total</strong></li> <li>Complete access to file system, stored images, and system memory</li> <li>Ability to install arbitrary Android applications</li> </ul> <p><strong>Quote from demonstration</strong>:</p> <blockquote> <p>“The password for that access point is [REDACTED] in all lowercase. For every single camera that we’ve tried, it all has that hard-coded password. Then you just send it a command to enable ADB…it’s probably under 30 seconds, you can completely shell the device and have full access to it.”</p> </blockquote> <h3 id="vendor-response"> <a href="#vendor-response" title="Vendor Response">Vendor Response</a> <a href="#vendor-response" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Vendor Response">#</a> </h3> <p>Flock Safety initially claimed the vulnerability only affected <strong>devices not connected to the cloud</strong>, comparing them to <strong>“an iPhone stolen off a truck before being connected”</strong>.</p> <p><em>This claim was disproven.</em> Researchers reproduced the vulnerability <strong>on cloud-connected devices</strong>, across multiple camera models showing <strong>identical vulnerability</strong>, and on devices acquired from <strong>different sources</strong>. All exhibited the flaw.</p> <hr> <h2 id="critical-vulnerability-2-outdated-android-operating-system"> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-2-outdated-android-operating-system" title="Critical Vulnerability #2: Outdated Android Operating System">Critical Vulnerability #2: Outdated Android Operating System</a> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-2-outdated-android-operating-system" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Critical Vulnerability #2: Outdated Android Operating System">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="cve-2025-xxxxx-series-multiple-cves"> <a href="#cve-2025-xxxxx-series-multiple-cves" title="CVE-2025-XXXXX Series (Multiple CVEs)">CVE-2025-XXXXX Series (Multiple CVEs)</a> <a href="#cve-2025-xxxxx-series-multiple-cves" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="CVE-2025-XXXXX Series (Multiple CVEs)">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Severity</strong>: CRITICAL (Multiple)</p> <h3 id="the-problem"> <a href="#the-problem" title="The Problem">The Problem</a> <a href="#the-problem" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Problem">#</a> </h3> <p>Flock Safety cameras run <strong>Android Things 8.0 or 8.1</strong>, which:</p> <ul> <li>Was <strong>discontinued by Google in 2021</strong></li> <li>Has <strong>NO security updates since 2021</strong></li> <li>Has <strong>900+ published vulnerabilities</strong> as of 2026</li> <li>Is <strong>5+ years out of support</strong></li> </ul> <h3 id="technical-analysis"> <a href="#technical-analysis" title="Technical Analysis">Technical Analysis</a> <a href="#technical-analysis" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Technical Analysis">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Operating System</strong>: Android Things 8.0/8.1 <strong>End of Life</strong>: January 2021 <strong>Known Vulnerabilities</strong>: 900+ <strong>Security Patches</strong>: None since EOL <strong>Affected Devices</strong>: Falcon, Sparrow, Condor, Bravo compute boxes</p> <h3 id="comparison-to-consumer-devices"> <a href="#comparison-to-consumer-devices" title="Comparison to Consumer Devices">Comparison to Consumer Devices</a> <a href="#comparison-to-consumer-devices" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Comparison to Consumer Devices">#</a> </h3> <p>For perspective:</p> <ul> <li>Your <strong>smartphone</strong> refuses to update after ~5 years and manufacturers <strong>stop selling them</strong></li> <li>Your <strong>home security camera</strong> running 5-year-old software would be considered <strong>critically insecure</strong></li> <li>Government agencies require <strong>up-to-date, supported software</strong> for sensitive systems</li> </ul> <p><em>Yet Flock Safety continues deploying and selling cameras running software that has no vendor support, receives no security patches, contains hundreds of known exploits, and processes sensitive law enforcement data.</em></p> <h3 id="why-this-matters"> <a href="#why-this-matters" title="Why This Matters">Why This Matters</a> <a href="#why-this-matters" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Why This Matters">#</a> </h3> <p>Running <strong>EOL software</strong> on surveillance devices means:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Any published Android 8.x vulnerability</strong> works on these cameras</li> <li><strong>Exploit code is publicly available</strong> for many vulnerabilities</li> <li><strong>No patches will ever fix</strong> newly discovered issues</li> <li><strong>Regulatory compliance</strong> (FISMA, NIST, CMMC) is impossible to achieve</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="critical-vulnerability-3-lack-of-encryption-at-runtime"> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-3-lack-of-encryption-at-runtime" title="Critical Vulnerability #3: Lack of Encryption at Runtime">Critical Vulnerability #3: Lack of Encryption at Runtime</a> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-3-lack-of-encryption-at-runtime" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Critical Vulnerability #3: Lack of Encryption at Runtime">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="cve-2025-xxxxx-multiple-findings"> <a href="#cve-2025-xxxxx-multiple-findings" title="CVE-2025-XXXXX (Multiple Findings)">CVE-2025-XXXXX (Multiple Findings)</a> <a href="#cve-2025-xxxxx-multiple-findings" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="CVE-2025-XXXXX (Multiple Findings)">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Severity</strong>: HIGH (CVSS 7.5-8.5)</p> <h3 id="flock-safetys-claims-vs-reality"> <a href="#flock-safetys-claims-vs-reality" title="Flock Safety’s Claims vs. Reality">Flock Safety’s Claims vs. Reality</a> <a href="#flock-safetys-claims-vs-reality" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Flock Safety’s Claims vs. Reality">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Flock Safety’s Website States</strong>:</p> <blockquote> <p>“Data and footage is encrypted throughout the entire life cycle”</p> </blockquote> <p><strong>Independent Research Findings</strong> (GainSec & Ben Jordan):</p> <blockquote> <p>“In all of our research, we’ve not unencrypted or cracked a thing. All of it was unencrypted at runtime.”</p> </blockquote> <p><em>The gap between the marketing claim and the technical reality is total.</em></p> <h3 id="what-is-unencrypted"> <a href="#what-is-unencrypted" title="What Is Unencrypted">What Is Unencrypted</a> <a href="#what-is-unencrypted" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Is Unencrypted">#</a> </h3> <p>When researchers examined devices, they found <strong>unencrypted</strong>:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Stored video footage and imagery</strong></li> <li><strong>License plate data and metadata</strong></li> <li><strong>Wi-Fi credentials and API keys</strong></li> <li><strong>Database files with detection records</strong></li> <li><strong>Network traffic</strong> (see Vulnerability #6)</li> <li><strong>System logs with sensitive information</strong></li> </ol> <h3 id="data-retention-contradiction"> <a href="#data-retention-contradiction" title="Data Retention Contradiction">Data Retention Contradiction</a> <a href="#data-retention-contradiction" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Data Retention Contradiction">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Flock Safety Claims</strong>: Data automatically removed after 7 days <strong>Research Findings</strong>: Images found dating back to <strong>device manufacturing</strong> (months or years old)</p> <p>GainSec white paper excerpt:</p> <blockquote> <p>“We found images older than 7 days. In fact, stored images were captured when the camera was triggered inside the factory where the device was made.”</p> </blockquote> <p><em>This means stated privacy policies are not enforced at the device level.</em></p> <h3 id="privacy-implications"> <a href="#privacy-implications" title="Privacy Implications">Privacy Implications</a> <a href="#privacy-implications" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Privacy Implications">#</a> </h3> <p>This encryption failure means:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Physical device theft</strong> = immediate data breach</li> <li><strong>Network interception</strong> exposes clear-text data</li> <li><strong>Insider threats</strong> can easily exfiltrate data</li> <li><strong>Warrant protections</strong> may not apply to unencrypted data</li> <li><strong>Compliance violations</strong> for numerous regulations (HIPAA, CCPA, etc.)</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="critical-vulnerability-4-hardcoded-credentials-throughout-system"> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-4-hardcoded-credentials-throughout-system" title="Critical Vulnerability #4: Hardcoded Credentials Throughout System">Critical Vulnerability #4: Hardcoded Credentials Throughout System</a> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-4-hardcoded-credentials-throughout-system" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Critical Vulnerability #4: Hardcoded Credentials Throughout System">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="cve-2025-xxxxx-series-multiple"> <a href="#cve-2025-xxxxx-series-multiple" title="CVE-2025-XXXXX Series (Multiple)">CVE-2025-XXXXX Series (Multiple)</a> <a href="#cve-2025-xxxxx-series-multiple" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="CVE-2025-XXXXX Series (Multiple)">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Severity</strong>: CRITICAL (CVSS 9.1)</p> <h3 id="categories-of-hardcoded-secrets"> <a href="#categories-of-hardcoded-secrets" title="Categories of Hardcoded Secrets">Categories of Hardcoded Secrets</a> <a href="#categories-of-hardcoded-secrets" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Categories of Hardcoded Secrets">#</a> </h3> <p>Security researchers discovered <strong>extensive hardcoded credentials</strong>:</p> <h4 id="1-wifi-access-point-passwords"> <a href="#1-wifi-access-point-passwords" title="1. WiFi Access Point Passwords">1. WiFi Access Point Passwords</a> <a href="#1-wifi-access-point-passwords" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="1. WiFi Access Point Passwords">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Universal password</strong> across all Falcon/Sparrow cameras</li> <li><strong>Cannot be changed</strong> by users or administrators</li> <li><strong>Known to researchers</strong> and published in white paper (redacted sections)</li> </ul> <h4 id="2-api-keys-and-tokens"> <a href="#2-api-keys-and-tokens" title="2. API Keys and Tokens">2. API Keys and Tokens</a> <a href="#2-api-keys-and-tokens" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="2. API Keys and Tokens">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Hard-coded in firmware</strong> and application code</li> <li><strong>Grants backend access</strong> to various services</li> <li><strong>Found via reverse engineering</strong> of Android APKs</li> </ul> <h4 id="3-database-credentials"> <a href="#3-database-credentials" title="3. Database Credentials">3. Database Credentials</a> <a href="#3-database-credentials" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="3. Database Credentials">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>SQLite databases</strong> with no password protection</li> <li><strong>MySQL/PostgreSQL</strong> credentials in configuration files</li> <li><strong>Direct database access</strong> from local shell</li> </ul> <h4 id="4-wi-fi-network-names"> <a href="#4-wi-fi-network-names" title="4. Wi-Fi Network Names">4. Wi-Fi Network Names</a> <a href="#4-wi-fi-network-names" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="4. Wi-Fi Network Names">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>List of preferred</strong> networks hard-coded in firmware</li> <li>Enables <strong>rogue access point</strong> attacks (see Vulnerability #6)</li> </ul> <h4 id="5-cloud-service-credentials"> <a href="#5-cloud-service-credentials" title="5. Cloud Service Credentials">5. Cloud Service Credentials</a> <a href="#5-cloud-service-credentials" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="5. Cloud Service Credentials">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>AWS/Azure tokens</strong> embedded in code</li> <li><strong>Third-party API keys</strong> (ArcGIS, mapping services)</li> <li><strong>OAuth tokens</strong> never rotated</li> </ul> <h3 id="attack-scenario-rogue-network"> <a href="#attack-scenario-rogue-network" title="Attack Scenario: Rogue Network">Attack Scenario: Rogue Network</a> <a href="#attack-scenario-rogue-network" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Attack Scenario: Rogue Network">#</a> </h3> <p>Using hardcoded WiFi network names, researchers demonstrated:</p> <ol> <li>Attacker sets up <strong>fake WiFi network</strong> matching hardcoded SSID</li> <li>Camera <strong>automatically connects</strong> (prioritizes hardcoded networks)</li> <li>Attacker captures <strong>all network traffic</strong> via man-in-the-middle</li> <li>Clear-text credentials extracted from traffic</li> <li>Attacker gains <strong>backend system access</strong></li> </ol> <p><strong>No physical access to camera required.</strong> Proximity within WiFi range is enough.</p> <hr> <h2 id="critical-vulnerability-5-lack-of-multi-factor-authentication"> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-5-lack-of-multi-factor-authentication" title="Critical Vulnerability #5: Lack of Multi-Factor Authentication">Critical Vulnerability #5: Lack of Multi-Factor Authentication</a> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-5-lack-of-multi-factor-authentication" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Critical Vulnerability #5: Lack of Multi-Factor Authentication">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="organizational-policy-failure"> <a href="#organizational-policy-failure" title="Organizational Policy Failure">Organizational Policy Failure</a> <a href="#organizational-policy-failure" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Organizational Policy Failure">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Severity</strong>: HIGH (Organizational)</p> <h3 id="the-revelation"> <a href="#the-revelation" title="The Revelation">The Revelation</a> <a href="#the-revelation" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Revelation">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Flock Safety doesn’t require 2FA/MFA</strong> for some law enforcement agencies, <em>the same agencies accessing tracking data on 150 million vehicles daily.</em></p> <p>Security researcher quote:</p> <blockquote> <p>“When I first found this out, I simply couldn’t believe it. The security process you go through when you log into Disney Plus is just too much to ask some police departments to do when accessing confidential information and the location of, in some cases, virtually everyone.”</p> </blockquote> <h3 id="why-this-matters-1"> <a href="#why-this-matters-1" title="Why This Matters">Why This Matters</a> <a href="#why-this-matters-1" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Why This Matters">#</a> </h3> <p>Without MFA/2FA:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Single compromised password</strong> = full system access</li> <li><strong>Phishing attacks</strong> are highly effective</li> <li><strong>Credential stuffing</strong> from other breaches works</li> <li><strong>Insider threats</strong> are easier to execute</li> <li><strong>Stolen devices</strong> grant immediate access</li> </ul> <h3 id="comparison"> <a href="#comparison" title="Comparison">Comparison</a> <a href="#comparison" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Comparison">#</a> </h3> <p>Security requirements for <strong>less sensitive</strong> systems:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Disney+</strong>: 2FA available</li> <li><strong>Gmail</strong>: 2FA default for new accounts</li> <li><strong>Banking</strong>: 2FA required by law</li> <li><strong>Social media</strong>: 2FA standard</li> </ul> <p>Security requirements for <strong>tracking 150M+ vehicles daily</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Flock Safety</strong>: 2FA optional (some agencies don’t use it)</li> </ul> <h3 id="us-senator-response"> <a href="#us-senator-response" title="U.S. Senator Response">U.S. Senator Response</a> <a href="#us-senator-response" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="U.S. Senator Response">#</a> </h3> <p>This finding was specifically highlighted in <strong>Senator Wyden’s letter</strong> requesting an FTC investigation, citing it as evidence Flock Safety has:</p> <blockquote> <p>“unnecessarily exposed Americans sensitive personal data to theft by hackers and foreign spies”</p> </blockquote> <h3 id="simple-solution"> <a href="#simple-solution" title="Simple Solution">Simple Solution</a> <a href="#simple-solution" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Simple Solution">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>USB/NFC security keys</strong> cost <strong>$10-25</strong> and provide:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Phishing-resistant</strong> authentication</li> <li><strong>No additional apps</strong> or codes needed</li> <li><strong>FIDO2/WebAuthn</strong> standard compliance</li> <li><strong>Simple user experience</strong> (plug in or tap device)</li> </ul> <p><em>If this is “too much hassle” for law enforcement personnel, they <strong>shouldn’t have access</strong> to national surveillance systems.</em></p> <hr> <h2 id="critical-vulnerability-6-clear-text-network-traffic"> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-6-clear-text-network-traffic" title="Critical Vulnerability #6: Clear-Text Network Traffic">Critical Vulnerability #6: Clear-Text Network Traffic</a> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-6-clear-text-network-traffic" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Critical Vulnerability #6: Clear-Text Network Traffic">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="cve-2025-xxxxx-pending"> <a href="#cve-2025-xxxxx-pending" title="CVE-2025-XXXXX (Pending)">CVE-2025-XXXXX (Pending)</a> <a href="#cve-2025-xxxxx-pending" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="CVE-2025-XXXXX (Pending)">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Severity</strong>: HIGH (CVSS 7.8)</p> <h3 id="the-vulnerability"> <a href="#the-vulnerability" title="The Vulnerability">The Vulnerability</a> <a href="#the-vulnerability" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Vulnerability">#</a> </h3> <p>When cameras connect to networks (LTE or WiFi), they transmit data <strong>without adequate encryption</strong>:</p> <p><strong>Affected Traffic</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>License plate images and text</li> <li>Detection metadata</li> <li>System logs</li> <li>Configuration data</li> <li>Credentials (in some cases)</li> </ul> <h3 id="attack-vector-1-rogue-wifi-network"> <a href="#attack-vector-1-rogue-wifi-network" title="Attack Vector #1: Rogue WiFi Network">Attack Vector #1: Rogue WiFi Network</a> <a href="#attack-vector-1-rogue-wifi-network" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Attack Vector #1: Rogue WiFi Network">#</a> </h3> <p>Researchers demonstrated:</p> <ol> <li>Remove SIM card from camera <strong>OR</strong> set up fake network with hardcoded SSID</li> <li>Camera connects to rogue WiFi</li> <li>Capture traffic with <strong>Wireshark</strong> or similar packet analyzer</li> <li>Analyze with tools like <strong>NetworkMiner</strong> or <strong>Unblo</strong></li> <li>Extract <strong>clear-text credentials</strong> and sensitive data</li> </ol> <p>Quote from GainSec research:</p> <blockquote> <p>“I captured the pcap data being transmitted from one of these cameras for a little while and analyzed it with Wireshark and Unblo…and sure enough there were clear text credentials in the data.”</p> </blockquote> <h3 id="attack-vector-2-imsi-catcher--stingray"> <a href="#attack-vector-2-imsi-catcher--stingray" title="Attack Vector #2: IMSI Catcher / Stingray">Attack Vector #2: IMSI Catcher / Stingray</a> <a href="#attack-vector-2-imsi-catcher--stingray" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Attack Vector #2: IMSI Catcher / Stingray">#</a> </h3> <p>More sophisticated attack doesn’t require physical proximity:</p> <ol> <li>Use <strong>IMSI catcher</strong> (DIY Stingray device) or professional-grade SDR</li> <li><strong>Hijack LTE connection</strong> by impersonating cell tower</li> <li>Camera connects to <strong>rogue base station</strong></li> <li><strong>Man-in-the-middle</strong> all traffic</li> <li>Extract credentials, imagery, and metadata</li> </ol> <p><strong>No physical access required.</strong> This works from <strong>hundreds of feet away.</strong></p> <h3 id="modern-tempest-attack-potential"> <a href="#modern-tempest-attack-potential" title="Modern Tempest Attack Potential">Modern Tempest Attack Potential</a> <a href="#modern-tempest-attack-potential" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Modern Tempest Attack Potential">#</a> </h3> <p>The clear-text transmission also enables <strong>Tempest-style attacks</strong> where:</p> <ul> <li>RF emissions from camera can be captured</li> <li>Video stream can be <strong>reconstructed remotely</strong></li> <li>Similar to <strong>Cold War-era CIA</strong> techniques</li> <li>Modern SDR equipment makes this <strong>accessible to hobbyists</strong></li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="critical-vulnerability-7-exposed-public-camera-feeds"> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-7-exposed-public-camera-feeds" title="Critical Vulnerability #7: Exposed Public Camera Feeds">Critical Vulnerability #7: Exposed Public Camera Feeds</a> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-7-exposed-public-camera-feeds" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Critical Vulnerability #7: Exposed Public Camera Feeds">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="january-2026-discovery"> <a href="#january-2026-discovery" title="January 2026 Discovery">January 2026 Discovery</a> <a href="#january-2026-discovery" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="January 2026 Discovery">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Severity</strong>: CRITICAL (CVSS 10.0 - Complete System Exposure)</p> <h3 id="the-discovery"> <a href="#the-discovery" title="The Discovery">The Discovery</a> <a href="#the-discovery" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Discovery">#</a> </h3> <p>In January 2026, Ben Jordan and <strong>404 Media</strong> discovered that using <strong>simple Google searches</strong>, they could find:</p> <ul> <li><strong>60+ completely exposed</strong> Flock Safety camera administrative interfaces</li> <li><strong>No username or password required</strong></li> <li><strong>Live video streams</strong> and 30 days of archived footage</li> <li><strong>Complete camera control</strong> panel access</li> </ul> <h3 id="what-was-accessible"> <a href="#what-was-accessible" title="What Was Accessible">What Was Accessible</a> <a href="#what-was-accessible" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Was Accessible">#</a> </h3> <p>From these exposed interfaces, anyone could:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Watch live video feeds</strong> in real-time</li> <li><strong>Review 30 days</strong> of archived footage</li> <li><strong>Download video files</strong> directly</li> <li><strong>Control PTZ cameras</strong> (Pan/Tilt/Zoom) on Condor units</li> <li><strong>See file paths and hashes</strong> of evidence</li> <li><strong>Delete evidence</strong> with single button click</li> <li><strong>Modify camera settings</strong></li> <li><strong>View device serial numbers and locations</strong></li> </ul> <h3 id="privacy-violations-observed"> <a href="#privacy-violations-observed" title="Privacy Violations Observed">Privacy Violations Observed</a> <a href="#privacy-violations-observed" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Privacy Violations Observed">#</a> </h3> <p>Ben Jordan documented witnessing:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Family loading infant</strong> into car at Lowe’s parking lot</li> <li><strong>Man leaving his home</strong> in New York in the morning</li> <li><strong>Woman jogging alone</strong> on forest trail in Georgia</li> <li><strong>Couple arguing</strong> at street market in Atlanta (AI auto-zoomed on faces)</li> <li><strong>Officer and ambulance</strong> assisting mental health crisis in Iowa</li> <li><strong>Children playing</strong> at playground in California Bay Area</li> <li><strong>Man on swing set</strong> in empty park having private moment</li> </ul> <p>Quote from Ben Jordan:</p> <blockquote> <p>“Just saying this stuff out loud nauseates me. But I’m trying to show you just a fraction of the information that anyone in the world with access to a commercial search engine has had regarding anyone who attended this market or walked on this trail in the last 31 days.”</p> </blockquote> <h3 id="targeting-capabilities"> <a href="#targeting-capabilities" title="Targeting Capabilities">Targeting Capabilities</a> <a href="#targeting-capabilities" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Targeting Capabilities">#</a> </h3> <p>The exposed Condor PTZ cameras feature <strong>AI-powered tracking</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Automatically zoom in</strong> on people</li> <li><strong>Follow individuals</strong> as they move</li> <li><strong>Detect and focus</strong> on smartphones (to read screens)</li> <li><strong>Track multiple targets</strong> simultaneously</li> <li><strong>Record continuously</strong> with no oversight</li> </ul> <h3 id="doxing-potential"> <a href="#doxing-potential" title="Doxing Potential">Doxing Potential</a> <a href="#doxing-potential" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Doxing Potential">#</a> </h3> <p>Ben Jordan demonstrated how easily exposed footage leads to identity:</p> <blockquote> <p>“Within two minutes of open source intelligence using a commercial facial recognition engine, I found out that one of them just finished medical school and the other is dealing with chronic irritable bowel syndrome. The couple also just had a baby last year…I also know that they drove over 45 minutes from their address in the suburbs.”</p> </blockquote> <p>Cross-referencing with:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Facial recognition</strong> services</li> <li><strong>Social media</strong> profiles</li> <li><strong>Public records</strong> databases</li> <li><strong>Data breach</strong> information (Park Mobile, etc.)</li> <li><strong>Emergency call logs</strong> (many cities publish these)</li> </ul> <p>Result: <strong>Complete deanonymization</strong> in minutes</p> <hr> <h2 id="critical-vulnerability-8-unauthorized-data-collection"> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-8-unauthorized-data-collection" title="Critical Vulnerability #8: Unauthorized Data Collection">Critical Vulnerability #8: Unauthorized Data Collection</a> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-8-unauthorized-data-collection" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Critical Vulnerability #8: Unauthorized Data Collection">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="contradiction-to-public-statements"> <a href="#contradiction-to-public-statements" title="Contradiction to Public Statements">Contradiction to Public Statements</a> <a href="#contradiction-to-public-statements" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Contradiction to Public Statements">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Severity</strong>: HIGH (Privacy/Legal Implications)</p> <h3 id="flock-safetys-claims"> <a href="#flock-safetys-claims" title="Flock Safety’s Claims">Flock Safety’s Claims</a> <a href="#flock-safetys-claims" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Flock Safety’s Claims">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Official Website Statement</strong>:</p> <blockquote> <p>“Flock Safety doesn’t capture or record data of people, only vehicles”</p> </blockquote> <h3 id="independent-research-findings"> <a href="#independent-research-findings" title="Independent Research Findings">Independent Research Findings</a> <a href="#independent-research-findings" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Independent Research Findings">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>What cameras actually do</strong>:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Motion detection</strong> triggers camera <strong>for anything</strong>, not just vehicles</li> <li><strong>AI looks for license plate</strong> in image</li> <li><strong>If no plate found</strong>, image is <strong>still stored</strong> (not deleted)</li> <li><strong>Separate folder</strong> stores images without plates</li> <li><strong>People, pedestrians, cyclists</strong> captured and retained</li> </ol> <p><em>The “vehicles only” claim is false. The camera stores images of anyone who walks in front of it.</em></p> <h3 id="verified-observations"> <a href="#verified-observations" title="Verified Observations">Verified Observations</a> <a href="#verified-observations" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Verified Observations">#</a> </h3> <p>Researchers documented cameras capturing:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Person walking</strong> in front of camera (stored image)</li> <li><strong>Hand waving</strong> at lens (stored image)</li> <li><strong>Desk or object</strong> moved near camera (stored image)</li> <li><strong>Factory workers</strong> at manufacturing facility (stored for months)</li> </ul> <p>GainSec quote:</p> <blockquote> <p>“When I moved in front of the camera, the radar module triggered the camera module to take a picture of me. Then the onboard AI looked for a license plate and didn’t find one, but it stored the image anyway to a separate folder.”</p> </blockquote> <h3 id="legal-implications"> <a href="#legal-implications" title="Legal Implications">Legal Implications</a> <a href="#legal-implications" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Legal Implications">#</a> </h3> <p>This unauthorized data collection could:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Violate stated privacy policies</strong> (deceptive practices)</li> <li><strong>Breach procurement contracts</strong> (if cities specified “vehicles only”)</li> <li><strong>Trigger GDPR/CCPA</strong> violations (EU citizens, California residents)</li> <li><strong>Invalidate consent</strong> agreements</li> <li><strong>Expose company to litigation</strong> for false advertising</li> </ul> <h3 id="surveillance-scope-expansion"> <a href="#surveillance-scope-expansion" title="Surveillance Scope Expansion">Surveillance Scope Expansion</a> <a href="#surveillance-scope-expansion" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Surveillance Scope Expansion">#</a> </h3> <p>The <strong>Condor PTZ cameras</strong> (deployed 2025-2026) are <strong>explicitly designed</strong> to track people:</p> <ul> <li><strong>AI-powered person detection</strong></li> <li><strong>Automatic zoom</strong> on faces</li> <li><strong>Follow tracking</strong> as people move</li> <li><strong>No opt-out</strong> mechanism</li> <li><strong>Often deployed</strong> at parks, trails, transit stations</li> </ul> <p><em>This represents a massive scope expansion from “license plate readers” to general population surveillance, with no policy change, no new contracts, and no public notice.</em></p> <hr> <h2 id="critical-vulnerability-9-exposed-usb-ports-for-rubber-ducky-attacks"> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-9-exposed-usb-ports-for-rubber-ducky-attacks" title="Critical Vulnerability #9: Exposed USB Ports for Rubber Ducky Attacks">Critical Vulnerability #9: Exposed USB Ports for Rubber Ducky Attacks</a> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-9-exposed-usb-ports-for-rubber-ducky-attacks" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Critical Vulnerability #9: Exposed USB Ports for Rubber Ducky Attacks">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="physical-access-exploitation"> <a href="#physical-access-exploitation" title="Physical Access Exploitation">Physical Access Exploitation</a> <a href="#physical-access-exploitation" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Physical Access Exploitation">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Severity</strong>: HIGH (With Physical Access)</p> <h3 id="the-vulnerability-1"> <a href="#the-vulnerability-1" title="The Vulnerability">The Vulnerability</a> <a href="#the-vulnerability-1" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Vulnerability">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Bravo Compute Boxes</strong> and some camera models have:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Exposed USB-C ports</strong> on exterior</li> <li><strong>No physical security</strong> (enclosure seals)</li> <li><strong>Auto-execution</strong> of USB devices</li> <li><strong>No authentication</strong> required</li> </ul> <h3 id="rubber-ducky--badusb-attack"> <a href="#rubber-ducky--badusb-attack" title="Rubber Ducky / BadUSB Attack">Rubber Ducky / BadUSB Attack</a> <a href="#rubber-ducky--badusb-attack" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Rubber Ducky / BadUSB Attack">#</a> </h3> <p>A <strong>BadUSB device</strong> (costs $5-15):</p> <ul> <li>Appears as <strong>USB keyboard</strong> to system</li> <li><strong>Executes pre-programmed scripts</strong> (payloads)</li> <li><strong>Runs automatically</strong> when plugged in</li> <li><strong>No user interaction</strong> needed</li> </ul> <h3 id="attack-scenario"> <a href="#attack-scenario" title="Attack Scenario">Attack Scenario</a> <a href="#attack-scenario" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Attack Scenario">#</a> </h3> <p>GainSec demonstrated:</p> <ol> <li>Approach deployed Flock device</li> <li>Plug in <strong>BadUSB device</strong> to exposed port</li> <li>Device executes payload (script)</li> <li>Payload installs <strong>persistent malware</strong></li> <li>Attacker <strong>walks away</strong></li> <li>Malware <strong>phones home</strong> over LTE/WiFi</li> </ol> <p><strong>Total time</strong>: Under 30 seconds</p> <h3 id="what-payloads-can-do"> <a href="#what-payloads-can-do" title="What Payloads Can Do">What Payloads Can Do</a> <a href="#what-payloads-can-do" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Payloads Can Do">#</a> </h3> <p>Automated scripts can:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Enable wireless access point</strong> (bypass button presses)</li> <li><strong>Install backdoor</strong> for remote access</li> <li><strong>Exfiltrate data</strong> to remote server</li> <li><strong>Modify footage</strong> or evidence</li> <li><strong>Disable camera</strong> or create DoS</li> <li><strong>Join botnet</strong> for DDoS attacks</li> <li><strong>Capture credentials</strong> from other systems</li> </ul> <h3 id="why-this-matters-2"> <a href="#why-this-matters-2" title="Why This Matters">Why This Matters</a> <a href="#why-this-matters-2" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Why This Matters">#</a> </h3> <p>With <strong>80,000+ cameras</strong> often in <strong>semi-rural locations</strong> with <strong>limited visibility</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Physical access</strong> is relatively easy</li> <li><strong>No guards</strong> or constant monitoring</li> <li>Attached with <strong>simple hose clamps</strong></li> <li>Often mounted <strong>7 feet off ground</strong> (reachable with stepstool)</li> </ul> <p>Quote from Ben Jordan:</p> <blockquote> <p>“These cameras aren’t exactly little impenetrable fortresses. They’re plastic Android cameras and compute boxes mounted 7 feet off the ground with hose clamps.”</p> </blockquote> <hr> <h2 id="critical-vulnerability-10-publicly-exposed-api-keys"> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-10-publicly-exposed-api-keys" title="Critical Vulnerability #10: Publicly Exposed API Keys">Critical Vulnerability #10: Publicly Exposed API Keys</a> <a href="#critical-vulnerability-10-publicly-exposed-api-keys" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Critical Vulnerability #10: Publicly Exposed API Keys">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="discovery-by-osint-researcher"> <a href="#discovery-by-osint-researcher" title="Discovery by OSINT Researcher">Discovery by OSINT Researcher</a> <a href="#discovery-by-osint-researcher" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Discovery by OSINT Researcher">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Severity</strong>: CRITICAL (CVSS 9.5)</p> <h3 id="the-discovery-1"> <a href="#the-discovery-1" title="The Discovery">The Discovery</a> <a href="#the-discovery-1" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Discovery">#</a> </h3> <p>In 2025, <strong>OSINT researcher Joshua Michael</strong> discovered:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Exposed Flock Safety demo website</strong> via Google dorking</li> <li><strong>5,000 lines of source code</strong> visible</li> <li><strong>Live API key</strong> embedded in code</li> <li><strong>Access to 50+ private layers</strong> of geospatial data</li> </ul> <h3 id="what-the-api-key-granted-access-to"> <a href="#what-the-api-key-granted-access-to" title="What The API Key Granted Access To">What The API Key Granted Access To</a> <a href="#what-the-api-key-granted-access-to" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What The API Key Granted Access To">#</a> </h3> <p>The exposed <strong>ArcGIS API key</strong> provided access to:</p> <p><strong>1. Registration Data</strong></p> <ul> <li>Customer names</li> <li>Email addresses</li> <li>Number of cameras per location</li> <li>File attachment capabilities</li> </ul> <p><strong>2. Live Patrol Car Locations</strong> (Multiple Departments)</p> <ul> <li>Real-time GPS tracking of police vehicles</li> <li>Aurora, Colorado deployment</li> <li>Carrollton Police Department</li> <li><strong>National security risk</strong></li> </ul> <p><strong>3. Officer Personal Information</strong></p> <ul> <li>Names and phone numbers</li> <li>Email addresses</li> <li>Expected patrol areas</li> <li>Home jurisdictions</li> </ul> <p><strong>4. Hot List Alerts Database</strong> (Dallas, Texas)</p> <ul> <li><strong>6,000 records</strong> of flagged vehicles</li> <li>License plates and reasons for flags</li> <li>Detection locations and timestamps</li> <li>Camera IDs that captured vehicles</li> <li><strong>5 months of movement tracking</strong></li> </ul> <p>Joshua Michael quote:</p> <blockquote> <p>“Anyone could Google, find this map, and trace these people’s movement patterns for 5 months. Also going to note the reason category has someone in there for just ‘suspect’ and a bunch of others literally have no reason or are blank.”</p> </blockquote> <h3 id="attack-surface"> <a href="#attack-surface" title="Attack Surface">Attack Surface</a> <a href="#attack-surface" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Attack Surface">#</a> </h3> <p>Exposed API keys enable:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Stalking</strong> of specific vehicles</li> <li><strong>Officer safety</strong> compromised (home addresses, patrol patterns)</li> <li><strong>Operational security</strong> breached (where police are/aren’t)</li> <li><strong>Witness intimidation</strong> (track people who reported crimes)</li> <li><strong>Criminal intelligence</strong> gathering by organized crime</li> </ul> <h3 id="breach-context-flax-typhoon"> <a href="#breach-context-flax-typhoon" title="Breach Context: Flax Typhoon">Breach Context: Flax Typhoon</a> <a href="#breach-context-flax-typhoon" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Breach Context: Flax Typhoon">#</a> </h3> <p>Just weeks before this discovery, <strong>Chinese state-sponsored</strong> hacking group <strong>Flax Typhoon</strong> compromised ArcGIS, according to security researchers Alexa Feminina and James Zhang.</p> <p>InfoSecurity Magazine quote:</p> <blockquote> <p>“The hackers allegedly targeted a legitimate public-facing ArcGIS application…used for disaster recovery, emergency management, and other critical functions.”</p> </blockquote> <p><strong>This means hostile nation-states had potential access to</strong> police vehicle locations, officer identities, surveillance camera placements, and emergency response patterns.</p> <hr> <h2 id="additional-vulnerabilities-summary-of-50"> <a href="#additional-vulnerabilities-summary-of-50" title="Additional Vulnerabilities (Summary of 50+)">Additional Vulnerabilities (Summary of 50+)</a> <a href="#additional-vulnerabilities-summary-of-50" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Additional Vulnerabilities (Summary of 50+)">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="vulnerabilities-11-51"> <a href="#vulnerabilities-11-51" title="Vulnerabilities 11-51">Vulnerabilities 11-51</a> <a href="#vulnerabilities-11-51" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Vulnerabilities 11-51">#</a> </h3> <p>The GainSec white paper documents <strong>41 additional findings</strong>, including:</p> <p><strong>System Design Flaws</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Debug mode enabled in production firmware</li> <li>Insecure boot process allows bootloader compromise</li> <li>No secure element or TPM for key storage</li> <li>Predictable firmware update mechanism</li> </ul> <p><strong>Authentication Issues</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Session tokens never expire</li> <li>No IP-based access restrictions</li> <li>Shared credentials across device fleets</li> <li>No certificate pinning</li> </ul> <p><strong>Network Security</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Open ports with debug services</li> <li>Telnet enabled on some units</li> <li>FTP servers with weak credentials</li> <li>DNS rebinding vulnerabilities</li> </ul> <p><strong>Privacy/Data Retention</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Images stored beyond stated retention</li> <li>Metadata never purged</li> <li>Facial recognition data collected</li> <li>Location tracking beyond vehicles</li> </ul> <p><strong>Physical Security</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Easy disassembly of enclosures</li> <li>Exposed serial interfaces</li> <li>JTAG debugging ports accessible</li> <li>No tamper-evident seals</li> </ul> <p><strong>Cryptographic Failures</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Weak random number generation</li> <li>Hardcoded encryption keys</li> <li>MD5 still used in hash chain</li> <li>Certificate validation disabled</li> </ul> <p><strong>Information Disclosure</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Verbose error messages reveal internals</li> <li>Directory listing enabled on web servers</li> <li>Source code comments contain credentials</li> <li>Configuration backups world-readable</li> </ul> <h3 id="cve-status"> <a href="#cve-status" title="CVE Status">CVE Status</a> <a href="#cve-status" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="CVE Status">#</a> </h3> <p>As of May 2026:</p> <ul> <li><strong>22 CVEs assigned</strong> and published</li> <li><strong>8 CVEs pending</strong> MITRE assignment</li> <li><strong>21 findings</strong> not submitted for CVE (researcher discretion)</li> <li><strong>All findings</strong> documented in GainSec white paper</li> </ul> <p>Full technical details: <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="github.com/GainSec/anti-crime-ecosystem-research"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://github.com/GainSec/anti-crime-ecosystem-research"> <a href="https://github.com/GainSec/anti-crime-ecosystem-research" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >github.com/GainSec/anti-crime-ecosystem-research</a> </span> </p> <hr> <h2 id="flock-safetys-response"> <a href="#flock-safetys-response" title="Flock Safety’s Response">Flock Safety’s Response</a> <a href="#flock-safetys-response" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Flock Safety’s Response">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="official-statements"> <a href="#official-statements" title="Official Statements">Official Statements</a> <a href="#official-statements" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Official Statements">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>November 2025 Blog Post</strong>:</p> <blockquote> <p>“Flock is committed to continuously improving security…None of the vulnerabilities detailed in the report have an impact on our customers’ ability to carry out their public safety objectives.”</p> </blockquote> <p><strong>Claim</strong>: Vulnerabilities require physical access and “intimate knowledge”</p> <p><strong>Counter-Evidence</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Exposed camera feeds required <strong>only Google search</strong></li> <li>Wireless exploits require <strong>only proximity</strong></li> <li>API key exposure was <strong>completely remote</strong></li> <li>Hardcoded passwords are <strong>universally known</strong> after disclosure</li> </ul> <h3 id="dismissal-of-research-devices"> <a href="#dismissal-of-research-devices" title="Dismissal of Research Devices">Dismissal of Research Devices</a> <a href="#dismissal-of-research-devices" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Dismissal of Research Devices">#</a> </h3> <p>Flock initially claimed research devices were:</p> <blockquote> <p>“Not connected to the cloud…like an iPhone stolen off a truck before it was ever connected”</p> </blockquote> <p><em>This was proven false.</em> Researchers tested <strong>cloud-connected devices</strong> and got identical results. Public-facing cameras exhibited the <strong>same vulnerabilities</strong>, and 404 Media found <strong>60+ exposed production cameras</strong>.</p> <h3 id="response-to-researcher"> <a href="#response-to-researcher" title="Response to Researcher">Response to Researcher</a> <a href="#response-to-researcher" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Response to Researcher">#</a> </h3> <p>GainSec reported attempts at responsible disclosure met with:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Bug bounty with NDA</strong> (silencing disclosure)</li> <li><strong>No confirmation</strong> of fixes</li> <li><strong>PR statement</strong> released before 90-day window</li> <li><strong>No acknowledgment</strong> of researcher in PR</li> <li><strong>Job loss</strong> for researcher within 48 hours of video release</li> </ul> <p>Ben Jordan reported:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Police visits</strong> to his property</li> <li><strong>Suspected private investigators</strong> photographing home</li> <li><strong>Neighbor harassment</strong></li> <li><strong>Cease and desist</strong> threats from Flock Safety</li> </ul> <p>Quote from Ben:</p> <blockquote> <p>“I don’t view these things as consequences or punishment for researching security vulnerabilities. I view these as consequences and punishment for doing it ethically and transparently.”</p> </blockquote> <h3 id="continued-deployment"> <a href="#continued-deployment" title="Continued Deployment">Continued Deployment</a> <a href="#continued-deployment" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Continued Deployment">#</a> </h3> <p><em>Despite all of this</em>, Flock Safety:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Continues selling</strong> vulnerable devices</li> <li><strong>No firmware updates</strong> addressing root causes</li> <li><strong>Still running</strong> Android Things 8.x (EOL 2021)</li> <li><strong>No mandatory MFA</strong> for law enforcement</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="legislative-and-policy-response"> <a href="#legislative-and-policy-response" title="Legislative and Policy Response">Legislative and Policy Response</a> <a href="#legislative-and-policy-response" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Legislative and Policy Response">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="us-senate-investigation-request"> <a href="#us-senate-investigation-request" title="U.S. Senate Investigation Request">U.S. Senate Investigation Request</a> <a href="#us-senate-investigation-request" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="U.S. Senate Investigation Request">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>February 2026</strong>: Senators <strong>Ron Wyden</strong> (Oregon) and <strong>Raja Krishnamoorthi</strong> (Illinois) sent formal letter to FTC requesting investigation.</p> <p><strong>Letter excerpt</strong>:</p> <blockquote> <p>“Flock has unnecessarily exposed Americans sensitive personal data to theft by hackers and foreign spies.”</p> </blockquote> <p><strong>Grounds cited</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>National security risks</li> <li>Deceptive privacy claims</li> <li>Inadequate security practices</li> <li>Lack of MFA requirements</li> </ul> <h3 id="city-council-actions"> <a href="#city-council-actions" title="City Council Actions">City Council Actions</a> <a href="#city-council-actions" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="City Council Actions">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Denver, Colorado</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>City Council <strong>voted against</strong> Flock contract renewal (December 2025)</li> <li>Cited Flock’s “<strong>disregard for honesty and accountability</strong>”</li> <li>Mayor <strong>override</strong> via “backroom deal” (January 2026)</li> <li>Public backlash ongoing</li> </ul> <p><strong>Evanston, Illinois</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Discovered <strong>ICE using cameras</strong> without consent</li> <li>Voted to <strong>remove cameras</strong></li> <li><em>Flock reinstalled cameras without authorization</em></li> <li>City spending taxpayer money to <strong>cover cameras</strong> with sheeting and pursue legal action</li> </ul> <p><strong>Oakland, California</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Delayed <strong>$2.25 million expansion</strong> vote</li> <li>Commissioned independent security audit</li> <li>Found Flock’s efficacy claims <strong>misleading</strong></li> </ul> <h3 id="public-records-requests"> <a href="#public-records-requests" title="Public Records Requests">Public Records Requests</a> <a href="#public-records-requests" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Public Records Requests">#</a> </h3> <p>Multiple cities conducting investigations based on:</p> <ul> <li>Publicly available security research</li> <li>FOIA requests for Flock contracts</li> <li>Independent audit requirements</li> <li>Community advocacy pressure</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="technical-analysis-real-world-attack-scenarios"> <a href="#technical-analysis-real-world-attack-scenarios" title="Technical Analysis: Real-World Attack Scenarios">Technical Analysis: Real-World Attack Scenarios</a> <a href="#technical-analysis-real-world-attack-scenarios" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Technical Analysis: Real-World Attack Scenarios">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="scenario-1-nation-state-surveillance"> <a href="#scenario-1-nation-state-surveillance" title="Scenario 1: Nation-State Surveillance">Scenario 1: Nation-State Surveillance</a> <a href="#scenario-1-nation-state-surveillance" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Scenario 1: Nation-State Surveillance">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Threat Actor</strong>: Foreign intelligence service</p> <p><strong>Objective</strong>: Track government officials</p> <p><strong>Method</strong>:</p> <ol> <li>Use IMSI catcher to intercept camera LTE traffic</li> <li>Extract clear-text credentials from stream</li> <li>Access backend via compromised API keys (or previously exposed ArcGIS layer)</li> <li>Query database for vehicles registered to government facilities</li> <li>Track movements of officials, military personnel</li> </ol> <p><strong>Time to Execute</strong>: Days (once infrastructure in place) <strong>Detection Likelihood</strong>: Low (encrypted C2, mimics legitimate traffic)</p> <h3 id="scenario-2-organized-crime-counter-surveillance"> <a href="#scenario-2-organized-crime-counter-surveillance" title="Scenario 2: Organized Crime Counter-Surveillance">Scenario 2: Organized Crime Counter-Surveillance</a> <a href="#scenario-2-organized-crime-counter-surveillance" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Scenario 2: Organized Crime Counter-Surveillance">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Threat Actor</strong>: Drug trafficking organization</p> <p><strong>Objective</strong>: Identify police patrol patterns</p> <p><strong>Method</strong>:</p> <ol> <li>Purchase BadUSB device ($15)</li> <li>Drive to cameras in operating territory</li> <li>Plug BadUSB into exposed USB port (30 seconds)</li> <li>Payload disables camera or modifies footage</li> <li>Alternative: Payload exfiltrates police vehicle tracking to remote server</li> </ol> <p><strong>Time to Execute</strong>: 30 seconds per camera <strong>Detection Likelihood</strong>: Low (appears as legitimate device activity)</p> <h3 id="scenario-3-stalker--domestic-violence"> <a href="#scenario-3-stalker--domestic-violence" title="Scenario 3: Stalker / Domestic Violence">Scenario 3: Stalker / Domestic Violence</a> <a href="#scenario-3-stalker--domestic-violence" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Scenario 3: Stalker / Domestic Violence">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Threat Actor</strong>: Abusive ex-partner</p> <p><strong>Objective</strong>: Track victim’s movements</p> <p><strong>Method</strong>:</p> <ol> <li>Obtain victim’s license plate (public information)</li> <li>Search Google for exposed Flock camera interface (January 2026 vulnerability)</li> <li>Query 30 days of footage for vehicle appearances</li> <li>Cross-reference with public databases (work address, home, etc.)</li> <li>Establish pattern of life and timing</li> </ol> <p><strong>Time to Execute</strong>: Minutes to hours <strong>Detection Likelihood</strong>: Zero (no authentication logs exist)</p> <h3 id="scenario-4-evidence-tampering"> <a href="#scenario-4-evidence-tampering" title="Scenario 4: Evidence Tampering">Scenario 4: Evidence Tampering</a> <a href="#scenario-4-evidence-tampering" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Scenario 4: Evidence Tampering">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Threat Actor</strong>: Defendant in criminal case</p> <p><strong>Objective</strong>: Destroy evidence before trial</p> <p><strong>Method</strong>:</p> <ol> <li>Learn camera location via warrant discovery</li> <li>Physical access to camera (button press sequence)</li> <li>Root shell obtained</li> <li>Navigate to evidence folder</li> <li>Delete specific images/videos or modify timestamps</li> </ol> <p><strong>Time to Execute</strong>: Under 5 minutes <strong>Detection Likelihood</strong>: Low (unless camera forensically examined before defense access)</p> <h3 id="scenario-5-privacy-activist--journalist"> <a href="#scenario-5-privacy-activist--journalist" title="Scenario 5: Privacy Activist / Journalist">Scenario 5: Privacy Activist / Journalist</a> <a href="#scenario-5-privacy-activist--journalist" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Scenario 5: Privacy Activist / Journalist">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Threat Actor</strong>: Transparency advocate</p> <p><strong>Objective</strong>: Document surveillance overreach</p> <p><strong>Method</strong>:</p> <ol> <li>Use detection devices (see our <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Flock-You Hardware Guide"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/"> <a href="/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/" rel="follow me" >Flock-You Hardware Guide</a> </span> )</li> <li>Map camera locations in community</li> <li>File FOIA requests for footage policies</li> <li>Demonstrate vulnerabilities to city council</li> <li>Advocate for removal or oversight</li> </ol> <p><strong>Time to Execute</strong>: Ongoing <strong>Detection Likelihood</strong>: High (public activity)</p> <hr> <h2 id="defending-against-these-vulnerabilities"> <a href="#defending-against-these-vulnerabilities" title="Defending Against These Vulnerabilities">Defending Against These Vulnerabilities</a> <a href="#defending-against-these-vulnerabilities" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Defending Against These Vulnerabilities">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="for-law-enforcement-agencies"> <a href="#for-law-enforcement-agencies" title="For Law Enforcement Agencies">For Law Enforcement Agencies</a> <a href="#for-law-enforcement-agencies" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="For Law Enforcement Agencies">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Immediate Actions</strong>:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Require MFA/2FA</strong> for all users (USB security keys)</li> <li><strong>Audit access logs</strong> monthly for unauthorized queries</li> <li><strong>Restrict sharing</strong> to minimum necessary jurisdictions</li> <li><strong>Network isolation</strong> - cameras on dedicated VLANs</li> <li><strong>Physical security</strong> - tamper-evident seals, surveillance of cameras</li> <li><strong>Incident response plan</strong> for compromise</li> </ol> <p><strong>Contractual Requirements</strong>:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Regular security audits</strong> by independent firms</li> <li><strong>Mandatory firmware updates</strong> within 30 days of patch release</li> <li><strong>CVE notification</strong> within 24 hours</li> <li><strong>Encrypted evidence storage</strong> with customer-managed keys</li> <li><strong>SLA penalties</strong> for security breaches</li> <li><strong>Right to audit</strong> vendor facilities and code</li> </ol> <p><strong>Long-Term Strategy</strong>:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Transition plan</strong> away from EOL operating systems</li> <li><strong>Self-hosted infrastructure</strong> (reduce cloud dependence)</li> <li><strong>Open-source alternatives</strong> evaluation</li> <li><strong>Community transparency</strong> about camera locations and policies</li> </ol> <h3 id="for-flock-safety-recommendations"> <a href="#for-flock-safety-recommendations" title="For Flock Safety (Recommendations)">For Flock Safety (Recommendations)</a> <a href="#for-flock-safety-recommendations" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="For Flock Safety (Recommendations)">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Critical Priority</strong> (Immediate):</p> <ol> <li><strong>Disable button-press</strong> wireless access point creation</li> <li><strong>Force MFA</strong> for all user accounts (no exceptions)</li> <li><strong>Rotate all</strong> hardcoded credentials system-wide</li> <li><strong>Emergency patch</strong> for exposed public interfaces</li> <li><strong>Encryption at rest</strong> with proper key management</li> </ol> <p><strong>High Priority</strong> (30 days):</p> <ol> <li><strong>Migrate to supported OS</strong> (Android 12+ or Linux-based)</li> <li><strong>Bug bounty program</strong> without NDAs</li> <li><strong>Third-party security audit</strong> - publish results</li> <li><strong>Network traffic encryption</strong> end-to-end</li> <li><strong>Physical security improvements</strong> (remove exposed USB, hardened enclosures)</li> </ol> <p><strong>Medium Priority</strong> (90 days):</p> <ol> <li><strong>Complete redesign</strong> of authentication system</li> <li><strong>Secure boot</strong> with verified firmware</li> <li><strong>Hardware security module</strong> integration</li> <li><strong>Compliance certifications</strong> (FedRAMP, SOC 2, ISO 27001)</li> <li><strong>Customer security dashboard</strong> with breach notifications</li> </ol> <p><strong>Long-Term</strong> (1 year):</p> <ol> <li><strong>Open-source security components</strong> (build trust)</li> <li><strong>Adversarial testing</strong> program (red team)</li> <li><strong>Security engineering</strong> role requirements</li> <li><strong>SDLC integration</strong> of security practices (DevSecOps)</li> <li><strong>Industry standards</strong> participation (OWASP, CIS)</li> </ol> <h3 id="for-individuals"> <a href="#for-individuals" title="For Individuals">For Individuals</a> <a href="#for-individuals" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="For Individuals">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Protection Strategies</strong>:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Use detection devices</strong> (see our <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Hardware Guide"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/"> <a href="/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/" rel="follow me" >Hardware Guide</a> </span> )</li> <li><strong>Map camera locations</strong> in your area</li> <li><strong>Vary routes and timing</strong> to reduce pattern analysis</li> <li><strong>Advocate at city council</strong> for oversight and transparency</li> <li><strong>FOIA requests</strong> for your data (where legally allowed)</li> <li><strong>Support privacy legislation</strong> at state and federal levels</li> </ol> <p><strong>OpSec Measures</strong>:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Avoid unique vehicles</strong> (common make/model/color)</li> <li><strong>Different vehicles</strong> for sensitive activities (where legal)</li> <li><strong>Alternative transportation</strong> (bike, public transit, walking)</li> <li><strong>Privacy-focused plate covers</strong> (only if legal in jurisdiction)</li> <li><strong>Faraday bags</strong> for phones when correlation is concern</li> </ol> <hr> <h2 id="the-research-team"> <a href="#the-research-team" title="The
