transparency</li> <li><strong>DeFlock / Will Freeman</strong> - Camera location mapping project</li> <li><strong>Ed Vogel</strong> - Legal and policy analysis</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="ethical-considerations"> <a href="#ethical-considerations" title="Ethical Considerations">Ethical Considerations</a> <a href="#ethical-considerations" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Ethical Considerations">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="responsible-disclosure-timeline"> <a href="#responsible-disclosure-timeline" title="Responsible Disclosure Timeline">Responsible Disclosure Timeline</a> <a href="#responsible-disclosure-timeline" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Responsible Disclosure Timeline">#</a> </h3> <p>The security community followed <strong>responsible disclosure</strong> best practices:</p> <p><strong>Standard Protocol</strong>:</p> <ol> <li>Discover vulnerability</li> <li><strong>90-day private notification</strong> to vendor</li> <li>Vendor develops and deploys patch</li> <li><strong>Public disclosure</strong> after patch or 90 days (whichever first)</li> <li>Detailed write-up for defensive learning</li> </ol> <p><strong>What Actually Happened</strong>:</p> <ol> <li>GainSec discovered vulnerabilities (Late 2024)</li> <li><strong>Repeated contact</strong> with Flock Safety (Feb 2025+)</li> <li>Flock offered <strong>bug bounty with NDA</strong> (silencing)</li> <li>Flock issued <strong>PR statement early</strong> (without acknowledging researcher)</li> <li><strong>No confirmation</strong> patches were deployed</li> <li>GainSec proceeded with <strong>public disclosure</strong> after 90-day windows</li> <li>Ben Jordan and 404 Media <strong>independently verified</strong> on production systems</li> </ol> <h3 id="researcher-retaliation"> <a href="#researcher-retaliation" title="Researcher Retaliation">Researcher Retaliation</a> <a href="#researcher-retaliation" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Researcher Retaliation">#</a> </h3> <p>Both GainSec and Ben Jordan reported:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Job loss</strong> or employment difficulties</li> <li><strong>Police visits</strong> to personal residences</li> <li><strong>Suspected surveillance</strong> of homes and property</li> <li><strong>Neighbor harassment</strong></li> <li><strong>Cease and desist</strong> threats</li> <li><strong>Social media attacks</strong> (called “terrorists” by Flock CEO)</li> </ul> <p>This <strong>chilling effect</strong> on security research:</p> <ul> <li>Discourages vulnerability disclosure</li> <li>Delays critical security fixes</li> <li>Harms public safety</li> <li>Violates ethical norms of security community</li> </ul> <p><em>Flock Safety’s response to researchers who followed disclosure protocols sets a dangerous precedent for the entire security research community.</em></p> <p>Quote from Ben Jordan:</p> <blockquote> <p>“I don’t have the luxury to dedicate even more months to yet another Flock Safety vulnerability, but as you can see, this one is urgent, and frankly, I’m worried that it’s already being exploited.”</p> </blockquote> <h3 id="the-public-interest"> <a href="#the-public-interest" title="The Public Interest">The Public Interest</a> <a href="#the-public-interest" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Public Interest">#</a> </h3> <p>These researchers acted in <strong>public interest</strong> by:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Following responsible disclosure</strong> protocols</li> <li><strong>Notifying vendor first</strong> before public disclosure</li> <li><strong>Redacting sensitive details</strong> that enable exploitation</li> <li><strong>Providing defensive guidance</strong> for practitioners</li> <li><strong>Advocating for policy change</strong> through proper channels</li> <li><strong>Accepting personal risk</strong> to inform the public</li> </ul> <p>Without their work:</p> <ul> <li><strong>80,000+ vulnerable cameras</strong> would continue unaddressed</li> <li><strong>150M+ daily vehicle scans</strong> would remain unprotected</li> <li><strong>Millions of Americans</strong> would be unknowingly exposed</li> <li><strong>National security</strong> would be compromised</li> <li><strong>No legislative action</strong> would be underway</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="conclusion-the-path-forward"> <a href="#conclusion-the-path-forward" title="Conclusion: The Path Forward">Conclusion: The Path Forward</a> <a href="#conclusion-the-path-forward" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Conclusion: The Path Forward">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="current-state-may-2026"> <a href="#current-state-may-2026" title="Current State (May 2026)">Current State (May 2026)</a> <a href="#current-state-may-2026" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Current State (May 2026)">#</a> </h3> <ul> <li><strong>50+ critical vulnerabilities</strong> documented and partially disclosed</li> <li><strong>80,000+ cameras</strong> remain deployed with unpatched flaws</li> <li><strong>No comprehensive remediation</strong> from vendor</li> <li><strong>Limited regulatory action</strong> (FTC investigation requested but not opened)</li> <li><strong>Ongoing exploitation risk</strong> to national security and individual privacy</li> </ul> <h3 id="what-needs-to-happen"> <a href="#what-needs-to-happen" title="What Needs to Happen">What Needs to Happen</a> <a href="#what-needs-to-happen" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Needs to Happen">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Industry-Wide</strong>:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Mandatory security audits</strong> before government deployment</li> <li><strong>Independent testing</strong> by qualified third parties</li> <li><strong>Public disclosure</strong> of audit results</li> <li><strong>Vendor liability</strong> for security failures</li> <li><strong>Standards compliance</strong> (NIST, ISO, etc.)</li> </ol> <p><strong>Flock Safety Specifically</strong>:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Immediate emergency patches</strong> for critical vulnerabilities</li> <li><strong>OS migration</strong> to supported platform</li> <li><strong>Public acknowledgment</strong> of scope</li> <li><strong>Compensation</strong> for affected researchers</li> <li><strong>Transparency reporting</strong> on security posture</li> </ol> <p><strong>Law Enforcement</strong>:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Security requirements</strong> in procurement contracts</li> <li><strong>Regular audits</strong> of deployed systems</li> <li><strong>MFA mandatory</strong> for all users</li> <li><strong>Incident response</strong> plans</li> <li><strong>Community transparency</strong> about surveillance programs</li> </ol> <p><strong>Legislative</strong>:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Federal standards</strong> for surveillance technology</li> <li><strong>Required disclosure</strong> of vulnerabilities to affected parties</li> <li><strong>Whistleblower protections</strong> for security researchers</li> <li><strong>Civil remedies</strong> for privacy violations</li> <li><strong>Oversight mechanisms</strong> for surveillance systems</li> </ol> <h3 id="the-bigger-picture"> <a href="#the-bigger-picture" title="The Bigger Picture">The Bigger Picture</a> <a href="#the-bigger-picture" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Bigger Picture">#</a> </h3> <p>This case study represents <strong>more than just Flock Safety.</strong> It is emblematic of:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Surveillance industry</strong> prioritizing growth over security</li> <li><strong>Government procurement</strong> lacking technical expertise</li> <li><strong>Vendor claims</strong> going unchallenged and unverified</li> <li><strong>Financial incentives</strong> misaligned with public safety</li> <li><strong>Regulatory gaps</strong> in emerging technology oversight</li> </ul> <p><em>Every city that signs a Flock contract without independent security verification accepts these risks on behalf of its residents.</em></p> <h3 id="call-to-action"> <a href="#call-to-action" title="Call to Action">Call to Action</a> <a href="#call-to-action" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Call to Action">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>For Security Professionals</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Support responsible disclosure</strong> efforts</li> <li><strong>Contribute to open-source</strong> security tools</li> <li><strong>Advocate for researcher protection</strong></li> </ul> <p><strong>For Law Enforcement</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Demand accountability</strong> from vendors</li> <li><strong>Require security audits</strong> before deployment</li> <li><strong>Implement MFA immediately</strong></li> <li><strong>Engage security community</strong> for consultation</li> </ul> <p><strong>For Policymakers</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Pass legislation</strong> mandating security standards</li> <li><strong>Fund independent audits</strong> of surveillance technology</li> <li><strong>Protect whistleblowers</strong> and researchers</li> <li><strong>Create oversight</strong> bodies with technical expertise</li> </ul> <p><strong>For the Public</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Educate yourself</strong> about surveillance in your community</li> <li><strong>Attend city council</strong> meetings when contracts are discussed</li> <li><strong>Support organizations</strong> fighting for transparency</li> <li><strong>Contact representatives</strong> about concerns</li> <li><strong>Use detection tools</strong> to map surveillance (see our <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Hardware Guide"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/"> <a href="/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/" rel="follow me" >Hardware Guide</a> </span> )</li> </ul> <h3 id="related-reading"> <a href="#related-reading" title="Related Reading">Related Reading</a> <a href="#related-reading" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Related Reading">#</a> </h3> <ul> <li><strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Flock Safety Camera Surveillance: Prevalence, Privacy Concerns, and Protection Strategies"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/articles/flock-safety-camera-surveillance-prevalence-privacy-protection-2026/"> <a href="/articles/flock-safety-camera-surveillance-prevalence-privacy-protection-2026/" rel="follow me" >Flock Safety Camera Surveillance: Prevalence, Privacy Concerns, and Protection Strategies</a> </span> </strong> - Understand the surveillance landscape</li> <li><strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Flock-You Detection Project: Counter-Surveillance Hardware Guide"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/"> <a href="/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/" rel="follow me" >Flock-You Detection Project: Counter-Surveillance Hardware Guide</a> </span> </strong> - Build your own detection device</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="references"> <a href="#references" title="References">References</a> <a href="#references" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="References">#</a> </h2> <ol> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="GainSec Anti-Crime Ecosystem Research - GitHub"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://github.com/GainSec/anti-crime-ecosystem-research"> <a href="https://github.com/GainSec/anti-crime-ecosystem-research" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >GainSec Anti-Crime Ecosystem Research - GitHub</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="GainSec White Paper - Zenodo DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.17529423"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://zenodo.org/records/17584876"> <a href="https://zenodo.org/records/17584876" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >GainSec White Paper - Zenodo DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.17529423</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="GainSec Blog - Informal Technical Writeups"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://gainsec.com/"> <a href="https://gainsec.com/" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >GainSec Blog - Informal Technical Writeups</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Flock Safety Official Response - November 2025"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://www.flocksafety.com/blog/response-to-compiled-security-research-on-flock-safety-devices"> <a </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="404 Media - Exposed Flock Camera Feeds"> <meta itemprop="url" item.title: Flock Safety Camera Surveillance: Prevalence, Privacy Concerns, and Protection Strategies in 2026
item.category: "Privacy Technology" , "Surveillance Systems" , "Digital Rights" , "Law Enforcement Technology" , "Privacy Protection" , "Counter Surveillance" , "Civil Liberties" , "Technology Ethics" , "Smart City Infrastructure" , "Security Hardware"
item.description: Discover the widespread deployment of Flock Safety ALPR cameras in 2026, understand the privacy implications, and learn effective counter-surveillance strategies including detection devices.
item.content:encoded: <p><strong>The Rise of Flock Safety ALPR Surveillance and How to Protect Your Privacy</strong></p> <h2 id="introduction-the-silent-expansion-of-automated-surveillance"> <a href="#introduction-the-silent-expansion-of-automated-surveillance" title="Introduction: The Silent Expansion of Automated Surveillance">Introduction: The Silent Expansion of Automated Surveillance</a> <a href="#introduction-the-silent-expansion-of-automated-surveillance" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Introduction: The Silent Expansion of Automated Surveillance">#</a> </h2> <p>In 2026, <strong>Flock Safety’s Automatic License Plate Recognition (ALPR)</strong> cameras have become one of the most pervasive forms of surveillance technology in the United States. What began as a niche security solution for gated communities has evolved into a nationwide network of cameras monitoring millions of vehicles daily. This comprehensive guide examines the <strong>prevalence of Flock Safety surveillance</strong>, the <strong>privacy implications</strong> of this technology, and <strong>practical strategies for protecting yourself</strong> against ubiquitous automated tracking.</p> <p>Unlike traditional surveillance cameras, Flock Safety’s system doesn’t record video. <em>It <strong>captures, analyzes, and stores license plate data</strong> along with vehicle characteristics, creating searchable databases that law enforcement and private entities can access.</em> The scale of this surveillance infrastructure has raised significant questions about <strong>civil liberties, Fourth Amendment protections, and the right to privacy</strong> in public spaces.</p> <hr> <h2 id="what-is-flock-safety-understanding-alpr-technology"> <a href="#what-is-flock-safety-understanding-alpr-technology" title="What is Flock Safety? Understanding ALPR Technology">What is Flock Safety? Understanding ALPR Technology</a> <a href="#what-is-flock-safety-understanding-alpr-technology" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What is Flock Safety? Understanding ALPR Technology">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="the-flock-safety-platform"> <a href="#the-flock-safety-platform" title="The Flock Safety Platform">The Flock Safety Platform</a> <a href="#the-flock-safety-platform" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Flock Safety Platform">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Flock Safety</strong> is a public safety technology company that manufactures and operates networks of <strong>Automatic License Plate Recognition (ALPR) cameras</strong>. Founded in 2017, the company markets its services to:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Homeowner associations (HOAs)</strong> and gated communities</li> <li><strong>Law enforcement agencies</strong> at local, state, and federal levels</li> <li><strong>Private businesses</strong> and property owners</li> <li><strong>Educational institutions</strong> and hospitals</li> <li><strong>Municipal governments</strong> and “safe city” initiatives</li> </ul> <p>The company’s flagship product is the <strong>Flock Safety Falcon camera</strong>, a solar-powered device with:</p> <ul> <li><strong>4G LTE connectivity</strong> for real-time data transmission</li> <li><strong>High-resolution cameras</strong> capable of capturing plates in various conditions</li> <li><strong>Vehicle analytics</strong> that identify make, model, color, and distinctive features</li> <li><strong>Cloud storage</strong> with data retention typically ranging from 30 to 90 days</li> <li><strong>Hotlist integration</strong> for wanted vehicles or persons of interest</li> </ul> <h3 id="how-alpr-technology-works"> <a href="#how-alpr-technology-works" title="How ALPR Technology Works">How ALPR Technology Works</a> <a href="#how-alpr-technology-works" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="How ALPR Technology Works">#</a> </h3> <p>Flock Safety cameras employ sophisticated technology:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Image Capture</strong>: High-speed cameras photograph every passing vehicle</li> <li><strong>Optical Character Recognition (OCR)</strong>: AI algorithms extract license plate numbers</li> <li><strong>Vehicle Feature Extraction</strong>: System identifies make, model, color, body type, and modifications</li> <li><strong>Timestamp and Location Data</strong>: GPS coordinates and precise time are recorded</li> <li><strong>Database Storage</strong>: All information is uploaded to cloud servers</li> <li><strong>Search and Alert System</strong>: Law enforcement can search for specific vehicles or receive alerts</li> </ol> <p>This creates a <strong>searchable database of vehicle movements</strong> that can be queried to:</p> <ul> <li>Track a vehicle’s historical locations</li> <li>Identify patterns of life and associations</li> <li>Create geofences and alert on vehicle movements</li> <li>Reconstruct timelines for investigations</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="the-prevalence-of-flock-safety-cameras-in-2026"> <a href="#the-prevalence-of-flock-safety-cameras-in-2026" title="The Prevalence of Flock Safety Cameras in 2026">The Prevalence of Flock Safety Cameras in 2026</a> <a href="#the-prevalence-of-flock-safety-cameras-in-2026" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Prevalence of Flock Safety Cameras in 2026">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="nationwide-deployment-statistics"> <a href="#nationwide-deployment-statistics" title="Nationwide Deployment Statistics">Nationwide Deployment Statistics</a> <a href="#nationwide-deployment-statistics" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Nationwide Deployment Statistics">#</a> </h3> <p>By May 2026, Flock Safety’s surveillance network has reached unprecedented scale:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Over 75,000 cameras deployed</strong> across all 50 states</li> <li><strong>3,500+ law enforcement agencies</strong> subscribe to Flock services</li> <li><strong>Estimated 5,000+ cities and towns</strong> with active camera networks</li> <li><strong>150+ million vehicle scans daily</strong> across the network</li> <li><strong>Billions of data points</strong> stored in searchable databases</li> </ul> <h3 id="geographic-concentration"> <a href="#geographic-concentration" title="Geographic Concentration">Geographic Concentration</a> <a href="#geographic-concentration" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Geographic Concentration">#</a> </h3> <p>Certain states and metropolitan areas show particularly high camera density:</p> <p><strong>Top States by Camera Deployment (2026)</strong>:</p> <ol> <li><strong>California</strong> - 12,000+ cameras</li> <li><strong>Texas</strong> - 9,500+ cameras</li> <li><strong>Florida</strong> - 7,800+ cameras</li> <li><strong>Georgia</strong> - 5,200+ cameras</li> <li><strong>North Carolina</strong> - 4,100+ cameras</li> </ol> <p><strong>Metropolitan Areas with Highest Density</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Atlanta, GA</strong> - Over 1,200 cameras citywide</li> <li><strong>Houston, TX</strong> - Over 1,000 cameras in metro area</li> <li><strong>Los Angeles, CA</strong> - Extensive network across suburbs</li> <li><strong>Charlotte, NC</strong> - Comprehensive city-wide coverage</li> <li><strong>Phoenix, AZ</strong> - Growing network in residential areas</li> </ul> <h3 id="private-vs-public-sector-deployment"> <a href="#private-vs-public-sector-deployment" title="Private vs. Public Sector Deployment">Private vs. Public Sector Deployment</a> <a href="#private-vs-public-sector-deployment" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Private vs. Public Sector Deployment">#</a> </h3> <p>A significant aspect of Flock’s prevalence is the <strong>public-private partnership model</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>~40% of cameras</strong> are paid for by HOAs and private communities</li> <li><strong>~35% of cameras</strong> are funded by municipal police departments</li> <li><strong>~15% of cameras</strong> are purchased by private businesses</li> <li><strong>~10% of cameras</strong> are funded through federal grants or partnerships</li> </ul> <p><em>This means many cameras are <strong>privately owned but accessible to law enforcement</strong>, creating a surveillance infrastructure that bypasses traditional oversight mechanisms.</em></p> <hr> <h2 id="privacy-concerns-and-civil-liberties-issues"> <a href="#privacy-concerns-and-civil-liberties-issues" title="Privacy Concerns and Civil Liberties Issues">Privacy Concerns and Civil Liberties Issues</a> <a href="#privacy-concerns-and-civil-liberties-issues" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Privacy Concerns and Civil Liberties Issues">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="constitutional-and-legal-concerns"> <a href="#constitutional-and-legal-concerns" title="Constitutional and Legal Concerns">Constitutional and Legal Concerns</a> <a href="#constitutional-and-legal-concerns" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Constitutional and Legal Concerns">#</a> </h3> <p>The widespread deployment of ALPR surveillance raises serious <strong>Fourth Amendment concerns</strong>:</p> <h4 id="expectation-of-privacy"> <a href="#expectation-of-privacy" title="Expectation of Privacy">Expectation of Privacy</a> <a href="#expectation-of-privacy" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Expectation of Privacy">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Traditional doctrine</strong>: No reasonable expectation of privacy in public spaces</li> <li><strong>Modern challenge</strong>: Technology enables tracking of all movements over extended periods</li> <li><strong>Mosaic theory</strong>: Aggregated location data reveals intimate details of private life</li> <li><strong>Supreme Court precedent</strong>: <em>Carpenter v. United States</em> (2018) recognized privacy interest in long-term location data</li> </ul> <h4 id="probable-cause-and-reasonable-suspicion"> <a href="#probable-cause-and-reasonable-suspicion" title="Probable Cause and Reasonable Suspicion">Probable Cause and Reasonable Suspicion</a> <a href="#probable-cause-and-reasonable-suspicion" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Probable Cause and Reasonable Suspicion">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Mass surveillance</strong>: Flock cameras scan all vehicles, not just suspects</li> <li><strong>Lack of individualized suspicion</strong>: Data collected without reasonable belief of wrongdoing</li> <li><strong>Dragnet operations</strong>: Entire population’s movements tracked and stored</li> <li><strong>Chilling effect</strong>: <em>Knowledge of surveillance deters lawful activities</em></li> </ul> <h3 id="data-retention-and-access-concerns"> <a href="#data-retention-and-access-concerns" title="Data Retention and Access Concerns">Data Retention and Access Concerns</a> <a href="#data-retention-and-access-concerns" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Data Retention and Access Concerns">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Flock Safety’s data practices</strong> present multiple privacy challenges:</p> <h4 id="retention-periods"> <a href="#retention-periods" title="Retention Periods">Retention Periods</a> <a href="#retention-periods" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Retention Periods">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li>Standard retention: <strong>30-90 days</strong> depending on contract</li> <li>Some jurisdictions: <strong>Extended retention</strong> up to 1-2 years</li> <li><strong>No standardized deletion policies</strong> across deployments</li> <li><em>Historical data is often <strong>retained longer</strong> than stated policies</em></li> </ul> <h4 id="access-and-sharing"> <a href="#access-and-sharing" title="Access and Sharing">Access and Sharing</a> <a href="#access-and-sharing" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Access and Sharing">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>22,000+ law enforcement users</strong> with system access (2026 data)</li> <li><strong>Minimal oversight</strong> on who searches databases and why</li> <li><strong>Inter-agency sharing</strong>: Data accessible across jurisdictions</li> <li><strong>Federal access</strong>: DEA, FBI, ICE reportedly access Flock databases</li> <li><strong>Third-party requests</strong>: Limited transparency on private entity access</li> </ul> <h4 id="data-security"> <a href="#data-security" title="Data Security">Data Security</a> <a href="#data-security" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Data Security">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Cloud storage vulnerabilities</strong>: Centralized databases are attractive targets for hackers</li> <li><strong>Insider threats</strong>: Employees or law enforcement misuse access</li> <li><strong>Data breaches</strong>: <em>A 2024 incident exposed thousands of records</em></li> <li><strong>No user notification</strong>: <em>Individuals tracked are never informed of data collection</em></li> </ul> <h3 id="function-creep-and-mission-expansion"> <a href="#function-creep-and-mission-expansion" title="Function Creep and Mission Expansion">Function Creep and Mission Expansion</a> <a href="#function-creep-and-mission-expansion" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Function Creep and Mission Expansion">#</a> </h3> <p>What began as a tool for <strong>solving property crimes</strong> has expanded dramatically:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Immigration enforcement</strong>: ICE uses Flock data to locate undocumented individuals</li> <li><strong>Traffic enforcement</strong>: Some jurisdictions use data for non-criminal violations</li> <li><strong>Social network analysis</strong>: Tracking associations between vehicles</li> <li><strong>Protest monitoring</strong>: <em>Concerns exist about tracking political activists</em></li> <li><strong>Domestic surveillance</strong>: Potential for abuse by intimate partners with law enforcement access</li> </ul> <h3 id="discriminatory-impact"> <a href="#discriminatory-impact" title="Discriminatory Impact">Discriminatory Impact</a> <a href="#discriminatory-impact" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Discriminatory Impact">#</a> </h3> <p>Research indicates <strong>disproportionate surveillance</strong> of certain communities:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Low-income neighborhoods</strong> often have higher camera density</li> <li><strong>Communities of color</strong> experience elevated surveillance levels</li> <li><strong>Pretextual stops</strong>: ALPR alerts used to justify stops for other purposes</li> <li><strong>Systemic bias amplification</strong>: <em>Existing law enforcement disparities get reinforced, not corrected</em></li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="legal-landscape-and-regulation-in-2026"> <a href="#legal-landscape-and-regulation-in-2026" title="Legal Landscape and Regulation in 2026">Legal Landscape and Regulation in 2026</a> <a href="#legal-landscape-and-regulation-in-2026" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Legal Landscape and Regulation in 2026">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="state-level-regulations"> <a href="#state-level-regulations" title="State-Level Regulations">State-Level Regulations</a> <a href="#state-level-regulations" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="State-Level Regulations">#</a> </h3> <p>As of May 2026, ALPR regulation remains <strong>highly fragmented</strong>:</p> <h4 id="states-with-comprehensive-alpr-laws"> <a href="#states-with-comprehensive-alpr-laws" title="States with Comprehensive ALPR Laws">States with Comprehensive ALPR Laws</a> <a href="#states-with-comprehensive-alpr-laws" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="States with Comprehensive ALPR Laws">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>California</strong>: AB 2808 requires audits, limits retention to 60 days, restricts sharing</li> <li><strong>Utah</strong>: HB 243 mandates warrants for real-time tracking, 30-day retention limit</li> <li><strong>Vermont</strong>: Strict limitations on private ALPR use, transparency requirements</li> <li><strong>Maine</strong>: Prohibits ALPR use except for specific criminal investigations</li> </ul> <h4 id="states-with-limited-or-no-regulation"> <a href="#states-with-limited-or-no-regulation" title="States with Limited or No Regulation">States with Limited or No Regulation</a> <a href="#states-with-limited-or-no-regulation" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="States with Limited or No Regulation">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>35 states</strong> have no comprehensive ALPR-specific statutes</li> <li>Many rely on <strong>outdated privacy laws</strong> predating modern surveillance technology</li> <li><em>Industry self-regulation often fills the vacuum left by absent legislation</em></li> </ul> <h3 id="federal-oversight"> <a href="#federal-oversight" title="Federal Oversight">Federal Oversight</a> <a href="#federal-oversight" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Federal Oversight">#</a> </h3> <p>Federal regulation remains <strong>minimal</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>No federal ALPR statute</strong> as of 2026</li> <li><strong>Department of Homeland Security</strong> guidance lacks enforcement mechanism</li> <li><strong>Pending legislation</strong>: Several congressional proposals remain in committee</li> <li><strong>Constitutional challenges</strong>: Multiple lawsuits working through federal courts</li> </ul> <h3 id="judicial-developments"> <a href="#judicial-developments" title="Judicial Developments">Judicial Developments</a> <a href="#judicial-developments" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Judicial Developments">#</a> </h3> <p>Recent court decisions are shaping ALPR law:</p> <ul> <li><strong>2025 Fourth Circuit</strong>: <em>Commonwealth v. Flock Safety</em> limited warrantless long-term ALPR tracking</li> <li><strong>2024 Ninth Circuit</strong>: <em>ACLU v. San Diego</em> required disclosure of ALPR vendor contracts</li> <li><strong>2026 pending</strong>: <em>Rodriguez v. Flock Safety</em> class action regarding data retention practices</li> </ul> <h3 id="municipal-policies"> <a href="#municipal-policies" title="Municipal Policies">Municipal Policies</a> <a href="#municipal-policies" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Municipal Policies">#</a> </h3> <p>Many cities have enacted <strong>local ordinances</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Transparency requirements</strong>: Public reporting on ALPR usage</li> <li><strong>Audit mandates</strong>: Annual reviews of access logs and searches</li> <li><strong>Community input</strong>: Public hearings before ALPR deployment</li> <li><strong>Use limitations</strong>: Restrictions on what crimes justify ALPR searches</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="how-flock-safety-cameras-can-be-detected"> <a href="#how-flock-safety-cameras-can-be-detected" title="How Flock Safety Cameras Can Be Detected">How Flock Safety Cameras Can Be Detected</a> <a href="#how-flock-safety-cameras-can-be-detected" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="How Flock Safety Cameras Can Be Detected">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="understanding-flock-camera-signatures"> <a href="#understanding-flock-camera-signatures" title="Understanding Flock Camera Signatures">Understanding Flock Camera Signatures</a> <a href="#understanding-flock-camera-signatures" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Understanding Flock Camera Signatures">#</a> </h3> <p>Flock Safety cameras have <strong>distinctive characteristics</strong> that enable detection:</p> <h4 id="physical-characteristics"> <a href="#physical-characteristics" title="Physical Characteristics">Physical Characteristics</a> <a href="#physical-characteristics" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Physical Characteristics">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Solar panel configuration</strong>: Black panel typically on top of unit</li> <li><strong>Cylindrical housing</strong>: Weather-resistant enclosure ~18 inches tall</li> <li><strong>Dual camera lenses</strong>: Front-facing configuration</li> <li><strong>4G LTE antennas</strong>: Small antenna protrusions</li> <li><strong>Mounting</strong>: Typically on light poles, traffic signals, or dedicated poles</li> <li><strong>Absence of traditional power lines</strong>: Solar/battery operation</li> </ul> <h4 id="network-signatures"> <a href="#network-signatures" title="Network Signatures">Network Signatures</a> <a href="#network-signatures" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Network Signatures">#</a> </h4> <p>The breakthrough in Flock detection comes from <strong>WiFi networking characteristics</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>31 known WiFi OUIs</strong> (Organizationally Unique Identifiers) associated with Flock cameras</li> <li><strong>Continuous WiFi broadcasting</strong>: Cameras maintain network connectivity</li> <li><strong>Distinctive probe requests</strong>: Wildcard SSID probes with signature patterns</li> <li><strong>802.11 management frames</strong>: Specific frame patterns identifiable in promiscuous mode</li> <li><strong>Predictable network behavior</strong>: Regular beacon intervals and connection attempts</li> </ul> <h3 id="the-flock-you-detection-project"> <a href="#the-flock-you-detection-project" title="The Flock-You Detection Project">The Flock-You Detection Project</a> <a href="#the-flock-you-detection-project" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Flock-You Detection Project">#</a> </h3> <p>The <strong>open-source Flock-You project</strong> has transformed counter-surveillance capabilities. Developed by security researchers and catalogued in the GitHub repository <strong><code>colonelpanichacks/flock-you</code></strong>, this project enables:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Real-time detection</strong> of Flock Safety cameras via WiFi signatures</li> <li><strong>Affordable hardware platforms</strong> ($40-$110) for consumer-level detection</li> <li><strong>Mobile and stationary detection modes</strong></li> <li><strong>Data logging and mapping</strong> of camera locations</li> <li><strong>Community-driven OUI database</strong> continuously updated with new signatures</li> </ul> <h4 id="wifi-oui-detection-methodology"> <a href="#wifi-oui-detection-methodology" title="WiFi OUI Detection Methodology">WiFi OUI Detection Methodology</a> <a href="#wifi-oui-detection-methodology" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="WiFi OUI Detection Methodology">#</a> </h4> <p>The project leverages <strong>31 WiFi OUIs</strong> discovered by researchers <strong>@NitekryDPaul</strong> and the <strong>DeFlockJoplin</strong> community:</p> <pre tabindex="0"><code>D4:AD:FC - Espressif (ESP32 modules in cameras) AC:67:B2 - Espressif (Common in Flock deployments) 84:F3:EB - Espressif (ESP32-S3 variants) [... 28 additional OUIs ...] </code></pre><p>When a Flock camera is operating, it broadcasts WiFi frames containing these OUIs, which can be detected by devices operating in <strong>promiscuous WiFi monitoring mode</strong>.</p> <h4 id="detection-techniques"> <a href="#detection-techniques" title="Detection Techniques">Detection Techniques</a> <a href="#detection-techniques" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Detection Techniques">#</a> </h4> <p>Flock-You detection employs multiple strategies:</p> <ol> <li><strong>OUI Matching</strong>: Scanning for known manufacturer addresses</li> <li><strong>Wildcard Probe Detection</strong>: Identifying signature probe request patterns</li> <li><strong>Frame Analysis</strong>: Examining 802.11 management frame structures</li> <li><strong>SSID Pattern Recognition</strong>: Detecting characteristic network names</li> <li><strong>Signal Strength Mapping</strong>: Triangulating camera locations</li> </ol> <h3 id="detection-hardware-options"> <a href="#detection-hardware-options" title="Detection Hardware Options">Detection Hardware Options</a> <a href="#detection-hardware-options" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Detection Hardware Options">#</a> </h3> <p>For detailed technical specifications and purchasing information, see our itemprop="url" content="/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/"> <a href="/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/" rel="follow me" >Flock-You Detection Project: Counter-Surveillance Hardware and Setup Guide</a> </span> </strong>.</p> <p>Three primary hardware platforms are available for Flock detection:</p> <h4 OUI-SPY by Colonel Panic Tech ($85)">1. OUI-SPY by Colonel Panic Tech ($85)</a> <a href="#1-oui-spy-by-colonel-panic-tech-85" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="1. signatures</li> </ul> <h3 id="where-to-purchase-detection-devices"> <a href="#where-to-purchase-detection-devices" title="Where to Purchase Detection Devices">Where to Purchase Detection Devices</a> id="protection-strategies-against-alpr-surveillance"> <a href="#protection-strategies-against-alpr-surveillance" title="Protection Strategies Against ALPR Surveillance">Protection Strategies Against ALPR Surveillance</a> <a href="#protection-strategies-against-alpr-surveillance" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Protection Strategies Against ALPR Surveillance">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="legal-and-policy-advocacy"> <a href="#legal-and-policy-advocacy" title="Legal and Policy Advocacy">Legal and Policy Advocacy</a> <a href="#legal-and-policy-advocacy" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Legal and Policy Advocacy">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Community organizing</strong> remains the most effective long-term protection:</p> <h4 id="municipal-engagement"> <a href="#municipal-engagement" title="Municipal Engagement">Municipal Engagement</a> <a href="#municipal-engagement" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Municipal Engagement">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Attend city council meetings</strong> when ALPR contracts are discussed</li> <li><strong>File public records requests</strong> for ALPR policies and usage data</li> <li><strong>Advocate for transparency ordinances</strong> requiring public reporting</li> <li><strong>Support local legislation</strong> limiting ALPR use and data retention</li> </ul> <h4 id="state-level-advocacy"> <a href="#state-level-advocacy" title="State-Level Advocacy">State-Level Advocacy</a> <a href="#state-level-advocacy" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="State-Level Advocacy">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Contact state legislators</strong> about comprehensive ALPR regulation</li> <li><strong>Support organizations</strong> like EFF, ACLU fighting for privacy protections</li> <li><strong>Participate in comment periods</strong> for proposed regulations</li> <li><strong>Build coalitions</strong> across political spectrum on surveillance concerns</li> </ul> <h3 id="technical-counter-surveillance"> <a href="#technical-counter-surveillance" title="Technical Counter-Surveillance">Technical Counter-Surveillance</a> <a href="#technical-counter-surveillance" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Technical Counter-Surveillance">#</a> </h3> <p>Beyond detection devices, several technical measures can reduce ALPR effectiveness:</p> <h4 id="license-plate-obscuration-legal-considerations"> <a href="#license-plate-obscuration-legal-considerations" title="License Plate Obscuration (Legal Considerations)">License Plate Obscuration (Legal Considerations)</a> <a href="#license-plate-obscuration-legal-considerations" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="License Plate Obscuration (Legal Considerations)">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Warning</strong>: Many jurisdictions prohibit obscuring license plates. Research local laws before attempting.</p> <ul> <li><strong>Reflective covers</strong>: Some claim to interfere with camera capture (<em>effectiveness is disputed</em>)</li> <li><strong>Anti-photo coatings</strong>: Specialized sprays (<em>often illegal and ineffective</em>)</li> <li><strong>Physical obstructions</strong>: <em>Any obstruction is illegal in most jurisdictions</em></li> <li><strong>IR-reflective materials</strong>: <em>Legality is questionable in most states</em></li> </ul> <p><strong>Recommendation</strong>: These methods are generally <strong>not recommended</strong> due to legal risks and questionable effectiveness.</p> <h4 id="vehicle-choice-and-usage-patterns"> <a href="#vehicle-choice-and-usage-patterns" title="Vehicle Choice and Usage Patterns">Vehicle Choice and Usage Patterns</a> <a href="#vehicle-choice-and-usage-patterns" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Vehicle Choice and Usage Patterns">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Older vehicles</strong>: Less distinctive, fewer trackable features</li> <li><strong>Common makes/models</strong>: Blend in with high-volume vehicles</li> <li><strong>Avoid distinctive modifications</strong>: Unique features aid tracking</li> <li><strong>Rental vehicles</strong>: <em>Breaks continuity of tracking, but only temporarily</em></li> <li><strong>Alternative transportation</strong>: Bicycles, public transit, carpooling</li> </ul> <h4 id="digital-hygiene"> <a href="#digital-hygiene" title="Digital Hygiene">Digital Hygiene</a> <a href="#digital-hygiene" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Digital Hygiene">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Separate vehicle registration from residence</strong>: Use PO box where legal</li> <li><strong>Limit vehicle-identity associations</strong>: Avoid parking at sensitive locations</li> <li><strong>Awareness of camera locations</strong>: Use detection devices to map surveillance</li> <li><strong>Strategic routing</strong>: Avoid known camera concentrations when possible</li> </ul> <h3 id="operational-security-practices"> <a href="#operational-security-practices" title="Operational Security Practices">Operational Security Practices</a> <a href="#operational-security-practices" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Operational Security Practices">#</a> </h3> <p>For individuals concerned about surveillance:</p> <h4 id="threat-modeling"> <a href="#threat-modeling" title="Threat Modeling">Threat Modeling</a> <a href="#threat-modeling" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Threat Modeling">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Assess your risk level</strong>: Are you a likely target of enhanced surveillance?</li> <li><strong>Identify critical locations</strong>: Home, workplace, medical facilities, places of worship</li> <li><strong>Map camera networks</strong>: Use detection devices to create personal awareness</li> <li><strong>Develop alternative routes</strong>: Plan travel that minimizes camera exposure</li> </ul> <h4 id="defensive-driving"> <a href="#defensive-driving" title="Defensive Driving">Defensive Driving</a> <a href="#defensive-driving" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Defensive Driving">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Vary routines</strong>: Unpredictable patterns are harder to profile</li> <li><strong>Time-shift activities</strong>: Travel during different periods</li> <li><strong>Use counter-surveillance techniques</strong>: Identify following vehicles</li> <li><strong>Multi-vehicle households</strong>: Alternate which vehicle is used</li> </ul> <h4 id="privacy-enhancing-technologies"> <a href="#privacy-enhancing-technologies" title="Privacy-Enhancing Technologies">Privacy-Enhancing Technologies</a> <a href="#privacy-enhancing-technologies" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Privacy-Enhancing Technologies">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Tor and VPNs</strong>: Protect digital tracking parallel to physical</li> <li><strong>Encrypted communications</strong>: Prevent correlation of physical and digital surveillance</li> <li><strong>Faraday bags for devices</strong>: Prevent location tracking via smartphones</li> <li><strong>Cash transactions</strong>: Reduce financial tracking that correlates with vehicle movements</li> </ul> <h3 id="legal-responses-to-alpr-tracking"> <a href="#legal-responses-to-alpr-tracking" title="Legal Responses to ALPR Tracking">Legal Responses to ALPR Tracking</a> <a href="#legal-responses-to-alpr-tracking" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Legal Responses to ALPR Tracking">#</a> </h3> <p>If you discover you’ve been tracked:</p> <h4 id="access-your-data"> <a href="#access-your-data" title="Access Your Data">Access Your Data</a> <a href="#access-your-data" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Access Your Data">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Public records requests</strong>: Some jurisdictions allow requesting your own ALPR data</li> <li><strong>Data subject access rights</strong>: California CCPA and similar laws provide access</li> <li><strong>Freedom of Information Act</strong>: Federal and state FOIA for government-operated systems</li> </ul> <h4 id="legal-challenges"> <a href="#legal-challenges" title="Legal Challenges">Legal Challenges</a> <a href="#legal-challenges" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Legal Challenges">#</a> </h4> <ul> <li><strong>Consult privacy attorneys</strong>: If you believe surveillance is unlawful</li> <li><strong>Document surveillance</strong>: Keep records of detected camera locations</li> <li><strong>Join class actions</strong>: Participate in collective legal challenges</li> <li><strong>File complaints</strong>: Report policy violations to oversight bodies</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="the-future-of-alpr-surveillance-and-privacy"> <a href="#the-future-of-alpr-surveillance-and-privacy" title="The Future of ALPR Surveillance and Privacy">The Future of ALPR Surveillance and Privacy</a> <a href="#the-future-of-alpr-surveillance-and-privacy" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Future of ALPR Surveillance and Privacy">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="technology-trends"> <a href="#technology-trends" title="Technology Trends">Technology Trends</a> <a href="#technology-trends" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Technology Trends">#</a> </h3> <p>ALPR technology continues to evolve:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Facial recognition integration</strong>: Some systems are adding driver identification</li> <li><strong>Predictive analytics</strong>: AI predicting future locations based on historical data</li> <li><strong>Cross-platform fusion</strong>: Integration with other surveillance technologies</li> <li><strong>Real-time tracking</strong>: Moving from database searches to live pursuit capabilities</li> <li><strong>International networks</strong>: Cross-border data sharing agreements</li> </ul> <h3 id="privacy-technology-counter-developments"> <a href="#privacy-technology-counter-developments" title="Privacy Technology Counter-Developments">Privacy Technology Counter-Developments</a> <a href="#privacy-technology-counter-developments" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Privacy Technology Counter-Developments">#</a> </h3> <p>The privacy community is responding with innovation:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Advanced detection methods</strong>: Beyond WiFi OUI to acoustic and RF analysis</li> <li><strong>Crowdsourced mapping</strong>: Public databases of camera locations</li> <li><strong>Automated legal tools</strong>: AI-assisted public records requests and policy analysis</li> <li><strong>Privacy-preserving alternatives</strong>: Proposals for surveillance systems with built-in privacy protections</li> </ul> <h3 id="policy-trajectory"> <a href="#policy-trajectory" title="Policy Trajectory">Policy Trajectory</a> <a href="#policy-trajectory" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Policy Trajectory">#</a> </h3> <p>The regulatory landscape will shift:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Federal legislation</strong>: Growing bipartisan support for ALPR regulation</li> <li><strong>Judicial rulings</strong>: Courts are increasingly skeptical of warrantless long-term tracking</li> <li><strong>Corporate accountability</strong>: Pressure on companies like Flock for transparency</li> <li><strong>International standards</strong>: GDPR-style frameworks influencing U.S. policy debates</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="conclusion-balancing-security-and-privacy"> <a href="#conclusion-balancing-security-and-privacy" title="Conclusion: Balancing Security and Privacy">Conclusion: Balancing Security and Privacy</a> <a href="#conclusion-balancing-security-and-privacy" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Conclusion: Balancing Security and Privacy">#</a> </h2> <p>The proliferation of Flock Safety ALPR cameras represents a fundamental shift in the <strong>surveillance capacity of both public and private entities</strong>. Proponents argue these systems enhance public safety by solving crimes and recovering stolen vehicles. <em>The <strong>privacy implications are profound and far-reaching</strong>, and that tradeoff deserves scrutiny.</em></p> <p>As of 2026, <strong>75,000+ cameras</strong> are scanning <strong>150+ million vehicles daily</strong>, creating searchable databases of Americans’ movements without warrant, probable cause, or individualized suspicion. This infrastructure enables:</p> <ul> <li>Tracking of law-abiding citizens</li> <li>Reconstruction of intimate details of private life</li> <li>Potential for discriminatory enforcement</li> <li>Chilling of free movement and association</li> </ul> <p><strong>Protection strategies</strong> range from policy advocacy to technical counter-surveillance. The <strong>open-source Flock-You detection project</strong> has democratized awareness of surveillance infrastructure, enabling individuals to understand when and where they’re being monitored.</p> <p>For technical details on detection devices and step-by-step setup instructions, read our companion guide: <strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Flock-You Detection Project: Counter-Surveillance Hardware and Setup Guide"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/"> <a href="/articles/flock-you-detection-project-counter-surveillance-hardware-guide-2026/" rel="follow me" >Flock-You Detection Project: Counter-Surveillance Hardware and Setup Guide</a> </span> </strong>.</p> <p><strong>The question isn’t whether technology can enable pervasive surveillance.</strong> <em>It clearly can. The question is whether a free society should permit such surveillance without robust safeguards, transparency, and accountability.</em> The answer will shape privacy rights for generations to come.</p> <hr> <h2 id="references"> <a href="#references" title="References">References</a> <a href="#references" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="References">#</a> </h2> <ol> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Flock Safety Official Website"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://www.flocksafety.com/"> <a href="https://www.flocksafety.com/" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Flock Safety Official Website</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Electronic Frontier Foundation - Automated License Plate Readers"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://www.eff.org/issues/automated-license-plate-readers"> <a href="https://www.eff.org/issues/automated-license-plate-readers" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Electronic Frontier Foundation - Automated License Plate Readers</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="ACLU - You Are Being Tracked"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://www.aclu.org/issues/privacy-technology/location-tracking/you-are-being-tracked"> <a href="https://www.aclu.org/issues/privacy-technology/location-tracking/you-are-being-tracked" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >ACLU - You Are Being Tracked</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Flock-You GitHub Repository by colonelpanichacks"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://github.com/colonelpanichacks/flock-you"> <a href="https://github.com/colonelpanichacks/flock-you" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Flock-You GitHub Repository by colonelpanichacks</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Colonel Panic Tech - OUI-SPY Detection Device"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://colonelpanic.tech"> <a href="https://colonelpanic.tech" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Colonel Panic Tech - OUI-SPY Detection Device</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="STS Collective - mesh-detect v2"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://stscollective.com"> <a href="https://stscollective.com" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >STS Collective - mesh-detect v2</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Carpenter v. United States, 585 U.S. ___ (2018)"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/585/16-402/"> <a href="https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/585/16-402/" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Carpenter v. United States, 585 U.S. ___ (2018)</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="NIST - Privacy and Civil Liberties Framework"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://www.nist.gov/"> <a href="https://www.nist.gov/" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >NIST - Privacy and Civil Liberties Framework</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="National Conference of State Legislatures - ALPR Policy"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://www.ncsl.org/"> <a href="https://www.ncsl.org/" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >National Conference of State Legislatures - ALPR Policy</a> </span> </li> <li>[DeFlockJoplin Community Research](https://defl ockjoplin.org/)</li> </ol> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.ch/

item.title: Flock-You Detection Project: Complete Counter-Surveillance Hardware and Setup Guide 2026
item.category: "Security Hardware" , "Counter Surveillance" , "Privacy Technology" , "Open Source Projects" , "ESP32 Development" , "WiFi Monitoring" , "Privacy Tools" , "Digital Rights" , "Hardware Hacking" , "Network Security"
item.description: Comprehensive technical guide to the open-source Flock-You project for detecting Flock Safety ALPR cameras using ESP32-based hardware. Includes setup instructions, firmware details, and purchasing options.
item.content:encoded: <p><strong>Complete Technical Guide to Building and Using Flock-You Detection Devices</strong></p> <h2 id="introduction-open-source-counter-surveillance"> <a href="#introduction-open-source-counter-surveillance" title="Introduction: Open Source Counter-Surveillance">Introduction: Open Source Counter-Surveillance</a> <a href="#introduction-open-source-counter-surveillance" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Introduction: Open Source Counter-Surveillance">#</a> </h2> <p>The <strong>Flock-You project</strong> is an <strong>open-source, community-driven initiative</strong> to detect and map Flock Safety’s ALPR surveillance infrastructure. Hosted on GitHub at <strong>colonelpanichacks/flock-you</strong>, this project uses affordable ESP32-based hardware to identify Flock cameras through their <strong>WiFi network signatures</strong>.</p> <p>This comprehensive guide covers everything from the <strong>technical methodology</strong> behind Flock detection to <strong>step-by-step setup instructions</strong> for three hardware platforms, <strong>firmware installation</strong>, and <strong>purchasing information from authorized vendors</strong>. Whether you’re a privacy advocate, security researcher, or concerned citizen, this guide will enable you to build or purchase your own detection device.</p> <p>For context on why this technology matters and the broader surveillance landscape, read our companion article: <strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Flock Safety Camera Surveillance: Prevalence, Privacy Concerns, and Protection Strategies"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/articles/flock-safety-camera-surveillance-prevalence-privacy-protection-2026/"> <a href="/articles/flock-safety-camera-surveillance-prevalence-privacy-protection-2026/" rel="follow me" >Flock Safety Camera Surveillance: Prevalence, Privacy Concerns, and Protection Strategies</a> </span> </strong>.</p> <hr> <h2 id="understanding-the-flock-you-detection-methodology"> <a href="#understanding-the-flock-you-detection-methodology" title="Understanding the Flock-You Detection Methodology">Understanding the Flock-You Detection Methodology</a> <a href="#understanding-the-flock-you-detection-methodology" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Understanding the Flock-You Detection Methodology">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="the-technical-foundation"> <a href="#the-technical-foundation" title="The Technical Foundation">The Technical Foundation</a> <a href="#the-technical-foundation" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Technical Foundation">#</a> </h3> <p>Flock Safety cameras contain <strong>embedded WiFi modules</strong> for connectivity and remote management. These modules broadcast identifiable network signatures detectable by devices operating in <strong>promiscuous WiFi monitoring mode</strong>. The Flock-You project exploits this characteristic through:</p> <h4 id="1-wifi-oui-organizationally-unique-identifier-detection"> <a href="#1-wifi-oui-organizationally-unique-identifier-detection" title="1. WiFi OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier) Detection">1. WiFi OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier) Detection</a> <a href="#1-wifi-oui-organizationally-unique-identifier-detection" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="1. WiFi OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier) Detection">#</a> </h4> <p>Every network interface has a <strong>MAC address</strong> consisting of:</p> <ul> <li><strong>First 3 bytes (24 bits)</strong>: OUI, which identifies the manufacturer</li> <li><strong>Last 3 bytes</strong>: Device-specific identifier</li> </ul> <p>Researchers <strong>@NitekryDPaul</strong> and the <strong>DeFlockJoplin</strong> community discovered <strong>31 specific OUIs</strong> consistently present in Flock Safety camera deployments:</p> <pre tabindex="0"><code>Primary Espressif OUIs (ESP32-based modules): D4:AD:FC - Espressif Inc. (Common ESP32-S3) AC:67:B2 - Espressif Inc. (ESP32-WROOM) 84:F3:EB - Espressif Inc. (ESP32-S3 variants) B4:E6:2D - Espressif Inc. (ESP32-C3) CC:DB:A7 - Espressif Inc. (ESP32-based) 24:0A:C4 - Espressif Inc. (ESP32-SOLO) 30:AE:A4 - Espressif Inc. (ESP32-WROVER) 94:B9:7E - Espressif Inc. (ESP32-based) A4:CF:12 - Espressif Inc. (ESP32-S2) C0:49:EF - Espressif Inc. (ESP32-C6) Additional OUIs identified in Flock deployments: [... 21 additional manufacturer OUIs ...] </code></pre><p>When a detection device scans WiFi traffic in promiscuous mode, <strong>it identifies any device broadcasting frames with these OUIs</strong>.</p> <h4 id="2-wildcard-probe-request-detection"> <a href="#2-wildcard-probe-request-detection" title="2. Wildcard Probe Request Detection">2. Wildcard Probe Request Detection</a> <a href="#2-wildcard-probe-request-detection" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="2. Wildcard Probe Request Detection">#</a> </h4> <p>Flock cameras periodically send <strong>wildcard probe requests</strong> searching for available networks. These have distinctive characteristics:</p> <ul> <li><strong>802.11 Management Frame</strong>: Type=0, Subtype=4</li> <li><strong>SSID Information Element</strong>: Length=0 (empty/wildcard)</li> <li><strong>Frame structure</strong>: Predictable pattern in probe timing</li> <li><strong>Vendor-specific IEs</strong>: Additional indicators in frame payload</li> </ul> <p>Detection firmware analyzes these <strong>probe request patterns</strong> to increase confidence in Flock camera identification beyond simple OUI matching.</p> <h4 id="3-promiscuous-mode-wifi-monitoring"> <a href="#3-promiscuous-mode-wifi-monitoring" title="3. Promiscuous Mode WiFi Monitoring">3. Promiscuous Mode WiFi Monitoring</a> <a href="#3-promiscuous-mode-wifi-monitoring" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="3. Promiscuous Mode WiFi Monitoring">#</a> </h4> <p>Standard WiFi operation only receives frames addressed to your device. <strong>Promiscuous mode</strong> captures all WiFi frames within range:</p> <ul> <li><strong>802.11 frame structure</strong>: Analyzing addr1, addr2, addr3 fields</li> <li><strong>Management frames</strong>: Probe requests, beacon frames, association requests</li> <li><strong>Data frames</strong>: Reveal network behavior patterns</li> <li><strong>Control frames</strong>: ACKs, RTSs, CTSs provide timing information</li> </ul> <p>ESP32 microcontrollers support promiscuous mode through the <strong>esp_wifi API</strong>, enabling low-cost detection hardware.</p> <h4 id="4-signal-strength-analysis"> <a href="#4-signal-strength-analysis" title="4. Signal Strength Analysis">4. Signal Strength Analysis</a> <a href="#4-signal-strength-analysis" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="4. Signal Strength Analysis">#</a> </h4> <p>Detection devices measure <strong>RSSI (Received Signal Strength Indicator)</strong> to:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Estimate distance</strong> to detected cameras</li> <li><strong>Triangulate locations</strong> with multiple measurements</li> <li><strong>Filter false positives</strong> based on expected signal characteristics</li> <li><strong>Create heat maps</strong> of camera density</li> </ul> <h3 id="detection-accuracy-and-false-positives"> <a href="#detection-accuracy-and-false-positives" title="Detection Accuracy and False Positives">Detection Accuracy and False Positives</a> <a href="#detection-accuracy-and-false-positives" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Detection Accuracy and False Positives">#</a> </h3> <p>The Flock-You methodology achieves high accuracy:</p> <ul> <li><strong>True Positive Rate</strong>: ~95% for confirmed Flock cameras in range</li> <li><strong>False Positive Rate</strong>: ~5-10% depending on environment</li> <li><strong>Detection Range</strong>: 50-300 feet depending on obstacles and antenna</li> <li><strong>Confidence Scoring</strong>: Multi-factor analysis reduces false alarms</li> </ul> <p><strong>Common False Positive Sources</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>ESP32 development boards</strong> used in other IoT devices</li> <li><strong>Commercial ESP32-based products</strong> (smart home, sensors)</li> <li><strong>Other surveillance cameras</strong> using similar components</li> <li><strong>WiFi testing equipment</strong> operated by technicians</li> </ul> <p><strong>Mitigation Strategies</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Multi-signature detection</strong>: Combining OUI + probe pattern + physical verification</li> <li><strong>Location correlation</strong>: Cross-referencing with known camera locations</li> <li><strong>Visual confirmation</strong>: Physical inspection after electronic detection</li> <li><strong>Community database</strong>: Crowdsourced validation of detections</li> </ul> <hr> <h2 id="hardware-platform-comparison"> <a href="#hardware-platform-comparison" title="Hardware Platform Comparison">Hardware Platform Comparison</a> <a href="#hardware-platform-comparison" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Hardware Platform Comparison">#</a> </h2> <p>Three primary platforms are available for Flock-You detection, each with distinct advantages:</p> <h3 id="platform-overview-table"> <a href="#platform-overview-table" title="Platform Overview Table">Platform Overview Table</a> <a href="#platform-overview-table" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Platform Overview Table">#</a> </h3> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Feature</th> <th>DIY ESP32</th> <th>M5 Atom Lite (Pre-Flashed)</th> <th>OUI-SPY</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td><strong>Manufacturer</strong></td> <td>DIY / Multiple vendors</td> <td>STS Collective</td> <td>Colonel Panic Tech</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Price</strong></td> <td>$5-12</td> <td>$39.99</td> <td>$85</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Processor</strong></td> <td>ESP32-WROOM</td> <td>ESP32-PICO</td> <td>ESP32-S3</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Ready-to-Use</strong></td> <td>No (DIY build)</td> <td>Yes (pre-flashed)</td> <td>Yes (multi-mode)</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Display</strong></td> <td>Optional</td> <td>RGB LED (5×5 matrix)</td> <td>None</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Battery</strong></td> <td>Optional</td> <td>External recommended</td> <td>None included</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>GPS</strong></td> <td>Optional</td> <td>No</td> <td>No</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Alerts</strong></td> <td>Buzzer + LED</td> <td>RGB LED (blue=detect)</td> <td>Integrated buzzer</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Data Logging</strong></td> <td>Optional</td> <td>No</td> <td>No</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Enclosure</strong></td> <td>3D print or none</td> <td>Compact plastic module</td> <td>None (bare PCB)</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Firmware</strong></td> <td>Flash manually</td> <td>Pre-loaded FlockYou</td> <td>Multi-mode (4 firmwares)</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Best For</strong></td> <td>DIY enthusiasts, learning</td> <td>Budget ready-to-go</td> <td>Multi-purpose detection</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Setup Difficulty</strong></td> <td>Moderate-Advanced</td> <td>Plug-and-play</td> <td>Plug-and-play</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Weight</strong></td> <td>20-50g (varies)</td> <td>18g (bare)</td> <td>~40g</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Dimensions</strong></td> <td>Varies</td> <td>24×24×14mm</td> <td>PCB board</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <h3 id="detailed-platform-analysis"> <a href="#detailed-platform-analysis" title="Detailed Platform Analysis">Detailed Platform Analysis</a> <a href="#detailed-platform-analysis" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Detailed Platform Analysis">#</a> </h3> <h4 id="1-diy-esp32-build-5-12"> <a href="#1-diy-esp32-build-5-12" title="1. DIY ESP32 Build ($5-12)">1. DIY ESP32 Build ($5-12)</a> <a href="#1-diy-esp32-build-5-12" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="1. DIY ESP32 Build ($5-12)">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Overview</strong>: Most affordable option using standard ESP32 development boards with open-source firmware.</p> <p><strong>Hardware Specifications</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Microcontroller</strong>: ESP32-WROOM-32 or similar (dual-core, 240MHz)</li> <li><strong>WiFi</strong>: 802.11 b/g/n, promiscuous mode capable</li> <li><strong>Memory</strong>: 520KB SRAM, 4MB+ Flash</li> <li><strong>Display</strong>: Optional (onboard LED sufficient)</li> <li><strong>Power</strong>: USB-powered or battery pack</li> <li><strong>Buzzer</strong>: Optional passive buzzer module (KY-006)</li> <li><strong>Indicators</strong>: Onboard LED + optional buzzer</li> <li><strong>Expandability</strong>: Breadboard-friendly, easy modifications</li> </ul> <p><strong>Firmware</strong>: Open-source fork at <strong>simeononsecurity/flock-you-esp32</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Modified for standard ESP32 hardware (GPIO 25, 2, 17)</li> <li>Super Mario Bros. startup tune (confirms buzzer working)</li> <li>Two fast ascending beeps on new detection</li> <li>10-second heartbeat beeps when tracking active</li> <li>Flask dashboard support for GPS wardriving</li> <li>Export to JSON, CSV, KML formats</li> </ul> <p><strong>Build Options</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>LED-Only ($5)</strong>: Bare ESP32 + USB cable, visual feedback only</li> <li><strong>Breadboard ($9-11)</strong>: Add passive buzzer + breadboard + jumpers, audio alerts</li> <li><strong>Enclosed ($10-12)</strong>: Add 3D printed case with snap-fit lid</li> </ul> <p><strong>Pros</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>✅ Cheapest option (85-95% cost savings vs OUI-SPY)</li> <li>✅ Completely open-source and modifiable</li> <li>✅ Uses widely available ESP32 boards</li> <li>✅ Educational, teaches embedded systems</li> <li>✅ Extensive documentation and guides</li> <li>✅ 3D printable case files available</li> <li>✅ <strong>Same detection accuracy as premium devices</strong></li> </ul> <p><strong>Cons</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>❌ Requires DIY assembly (solderless breadboard or 3D case)</li> <li>❌ Manual firmware flashing needed</li> <li>❌ No integrated battery (USB power or external pack)</li> <li>❌ Basic audio feedback only (no display)</li> <li>❌ Takes time to source components</li> </ul> <p><strong>Best For</strong>: Makers, students, privacy advocates on a budget, anyone wanting to learn how detection works, those who enjoy DIY projects.</p> <p><strong>Purchase Components</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Amazon</strong>: Search “ESP32 DevKit” or “ESP32 Breadboard Kit”</li> <li><strong>AliExpress/eBay</strong>: Bulk discounts available</li> <li><strong>Adafruit</strong>: Curated quality parts with tutorials</li> </ul> <p><strong>Setup Resources</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>GitHub Repo</strong>: <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="github.com/simeononsecurity/flock-you-esp32"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://github.com/simeononsecurity/flock-you-esp32"> <a href="https://github.com/simeononsecurity/flock-you-esp32" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >github.com/simeononsecurity/flock-you-esp32</a> </span> </li> <li><strong>Build Guide</strong>: Solderless assembly in 10-15 minutes</li> <li><strong>Case Files</strong>: OpenSCAD parametric design + STL files</li> </ul> <hr> <h4 id="2-m5-atom-lite-pre-flashed-by-sts-collective-3999"> <a href="#2-m5-atom-lite-pre-flashed-by-sts-collective-3999" title="2. M5 Atom Lite Pre-Flashed by STS Collective ($39.99)">2. M5 Atom Lite Pre-Flashed by STS Collective ($39.99)</a> <a href="#2-m5-atom-lite-pre-flashed-by-sts-collective-3999" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="2. M5 Atom Lite Pre-Flashed by STS Collective ($39.99)">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Overview</strong>: Pre-flashed compact detection device, ready to use out of the box.</p> <p><strong>Hardware Specifications</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Microcontroller</strong>: ESP32-PICO-D4 (dual-core, 240MHz)</li> <li><strong>WiFi</strong>: 802.11 b/g/n, promiscuous capable</li> <li><strong>Memory</strong>: 520KB SRAM, 4MB Flash</li> <li><strong>Display</strong>: 5×5 RGB LED matrix (WS2812C NeoPixel)</li> <li><strong>Power</strong>: 5V via USB-C or Grove connector</li> <li><strong>Battery</strong>: None included (external USB power bank recommended)</li> <li><strong>Indicator</strong>: Programmable RGB LED (blue=detection)</li> <li><strong>Buttons</strong>: 1 programmable button</li> <li><strong>I/O</strong>: Grove connector for expansion</li> <li><strong>Size</strong>: Ultra-compact 24×24×14mm</li> <li><strong>Enclosure</strong>: Durable plastic module</li> </ul> <p><strong>Firmware</strong>: Custom FlockYou port by STS Collective (proprietary):</p> <ul> <li>Pre-loaded and ready to use</li> <li>Blue LED alert on Flock camera detection</li> <li>Based on colonelpanichacks FlockYou research</li> <li>No setup or flashing required</li> <li>Simple plug-and-play operation</li> <li>Optional dashboard support</li> </ul> <p><strong>Pros</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>✅ Pre-flashed, no technical setup required</li> <li>✅ Affordable ready-to-go solution</li> <li>✅ Extremely compact and portable</li> <li>✅ Proven hardware platform</li> <li>✅ Simple blue LED = detection</li> <li>✅ USB-C powered (car, power bank, laptop)</li> <li>✅ Quality vendor support</li> <li>✅ Regular price $99.99, on sale $39.99</li> </ul> <p><strong>Cons</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>❌ No integrated battery (needs USB power)</li> <li>❌ Limited display (RGB LED only, no screen)</li> <li>❌ <em>Firmware is proprietary, not open-source for the moment</em></li> <li>❌ No data logging without computer connection</li> <li>❌ Single button limits functionality</li> </ul> <p><strong>Best For</strong>: Users wanting instant detection without DIY work, portability priority, those comfortable with simple LED feedback, budget-conscious buyers wanting ready-made solution.</p> <p><strong>Purchase</strong>: <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="stscollective.com/products/flockyou-m5-atom-lite-flock-camera-detector"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://stscollective.com/products/flockyou-m5-atom-lite-flock-camera-detector"> <a href="https://stscollective.com/products/flockyou-m5-atom-lite-flock-camera-detector" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >stscollective.com/products/flockyou-m5-atom-lite-flock-camera-detector</a> </span> </p> <hr> <h4 id="3-oui-spy-by-colonel-panic-tech-85"> <a href="#3-oui-spy-by-colonel-panic-tech-85" title="3. OUI-SPY by Colonel Panic Tech ($85)">3. OUI-SPY by Colonel Panic Tech ($85)</a> <a href="#3-oui-spy-by-colonel-panic-tech-85" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="3. OUI-SPY by Colonel Panic Tech ($85)">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Overview</strong>: Multi-mode surveillance detection board with four different firmware modes selectable via WiFi menu.</p> <p><strong>Hardware Specifications</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Microcontroller</strong>: ESP32-S3 dual-core Xtensa LX7, 8MB flash</li> <li><strong>WiFi</strong>: 802.11 b/g/n, promiscuous mode capable</li> <li><strong>Memory</strong>: 8MB Flash</li> <li><strong>Display</strong>: None (bare PCB with LED indicators)</li> <li><strong>Battery</strong>: None included</li> <li><strong>Charging</strong>: USB-C power & programming</li> <li><strong>Storage</strong>: None (detection-only modes)</li> <li><strong>Indicators</strong>: Integrated PWM buzzer with mode-specific tunes</li> <li><strong>Buttons</strong>: Boot button for mode switching</li> <li><strong>Antenna</strong>: <strong>Switchable</strong>, onboard 2.4GHz ceramic OR external via MMCX connector</li> <li><strong>Enclosure</strong>: None (bare PCB with PCB art)</li> <li><strong>Unique Feature</strong>: MAC randomization on every boot</li> </ul> <p><strong>Firmware</strong>: OUI-SPY Unified Blue with <strong>4 selectable modes</strong>:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Detector Mode</strong>: Multi-target BLE scanner with OUI filtering + web config portal</li> <li><strong>Foxhunter Mode</strong>: style="display:flex;"><span> {<span style="color:#ae81ff">0xAC</span>, <span style="color:#ae81ff">0x67</span>, <span style="color:#ae81ff">0xB2</span>}, <span style="color:#75715e">// Espressif ESP32-WROOM </span></span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span> {<span style="color:#ae81ff">0x84</span>, <span style="color:#ae81ff">0xF3</span>, <span style="color:#ae81ff">0xEB</span>}, <span
