item.title: How to Flash Rayhunter Devices: Complete Installation and Configuration Guide for IMSI Catcher Detection
item.category: "Rayhunter" , "IMSI catcher" , "cell site simulator" , "mobile security" , "surveillance detection" , "privacy protection" , "Orbic RC400L" , "TP-Link M7350" , "mobile hotspot" , "device flashing" , "cybersecurity" , "wireless security" , "surveillance countermeasures" , "EFF" , "privacy tools" , "Rayhunter for sale" , "installation guide" , "configuration" , "supported devices" , "network security"
item.description: Comprehensive guide on how to flash and configure Rayhunter devices for IMSI catcher detection. Learn installation procedures, supported devices, and where to find Rayhunter for sale.
item.content:encoded: <p><strong>Complete Guide to Flashing and Configuring Rayhunter - The Ultimate IMSI Catcher Detection System</strong></p> <h2 id="tldr"> <a href="#tldr" title="TL;DR">TL;DR</a> <a href="#tldr" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="TL;DR">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> is an open-source IMSI catcher detection system that runs on modified mobile hotspots to alert users when surveillance equipment attempts to intercept cellular communications. This guide covers complete installation for Orbic RC400L and TP-Link M7350 devices, configuration options, threat actor analysis, and effectiveness in 5G networks. Key points: requires compatible Qualcomm-based device, detects 2G/3G/4G surveillance through multiple heuristics, remains effective despite 5G adoption due to continued downgrade attacks, and provides essential protection for journalists, activists, and privacy-conscious individuals against government, criminal, and corporate surveillance.</p> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> is an open-source tool designed to detect IMSI catchers (cell site simulators) that can intercept mobile communications. If you’re looking for <strong>Rayhunter for sale</strong> or want to learn how to properly flash and configure these devices, this comprehensive guide covers everything you need to know about installation, configuration, and usage of <strong>Rayhunter</strong> systems.</p> <p><strong>Sponsorship Disclosure</strong>: This article is sponsored by <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="STS Collective"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://stscollective.com"> <a href="https://stscollective.com" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >STS Collective</a> </span> , the main provider of Rayhunter-compatible devices. Despite this sponsorship, all technical information, analysis, and recommendations in this guide remain completely unbiased and based solely on the official Rayhunter documentation, community feedback, and objective technical assessment.</p> <h2 id="introduction-to-rayhunter"> <a href="#introduction-to-rayhunter" title="Introduction to Rayhunter">Introduction to Rayhunter</a> <a href="#introduction-to-rayhunter" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Introduction to Rayhunter">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> is an advanced IMSI catcher detection system developed by the <strong>Electronic Frontier Foundation (EFF)</strong>, a leading nonprofit organization dedicated to defending civil liberties in the digital world. The EFF has been at the forefront of privacy rights advocacy since 1990, fighting for digital privacy, free expression, and innovation through impact litigation, policy analysis, grassroots activism, and technology development.</p> <p>This powerful tool runs on compatible mobile hotspot devices and continuously monitors cellular networks for suspicious activity that might indicate the presence of cell site simulators or “Stingrays” used for surveillance. <strong>Rayhunter</strong> represents the EFF’s commitment to providing practical privacy protection tools to individuals and organizations who need protection from surveillance.</p> <p>The <strong>Rayhunter</strong> system provides real-time alerts when potentially malicious cellular behavior is detected, making it an essential privacy protection tool for journalists, activists, security professionals, and privacy-conscious individuals. When you’re ready to purchase compatible devices, you can find <strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Rayhunter for sale"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://stscollective.com/collections/rayhunter"> <a href="https://stscollective.com/collections/rayhunter" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Rayhunter for sale</a> </span> </strong> through STS Collective, which serves as the main provider of these specialized devices.</p> <h3 id="supporting-the-rayhunter-project"> <a href="#supporting-the-rayhunter-project" title="Supporting the Rayhunter Project">Supporting the Rayhunter Project</a> <a href="#supporting-the-rayhunter-project" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Supporting the Rayhunter Project">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> is an open-source project that benefits from community support. You can contribute to the project’s continued development in several ways:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Purchase through STS Collective</strong>: STS Collective donates a portion of profits from every Rayhunter device sale back to the EFF to support continued <strong>Rayhunter</strong> development and privacy research</li> <li><strong>Direct donations</strong>: Support the EFF’s broader privacy advocacy and <strong>Rayhunter</strong> development directly at <strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="https://supporters.eff.org/donate/donate"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://supporters.eff.org/donate/donate"> <a href="https://supporters.eff.org/donate/donate" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >https://supporters.eff.org/donate/donate</a> </span> </strong></li> <li><strong>Community participation</strong>: Contribute to discussions, documentation, and testing through the official GitHub repository</li> </ul> <p>Your support helps ensure <strong>Rayhunter</strong> remains free, open-source, and continuously improved to address evolving surveillance threats.</p> <h2 id="support-feedback-and-community"> <a href="#support-feedback-and-community" title="Support, Feedback, and Community">Support, Feedback, and Community</a> <a href="#support-feedback-and-community" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Support, Feedback, and Community">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> is supported by an active community of security researchers, privacy advocates, and concerned citizens. You can find support and contribute to the project through various channels:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Official Documentation</strong>: <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Rayhunter Documentation"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://efforg.github.io/rayhunter/introduction.html"> <a href="https://efforg.github.io/rayhunter/introduction.html" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Rayhunter Documentation</a> </span> - Complete installation, configuration, and usage guides</li> <li><strong>GitHub Repository</strong>: <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="EFForg/rayhunter"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://github.com/EFForg/rayhunter"> <a href="https://github.com/EFForg/rayhunter" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >EFForg/rayhunter</a> </span> </li> <li><strong>Community Discussions</strong>: Open discussions on GitHub for device compatibility and troubleshooting</li> <li><strong>Security Research</strong>: Collaborate with researchers to improve <strong>Rayhunter</strong> detection capabilities</li> </ul> <p>If you’re looking for compatible devices, <strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Rayhunter for sale"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://stscollective.com/collections/rayhunter"> <a href="https://stscollective.com/collections/rayhunter" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Rayhunter for sale</a> </span> </strong> options are available through STS Collective.</p> <h2 id="frequently-asked-questions"> <a href="#frequently-asked-questions" title="Frequently Asked Questions">Frequently Asked Questions</a> <a href="#frequently-asked-questions" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Frequently Asked Questions">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="do-i-need-an-active-sim-card-to-use-rayhunter"> <a href="#do-i-need-an-active-sim-card-to-use-rayhunter" title="Do I need an active SIM card to use Rayhunter?">Do I need an active SIM card to use Rayhunter?</a> <a href="#do-i-need-an-active-sim-card-to-use-rayhunter" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Do I need an active SIM card to use Rayhunter?">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> requires a SIM card to be inserted into the device, but it doesn’t need to have an active service plan. The SIM card is necessary for the device to connect to cellular networks and monitor for suspicious activity. If you want to use the device as a hotspot while running <strong>Rayhunter</strong>, then an active plan would be required.</p> <h3 id="how-can-i-test-that-my-rayhunter-device-is-working"> <a href="#how-can-i-test-that-my-rayhunter-device-is-working" title="How can I test that my Rayhunter device is working?">How can I test that my Rayhunter device is working?</a> <a href="#how-can-i-test-that-my-rayhunter-device-is-working" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="How can I test that my Rayhunter device is working?">#</a> </h3> <p>You can enable the Test Heuristic in the <strong>Rayhunter</strong> configuration settings. This will trigger alerts every time your device detects a cell tower, helping you verify that the system is functioning properly. This test mode is noisy, so disable it after testing.</p> <h3 id="should-i-get-a-locked-or-unlocked-device"> <a href="#should-i-get-a-locked-or-unlocked-device" title="Should I get a locked or unlocked device?">Should I get a locked or unlocked device?</a> <a href="#should-i-get-a-locked-or-unlocked-device" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Should I get a locked or unlocked device?">#</a> </h3> <p>For <strong>Rayhunter</strong> compatibility, unlocked devices are generally preferred, especially if you plan to use non-Verizon SIM cards. Most Verizon-branded Orbic devices are actually unlocked, but verify compatibility before purchase. You can find verified compatible devices <strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Rayhunter for sale"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://stscollective.com/collections/rayhunter"> <a href="https://stscollective.com/collections/rayhunter" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Rayhunter for sale</a> </span> </strong> from STS Collective.</p> <h2 id="installation-guide"> <a href="#installation-guide" title="Installation Guide">Installation Guide</a> <a href="#installation-guide" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Installation Guide">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="installing-from-the-latest-release"> <a href="#installing-from-the-latest-release" title="Installing from the Latest Release">Installing from the Latest Release</a> <a href="#installing-from-the-latest-release" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Installing from the Latest Release">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> installation has been tested on macOS and Ubuntu 24.04. If you encounter issues with pre-built releases, you may need to install from source.</p> <h4 id="prerequisites"> <a href="#prerequisites" title="Prerequisites">Prerequisites</a> <a href="#prerequisites" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Prerequisites">#</a> </h4> <p>Before installing <strong>Rayhunter</strong>, ensure you have a compatible device. For TP-Link devices, insert a FAT-formatted SD card for storing recordings.</p> <h4 id="download-and-setup"> <a href="#download-and-setup" title="Download and Setup">Download and Setup</a> <a href="#download-and-setup" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Download and Setup">#</a> </h4> <ol> <li> <p><strong>Download the latest release</strong>: Get the appropriate <code>rayhunter-vX.X.X-PLATFORM.zip</code> from the official releases page:</p> <ul> <li>Linux x64: <code>linux-x64</code></li> <li>Linux ARM64: <code>linux-aarch64</code></li> <li>Linux ARM v7/v8: <code>linux-armv7</code></li> <li>macOS Intel: <code>macos-intel</code></li> <li>macOS ARM (M1/M2): <code>macos-arm</code></li> <li>Windows: <code>windows-x86_64</code></li> </ul> </li> <li> <p><strong>Extract and navigate to the folder</strong>:</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span>unzip ~/Downloads/rayhunter-vX.X.X-PLATFORM.zip </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>cd ~/Downloads/rayhunter-vX.X.X-PLATFORM </span></span></code></pre></div></li> <li> <p><strong>Connect to your device</strong>: Turn on your device and connect via WiFi or USB tethering. You should be able to access:</p> <ul> <li>Orbic devices: <code>http://192.168.1.1</code></li> <li>TP-Link devices: <code>http://192.168.0.1</code></li> </ul> </li> </ol> <h4 id="device-specific-installation"> <a href="#device-specific-installation" title="Device-Specific Installation">Device-Specific Installation</a> <a href="#device-specific-installation" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Device-Specific Installation">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>For Orbic RC400L devices</strong> (available <strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Rayhunter for sale"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://stscollective.com"> <a href="https://stscollective.com" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Rayhunter for sale</a> </span> </strong>):</p> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> supports two installation methods for Orbic RC400L devices. The WiFi method is recommended for most users, while the USB method provides additional debugging capabilities.</p> <p><strong>WiFi Installation Method (Recommended)</strong>:</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># macOS users need to run this first:</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>xattr -d com.apple.quarantine installer </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># For Verizon RC400L (admin password is same as WiFi password):</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>./installer orbic --admin-password <span style="color:#e6db74">'YourWiFiPassword'</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># For Kajeet/Smartspot RC400L:</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>./installer orbic --admin-password<span style="color:#f92672">=</span><span style="color:#e6db74">'$m@rt$p0tc0nf!g'</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># For Moxee devices (password format: 12$ + last 3 digits of WiFi password):</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>./installer orbic --admin-password<span style="color:#f92672">=</span><span style="color:#e6db74">'12$XXX'</span> </span></span></code></pre></div><p><strong>USB Installation Method (Advanced Users)</strong>: The USB method enables ADB (Android Debug Bridge) access for advanced debugging but isn’t recommended for typical installations:</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># WARNING: USB installer is not recommended for most use cases</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>./installer orbic-usb </span></span></code></pre></div><p>This method forces the device into debug mode to enable ADB access, installs the rootshell and <strong>Rayhunter</strong>, then reboots the device. Use this method only if you need ADB access for other purposes.</p> <p><strong>For TP-Link M7350 devices</strong> (available <strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Rayhunter for sale"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://stscollective.com"> <a href="https://stscollective.com" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Rayhunter for sale</a> </span> </strong>):</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span>./installer tplink </span></span></code></pre></div><p>The installer will complete the <strong>Rayhunter</strong> flashing process and reboot the device. You’ll see a green line on the device display indicating <strong>Rayhunter</strong> is running successfully.</p> <h3 id="installing-from-source"> <a href="#installing-from-source" title="Installing from Source">Installing from Source</a> <a href="#installing-from-source" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Installing from Source">#</a> </h3> <p>For developers or users who need to build <strong>Rayhunter</strong> from source, the project includes:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Frontend</strong>: JavaScript SvelteKit application (<code>./daemon/web/</code>)</li> <li><strong>Backend</strong>: Rust binary <code>rayhunter-daemon</code> (<code>./daemon/</code>)</li> <li><strong>Installer</strong>: Rust binary that bundles everything (<code>./installer</code>)</li> </ul> <h4 id="build-dependencies"> <a href="#build-dependencies" title="Build Dependencies">Build Dependencies</a> <a href="#build-dependencies" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Build Dependencies">#</a> </h4> <p>Install the following dependencies:</p> <ul> <li>Rust programming language</li> <li>Node.js/npm</li> <li>C compiler tools (<code>build-essential</code> on Linux, <code>xcode-select --install</code> on macOS)</li> </ul> <h4 id="build-process"> <a href="#build-process" title="Build Process">Build Process</a> <a href="#build-process" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Build Process">#</a> </h4> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span>./scripts/build-dev.sh </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>./scripts/install-dev.sh orbic <span style="color:#75715e"># Replace 'orbic' with your device type</span> </span></span></code></pre></div><p>For frontend development with hot-reloading:</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span>cd daemon/web </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>npm run dev </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Or with custom target:</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>API_TARGET<span style="color:#f92672">=</span>http://192.168.1.1:8080 npm run dev </span></span></code></pre></div><h3 id="updating-rayhunter"> <a href="#updating-rayhunter" title="Updating Rayhunter">Updating Rayhunter</a> <a href="#updating-rayhunter" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Updating Rayhunter">#</a> </h3> <p>Updating <strong>Rayhunter</strong> is identical to the installation process. Simply repeat the installation steps with the latest release to update your device to the newest <strong>Rayhunter</strong> version.</p> <h2 id="configuration"> <a href="#configuration" title="Configuration">Configuration</a> <a href="#configuration" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Configuration">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> can be configured through the web interface or by editing <code>/data/rayhunter/config.toml</code> directly on the device.</p> <h3 id="web-interface-configuration"> <a href="#web-interface-configuration" title="Web Interface Configuration">Web Interface Configuration</a> <a href="#web-interface-configuration" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Web Interface Configuration">#</a> </h3> <p>Access the <strong>Rayhunter</strong> web interface by connecting to your device’s network and visiting:</p> <ul> <li>Orbic: <code>http://192.168.1.1:8080</code></li> <li>TP-Link: <code>http://192.168.0.1:8080</code></li> </ul> <script type="application/ld+json"> { "@context": "https://schema.org/", "@type": "ImageObject", "contentUrl": "https:\/\/simeononsecurity.ch\/", "caption": "Rayhunter Web Interface Dashboard", "license": "https://simeononsecurity.com/termsandconditions/", "acquireLicensePage": "https://simeononsecurity.com/contactus/", "creditText": "SimeonOnSecurity", "creator": { "@type": "Person", "name": "SimeonOnSecurity" }, "copyrightNotice": "SimeonOnSecurity", "associatedArticle": { "@type": "NewsArticle", "headline": "simeononsecurity", "url": "https:\/\/simeononsecurity.ch\/", "datePublished": "2026-06-29 14:39:30.505617091 \u002b0000 UTC m=\u002b119.730343294", "author": { "@type": "Person", "name": "SimeonOnSecurity", "url": "https:\/\/simeononsecurity.ch\/authors\/simeononsecurity" } } } </script> <center> <figure align="center" style="text-align: center; margin: 0 auto;" itemscope itemtype="http://schema.org/ImageObject"> <meta property="article:published_time" content="2026-06-29 14:39:30.505617091 +0000 UTC m=+119.730343294"> <meta property="article:author" content="SimeonOnSecurity"> <meta property="article:author:url" content="https://simeononsecurity.ch/authors/simeononsecurity"> <meta property="og:license" content="https://simeononsecurity.com/termsandconditions/"> <meta property="og:see_also" content="https://simeononsecurity.com/contactus/"> <meta property="og:copyright" content="SimeonOnSecurity"> <img align="center" style="text-align: center; margin: 0 auto; max-width: 80%; max-height: 600px; min-width: 100px; min-height: 100px; height: auto; width: auto;" src="https://simeononsecurity.ch/rayhunter-dashboard.webp" alt="Rayhunter Web Interface Dashboard"loading="lazy" fetchpriority="low" itemprop="contentUrl" /> <figcaption align="center" style="text-align: center; margin: 0 auto;" itemprop="caption description"> <p>The Rayhunter web interface provides comprehensive monitoring and configuration options</p> </figcaption> </figure> </center> <h4 id="key-configuration-options"> <a href="#key-configuration-options" title="Key Configuration Options">Key Configuration Options</a> <a href="#key-configuration-options" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Key Configuration Options">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Device UI Level</strong>: Controls what <strong>Rayhunter</strong> displays on the device screen:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Invisible mode</strong>: No display output</li> <li><strong>Subtle mode</strong>: Colored line indicator (green=safe, red=alert, white=paused)</li> <li><strong>Demo mode</strong>: Shows orca graphics with status line</li> <li><strong>EFF logo</strong>: Displays EFF logo with status line</li> <li><strong>High visibility</strong>: Full-screen color display</li> </ul> <p><strong>Device Input Mode</strong>: Configure power button behavior:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Disable button control</strong>: Power button disabled for <strong>Rayhunter</strong></li> <li><strong>Double-tap to restart recording</strong>: Reset alerts and restart monitoring</li> </ul> <p><strong>Notification Settings</strong>: Configure ntfy URL for remote alerts and enable/disable notification types for warnings and low battery alerts.</p> <p><strong>Analyzer Heuristics</strong>: Enable or disable specific <strong>Rayhunter</strong> detection algorithms based on your environment and threat model.</p> <h2 id="uninstalling-rayhunter"> <a href="#uninstalling-rayhunter" title="Uninstalling Rayhunter">Uninstalling Rayhunter</a> <a href="#uninstalling-rayhunter" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Uninstalling Rayhunter">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="orbic-devices"> <a href="#orbic-devices" title="Orbic Devices">Orbic Devices</a> <a href="#orbic-devices" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Orbic Devices">#</a> </h3> <p>To remove <strong>Rayhunter</strong> from Orbic devices:</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span>./installer util orbic-shell --admin-password mypassword </span></span></code></pre></div><p>Inside the shell:</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span>echo <span style="color:#ae81ff">3</span> > /usrdata/mode.cfg </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>rm -rf /data/rayhunter /etc/init.d/rayhunter_daemon /bin/rootshell </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>reboot </span></span></code></pre></div><h3 id="tp-link-devices"> <a href="#tp-link-devices" title="TP-Link Devices">TP-Link Devices</a> <a href="#tp-link-devices" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="TP-Link Devices">#</a> </h3> <p>For TP-Link device uninstallation:</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span>./installer util tplink-shell </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>rm /data/rayhunter /etc/init.d/rayhunter_daemon </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>update-rc.d rayhunter_daemon remove </span></span></code></pre></div><p>Remove any leftover port triggers in the TP-Link admin interface under Settings > NAT Settings > Port Triggers.</p> <h2 id="using-rayhunter"> <a href="#using-rayhunter" title="Using Rayhunter">Using Rayhunter</a> <a href="#using-rayhunter" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Using Rayhunter">#</a> </h2> <p>Once installed, <strong>Rayhunter</strong> runs automatically and continuously monitors cellular networks for suspicious activity. The device display shows a green line during normal operation, which changes to yellow dots, orange dashes, or red solid when potential IMSI catchers are detected.</p> <h3 id="rayhunters-detection-heuristics"> <a href="#rayhunters-detection-heuristics" title="Rayhunter’s Detection Heuristics">Rayhunter’s Detection Heuristics</a> <a href="#rayhunters-detection-heuristics" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Rayhunter’s Detection Heuristics">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> employs multiple sophisticated detection algorithms to identify potential IMSI catcher activity:</p> <h4 id="imsi-requested-detection"> <a href="#imsi-requested-detection" title="IMSI Requested Detection">IMSI Requested Detection</a> <a href="#imsi-requested-detection" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="IMSI Requested Detection">#</a> </h4> <p>This heuristic identifies suspicious sequences where cellular towers request device identity (IMSI - International Mobile Subscriber Identity) without following proper authentication protocols. <strong>Rayhunter</strong> monitors the timing and context of these requests, flagging abnormal patterns that indicate potential surveillance equipment.</p> <p>Legitimate cellular networks follow standardized authentication procedures before requesting sensitive identifiers. IMSI catchers often bypass these protocols to quickly harvest device identities, creating detectable anomalies in the authentication sequence. This detection method is particularly effective against basic “passive” IMSI catchers that simply collect device information.</p> <h4 id="connection-release2g-downgrade-detection"> <a href="#connection-release2g-downgrade-detection" title="Connection Release/2G Downgrade Detection">Connection Release/2G Downgrade Detection</a> <a href="#connection-release2g-downgrade-detection" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Connection Release/2G Downgrade Detection">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> continuously monitors for suspicious attempts to force mobile devices to disconnect from secure 4G/5G networks and reconnect to less secure 2G networks. This downgrade attack is a common tactic used by IMSI catchers because 2G networks have weaker encryption and are easier to intercept.</p> <p>The system analyzes patterns in connection releases, looking for abnormal frequency, timing, or contextual indicators that suggest malicious intent rather than normal network optimization. Legitimate networks may occasionally suggest downgrades for coverage or capacity reasons, but <strong>Rayhunter</strong> can distinguish between normal network behavior and potential surveillance activities.</p> <h4 id="lte-sib67-downgrade-detection"> <a href="#lte-sib67-downgrade-detection" title="LTE SIB6/7 Downgrade Detection">LTE SIB6/7 Downgrade Detection</a> <a href="#lte-sib67-downgrade-detection" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="LTE SIB6/7 Downgrade Detection">#</a> </h4> <p>System Information Blocks (SIB) are broadcast messages that cellular networks use to provide configuration information to mobile devices. <strong>Rayhunter</strong> specifically monitors SIB6 and SIB7 messages, which contain information about neighboring cells and frequency redirection.</p> <p>Malicious actors can broadcast fake SIB6/7 messages to force devices to abandon secure 4G connections and connect to attacker-controlled 2G networks. <strong>Rayhunter</strong> analyzes these broadcasts for inconsistencies, abnormal redirection patterns, and other indicators that suggest the presence of rogue base stations attempting to downgrade device connections for interception purposes.</p> <h4 id="null-cipher-detection"> <a href="#null-cipher-detection" title="Null Cipher Detection">Null Cipher Detection</a> <a href="#null-cipher-detection" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Null Cipher Detection">#</a> </h4> <p>This critical detection method identifies when cellular networks suggest using no encryption (null cipher) for communications. Legitimate commercial cellular networks should never propose unencrypted connections, as this would violate security standards and regulatory requirements.</p> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> flags any network that suggests null cipher usage as highly suspicious, <em>A legitimate network will never propose null cipher.</em> This is a clear indicator of surveillance equipment attempting to intercept communications in plaintext. This heuristic is particularly effective against IMSI catchers that prioritize data collection over maintaining the appearance of legitimate network operations.</p> <h4 id="incomplete-sib-detection"> <a href="#incomplete-sib-detection" title="Incomplete SIB Detection">Incomplete SIB Detection</a> <a href="#incomplete-sib-detection" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Incomplete SIB Detection">#</a> </h4> <p>Legitimate cellular base stations broadcast complete System Information Blocks containing essential network configuration data. <strong>Rayhunter</strong> monitors for base stations that provide incomplete or missing system information, which often indicates hastily deployed or improperly configured surveillance equipment.</p> <p>IMSI catchers frequently fail to implement complete cellular network functionality, focusing only on the minimum features required for device connection and data interception. By detecting these incomplete implementations, <strong>Rayhunter</strong> can identify potentially malicious base stations that lack the full feature set expected from legitimate cellular infrastructure.</p> <h2 id="threat-actors-and-potential-targets"> <a href="#threat-actors-and-potential-targets" title="Threat Actors and Potential Targets">Threat Actors and Potential Targets</a> <a href="#threat-actors-and-potential-targets" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Threat Actors and Potential Targets">#</a> </h2> <p>Understanding who deploys IMSI catchers and who they target is crucial for assessing your personal threat model and determining appropriate <strong>Rayhunter</strong> configurations. Different threat actors use varying levels of sophistication, and their targeting strategies directly inform the types of surveillance activities <strong>Rayhunter</strong> is designed to detect.</p> <h3 id="government-and-law-enforcement-agencies"> <a href="#government-and-law-enforcement-agencies" title="Government and Law Enforcement Agencies">Government and Law Enforcement Agencies</a> <a href="#government-and-law-enforcement-agencies" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Government and Law Enforcement Agencies">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Threat Sophistication</strong>: Advanced to Professional Grade <strong>Primary Targets</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Criminal suspects under investigation</li> <li>Persons of interest in national security cases</li> <li>Protesters and activists during demonstrations</li> <li>General population surveillance in high-security areas</li> <li>Foreign nationals and diplomatic personnel</li> </ul> <p><strong>Detection Patterns</strong>: Government-operated IMSI catchers often use sophisticated equipment that may avoid triggering basic detection heuristics. However, they frequently employ <strong>2G downgrade attacks</strong> and <strong>connection release patterns</strong> to force devices onto less secure networks for easier interception. <strong>Rayhunter’s</strong> LTE SIB6/7 downgrade detection and connection release monitoring are particularly effective against law enforcement surveillance techniques.</p> <p><strong>Typical Scenarios</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Event-based surveillance at protests, rallies, or public gatherings</li> <li>Location-based monitoring near government facilities or sensitive infrastructure</li> <li>Targeted surveillance of specific individuals under investigation</li> <li>Border and airport surveillance operations</li> </ul> <h3 id="intelligence-agencies-domestic-and-foreign"> <a href="#intelligence-agencies-domestic-and-foreign" title="Intelligence Agencies (Domestic and Foreign)">Intelligence Agencies (Domestic and Foreign)</a> <a href="#intelligence-agencies-domestic-and-foreign" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Intelligence Agencies (Domestic and Foreign)">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Threat Sophistication</strong>: Professional to Military Grade <strong>Primary Targets</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Foreign intelligence operatives and assets</li> <li>Government officials and diplomatic personnel</li> <li>Defense contractors and researchers</li> <li>Corporate executives in strategic industries</li> <li>Journalists covering national security topics</li> <li>Activists working on sensitive political issues</li> </ul> <p><strong>Detection Patterns</strong>: Intelligence-grade equipment is designed to be stealthy, but <strong>Rayhunter’s</strong> null cipher detection and incomplete SIB monitoring can identify even sophisticated surveillance operations. Intelligence agencies often use equipment that requests <strong>IMSI information</strong> during targeted operations, triggering <strong>Rayhunter’s</strong> IMSI requested detection algorithms.</p> <p><strong>Typical Scenarios</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Long-term surveillance of high-value intelligence targets</li> <li>Industrial espionage operations near corporate headquarters</li> <li>Diplomatic and embassy monitoring</li> <li>Counter-intelligence operations</li> <li>Foreign intelligence services targeting domestic assets</li> </ul> <h3 id="criminal-organizations"> <a href="#criminal-organizations" title="Criminal Organizations">Criminal Organizations</a> <a href="#criminal-organizations" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Criminal Organizations">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Threat Sophistication</strong>: Basic to Intermediate <strong>Primary Targets</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Wealthy individuals for kidnapping or extortion</li> <li>Business competitors for corporate intelligence</li> <li>Law enforcement personnel investigating organized crime</li> <li>Witnesses and informants in criminal cases</li> <li>Victims of stalking, harassment, or domestic abuse</li> </ul> <p><strong>Detection Patterns</strong>: Criminal organizations typically use lower-cost, commercially available IMSI catchers that trigger multiple <strong>Rayhunter</strong> heuristics simultaneously. These devices often exhibit <strong>incomplete SIB implementations</strong>, use <strong>null ciphers</strong> to maximize data collection, and employ aggressive <strong>2G downgrade attacks</strong> due to limited technical sophistication.</p> <p><strong>Typical Scenarios</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Tracking potential kidnapping or robbery targets</li> <li>Monitoring law enforcement communications during criminal operations</li> <li>Corporate espionage and competitive intelligence gathering</li> <li>Stalking and harassment campaigns</li> <li>Witness intimidation and location tracking</li> </ul> <h3 id="corporate-espionage-and-private-intelligence"> <a href="#corporate-espionage-and-private-intelligence" title="Corporate Espionage and Private Intelligence">Corporate Espionage and Private Intelligence</a> <a href="#corporate-espionage-and-private-intelligence" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Corporate Espionage and Private Intelligence">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Threat Sophistication</strong>: Intermediate to Advanced <strong>Primary Targets</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Executive leadership of competing companies</li> <li>Research and development teams</li> <li>Merger and acquisition negotiators</li> <li>Intellectual property holders</li> <li>Trade secret custodians</li> <li>Corporate whistleblowers and insider threat sources</li> </ul> <p><strong>Detection Patterns</strong>: Corporate surveillance operations often use mid-tier equipment that balances cost with capability. These deployments frequently trigger <strong>connection release detection</strong> and <strong>IMSI requested alerts</strong> when targeting specific individuals during business negotiations or product development cycles.</p> <p><strong>Typical Scenarios</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Surveillance during merger and acquisition negotiations</li> <li>Competitive intelligence gathering at trade shows and conferences</li> <li>Intellectual property theft operations</li> <li>Employee monitoring and insider threat detection</li> <li>Customer data harvesting for competitive advantage</li> </ul> <h3 id="private-investigators-and-surveillance-firms"> <a href="#private-investigators-and-surveillance-firms" title="Private Investigators and Surveillance Firms">Private Investigators and Surveillance Firms</a> <a href="#private-investigators-and-surveillance-firms" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Private Investigators and Surveillance Firms">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Threat Sophistication</strong>: Basic to Intermediate <strong>Primary Targets</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Individuals under investigation for insurance fraud</li> <li>Spouses in divorce proceedings</li> <li>Employees suspected of misconduct</li> <li>Individuals involved in legal disputes</li> <li>Celebrity and high-profile targets</li> </ul> <p><strong>Detection Patterns</strong>: Private surveillance operations typically use commercially available equipment with limited customization. These systems often trigger <strong>Rayhunter’s</strong> basic detection heuristics, particularly <strong>null cipher detection</strong> and <strong>incomplete SIB monitoring</strong>, due to cost constraints and technical limitations.</p> <p><strong>Typical Scenarios</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Divorce and custody investigations</li> <li>Insurance fraud surveillance</li> <li>Employee misconduct investigations</li> <li>Celebrity stalking and paparazzi operations</li> <li>Legal case investigation and evidence gathering</li> </ul> <h3 id="malicious-individuals-and-hacktivists"> <a href="#malicious-individuals-and-hacktivists" title="Malicious Individuals and Hacktivists">Malicious Individuals and Hacktivists</a> <a href="#malicious-individuals-and-hacktivists" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Malicious Individuals and Hacktivists">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Threat Sophistication</strong>: Basic to Intermediate <strong>Primary Targets</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Personal enemies or romantic interests</li> <li>Public figures and celebrities</li> <li>Random victims in public spaces</li> <li>Participants at specific events or locations</li> <li>Members of targeted communities or groups</li> </ul> <p><strong>Detection Patterns</strong>: Individual actors typically use basic, often improvised IMSI catcher setups that trigger multiple <strong>Rayhunter</strong> detection algorithms simultaneously. These deployments commonly exhibit <strong>null cipher usage</strong>, <strong>incomplete SIB broadcasts</strong>, and crude <strong>2G downgrade attempts</strong>.</p> <p><strong>Typical Scenarios</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>Stalking and harassment campaigns</li> <li>Identity theft and financial fraud operations</li> <li>Political activism and protest disruption</li> <li>General cybercriminal activities</li> <li>Opportunistic surveillance at public events</li> </ul> <h3 id="threat-assessment-and-rayhunter-configuration"> <a href="#threat-assessment-and-rayhunter-configuration" title="Threat Assessment and Rayhunter Configuration">Threat Assessment and Rayhunter Configuration</a> <a href="#threat-assessment-and-rayhunter-configuration" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Threat Assessment and Rayhunter Configuration">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>High-Risk Individuals</strong> (journalists, activists, government officials, executives) should enable all <strong>Rayhunter</strong> heuristics and use <strong>high visibility</strong> display modes for maximum detection capability.</p> <p><strong>Moderate-Risk Individuals</strong> (general privacy-conscious users) can use <strong>subtle mode</strong> displays with core detection heuristics enabled for daily protection without attracting attention.</p> <p><strong>Event-Based Protection</strong> (protests, sensitive meetings, travel) warrants temporary activation of all detection methods and notification systems for comprehensive surveillance awareness.</p> <p>Understanding your threat model helps optimize <strong>Rayhunter</strong> configurations for your specific risk profile while ensuring appropriate detection coverage for the threat actors most likely to target you or your activities.</p> <h2 id="rayhunter-effectiveness-in-the-5g-era"> <a href="#rayhunter-effectiveness-in-the-5g-era" title="Rayhunter Effectiveness in the 5G Era">Rayhunter Effectiveness in the 5G Era</a> <a href="#rayhunter-effectiveness-in-the-5g-era" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Rayhunter Effectiveness in the 5G Era">#</a> </h2> <p>As cellular networks transition to 5G technology, questions arise about <strong>Rayhunter’s</strong> continued effectiveness given its support for 2G, 3G, and 4G/LTE detection but not native 5G surveillance methods. Understanding the current limitations and ongoing relevance of <strong>Rayhunter</strong> in a 5G world is crucial for planning long-term surveillance detection strategies.</p> <h3 id="why-rayhunter-remains-effective-in-5g-networks"> <a href="#why-rayhunter-remains-effective-in-5g-networks" title="Why Rayhunter Remains Effective in 5G Networks">Why Rayhunter Remains Effective in 5G Networks</a> <a href="#why-rayhunter-remains-effective-in-5g-networks" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Why Rayhunter Remains Effective in 5G Networks">#</a> </h3> <h4 id="downgrade-attack-persistence"> <a href="#downgrade-attack-persistence" title="Downgrade Attack Persistence">Downgrade Attack Persistence</a> <a href="#downgrade-attack-persistence" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Downgrade Attack Persistence">#</a> </h4> <p>The fundamental attack vector that <strong>Rayhunter</strong> detects - forcing devices to connect to less secure networks - remains highly relevant in 5G deployments. <strong>IMSI catchers continue to rely on 2G and 3G downgrade attacks</strong> because:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Lower implementation costs</strong>: Building surveillance equipment for older protocols requires significantly less investment than developing 5G-capable systems</li> <li><strong>Broader device compatibility</strong>: Targeting 2G/3G ensures surveillance works against older devices that may not support 5G</li> <li><strong>Established attack methodologies</strong>: Surveillance techniques for 2G/3G networks are well-documented and tested</li> <li><strong>Regulatory advantages</strong>: Some older surveillance technologies may face fewer legal restrictions than advanced 5G interception methods</li> </ul> <h4 id="network-backward-compatibility"> <a href="#network-backward-compatibility" title="Network Backward Compatibility">Network Backward Compatibility</a> <a href="#network-backward-compatibility" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Network Backward Compatibility">#</a> </h4> <p>5G networks maintain <strong>backward compatibility with 4G/LTE</strong>, and most mobile devices support multiple generations simultaneously. This compatibility creates ongoing opportunities for the downgrade attacks that <strong>Rayhunter</strong> specializes in detecting:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Automatic fallback mechanisms</strong>: Devices naturally fall back to 4G when 5G coverage is poor, creating opportunities for surveillance equipment to intercept these transitions</li> <li><strong>Protocol negotiation vulnerabilities</strong>: The handshake process between different network generations can be manipulated by sophisticated IMSI catchers</li> <li><strong>Coverage gap exploitation</strong>: Surveillance equipment strategically deployed in areas with incomplete 5G coverage can force devices onto monitored 4G/LTE networks</li> </ul> <h4 id="gradual-5g-deployment-timeline"> <a href="#gradual-5g-deployment-timeline" title="Gradual 5G Deployment Timeline">Gradual 5G Deployment Timeline</a> <a href="#gradual-5g-deployment-timeline" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Gradual 5G Deployment Timeline">#</a> </h4> <p>The <strong>slow rollout of comprehensive 5G coverage</strong> ensures <strong>Rayhunter</strong> remains relevant for years to come:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Urban vs. rural disparities</strong>: Many regions still rely primarily on 4G/LTE networks, making <strong>Rayhunter</strong> detection capabilities directly applicable</li> <li><strong>Indoor coverage limitations</strong>: 5G signals often struggle with building penetration, causing devices to fall back to 4G networks that <strong>Rayhunter</strong> can monitor effectively</li> <li><strong>International travel considerations</strong>: Global 5G deployment varies significantly by country, ensuring 4G/LTE surveillance remains a concern for travelers</li> </ul> <h4 id="threat-actor-technology-lag"> <a href="#threat-actor-technology-lag" title="Threat Actor Technology Lag">Threat Actor Technology Lag</a> <a href="#threat-actor-technology-lag" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Threat Actor Technology Lag">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Surveillance equipment acquisition and deployment cycles</strong> often lag behind commercial network technology:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Government procurement timelines</strong>: Law enforcement and intelligence agencies may continue using existing 4G-capable surveillance equipment for several years after 5G deployment</li> <li><strong>Cost-benefit analysis</strong>: Many surveillance operations achieve their objectives with less expensive 4G/LTE interception, reducing incentives to upgrade to 5G-capable systems</li> <li><strong>Training and expertise requirements</strong>: Operating 5G surveillance equipment requires specialized knowledge that may take time to develop within surveillance organizations</li> </ul> <h3 id="limitations-of-rayhunter-in-true-5g-environments"> <a href="#limitations-of-rayhunter-in-true-5g-environments" title="Limitations of Rayhunter in True 5G Environments">Limitations of Rayhunter in True 5G Environments</a> <a href="#limitations-of-rayhunter-in-true-5g-environments" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Limitations of Rayhunter in True 5G Environments">#</a> </h3> <h4 id="native-5g-surveillance-blind-spots"> <a href="#native-5g-surveillance-blind-spots" title="Native 5G Surveillance Blind Spots">Native 5G Surveillance Blind Spots</a> <a href="#native-5g-surveillance-blind-spots" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Native 5G Surveillance Blind Spots">#</a> </h4> <p><em>This is Rayhunter’s key limitation.</em> <strong>Rayhunter cannot detect native 5G IMSI catcher operations</strong> that don’t rely on downgrade attacks:</p> <ul> <li><strong>5G standalone (SA) networks</strong>: When devices connect to pure 5G networks without falling back to 4G, <strong>Rayhunter</strong> can’t monitor these communications</li> <li><strong>Advanced 5G surveillance equipment</strong>: Sophisticated threat actors with access to advanced technology could potentially intercept 5G communications without triggering <strong>Rayhunter’s</strong> detection heuristics</li> <li><strong>Network slicing exploitation</strong>: 5G network slicing capabilities could potentially be abused for surveillance purposes in ways that <strong>Rayhunter</strong> can’t detect</li> </ul> <h4 id="enhanced-5g-security-features"> <a href="#enhanced-5g-security-features" title="Enhanced 5G Security Features">Enhanced 5G Security Features</a> <a href="#enhanced-5g-security-features" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Enhanced 5G Security Features">#</a> </h4> <p>5G networks incorporate <strong>improved security measures</strong> that could reduce the effectiveness of traditional surveillance techniques:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Stronger encryption protocols</strong>: 5G implements more robust encryption standards that are harder to break than previous generations</li> <li><strong>Enhanced authentication procedures</strong>: Improved device authentication in 5G networks could make IMSI harvesting more difficult</li> <li><strong>Network security monitoring</strong>: 5G infrastructure includes better intrusion detection capabilities that might identify and counteract surveillance attempts</li> </ul> <h4 id="device-behavior-changes"> <a href="#device-behavior-changes" title="Device Behavior Changes">Device Behavior Changes</a> <a href="#device-behavior-changes" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Device Behavior Changes">#</a> </h4> <p>As 5G becomes more prevalent, <strong>mobile device behavior may evolve</strong> in ways that affect <strong>Rayhunter’s</strong> detection capabilities:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Reduced willingness to downgrade</strong>: Future device software updates might become more resistant to downgrade attacks as 5G coverage improves</li> <li><strong>5G-first connectivity preferences</strong>: Devices may increasingly prioritize 5G connections and become more suspicious of requests to use older network protocols</li> <li><strong>Enhanced security awareness</strong>: Mobile operating systems may implement better detection of suspicious network behavior</li> </ul> <h3 id="strategic-considerations-for-rayhunter-deployment"> <a href="#strategic-considerations-for-rayhunter-deployment" title="Strategic Considerations for Rayhunter Deployment">Strategic Considerations for Rayhunter Deployment</a> <a href="#strategic-considerations-for-rayhunter-deployment" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Strategic Considerations for Rayhunter Deployment">#</a> </h3> <h4 id="current-deployment-recommendations"> <a href="#current-deployment-recommendations" title="Current Deployment Recommendations">Current Deployment Recommendations</a> <a href="#current-deployment-recommendations" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Current Deployment Recommendations">#</a> </h4> <p>For immediate and near-term deployment, <strong>Rayhunter</strong> provides <strong>comprehensive surveillance detection capabilities</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Urban deployments</strong>: Even in major cities with extensive 5G coverage, <strong>Rayhunter</strong> detects surveillance attempts targeting 4G/LTE networks</li> <li><strong>Critical event protection</strong>: For protests, sensitive meetings, or high-risk activities, <strong>Rayhunter</strong> provides essential detection capabilities regardless of 5G availability</li> <li><strong>Travel security</strong>: <strong>Rayhunter</strong> remains highly effective for detecting surveillance during domestic and international travel</li> </ul> <h4 id="future-proofing-strategies"> <a href="#future-proofing-strategies" title="Future-Proofing Strategies">Future-Proofing Strategies</a> <a href="#future-proofing-strategies" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Future-Proofing Strategies">#</a> </h4> <p>Organizations and individuals planning long-term surveillance detection should consider:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Hybrid detection approaches</strong>: Combining <strong>Rayhunter</strong> with other security tools and monitoring techniques provides comprehensive coverage across multiple threat vectors</li> <li><strong>Regular threat assessment updates</strong>: Monitoring the evolution of surveillance technology helps inform decisions about when to supplement or replace <strong>Rayhunter</strong> capabilities</li> <li><strong>Community development support</strong>: Supporting <strong>Rayhunter’s</strong> open-source development increases the likelihood of 5G detection capabilities being added in future releases</li> </ul> <h4 id="technology-evolution-monitoring"> <a href="#technology-evolution-monitoring" title="Technology Evolution Monitoring">Technology Evolution Monitoring</a> <a href="#technology-evolution-monitoring" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Technology Evolution Monitoring">#</a> </h4> <p>The <strong>Rayhunter</strong> community and EFF continue researching <strong>5G surveillance detection capabilities</strong>:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Protocol analysis research</strong>: Ongoing research into 5G protocols may identify new detection opportunities for future <strong>Rayhunter</strong> versions</li> <li><strong>Hardware compatibility studies</strong>: Evaluating whether current <strong>Rayhunter</strong>-compatible devices can be enhanced to detect 5G surveillance attempts</li> <li><strong>Threat landscape monitoring</strong>: Tracking the development and deployment of 5G-capable surveillance equipment informs future <strong>Rayhunter</strong> development priorities</li> </ul> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> remains a <strong>critical surveillance detection tool</strong> in the current telecommunications landscape, with continued relevance expected for several years as 5G deployment progresses gradually and threat actors continue relying on proven downgrade attack methodologies.</p> <h3 id="re-analyzing-recordings"> <a href="#re-analyzing-recordings" title="Re-analyzing Recordings">Re-analyzing Recordings</a> <a href="#re-analyzing-recordings" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Re-analyzing Recordings">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> continuously improves its detection capabilities. You can re-analyze old recordings to benefit from updated heuristics by clicking “N warnings” in the web interface and selecting “re-analyze.”</p> <h3 id="desktop-analysis"> <a href="#desktop-analysis" title="Desktop Analysis">Desktop Analysis</a> <a href="#desktop-analysis" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Desktop Analysis">#</a> </h3> <p>For advanced users, <strong>Rayhunter</strong> includes <code>rayhunter-check</code>, a CLI tool for analyzing PCAP and QMDL files on desktop systems:</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span>rayhunter-check -p ~/Downloads/myfile.qmdl </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>rayhunter-check -p ~/Downloads/myfile.pcap </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>rayhunter-check -d -p ~/Downloads/myfile.qmdl <span style="color:#75715e"># Debug mode</span> </span></span></code></pre></div><h2 id="supported-devices"> <a href="#supported-devices" title="Supported Devices">Supported Devices</a> <a href="#supported-devices" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Supported Devices">#</a> </h2>
