If you have a device you’d like <strong>Rayhunter</strong> to support, open a discussion on the official GitHub repository.</p> <h2 id="rest-api-documentation"> <a href="#rest-api-documentation" title="REST API Documentation">REST API Documentation</a> <a href="#rest-api-documentation" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="REST API Documentation">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> provides a comprehensive REST API for programmatic access to device status, recordings, and configuration. The API enables integration with other security tools and automated monitoring systems.</p> <h3 id="official-api-documentation"> <a href="#official-api-documentation" title="Official API Documentation">Official API Documentation</a> <a href="#official-api-documentation" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Official API Documentation">#</a> </h3> <p>For complete API reference documentation, including detailed endpoint specifications, request/response examples, and authentication methods, visit the <strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Official Rayhunter REST API Documentation"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://efforg.github.io/rayhunter/api-docs/"> <a href="https://efforg.github.io/rayhunter/api-docs/" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Official Rayhunter REST API Documentation</a> </span> </strong>.</p> <h3 id="api-endpoints"> <a href="#api-endpoints" title="API Endpoints">API Endpoints</a> <a href="#api-endpoints" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="API Endpoints">#</a> </h3> <p>The <strong>Rayhunter</strong> API is accessible at <code>http://[device-ip]:8080/api/</code> and provides endpoints for:</p> <ul> <li><strong>System status</strong>: Monitor device health and <strong>Rayhunter</strong> operation</li> <li><strong>Recording management</strong>: Start, stop, and download recordings</li> <li><strong>Alert retrieval</strong>: Access detection alerts and analysis results</li> <li><strong>Configuration</strong>: Programmatically update <strong>Rayhunter</strong> settings</li> <li><strong>Heuristic control</strong>: Enable/disable specific detection algorithms</li> <li><strong>Device information</strong>: Retrieve device specifications and capabilities</li> <li><strong>Network monitoring</strong>: Access real-time cellular network data</li> <li><strong>Event logging</strong>: Query historical detection events and alerts</li> </ul> <h3 id="authentication"> <a href="#authentication" title="Authentication">Authentication</a> <a href="#authentication" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Authentication">#</a> </h3> <p>API access uses the same authentication as the web interface. Ensure your <strong>Rayhunter</strong> device is properly secured and accessible only to authorized users.</p> <h3 id="integration-examples"> <a href="#integration-examples" title="Integration Examples">Integration Examples</a> <a href="#integration-examples" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Integration Examples">#</a> </h3> <p>The <strong>Rayhunter</strong> REST API can be integrated with various security monitoring platforms, SIEM systems, and custom automation tools. Refer to the <strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="official API documentation"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://efforg.github.io/rayhunter/api-docs/"> <a href="https://efforg.github.io/rayhunter/api-docs/" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >official API documentation</a> </span> </strong> for complete implementation examples and best practices.</p> <h2 id="troubleshooting"> <a href="#troubleshooting" title="Troubleshooting">Troubleshooting</a> <a href="#troubleshooting" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Troubleshooting">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="common-installation-issues"> <a href="#common-installation-issues" title="Common Installation Issues">Common Installation Issues</a> <a href="#common-installation-issues" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Common Installation Issues">#</a> </h3> <p><strong>USB Connection Problems</strong>: Try different USB cables or ports. Faulty USB connections frequently cause installation failures. <em>This is the most common cause of failed installs.</em></p> <p><strong>macOS Security Issues</strong>: If you see “No Orbic device found,” temporarily change “Allow accessories to connect” to “Always” in security settings.</p> <p><strong>Device Detection</strong>: Enable the test heuristic after installation to verify <strong>Rayhunter</strong> is working properly.</p> <h3 id="getting-help"> <a href="#getting-help" title="Getting Help">Getting Help</a> <a href="#getting-help" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Getting Help">#</a> </h3> <p>For installation support and troubleshooting:</p> <ul> <li>Check the official <strong>Rayhunter</strong> documentation</li> <li>Use <code>./installer --help</code> and <code>./installer util --help</code> for command options</li> <li>Join the GitHub discussions for community support</li> <li>Contact <strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="STS Collective"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://stscollective.com"> <a href="https://stscollective.com" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >STS Collective</a> </span> </strong> for device compatibility questions</li> </ul> <h2 id="frequently-asked-questions-faq"> <a href="#frequently-asked-questions-faq" title="Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)">Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)</a> <a href="#frequently-asked-questions-faq" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)">#</a> </h2> <h3 id="general-questions"> <a href="#general-questions" title="General Questions">General Questions</a> <a href="#general-questions" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="General Questions">#</a> </h3> <h4 id="what-exactly-is-an-imsi-catcher-and-how-does-it-work"> <a href="#what-exactly-is-an-imsi-catcher-and-how-does-it-work" title="What exactly is an IMSI catcher and how does it work?">What exactly is an IMSI catcher and how does it work?</a> <a href="#what-exactly-is-an-imsi-catcher-and-how-does-it-work" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What exactly is an IMSI catcher and how does it work?">#</a> </h4> <p>An IMSI catcher (also called StingRay, cell site simulator, or fake cell tower) is surveillance equipment that mimics legitimate cellular towers to intercept mobile device communications. It forces nearby devices to connect to the malicious equipment instead of legitimate towers, allowing operators to harvest device identifiers (IMSI numbers), location data, call metadata, and potentially intercept communications. <strong>Rayhunter</strong> detects these devices by identifying abnormal cellular network behavior patterns.</p> <h4 id="can-rayhunter-detect-all-types-of-surveillance-equipment"> <a href="#can-rayhunter-detect-all-types-of-surveillance-equipment" title="Can Rayhunter detect all types of surveillance equipment?">Can Rayhunter detect all types of surveillance equipment?</a> <a href="#can-rayhunter-detect-all-types-of-surveillance-equipment" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Can Rayhunter detect all types of surveillance equipment?">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> specifically detects IMSI catchers and cell site simulators operating on 2G, 3G, and 4G/LTE networks. It can’t detect other types of surveillance such as WiFi monitoring, GPS tracking devices, physical surveillance, or native 5G surveillance that doesn’t use downgrade attacks. <strong>Rayhunter</strong> is one component of a comprehensive privacy protection strategy.</p> <h4 id="is-it-legal-to-use-rayhunter"> <a href="#is-it-legal-to-use-rayhunter" title="Is it legal to use Rayhunter?">Is it legal to use Rayhunter?</a> <a href="#is-it-legal-to-use-rayhunter" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Is it legal to use Rayhunter?">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> is completely legal to use in most jurisdictions as it’s a passive monitoring tool that doesn’t interfere with cellular networks or other devices. It simply analyzes cellular signals that your device receives naturally. However, local laws vary, so users should verify compatibility with their local regulations. The EFF developed <strong>Rayhunter</strong> as a legitimate privacy protection tool.</p> <h3 id="technical-questions"> <a href="#technical-questions" title="Technical Questions">Technical Questions</a> <a href="#technical-questions" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Technical Questions">#</a> </h3> <h4 id="why-does-rayhunter-require-a-sim-card-if-it-doesnt-need-active-service"> <a href="#why-does-rayhunter-require-a-sim-card-if-it-doesnt-need-active-service" title="Why does Rayhunter require a SIM card if it doesn’t need active service?">Why does Rayhunter require a SIM card if it doesn’t need active service?</a> <a href="#why-does-rayhunter-require-a-sim-card-if-it-doesnt-need-active-service" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Why does Rayhunter require a SIM card if it doesn’t need active service?">#</a> </h4> <p>The SIM card provides the International Mobile Subscriber Identity (IMSI) needed for your device to authenticate with cellular networks and participate in the cellular protocol exchanges that <strong>Rayhunter</strong> monitors. Without a SIM card, the device can’t connect to cellular networks and <strong>Rayhunter</strong> would have no network activity to analyze for suspicious patterns.</p> <h4 id="how-much-data-does-rayhunter-consume-during-normal-operation"> <a href="#how-much-data-does-rayhunter-consume-during-normal-operation" title="How much data does Rayhunter consume during normal operation?">How much data does Rayhunter consume during normal operation?</a> <a href="#how-much-data-does-rayhunter-consume-during-normal-operation" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="How much data does Rayhunter consume during normal operation?">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> uses minimal data for basic monitoring since it primarily analyzes cellular protocol information rather than internet traffic. However, if you enable remote notifications via ntfy or use the device as a hotspot simultaneously, data usage will increase accordingly. The monitoring itself consumes negligible bandwidth.</p> <h4 id="can-i-use-rayhunter-while-using-the-device-as-a-mobile-hotspot"> <a href="#can-i-use-rayhunter-while-using-the-device-as-a-mobile-hotspot" title="Can I use Rayhunter while using the device as a mobile hotspot?">Can I use Rayhunter while using the device as a mobile hotspot?</a> <a href="#can-i-use-rayhunter-while-using-the-device-as-a-mobile-hotspot" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Can I use Rayhunter while using the device as a mobile hotspot?">#</a> </h4> <p>Yes, <strong>Rayhunter</strong> can operate simultaneously while the device functions as a mobile hotspot. This requires an active cellular service plan. <strong>Rayhunter</strong> monitoring operates independently of hotspot functionality, though using both features simultaneously will consume more battery power.</p> <h4 id="whats-the-difference-between-wifi-and-usb-installation-methods"> <a href="#whats-the-difference-between-wifi-and-usb-installation-methods" title="What’s the difference between WiFi and USB installation methods?">What’s the difference between WiFi and USB installation methods?</a> <a href="#whats-the-difference-between-wifi-and-usb-installation-methods" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What’s the difference between WiFi and USB installation methods?">#</a> </h4> <p>The <strong>WiFi method</strong> (recommended for most users) installs <strong>Rayhunter</strong> over the device’s wireless interface and provides standard functionality. The <strong>USB method</strong> enables Android Debug Bridge (ADB) access for advanced users who need debugging capabilities but is more complex and not necessary for typical installations.</p> <h3 id="device-and-compatibility-questions"> <a href="#device-and-compatibility-questions" title="Device and Compatibility Questions">Device and Compatibility Questions</a> <a href="#device-and-compatibility-questions" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Device and Compatibility Questions">#</a> </h3> <h4 id="why-are-only-certain-devices-supported-by-rayhunter"> <a href="#why-are-only-certain-devices-supported-by-rayhunter" title="Why are only certain devices supported by Rayhunter?">Why are only certain devices supported by Rayhunter?</a> <a href="#why-are-only-certain-devices-supported-by-rayhunter" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Why are only certain devices supported by Rayhunter?">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> requires devices with Qualcomm modems that expose the <code>/dev/diag</code> interface, which provides access to cellular protocol information. Most consumer devices don’t expose this interface for security reasons. The supported devices have been specifically tested and confirmed to work with <strong>Rayhunter’s</strong> requirements.</p> <h4 id="can-i-install-rayhunter-on-my-regular-smartphone"> <a href="#can-i-install-rayhunter-on-my-regular-smartphone" title="Can I install Rayhunter on my regular smartphone?">Can I install Rayhunter on my regular smartphone?</a> <a href="#can-i-install-rayhunter-on-my-regular-smartphone" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Can I install Rayhunter on my regular smartphone?">#</a> </h4> <p>No, <strong>Rayhunter</strong> can’t be installed on regular smartphones. It requires specialized mobile hotspot devices with specific Qualcomm modems that expose diagnostic interfaces. Supported devices include the Orbic RC400L, TP-Link M7350, and several other mobile hotspot models.</p> <h4 id="what-happens-if-i-try-to-install-rayhunter-on-an-incompatible-device"> <a href="#what-happens-if-i-try-to-install-rayhunter-on-an-incompatible-device" title="What happens if I try to install Rayhunter on an incompatible device?">What happens if I try to install Rayhunter on an incompatible device?</a> <a href="#what-happens-if-i-try-to-install-rayhunter-on-an-incompatible-device" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What happens if I try to install Rayhunter on an incompatible device?">#</a> </h4> <p>The <strong>Rayhunter</strong> installer includes device detection and will typically refuse to install on incompatible hardware. Attempting to force installation on unsupported devices could potentially damage the device or render it inoperable. Always verify device compatibility before attempting installation.</p> <h4 id="do-i-need-different-devices-for-different-regions"> <a href="#do-i-need-different-devices-for-different-regions" title="Do I need different devices for different regions?">Do I need different devices for different regions?</a> <a href="#do-i-need-different-devices-for-different-regions" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Do I need different devices for different regions?">#</a> </h4> <p>Yes, cellular frequency bands vary by geographic region. The Orbic RC400L is optimized for Americas networks, while the TP-Link M7350 works well in Europe, Africa, and the Middle East. <strong>Rayhunter</strong> device compatibility information includes regional recommendations to ensure optimal performance in your location.</p> <h3 id="usage-and-configuration-questions"> <a href="#usage-and-configuration-questions" title="Usage and Configuration Questions">Usage and Configuration Questions</a> <a href="#usage-and-configuration-questions" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Usage and Configuration Questions">#</a> </h3> <h4 id="how-do-i-know-if-my-rayhunter-device-is-working-properly"> <a href="#how-do-i-know-if-my-rayhunter-device-is-working-properly" title="How do I know if my Rayhunter device is working properly?">How do I know if my Rayhunter device is working properly?</a> <a href="#how-do-i-know-if-my-rayhunter-device-is-working-properly" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="How do I know if my Rayhunter device is working properly?">#</a> </h4> <p>Enable the Test Heuristic in <strong>Rayhunter’s</strong> configuration settings. This will trigger alerts every time your device detects any cell tower, confirming the system is functioning. The device display should show a green line during normal operation. Remember to disable the test heuristic after verification as it produces many alerts.</p> <h4 id="what-should-i-do-if-rayhunter-triggers-an-alert"> <a href="#what-should-i-do-if-rayhunter-triggers-an-alert" title="What should I do if Rayhunter triggers an alert?">What should I do if Rayhunter triggers an alert?</a> <a href="#what-should-i-do-if-rayhunter-triggers-an-alert" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What should I do if Rayhunter triggers an alert?">#</a> </h4> <p>When <strong>Rayhunter</strong> detects suspicious activity, first note your location and circumstances. False positives can occur due to network optimization or equipment issues. If alerts persist in the same location or during sensitive activities, consider changing locations and reviewing the alert details in the web interface to understand what was detected.</p> <h4 id="how-often-should-i-update-rayhunter"> <a href="#how-often-should-i-update-rayhunter" title="How often should I update Rayhunter?">How often should I update Rayhunter?</a> <a href="#how-often-should-i-update-rayhunter" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="How often should I update Rayhunter?">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Update monthly.</strong> Check the releases page for new heuristics and compatibility improvements as new detection heuristics and device compatibility improvements are added frequently. The EFF continuously improves <strong>Rayhunter’s</strong> capabilities based on evolving surveillance techniques. Updating is identical to the initial installation process.</p> <h4 id="can-rayhunter-protect-multiple-people-simultaneously"> <a href="#can-rayhunter-protect-multiple-people-simultaneously" title="Can Rayhunter protect multiple people simultaneously?">Can Rayhunter protect multiple people simultaneously?</a> <a href="#can-rayhunter-protect-multiple-people-simultaneously" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Can Rayhunter protect multiple people simultaneously?">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> detects IMSI catchers in the surrounding area, so it can potentially protect multiple people within range (typically a few hundred meters depending on signal strength). However, each individual serious about surveillance detection should consider having their own <strong>Rayhunter</strong> device for maximum protection and configuration control.</p> <h3 id="security-and-privacy-questions"> <a href="#security-and-privacy-questions" title="Security and Privacy Questions">Security and Privacy Questions</a> <a href="#security-and-privacy-questions" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Security and Privacy Questions">#</a> </h3> <h4 id="does-using-rayhunter-make-me-more-conspicuous-to-surveillance"> <a href="#does-using-rayhunter-make-me-more-conspicuous-to-surveillance" title="Does using Rayhunter make me more conspicuous to surveillance?">Does using Rayhunter make me more conspicuous to surveillance?</a> <a href="#does-using-rayhunter-make-me-more-conspicuous-to-surveillance" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Does using Rayhunter make me more conspicuous to surveillance?">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> operates passively and doesn’t broadcast signals that would identify it to surveillance equipment. However, using any security tool indicates privacy consciousness. The benefits of detection typically outweigh risks, especially for high-risk individuals like journalists and activists.</p> <h4 id="can-surveillance-equipment-detect-that-im-using-rayhunter"> <a href="#can-surveillance-equipment-detect-that-im-using-rayhunter" title="Can surveillance equipment detect that I’m using Rayhunter?">Can surveillance equipment detect that I’m using Rayhunter?</a> <a href="#can-surveillance-equipment-detect-that-im-using-rayhunter" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Can surveillance equipment detect that I’m using Rayhunter?">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> is a passive monitoring system that doesn’t transmit identifying signals. Surveillance operators can’t detect <strong>Rayhunter</strong> usage from the cellular protocol level. Physical observation of the device or network traffic analysis might reveal <strong>Rayhunter</strong> usage if remote notifications are enabled.</p> <h4 id="what-information-does-rayhunter-collect-about-me"> <a href="#what-information-does-rayhunter-collect-about-me" title="What information does Rayhunter collect about me?">What information does Rayhunter collect about me?</a> <a href="#what-information-does-rayhunter-collect-about-me" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What information does Rayhunter collect about me?">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> analyzes cellular protocol information and stores detection alerts locally on the device. It doesn’t collect personal information, communications content, or location data beyond what’s necessary for detection algorithms. All data remains on your device unless you choose to enable remote notifications.</p> <h3 id="advanced-questions"> <a href="#advanced-questions" title="Advanced Questions">Advanced Questions</a> <a href="#advanced-questions" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Advanced Questions">#</a> </h3> <h4 id="can-i-analyze-rayhunter-data-on-my-computer"> <a href="#can-i-analyze-rayhunter-data-on-my-computer" title="Can I analyze Rayhunter data on my computer?">Can I analyze Rayhunter data on my computer?</a> <a href="#can-i-analyze-rayhunter-data-on-my-computer" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Can I analyze Rayhunter data on my computer?">#</a> </h4> <p>Yes, <strong>Rayhunter</strong> includes the <code>rayhunter-check</code> command-line tool for analyzing recorded PCAP and QMDL files on desktop systems. This enables advanced users to perform detailed analysis of detection events and investigate suspicious activity using full computer resources.</p> <h4 id="how-does-rayhunter-perform-in-dense-urban-environments"> <a href="#how-does-rayhunter-perform-in-dense-urban-environments" title="How does Rayhunter perform in dense urban environments?">How does Rayhunter perform in dense urban environments?</a> <a href="#how-does-rayhunter-perform-in-dense-urban-environments" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="How does Rayhunter perform in dense urban environments?">#</a> </h4> <p>Dense urban areas with many legitimate cell towers can increase false positive rates as network optimization and tower handoffs occur more frequently. <strong>Rayhunter’s</strong> algorithms are designed to distinguish between normal network behavior and surveillance activity, but users in dense areas may need to adjust sensitivity settings.</p> <h4 id="will-rayhunter-work-during-international-travel"> <a href="#will-rayhunter-work-during-international-travel" title="Will Rayhunter work during international travel?">Will Rayhunter work during international travel?</a> <a href="#will-rayhunter-work-during-international-travel" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Will Rayhunter work during international travel?">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> effectiveness during travel depends on device compatibility with local cellular bands and roaming agreements. The detection algorithms work globally, but ensure your device supports the frequency bands used in your destination country. International roaming may be required for full functionality.</p> <hr> <p><strong>Rayhunter</strong> represents a crucial advancement in personal privacy protection and surveillance detection. By properly flashing and configuring <strong>Rayhunter</strong> on compatible devices, you gain powerful capabilities to detect IMSI catchers and protect your mobile communications from unauthorized surveillance.</p> <p>Whether you’re a journalist, activist, security professional, or privacy-conscious individual, <strong>Rayhunter</strong> provides the tools necessary to maintain mobile security in an increasingly surveilled world. For verified compatible devices, check out <strong> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Rayhunter for sale"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://stscollective.com"> <a href="https://stscollective.com" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Rayhunter for sale</a> </span> </strong> options from trusted security equipment suppliers.</p> <h2 id="references"> <a href="#references" title="References">References</a> <a href="#references" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="References">#</a> </h2> <ul> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Rayhunter Official GitHub Repository"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://github.com/EFForg/rayhunter"> <a href="https://github.com/EFForg/rayhunter" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Rayhunter Official GitHub Repository</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Latest Rayhunter Release"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://github.com/EFForg/rayhunter/releases/tag/v0.10.2"> <a href="https://github.com/EFForg/rayhunter/releases/tag/v0.10.2" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Latest Rayhunter Release</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Electronic Frontier Foundation"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://www.eff.org/"> <a href="https://www.eff.org/" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >Electronic Frontier Foundation</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="STS Collective - Rayhunter Equipment Supplier"> <meta itemprop="url" content="https://stscollective.com"> <a href="https://stscollective.com" rel="noopener external" target="_blank" >STS Collective - Rayhunter Equipment Supplier</a> </span> </li> </ul> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.ch/

item.title: CEH v13: Cloud Computing Threats
item.category: "EC-Council CEH Course" , "Ethical Hacking" , "Penetration Testing" , "Offensive Security" , "Cybersecurity" , "Network Security" , "Security Testing" , "Hacking Techniques" , "CEH Certification" , "Information Security"
item.description: Master cloud computing threats for the EC-Council Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) exam. Learn the shared responsibility model, cloud misconfigurations, IAM weaknesses, ScoutSuite, Pacu, container escape, and CSPM.
item.content:encoded: <h4 id="click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page"> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page" title=" Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page "> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh-start/"> <a href="/ceh-start/" rel="follow me" >Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page</a> </span> </a> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title=" Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page ">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Cloud Computing Threats</strong> targets cloud environments in the <strong>EC-Council CEH v13</strong> course. This module covers the shared responsibility model, common misconfigurations, identity attacks, container risks, and the controls that secure cloud workloads. <em>Cloud providers require written authorization and often a formal pen-test request before any testing, so confirm the rules before you scan.</em></p> <p>Most cloud breaches come from customer misconfiguration, not a flaw in the provider. You learn where those gaps appear and how attackers exploit them.</p> <h2 id="shared-responsibility-model"> <a href="#shared-responsibility-model" title="Shared Responsibility Model">Shared Responsibility Model</a> <a href="#shared-responsibility-model" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Shared Responsibility Model">#</a> </h2> <p>The provider secures the cloud, and you secure what you put in it. The split shifts by service model.</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Model</th> <th>Provider secures</th> <th>You secure</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td><strong>IaaS</strong></td> <td>Hardware, hypervisor</td> <td>OS, apps, data, IAM</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>PaaS</strong></td> <td>OS, runtime</td> <td>Apps, data, access</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>SaaS</strong></td> <td>Most of the stack</td> <td>Data, user access</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p><em>A misread of this model is the root cause of most cloud incidents.</em></p> <p>To compare the major platforms, see <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="AWS vs Azure vs Google Cloud Platform"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/articles/aws-vs-azure-vs-google-cloud-platform/"> <a href="/articles/aws-vs-azure-vs-google-cloud-platform/" rel="follow me" >AWS vs Azure vs Google Cloud Platform</a> </span> .</p> <h2 id="common-cloud-misconfigurations"> <a href="#common-cloud-misconfigurations" title="Common Cloud Misconfigurations">Common Cloud Misconfigurations</a> <a href="#common-cloud-misconfigurations" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Common Cloud Misconfigurations">#</a> </h2> <p>Attackers scan for predictable mistakes that expose data and control.</p> <ul> <li><strong>Open storage buckets</strong> leave S3 or blob storage public to the internet.</li> <li><strong>Exposed APIs</strong> and metadata endpoints leak credentials and tokens.</li> <li><strong>Weak IAM policies</strong> grant broad permissions with wildcards.</li> <li><strong>Hardcoded keys</strong> sit in source code, containers, and config files.</li> </ul> <p>The <strong>instance metadata service</strong> is a frequent target. A server-side request forgery flaw can pull temporary credentials from it.</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Classic IMDSv1 credential theft from a vulnerable host</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ </span></span></code></pre></div><p>IMDSv2 requires a session token and blocks this simple request, so enforce it.</p> <h2 id="cloud-auditing-tools"> <a href="#cloud-auditing-tools" title="Cloud Auditing Tools">Cloud Auditing Tools</a> <a href="#cloud-auditing-tools" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Cloud Auditing Tools">#</a> </h2> <p>You assess cloud posture with purpose-built tools.</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Tool</th> <th>Use</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td><strong>ScoutSuite</strong></td> <td>Multi-cloud configuration audit</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Pacu</strong></td> <td>AWS exploitation framework</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>CloudSploit</strong></td> <td>Misconfiguration scanning</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p>These tools map permissions, find public resources, and flag risky settings across an account.</p> <h2 id="container-and-serverless-risks"> <a href="#container-and-serverless-risks" title="Container and Serverless Risks">Container and Serverless Risks</a> <a href="#container-and-serverless-risks" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Container and Serverless Risks">#</a> </h2> <p>Containers add their own attack surface. <strong>Container escape</strong> breaks out of a container to the host when the runtime is misconfigured or over-privileged.</p> <ul> <li><strong>Privileged containers</strong> share the host kernel with full rights.</li> <li><strong>Exposed Docker sockets</strong> hand an attacker control of the host.</li> <li><strong>Vulnerable images</strong> ship with known CVEs and embedded secrets.</li> </ul> <p>To understand the isolation tradeoffs, read <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Docker vs VMs"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/articles/docker-vs-vms/"> <a href="/articles/docker-vs-vms/" rel="follow me" >Docker vs VMs</a> </span> .</p> <h2 id="cloud-defense"> <a href="#cloud-defense" title="Cloud Defense">Cloud Defense</a> <a href="#cloud-defense" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Cloud Defense">#</a> </h2> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Control</th> <th>Purpose</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td><strong>CSPM</strong></td> <td>Finds and fixes misconfigurations continuously</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Least privilege</strong></td> <td>Limits IAM roles to required actions</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Encryption</strong></td> <td>Protects data at rest and in transit</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Logging</strong></td> <td>Records API calls for detection and forensics</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p>Least-privilege IAM and continuous posture management stop most cloud attacks before they start.</p> <h2 id="next-steps"> <a href="#next-steps" title="Next Steps">Next Steps</a> <a href="#next-steps" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Next Steps">#</a> </h2> <p>Continue with <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Cryptography and PKI"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh/cryptography-and-pki/"> <a href="/ceh/cryptography-and-pki/" rel="follow me" >Cryptography and PKI</a> </span> . Review the previous module on <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="IoT and OT Hacking"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh/iot-ot-hacking/"> <a href="/ceh/iot-ot-hacking/" rel="follow me" >IoT and OT Hacking</a> </span> , compare providers in <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="AWS vs Azure vs Google Cloud Platform"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/articles/aws-vs-azure-vs-google-cloud-platform/"> <a href="/articles/aws-vs-azure-vs-google-cloud-platform/" rel="follow me" >AWS vs Azure vs Google Cloud Platform</a> </span> , and study isolation in <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Docker vs VMs"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/articles/docker-vs-vms/"> <a href="/articles/docker-vs-vms/" rel="follow me" >Docker vs VMs</a> </span> . Return to the <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh-start/"> <a href="/ceh-start/" rel="follow me" >Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course</a> </span> .</p> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.ch/

item.title: CEH v13: Denial-of-Service
item.category: "EC-Council CEH Course" , "Ethical Hacking" , "Penetration Testing" , "Offensive Security" , "Cybersecurity" , "Network Security" , "Security Testing" , "Hacking Techniques" , "CEH Certification" , "Information Security"
item.description: Master denial-of-service for the EC-Council Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) exam. Learn DoS vs DDoS, volumetric, protocol, and application attacks, amplification, botnets, and defenses.
item.content:encoded: <h4 id="click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page"> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page" title=" Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page "> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh-start/"> <a href="/ceh-start/" rel="follow me" >Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page</a> </span> </a> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title=" Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page ">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Denial-of-Service</strong> attacks disrupt availability in the <strong>EC-Council CEH v13</strong> course. This module covers DoS and DDoS techniques, attack categories, botnets, and the defenses that absorb them. <em>Run flooding tools only against isolated lab systems you own, never against live targets.</em></p> <p>A denial-of-service attack overwhelms a system so legitimate users lose access. It targets the <strong>availability</strong> pillar of the CIA triad rather than stealing data.</p> <h2 id="dos-vs-ddos"> <a href="#dos-vs-ddos" title="DoS vs. DDoS">DoS vs. DDoS</a> <a href="#dos-vs-ddos" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="DoS vs. DDoS">#</a> </h2> <p>The difference is the number of sources.</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Attack</th> <th>Sources</th> <th>Harder to block</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td><strong>DoS</strong></td> <td>One host</td> <td>No</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>DDoS</strong></td> <td>Many hosts (a botnet)</td> <td>Yes</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p>A <strong>botnet</strong> is a network of compromised devices a <strong>command-and-control</strong> server directs to flood a target at once.</p> <h2 id="attack-categories"> <a href="#attack-categories" title="Attack Categories">Attack Categories</a> <a href="#attack-categories" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Attack Categories">#</a> </h2> <p>You group attacks by the layer they hit.</p> <ul> <li><strong>Volumetric</strong> attacks flood bandwidth (UDP floods, ICMP floods).</li> <li><strong>Protocol</strong> attacks exhaust connection tables (SYN floods).</li> <li><strong>Application-layer</strong> attacks exhaust server resources with valid-looking requests (HTTP floods).</li> </ul> <p><strong>Amplification and reflection</strong> make small requests produce huge responses. An attacker spoofs the victim’s IP, queries open DNS or NTP servers, and those servers blast large replies at the victim.</p> <h2 id="tools-in-the-lab"> <a href="#tools-in-the-lab" title="Tools in the Lab">Tools in the Lab</a> <a href="#tools-in-the-lab" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Tools in the Lab">#</a> </h2> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Tool</th> <th>Use</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td><strong>hping3</strong></td> <td>Crafts custom flood packets</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>LOIC / HOIC</strong></td> <td>Simple flooding utilities</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Slowloris</strong></td> <td>Holds many slow connections open</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Lab-only SYN flood simulation against an isolated host</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>sudo hping3 -S --flood -p <span style="color:#ae81ff">80</span> 10.0.0.5 </span></span></code></pre></div><h2 id="defenses"> <a href="#defenses" title="Defenses">Defenses</a> <a href="#defenses" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Defenses">#</a> </h2> <p>You absorb and filter attack traffic with layered controls.</p> <ul> <li><strong>Rate limiting</strong> caps requests per source.</li> <li><strong>Scrubbing centers</strong> filter malicious traffic before it reaches you.</li> <li><strong>Anycast</strong> spreads traffic across many data centers.</li> <li><strong>A CDN</strong> absorbs volumetric load at the edge.</li> </ul> <p><em>Defense works best upstream, because a flood that fills your link cannot be stopped at your firewall.</em></p> <h2 id="next-steps"> <a href="#next-steps" title="Next Steps">Next Steps</a> <a href="#next-steps" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Next Steps">#</a> </h2> <p>Take over live sessions in <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Session Hijacking"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh/session-hijacking/"> <a href="/ceh/session-hijacking/" rel="follow me" >Session Hijacking</a> </span> . Revisit human attacks in <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Social Engineering"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh/social-engineering/"> <a href="/ceh/social-engineering/" rel="follow me" >Social Engineering</a> </span> . Return to the <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh-start/"> <a href="/ceh-start/" rel="follow me" >Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course</a> </span> .</p> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.ch/

item.title: CEH v13: Enumeration
item.category: "EC-Council CEH Course" , "Ethical Hacking" , "Penetration Testing" , "Offensive Security" , "Cybersecurity" , "Network Security" , "Security Testing" , "Hacking Techniques" , "CEH Certification" , "Information Security"
item.description: Master enumeration for the EC-Council Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) exam. Learn NetBIOS, SNMP, LDAP, SMB, and DNS enumeration with tools like enum4linux, SNMPwalk, and NBTscan.
item.content:encoded: <h4 id="click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page"> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page" title=" Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page "> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh-start/"> <a href="/ceh-start/" rel="follow me" >Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page</a> </span> </a> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title=" Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page ">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Enumeration</strong> extracts detailed information from discovered services in the <strong>EC-Council CEH v13</strong> course. This module covers how you pull usernames, shares, groups, and configuration data from exposed protocols. <em>Enumeration is active and noisy, so it only belongs inside an authorized engagement.</em></p> <p>Scanning tells you a port is open. Enumeration connects to that service and asks it for specifics like account names, share lists, and software versions. These details fuel the attack phase.</p> <h2 id="common-enumeration-targets"> <a href="#common-enumeration-targets" title="Common Enumeration Targets">Common Enumeration Targets</a> <a href="#common-enumeration-targets" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Common Enumeration Targets">#</a> </h2> <p>Each protocol leaks different data when it is misconfigured.</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Protocol</th> <th>Port</th> <th>What you extract</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td><strong>NetBIOS</strong></td> <td>137-139</td> <td>Computer names, shares</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>SMB</strong></td> <td>445</td> <td>Shares, users, groups</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>SNMP</strong></td> <td>161</td> <td>Device config, interfaces, routes</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>LDAP</strong></td> <td>389</td> <td>Directory users and structure</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>DNS</strong></td> <td>53</td> <td>Records, zone data</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>SMTP</strong></td> <td>25</td> <td>Valid email accounts</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <h2 id="smb-and-netbios-enumeration"> <a href="#smb-and-netbios-enumeration" title="SMB and NetBIOS Enumeration">SMB and NetBIOS Enumeration</a> <a href="#smb-and-netbios-enumeration" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="SMB and NetBIOS Enumeration">#</a> </h2> <p>Windows file sharing exposes a wealth of data when null sessions are allowed.</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Enumerate shares, users, and groups over SMB</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>enum4linux -a 192.168.1.10 </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>nbtscan 192.168.1.0/24 </span></span></code></pre></div><p>A <strong>null session</strong> connects without credentials and lists users and shares on older or misconfigured systems. You map shares, then check which allow anonymous read or write.</p> <h2 id="snmp-and-ldap-enumeration"> <a href="#snmp-and-ldap-enumeration" title="SNMP and LDAP Enumeration">SNMP and LDAP Enumeration</a> <a href="#snmp-and-ldap-enumeration" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="SNMP and LDAP Enumeration">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>SNMP</strong> with a default community string like <code>public</code> reveals routing tables, running processes, and interface details.</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Walk the SNMP tree with the public community string</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>snmpwalk -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1 </span></span></code></pre></div><p><strong>LDAP</strong> enumeration queries the directory for users, groups, and the organizational structure, which maps targets for password attacks.</p> <h2 id="dns-zone-transfers"> <a href="#dns-zone-transfers" title="DNS Zone Transfers">DNS Zone Transfers</a> <a href="#dns-zone-transfers" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="DNS Zone Transfers">#</a> </h2> <p>A misconfigured name server allows a <strong>zone transfer</strong> that dumps every record in a domain.</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Attempt a zone transfer against a name server</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>dig axfr example.com @ns1.example.com </span></span></code></pre></div><p>A successful transfer hands you internal hostnames and IP addresses. <em>Most servers block this, but one that allows it gives you the whole map.</em></p> <h2 id="next-steps"> <a href="#next-steps" title="Next Steps">Next Steps</a> <a href="#next-steps" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Next Steps">#</a> </h2> <p>Turn this detail into a list of weaknesses in <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Vulnerability Analysis"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh/vulnerability-analysis/"> <a href="/ceh/vulnerability-analysis/" rel="follow me" >Vulnerability Analysis</a> </span> . Revisit port discovery in <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Scanning Networks"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh/scanning-networks/"> <a href="/ceh/scanning-networks/" rel="follow me" >Scanning Networks</a> </span> . Return to the <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh-start/"> <a href="/ceh-start/" rel="follow me" >Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course</a> </span> .</p> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.ch/

item.title: CEH v13: Evading IDS, Firewalls, and Honeypots
item.category: "EC-Council CEH Course" , "Ethical Hacking" , "Penetration Testing" , "Offensive Security" , "Cybersecurity" , "Network Security" , "Security Testing" , "Hacking Techniques" , "CEH Certification" , "Information Security"
item.description: Master evading IDS, firewalls, and honeypots for the EC-Council Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) exam. Learn IDS/IPS detection, packet fragmentation, firewall bypass, tunneling, and honeypot identification.
item.content:encoded: <h4 id="click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page"> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page" title=" Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page "> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh-start/"> <a href="/ceh-start/" rel="follow me" >Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page</a> </span> </a> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title=" Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page ">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Evading IDS, Firewalls, and Honeypots</strong> covers bypassing network defenses in the <strong>EC-Council CEH v13</strong> course. This module covers how these controls detect attacks and how testers slip past them. <em>Evasion testing belongs only in an authorized engagement against in-scope systems.</em></p> <p>Defenders deploy detection and filtering between you and the target. You learn how each control works so you measure whether it catches a real attack.</p> <h2 id="how-the-defenses-work"> <a href="#how-the-defenses-work" title="How the Defenses Work">How the Defenses Work</a> <a href="#how-the-defenses-work" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="How the Defenses Work">#</a> </h2> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Control</th> <th>Role</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td><strong>IDS</strong></td> <td>Detects and alerts on suspicious traffic</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>IPS</strong></td> <td>Detects and blocks in real time</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Firewall</strong></td> <td>Filters traffic by rules</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Honeypot</strong></td> <td>A decoy that lures and records attackers</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p>An IDS uses <strong>signature-based</strong> detection to match known patterns and <strong>anomaly-based</strong> detection to flag unusual behavior.</p> <h2 id="ids-and-ips-evasion"> <a href="#ids-and-ips-evasion" title="IDS and IPS Evasion">IDS and IPS Evasion</a> <a href="#ids-and-ips-evasion" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="IDS and IPS Evasion">#</a> </h2> <p>You shape traffic so it does not match a signature.</p> <ul> <li><strong>Packet fragmentation</strong> splits an attack across packets the IDS fails to reassemble.</li> <li><strong>TTL manipulation</strong> expires packets after the IDS but before the target.</li> <li><strong>Encoding and obfuscation</strong> disguise payloads (URL encoding, Unicode).</li> </ul> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Nmap fragmented scan with a decoy and a spoofed source port</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>nmap -f -D RND:5 --source-port <span style="color:#ae81ff">53</span> 192.168.1.10 </span></span></code></pre></div><h2 id="firewall-bypass"> <a href="#firewall-bypass" title="Firewall Bypass">Firewall Bypass</a> <a href="#firewall-bypass" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Firewall Bypass">#</a> </h2> <p>You abuse what the firewall already allows.</p> <ul> <li><strong>Tunneling</strong> wraps traffic inside an allowed protocol like DNS or HTTPS.</li> <li><strong>Port knocking</strong> sends a secret sequence to open a hidden port.</li> <li><strong>Allowed-protocol abuse</strong> rides outbound ports such as 443 that are rarely blocked.</li> </ul> <h2 id="identifying-honeypots"> <a href="#identifying-honeypots" title="Identifying Honeypots">Identifying Honeypots</a> <a href="#identifying-honeypots" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Identifying Honeypots">#</a> </h2> <p>A honeypot wastes your time and records your moves, so you learn to spot one.</p> <ul> <li>It exposes too many services with no real traffic.</li> <li>Responses look inconsistent or unusually inviting.</li> <li>The system has no real users or production data.</li> </ul> <p>You study <strong>Snort rules</strong> to understand what defenders watch for, then verify whether crafted traffic triggers an alert. <em>If a target looks too easy, treat it as a honeypot until proven otherwise.</em></p> <h2 id="next-steps"> <a href="#next-steps" title="Next Steps">Next Steps</a> <a href="#next-steps" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Next Steps">#</a> </h2> <p>Begin platform attacks with <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Hacking Web Servers"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh/hacking-web-servers/"> <a href="/ceh/hacking-web-servers/" rel="follow me" >Hacking Web Servers</a> </span> . Compare real defensive appliances in <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Fortinet vs Cisco network security"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/articles/fortinet-vs-cisco-network-security-comparison/"> <a href="/articles/fortinet-vs-cisco-network-security-comparison/" rel="follow me" >Fortinet vs Cisco network security</a> </span> . Return to the <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh-start/"> <a href="/ceh-start/" rel="follow me" >Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course</a> </span> .</p> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.ch/

item.title: CEH v13: Footprinting and Reconnaissance
item.category: "EC-Council CEH Course" , "Ethical Hacking" , "Penetration Testing" , "Offensive Security" , "Cybersecurity" , "Network Security" , "Security Testing" , "Hacking Techniques" , "CEH Certification" , "Information Security"
item.description: Master footprinting and reconnaissance for the EC-Council Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) exam. Learn passive and active recon, OSINT, Google dorking, WHOIS, DNS, and tools like Maltego, theHarvester, and Shodan.
item.content:encoded: <h4 id="click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page"> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page" title=" Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page "> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh-start/"> <a href="/ceh-start/" rel="follow me" >Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page</a> </span> </a> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-certified-ethical-hacker-ceh-v13-course-page" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title=" Click Here to Return To the Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course Page ">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Footprinting and Reconnaissance</strong> is the first phase of an engagement in the <strong>EC-Council CEH v13</strong> course. This module covers how you gather information about a target before sending a single attack packet. <em>The more you learn here, the less noise you make later.</em></p> <p>Reconnaissance builds a profile of the target: domains, IP ranges, employees, technologies, and exposed services. Good recon often decides whether a test succeeds.</p> <h2 id="passive-vs-active-reconnaissance"> <a href="#passive-vs-active-reconnaissance" title="Passive vs. Active Reconnaissance">Passive vs. Active Reconnaissance</a> <a href="#passive-vs-active-reconnaissance" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Passive vs. Active Reconnaissance">#</a> </h2> <p>You split recon by how much you touch the target.</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Type</th> <th>Contact with target</th> <th>Detection risk</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td><strong>Passive</strong></td> <td>None, uses third-party sources</td> <td>Very low</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Active</strong></td> <td>Direct queries to target systems</td> <td>Higher</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p>Passive recon uses <strong>OSINT</strong> (open-source intelligence) from search engines, social media, job posts, and public records. Active recon includes DNS queries and banner grabbing that reach the target.</p> <h2 id="osint-and-google-dorking"> <a href="#osint-and-google-dorking" title="OSINT and Google Dorking">OSINT and Google Dorking</a> <a href="#osint-and-google-dorking" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="OSINT and Google Dorking">#</a> </h2> <p>Search operators turn a search engine into a recon tool. This is <strong>Google dorking</strong>.</p> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-text" data-lang="text"><span style="display:flex;"><span>site:example.com filetype:pdf </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>intitle:"index of" site:example.com </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>inurl:admin site:example.com </span></span></code></pre></div><p>These find exposed documents, open directories, and login pages. Job listings reveal the technology stack, and employee names feed later social engineering.</p> <h2 id="dns-whois-and-network-records"> <a href="#dns-whois-and-network-records" title="DNS, WHOIS, and Network Records">DNS, WHOIS, and Network Records</a> <a href="#dns-whois-and-network-records" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="DNS, WHOIS, and Network Records">#</a> </h2> <p>You map the target’s external footprint with public records.</p> <ul> <li><strong>WHOIS</strong> returns registrar, contact, and registration dates for a domain.</li> <li><strong>DNS records</strong> (A, MX, NS, TXT) reveal mail servers, name servers, and services.</li> <li><strong>BGP</strong> data exposes the IP ranges an organization owns.</li> </ul> <div class="highlight"><pre tabindex="0" style="color:#f8f8f2;background-color:#272822;-moz-tab-size:4;-o-tab-size:4;tab-size:4;-webkit-text-size-adjust:none;"><code class="language-bash" data-lang="bash"><span style="display:flex;"><span><span style="color:#75715e"># Pull DNS records and WHOIS data</span> </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>dig example.com ANY +noall +answer </span></span><span style="display:flex;"><span>whois example.com </span></span></code></pre></div><h2 id="recon-tooling"> <a href="#recon-tooling" title="Recon Tooling">Recon Tooling</a> <a href="#recon-tooling" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Recon Tooling">#</a> </h2> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Tool</th> <th>Use</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td><strong>Maltego</strong></td> <td>Visual link analysis of people and infrastructure</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>theHarvester</strong></td> <td>Collects emails, subdomains, and hosts</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Shodan</strong></td> <td>Indexes internet-exposed devices and banners</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Recon-ng</strong></td> <td>Modular OSINT framework</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p>Try the on-site <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Shodan IP lookup tool"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/shodan_ip/"> <a href="/shodan_ip/" rel="follow me" >Shodan IP lookup tool</a> </span> to see exposed service data for an address. <em>Use these tools only against assets in your authorized scope.</em></p> <h2 id="next-steps"> <a href="#next-steps" title="Next Steps">Next Steps</a> <a href="#next-steps" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Next Steps">#</a> </h2> <p>Move from passive recon to active discovery in <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Scanning Networks"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh/scanning-networks/"> <a href="/ceh/scanning-networks/" rel="follow me" >Scanning Networks</a> </span> . Review the legal limits in <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Introduction to Ethical Hacking"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh/introduction-to-ethical-hacking/"> <a href="/ceh/introduction-to-ethical-hacking/" rel="follow me" >Introduction to Ethical Hacking</a> </span> . Return to the <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/ceh-start/"> <a href="/ceh-start/" rel="follow me" >Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH v13) Course</a> </span> .</p> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.ch/
